This is the precise meaning of holographic thinking in the manuscript brain: the same underlying identity-bearing object admits several lawful coordinate presentations, each revealing different structure, and none treated as the whole reality by itself. The lens calculus does not merely support alternative descriptions. It makes them formal, interoperable, transportable, and replayable.
CHAPTER 5 — LENS CALCULUS AND CONTAINER THEORY
Chapter 6 — Holographic Expansion, Collapse, and Tunneling
A manuscript brain cannot be called holographic merely because it stores many cross references or because it can summarize and re-expand text. Holography, in the present framework, is a stricter property. It means that a lawful seed, local fragment, or route bundle may be expanded into an addressable neighborhood of the atlas, that this neighborhood may be collapsed back into a stable canonical form without losing the declared identity core, and that nonlocal transitions between distant but invariant-compatible regions may occur through admissible tunnel operators rather than only by exhaustive stepwise traversal of every intermediate cell. The present chapter formalizes these three operations—expansion, collapse, and tunneling—as a single operator family governing the dynamic life cycle of manuscript states.
The foundational object introduced here is the holographic cycle. Let
z\in \mathbb{Z}
\ell\in\{\Square,\Flower,\Cloud,\Fractal\}
z
\xrightarrow{\;\mathcal{E}_{\ell,\kappa,B}\;}
X_\ell
\xrightarrow{\;\mathcal{R}_{\ell,\kappa}\;}
\mathcal{R}_\ell
\xrightarrow{\;\mathcal{C}_{\ell,\kappa}\;}
\widehat{z},
is the lens-specific expansion operator,
is the lens container field produced by expansion,
is the route-generation or route-execution stage,
is the resulting route bundle family, and
is the collapse operator that returns a canonical output, seed, or stabilized state.
The principal holographic law is:
\widehat{z}\approx_\kappa z
The chapter is organized around four distinct but coupled interpretations of this cycle. Under the Square lens, expansion and collapse are exact structural operations over addressable neighborhoods, normal forms, and reconstruction from partial data. Under the Flower lens, expansion creates superposed route amplitudes, collapse becomes phase-sensitive convergence, and tunneling emerges as a resonance-mediated nonlocal transport through zero-stabilized states. Under the Cloud lens, expansion yields candidate belief fields, collapse becomes a timing and pruning problem over uncertainty landscapes, and tunneling becomes posterior mass transfer between distant but compatible candidate basins. Under the Fractal lens, expansion and collapse are zoom cycles across scales, and tunneling becomes scale-bridging reuse of inherited routes and collapse traces.
The chapter employs the following global notational conventions. A seed object is denoted by . A lens-specific container is denoted or simply . A neighborhood extracted by expansion is denoted . A route bundle is denoted or . A collapsed output object is denoted . A tunnel operator is denoted
\mathcal{T}_{u\rightsquigarrow v}^{\star}
Z^\star
The chapter proves six large claims.
First, a seed with sufficient address hooks and invariant schema can be expanded into an addressable local neighborhood without reopening the whole atlas. Second, route bundles can be collapsed into canonical outputs or regenerated seeds without arbitrary information loss whenever the collapse witness is sufficient. Third, partial neighborhoods can reconstruct missing local structure if boundary, type, and route invariants close strongly enough. Fourth, coherent route superposition is a lawful state of the manuscript and is not equivalent to confusion or indecision. Fifth, collapse timing is an epistemic operation governed by entropy, risk, and budget, not a mere stopping convenience. Sixth, tunnel operators allow lawful nonlocal transport by collapsing to invariant cores and re-expanding in distant but compatible regions, thereby converting solution topology into procedural weights.
The chapter therefore sits at the center of the manuscript brain. Earlier chapters built objects, addresses, problem packets, and lenses. The present chapter defines how those objects actually breathe: how they open, how they close, how they jump, and how they stabilize.
Ch06.A1 — Expansion Maps from Seeds to Addressable Neighborhoods
The Square interpretation of holographic expansion begins from the claim that a properly formed seed does not merely name an object; it carries enough identity, address, and reconstruction structure to generate a bounded addressable neighborhood around that object. Expansion is therefore the operation by which a compressed or zero-point carrier is unfolded into a locally sufficient structural region of the atlas.
Let
\sigma
=
\big(
u_\sigma,\,
a_\sigma,\,
\operatorname{core}(\sigma),\,
\operatorname{hooks}(\sigma),\,
\operatorname{schema}(\sigma),\,
\operatorname{budget}(\sigma),\,
I_\sigma
\big)
\mathcal{E}_{\Square,\kappa,B}:
\Sigma \to \mathbb{X}_{\Square},
N_{\Square}(\sigma;B)
=
\big(
\Lambda_\sigma^B,\,
\operatorname{occ}_\sigma^B,\,
\operatorname{typ}_\sigma^B,\,
\operatorname{bdry}_\sigma^B,\,
\operatorname{addr}_\sigma^B,\,
I_{\Square,\sigma}^B
\big).
The budget is not a mere resource cap. It is part of the semantics of expansion. It determines how far the expansion unfolds along declared adjacency grammars, which branches are admissible, and which witnesses must be reopened. A seed may be expanded under proof depth budget, dependency radius budget, route valence budget, patch-reach budget, or mixed resource budgets. Thus expansion must be understood as a constrained local regeneration of structure.
The canonical Square expansion of proceeds in four phases.
The first phase is anchor resolution. The address anchor is resolved into its root crystal chamber and local atom context. If the anchor is not directly resolvable because the seed is compressed or lives in a folded regime, then the seed’s reconstruction schema must first produce a local address scaffold. No expansion may proceed without lawful anchor resolution.
The second phase is hook activation. The hook family is interpreted as a typed neighborhood frontier. These hooks identify which outgoing and incoming adjacency families may be opened. Hooks are not arbitrary references; they are typed boundary channels such as dependency edges, route continuations, witness scopes, or inheritance anchors. The hook grammar determines the kinds of cells expansion may lawfully encounter.
The third phase is bounded neighborhood growth. Starting from the anchor cell and its immediate hooks, the expansion operator recursively opens admissible neighboring cells according to a typed adjacency grammar
G_{\mathrm{exp}} = (\tau_1,\tau_2,\dots,\tau_m),
\Lambda_\sigma^B = \operatorname{Grow}(a_\sigma,\operatorname{hooks}(\sigma),G_{\mathrm{exp}},B,\kappa).
The fourth phase is local invariant sealing. Once the neighborhood is grown, the expansion operator computes the local invariant bundle
I_{\Square,\sigma}^B
The resulting neighborhood is addressable by construction. Every occupied cell in must either already possess a canonical address or be generated by a lawful seed-derived address schema inherited from the anchor. Thus expansion does not create anonymous local structure. It creates named local structure.
A basic abstract expansion law is:
\mathcal{E}_{\Square,\kappa,B}(\sigma)
=
\operatorname{Seal}_{\Square,\kappa}
\Big(
\operatorname{Grow}(a_\sigma,\operatorname{hooks}(\sigma),\operatorname{schema}(\sigma),B)
\Big).
The importance of this formalism is that it separates expansion from full-text retrieval or brute-force reopening of the atlas. A lawful seed may reconstruct a useful region of the manuscript with bounded cost because it already stores the right hooks, grammar, and invariants. This is the exact structural meaning of holographic local recoverability.
A minimal expansion protocol may be represented as:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Dict, List, Set, Tuple, Any
@dataclass
class SquareSeed:
seed_id: str
address_anchor: str
hooks: List[Dict[str, Any]]
schema: Dict[str, Any]
budget: Dict[str, Any]
invariants: Dict[str, Any]
def expand_square(seed: SquareSeed, adjacency_index, budget_override=None):
budget = dict(seed.budget)
if budget_override:
budget.update(budget_override)
frontier = {seed.address_anchor}
visited: Set[str] = set(frontier)
depth = 0
max_depth = budget.get("depth", 2)
while frontier and depth < max_depth:
nxt = set()
for addr in frontier:
for rel_type in seed.schema.get("adjacency_types", []):
for nb in adjacency_index.get((addr, rel_type), []):
if nb not in visited:
visited.add(nb)
nxt.add(nb)
frontier = nxt
depth += 1
return {
"anchor": seed.address_anchor,
"cells": sorted(visited),
"budget": budget,
"witness": {"expansion_depth": depth}
}
The code captures the essential logic: expansion begins from an anchor, follows typed hooks, respects budget, and returns a local cell field with witness metadata. The actual manuscript runtime will refine this skeleton considerably, but the formal ontology is already present.
Definition 6.1 — Square-Expansion Sufficiency
A seed is Square-expansion-sufficient for corridor and budget if
\mathcal{E}_{\Square,\kappa,B}(\sigma)
This definition makes clear that sufficiency is task-relative, not absolute. A seed may be sufficient for definitional explanation and insufficient for full theorem replay.
Proposition 6.1 — Bounded Local Expansion Strictly Dominates Flat Fragment Retrieval for Structured Replay
For any problem class whose admissible solution depends on typed neighborhood closure rather than isolated content fragments, a lawful Square seed expansion dominates flat fragment retrieval in replay fidelity.
Proof. Flat fragment retrieval may return individually relevant cells without preserving the typed boundary relations required for replayable local structure. A lawful seed expansion reconstructs the neighborhood under declared adjacency grammars and invariant sealing, thereby recovering both content and the local structure connecting that content. Hence replay fidelity is strictly improved whenever typed neighborhood closure matters.
The Square lens therefore defines expansion as exact local regeneration of named structure from lawful seeds.
Ch06.A2 — Collapse Maps from Route Bundles to Canonical Outputs
Expansion alone does not solve problems. The expanded neighborhood must give rise to route bundles, and those route bundles must eventually collapse into canonical outputs, stabilized seeds, or patch packets. The Square interpretation of collapse is the exact structural reduction of a route family into a normal-form artifact satisfying a declared output contract.
Let
\mathcal{R}
=
\{\rho_1,\rho_2,\dots,\rho_n\}
\mathcal{C}_{\Square,\kappa,\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{out}}}:
\mathcal{P}(\mathcal{R})\to \mathbb{Y}_{\Square}\times \Psi,
A Square output is not merely “the chosen route.” It is a canonical structural artifact derived from route evidence. Depending on the contract, the output may be:
a normal-form claim graph,
a theorem statement,
a witness-sealed answer schema,
a canonical patch proposal,
a compressed result seed,
a contradiction capsule,
a route certificate.
Thus the collapse operator must do more than select. It must canonize.
Given the route bundle , collapse proceeds through four structural stages.
The first stage is coverage consolidation. All route segments contributing materially to the output contract are aggregated into a claim-support graph
G_{\mathrm{claim}}(\mathcal{R}).
The second stage is equivalence reduction. Structurally redundant claims or repeated cells arising from multiple routes are quotiented under the active structural normal-form relation . This produces an equivalence-reduced support object
\widetilde{G}_{\mathrm{claim}}(\mathcal{R}).
The third stage is contract shaping. The reduced graph is then mapped into the artifact class specified by the output contract. If the contract requests a theorem artifact, the graph is shaped into statement plus proof witness shell. If the contract requests a patch artifact, the graph is shaped into delta operations plus rollback anchor. If the contract requests a reusable seed, the graph is compressed into a regenerable structure.
The fourth stage is canonicalization. The shaped artifact is then mapped into its structural normal form:
y = \operatorname{NF}_{\Square,\kappa}(\operatorname{Shape}_{\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{out}}}(\widetilde{G}_{\mathrm{claim}}(\mathcal{R}))).
\psi_y
A central law of Square collapse is idempotence up to normal form:
\mathcal{C}_{\Square}(\mathcal{C}_{\Square}(\mathcal{R})) \equiv \mathcal{C}_{\Square}(\mathcal{R}),
A second law is trace inclusion: every claim or structural component appearing in the output must be traceable to at least one contributing route segment or seed hook in the expansion history.
A minimal structural collapse sketch is:
def collapse_square_routes(route_bundle, output_contract):
claim_graph = build_claim_support_graph(route_bundle)
reduced_graph = quotient_equivalent_claims(claim_graph)
shaped = shape_to_contract(reduced_graph, output_contract)
canonical = canonicalize_square_region(
shaped.get("objects", []),
shaped.get("edges", []),
)
witness = {
"collapse_mode": "square_contract_collapse",
"route_count": len(route_bundle),
"contract": output_contract,
}
return canonical, witness
The formal importance of collapse is that it turns transient route geometry into stable cognitive memory. Without collapse, route bundles remain process traces; with collapse, they become reusable structural knowledge.
Definition 6.2 — Square Collapse Correctness
A Square collapse of route bundle into output is correct if and only if:
\operatorname{TraceComplete}(y,\mathcal{R})=1,\quad
\operatorname{ContractSatisfied}(y)=1,\quad
\operatorname{Canonical}(y)=1,\quad
\psi_y\downarrow.
Theorem 6.1 — Canonical Collapse Produces Replay-Stable Outputs
If a route bundle is collapsed through equivalence reduction, contract shaping, and structural normal-form canonicalization, then the resulting output is stable under replay of the same admissible route evidence.
Proof. Equivalence reduction removes incidental multiplicity. Contract shaping fixes the artifact class. Normal-form canonicalization fixes remaining structural variation. Therefore replay of the same route evidence under the same corridor and contract must yield the same canonical artifact.
The Square collapse operator thus defines how local structural motion crystallizes into reusable exact output.
Ch06.A3 — Local Reconstruction from Partial Neighborhoods
A genuinely holographic manuscript cannot require complete neighborhoods for every reconstruction. It must often recover missing local structure from partial neighborhoods, provided enough boundary, type, and route invariants survive. The Square lens therefore formalizes local reconstruction from fragments.
Let
N_{\Square}(\sigma;B)
P \subseteq N_{\Square}(\sigma;B)
\mathcal{R}^{\mathrm{loc}}_{\Square,\kappa}(P,\sigma)
\to \widehat{N}_{\Square}
Local reconstruction is possible only if the partial neighborhood retains sufficient structural closure. The relevant retained data include:
anchor presence or anchor recoverability,
typed boundary faces,
enough local addresses to infer missing adjacency,
type constraints on missing cells,
route hooks or witness stubs crossing the missing region,
any local normal-form constraints narrowing completion possibilities.
The reconstruction process may be described as a constrained completion problem. Let the missing cell set be
M = N_{\Square}(\sigma;B)\setminus P.
P \cup M
A basic formalization is:
\widehat{N}_{\Square}
=
\arg\min_{N'\supseteq P}
\operatorname{Defect}_{\Square,\kappa}(N',\sigma)
N' \models I_{\Square,\sigma}^{\mathrm{task}}.
This reconstruction is not assumed exact in every corridor. The chapter distinguishes several reconstruction classes:
exact local reconstruction,
invariant-preserving approximate reconstruction,
ambiguity-preserving partial reconstruction,
failure-sealed attempted reconstruction.
An exact reconstruction occurs when the partial neighborhood uniquely determines the missing region under the local invariants. An approximate reconstruction occurs when some degrees of freedom remain but do not matter for the task’s invariant subset. An ambiguity-preserving reconstruction occurs when several local completions remain admissible and must be kept plural. Failure-sealed reconstruction occurs when the missing region cannot be reconstructed lawfully, in which case the result is a typed gap or corruption object rather than a guessed structure.
A representative reconstruction protocol may be sketched as:
def reconstruct_square_partial(partial_region, seed_schema, invariants):
candidates = generate_local_completions(partial_region, seed_schema)
valid = [cand for cand in candidates if check_invariants(cand, invariants)]
if len(valid) == 1:
return {"mode": "exact", "region": valid[0]}
if len(valid) > 1:
return {"mode": "ambiguous", "candidates": valid}
return {"mode": "failure", "reason": "no admissible completion"}
The holographic significance of this operation is profound. It means that local atlas access need not reopen every stored object from scratch. Under lawful conditions, partial structural memory already contains enough information to regenerate what is missing. This is not heuristic hallucination. It is constraint-bound structural inference grounded in seeds, addresses, and invariants.
Definition 6.3 — Locally Holographic Partial Region
A partial Square region is locally holographic for seed and task class if
\mathcal{R}^{\mathrm{loc}}_{\Square,\kappa}(P,\sigma)
Proposition 6.2 — Boundary-Complete Partial Regions Admit Reconstruction More Often than Interior Fragments
If two partial regions preserve equal cell count but one preserves more boundary interfaces and route hooks, the boundary-richer region is strictly more reconstructible under the same invariant family.
Proof. Reconstruction is constrained by interface compatibility, hook continuation, and local closure. A region preserving more such boundary data narrows the admissible completion space more strongly than a purely interior fragment of equal size. Therefore reconstructibility is improved.
The Square lens therefore makes precise the holographic promise that fragments may regenerate wholes under lawful structural conditions.
Ch06.A4 — Invertibility Classes and Projection Defects
Expansion and collapse are not always exact inverses. Some projections are lossless, some are left-invertible, some are right-invertible, some are only approximately invertible, and some incur structural defects that must be recorded explicitly. The Square lens therefore concludes with a classification of invertibility and projection defect.
Let
\Pi_{\Square}:\mathbb{M}\to \mathbb{X}_{\Square}
\Re_{\Square}:\mathbb{X}_{\Square}\to \mathbb{M}
An exactly invertible projection satisfies
\Re_{\Square}\circ \Pi_{\Square} = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{M}}
A left-invertible projection satisfies
L_{\Square}\circ \Pi_{\Square}=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{M}}
A right-invertible projection satisfies
\Pi_{\Square}\circ R_{\Square}=\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{X}_{\Square}}
A corridor-stable approximate inverse satisfies
\Re_{\Square}\circ \Pi_{\Square}(x)\approx_\kappa x
A lossy projection does not admit any such identity preservation beyond a weaker invariant subset.
Projection defect measures what the lens fails to preserve. The Square defect of object under projection is a tuple
\delta_{\Square}(x)
=
\big(
\delta_{\mathrm{addr}}(x),\,
\delta_{\mathrm{adj}}(x),\,
\delta_{\mathrm{type}}(x),\,
\delta_{\mathrm{bdry}}(x),\,
\delta_{\mathrm{route}}(x)
\big),
A generic defect norm is
\|\delta_{\Square}(x)\|_{\kappa}
=
w_1\delta_{\mathrm{addr}}
+w_2\delta_{\mathrm{adj}}
+w_3\delta_{\mathrm{type}}
+w_4\delta_{\mathrm{bdry}}
+w_5\delta_{\mathrm{route}},
Projection defects are not accidents to be hidden. They are first-class witnessable properties. Whenever expansion followed by collapse, or projection followed by reconstruction, fails to preserve some invariant, the defect bundle must travel with the result. This enables later patching, transport refusal, or ambiguity preservation rather than silent degradation.
Definition 6.4 — Defect-Bounded Projection
A Square projection is defect-bounded under corridor if
\|\delta_{\Square}(x)\|_{\kappa}\le \varepsilon_\kappa
Theorem 6.2 — Exact Holography Is a Special Case of Defect-Bounded Holography
Every exact holographic expansion–collapse pair is defect-bounded with zero defect, but the converse holds only when the corridor defect budget is zero.
Proof. Exact holography implies all defect components vanish. Defect-bounded holography merely limits their weighted sum to corridor tolerance. If that tolerance is zero, the notions coincide; otherwise defect-bounded holography includes approximate but lawful cases.
The Square lens has now fully defined structural expansion, collapse, partial reconstruction, and defect-aware invertibility.
Ch06.B1 — Coherent Superposition of Route Candidates
The Flower interpretation of holographic expansion begins from a stronger claim than the Square lens required: before collapse, a manuscript may lawfully inhabit several route candidates at once. Such multiplicity is not mere indecision or lack of progress. It is a coherent superposed state of route motion whose amplitudes, phases, and couplings can be represented, updated, and eventually collapsed.
Let
\mathcal{R}=\{\rho_1,\rho_2,\dots,\rho_n\}
\Psi_{\mathcal{R}}
=
\sum_{i=1}^n
\alpha_i e^{i\theta_i}\,|\rho_i\rangle,
\sum_i \alpha_i^2 = 1.
The basis routes are not arbitrary labels. Each route carries its own structural support, transport possibilities, witness expectations, and boundary channels. The superposition is therefore not a pure abstract vector state; it is a bundle of physically meaningful manuscript trajectories.
A superposition is coherent if the pairwise coupling structure
\mathcal{K}_{ij}
=
g_{ij}\alpha_i\alpha_j\cos(\theta_i-\theta_j)
\operatorname{Coh}(\Psi_{\mathcal{R}})
=
\sum_{i\neq j}\omega_{ij}\mathcal{K}_{ij}
-
\sum_i \chi_i \operatorname{Conflict}(\rho_i),
\operatorname{Coh}(\Psi_{\mathcal{R}})\ge \tau_{\kappa}
This state is valuable because it allows the manuscript to defer collapse while still performing meaningful motion. During superposition the system may:
transfer amplitude toward more supported routes,
preserve minority candidate routes that remain plausible,
exploit constructive interference between partially overlapping interpretations,
delay destructive branching until more evidence is available,
tunnel between distant candidates through shared phase structure.
The Flower expansion of a seed therefore does not merely produce one route. It may produce a coherent wavepacket over several routes:
\mathcal{E}_{\Flower,\kappa,B}(\sigma)
=
\Psi_{\mathcal{R}(\sigma)}.
A simple update of route amplitudes under evidence pulse may take form
\alpha_i(t+1)
\propto
\alpha_i(t)\exp\big(\lambda\,s_i(E_t)\big),
The dynamic significance of coherent superposition is that the manuscript can think in several directions at once without flattening those directions into one prematurely averaged structure. Each candidate remains route-local, phase-bearing, and witness-relevant.
Definition 6.5 — Flower-Coherent Route Superposition
A Flower route superposition is coherent if its active route family is pairwise admissible under the corridor’s coupling and conflict laws, and if its total phase-drift rate remains below the superposition instability threshold.
Proposition 6.3 — Coherent Superposition Generalizes Single-Route Tracking
A single active route is the special case of route superposition in which one amplitude is nonzero and all others vanish.
Proof. Set for one route and for all . Then , which differs from ordinary single-route tracking only by a global phase factor.
The Flower lens thus legitimizes plural dynamic reasoning without surrendering structure.
Ch06.B2 — Resonance Bridges between Non-Adjacent Regions
The next Flower task is to formalize nonlocal interaction. A manuscript object or route family often finds high-value continuation not only through adjacent structural cells, but through distant regions sharing deep phase compatibility, harmonic structure, or transform resonance. These nonlocal connections are encoded by resonance bridges.
Let and be two regions of the atlas not necessarily adjacent in the Square sense. Let their Flower states be and . A resonance bridge exists from to if there is a dynamic coupling sufficiently strong to justify lawful transport or joint route activation despite non-adjacency.
Formally, a resonance bridge is a tuple
B_{U\rightsquigarrow V}
=
\big(
U,\,
V,\,
\mathcal{K}_{UV},\,
\Theta_{UV},\,
\Pi_{UV},\,
\psi_{UV}
\big),
is the resonance kernel,
is the phase-lock or phase-translation law,
is the bridge permission profile,
is the witness bundle.
A simple bridge existence criterion is
\mathcal{K}_{UV} \ge \eta_{\kappa}
\quad\text{and}\quad
\mathrm{InvariantCompat}(U,V)=1.
Resonance bridges enable the manuscript to discover solution paths that would be invisible in local structural search alone. A symbolic-allegorical region may resonate with a formal-operator region. A code schema may resonate with a theorem template. A contradiction capsule may resonate with a distant repair library. If the bridge is lawful, these regions may exchange route amplitude without traversing all intermediate structural cells.
The Flower lens makes clear that this is not random associative leaping. It is a witness-bound nonlocal dynamic interaction. The bridge must be typed, phase-compatible, and corridor-legal.
A bridge may be exact, conditional, or exploratory. Exact bridges preserve identity-bearing invariants strongly. Conditional bridges require auxiliary witnesses or external confirmation. Exploratory bridges are provisional and may generate hypotheses rather than direct route execution.
Definition 6.6 — Resonance Bridge Admissibility
A resonance bridge is admissible if:
\mathcal{K}_{UV}\ge \eta_{\kappa},
\qquad
\Theta_{UV}\downarrow,
\qquad
\Pi_{UV}=1,
\qquad
\psi_{UV}\downarrow.
Proposition 6.4 — Resonance Bridges Extend Reach beyond Local Adjacency
Any solution geometry restricted to local structural adjacency is incomplete whenever high-value nonlocal but invariant-compatible resonance bridges exist.
Proof. If and are non-adjacent structurally but possess a lawful resonance bridge, then route expansion restricted to local adjacency cannot reach from without indirect or exhaustive traversal. A bridge-aware expansion can do so lawfully in one higher-order step. Hence adjacency-only reach is incomplete.
Resonance bridges therefore supply the nonlocal connective tissue of dynamic manuscript reasoning.
Ch06.B3 — Tunnel Entry, Transfer, and Exit Conditions
Resonance bridges prepare nonlocal connection, but tunneling is stronger. A tunnel is an admissible transition between distant regions that proceeds not by ordinary path continuation but by collapse to a lawful invariant core—often a zero point or shared nexus—and re-expansion into the target regime. Tunneling is therefore the dynamic embodiment of the manuscript’s higher-dimensional shortcut logic.
Let and be source and target regions, with local zero-stabilized states
z_u,\qquad z_v,
Z^\star
\mathcal{T}^{Z^\star}_{u\rightsquigarrow v}
=
\mathcal{E}_{v}
\circ
\mathcal{H}_{u\to v}^{Z^\star}
\circ
\mathcal{C}_{u},
collapses the source state into a tunnelable invariant seed,
transports that seed through the zero hub or declared tunnel chamber,
re-expands the transported seed into the target region.
Tunnel validity is determined by three sets of conditions: entry, transfer, and exit.
The entry conditions require that the source region admit stable collapse into a seed preserving the identity fragment relevant for the tunnel. Formally, source entry is admissible if there exists a source collapse
\mathcal{C}_{u}(X_u)=\sigma_u
\operatorname{Trace}(\chi(X_u),\chi(\sigma_u))\downarrow,
\qquad
\mathrm{TunnelInvariant}(X_u,\sigma_u)=1,
\qquad
\mathrm{CorridorEntryPermitted}(u)=1.
The transfer conditions require that the transported seed remain lawful in the tunnel chamber. The transfer map
\mathcal{H}_{u\to v}^{Z^\star}
The exit conditions require that the target region admit a compatible re-expansion. Formally, exit is admissible if:
\mathcal{E}_{v}(\sigma_{u\to v}) = X_v'
\mathrm{CompatTarget}(X_v',v)=1,
\qquad
\mathrm{Trace}(\chi(\sigma_{u\to v}),\chi(X_v'))\downarrow,
\qquad
\psi_{\mathrm{exit}}\downarrow.
The tunnel may fail at any stage. Entry failure produces a collapse-defect object. Transfer failure produces a tunnel-corruption or invariant-mismatch object. Exit failure produces a target-reconstruction failure or incompatible-region object. These failures are first-class witnesses and must never be hidden.
A strong form of tunnel law is:
\mathcal{T}^{Z^\star}_{u\rightsquigarrow v}(X_u)
\approx_\kappa X_v
A generic tunnel executor can be sketched as:
def tunnel(source_state, collapse_fn, tunnel_transport_fn, expand_fn, target_context):
seed = collapse_fn(source_state)
if not seed.get("tunnel_admissible", False):
return {"mode": "failure", "stage": "entry"}
transported = tunnel_transport_fn(seed, target_context)
if not transported.get("transfer_admissible", False):
return {"mode": "failure", "stage": "transfer"}
target_state = expand_fn(transported, target_context)
if not target_state.get("exit_admissible", False):
return {"mode": "failure", "stage": "exit"}
return {"mode": "success", "state": target_state}
This protocol makes explicit that tunneling is a lawful three-stage act rather than a rhetorical leap.
Definition 6.7 — Tunnelable Invariant
An invariant family is tunnelable between regions and if it can survive source collapse, zero-hub transfer, and target re-expansion while preserving the identity fragment and contract relevant to the tunnel purpose.
Theorem 6.3 — Tunneling Requires a Shared Invariant Core
No lawful tunnel can exist between source and target regions unless there exists a nontrivial invariant family preserved across entry, transfer, and exit.
Proof. If no invariant survives collapse, then the source cannot be meaningfully encoded into a transferable seed. If no invariant survives transfer, the tunnel destroys identity. If no invariant survives re-expansion, the target output cannot be interpreted as a lawful continuation of the source. Therefore a shared invariant core is necessary.
Tunneling is therefore the dynamic shortcut operation of the manuscript brain: not a break in rigor, but a higher-order transport mediated by invariant-preserving collapse and re-expansion.
Ch06.B4 — Phase-Preserving Transport across Chart Families
The final Flower requirement is to transport states across chart families without destroying dynamic continuity. A tunnel or bridge may cross not only distant regions within one lens, but also distinct lens regimes. Such transport must preserve or lawfully transform phase.
Let
\ell,m\in\{\Square,\Flower,\Cloud,\Fractal\}
\Tau_{\ell\to m}:\mathbb{X}_\ell\to \mathbb{X}_m.
Suppose a Flower state is transported into a Square or Cloud or Fractal chart. A phase-preserving transport must specify:
how amplitude maps into target observables,
how phase maps into target orientation or uncertainty degrees,
how interference or coupling features translate,
how boundary channels are reinterpreted,
whether any phase information is intentionally collapsed, preserved symbolically, or converted to another invariant.
A general phase-preserving transport can be written
\Tau_{\Flower\to m}(\psi)
=
\big(
\operatorname{Body}_m(\psi),\,
\operatorname{PhaseTrace}_m(\psi),\,
\operatorname{BoundaryMap}_m(\psi),\,
\omega_{\Tau}
\big).
For example, Flower-to-Square transport may convert phase-ordered route competition into a canonical ordered route bundle or conflict graph while storing the phase trace as a witness or annotation field. Flower-to-Cloud transport may convert amplitudes into posterior mass and phase opposition into anticorrelation structure. Flower-to-Fractal transport may convert dynamic oscillation between scales into explicit zoom cycles.
The crucial property is that phase is not silently dropped unless the corridor allows such loss and the defect is recorded. Thus one defines a phase defect component
\delta_{\mathrm{phase}}(\Tau_{\ell\to m}(x)),
Proposition 6.5 — Cross-Chart Transport without Phase Trace Is Non-Replayable for Dynamic Problems
If a dynamic route state is transported into another chart without preserving a lawful phase trace, then later replay cannot reconstruct the role of timing, opposition, or synchronization in the original solve episode.
Proof. Dynamic problems depend not only on structural support but on how alternatives interfered, synchronized, or drifted over time. If this phase information is discarded without a trace, the target chart loses essential causal structure of the original solve episode. Therefore such transport is not replayable for dynamic problems.
The Flower lens thus concludes with a general transport law: chart changes are lawful only when dynamic phase is preserved, translated, or explicitly defect-tagged.
Ch06.C1 — Delayed Collapse under Uncertainty Pressure
The Cloud interpretation of holographic expansion begins from the observation that collapse is not merely the endpoint of reasoning; it is a timing problem. Collapsing too early destroys alternative solution basins that remain plausible. Collapsing too late wastes budget, delays action, and can increase contradiction cost. The Cloud lens formalizes collapse timing under uncertainty pressure.
Let
p_t
\Pi_t
=
\alpha H(p_t)
+
\beta \operatorname{Flatness}(p_t)
+
\gamma C_t
-
\delta B_t
+
\varepsilon R_t.
The key idea is that collapse should not occur simply when one candidate is slightly ahead. Instead, collapse timing is governed by the balance between uncertainty reduction value and the cost of continued exploration. A delayed collapse policy therefore holds the state open while:
\operatorname{ValueOfInformation}(p_t) > \operatorname{CostOfDelay}(B_t,R_t,C_t).
The Cloud collapse timing operator may be written:
\operatorname{CollapseNow}(p_t)
=
1
\iff
\Big(
\operatorname{Dominance}(p_t)\ge \theta_{\mathrm{dom}}
\Big)
\;\vee\;
\Big(
\Pi_t\ge \theta_{\mathrm{press}}
\Big)
\;\vee\;
\Big(
\operatorname{PolicyForced}=1
\Big).
Delayed collapse is not inertia. It is a controlled state of continued superposition or plurality. During delay, evidence is gathered, routes are reweighted, contradictions are localized, and ambiguity capsules may shrink or split. The Cloud lens makes this waiting lawful and computationally meaningful.
A delayed-collapse witness must record why the state remained open and why it eventually collapsed. This is crucial for learning. A candidate suppressed by forced budget collapse should not be penalized as if it were epistemically disproven.
Definition 6.8 — Delay-Admissible Cloud State
A Cloud state is delay-admissible if the expected gain from additional evidence exceeds the corridor-adjusted cost of keeping the candidate family unresolved.
Proposition 6.6 — Delay Is Rational When Information Gradient Exceeds Budget Gradient
If the expected information gain of continued exploration dominates the marginal cost of delay, then immediate collapse is suboptimal under the manuscript’s utility model.
Proof. By assumption, continued exploration increases expected solve utility more than it decreases utility through cost or risk. Therefore immediate collapse would abandon positive expected value. Hence delayed collapse is rational.
The Cloud lens therefore turns “wait before deciding” into a formal epistemic act.
Ch06.C2 — Branch Competition and Probability Redistribution
While collapse is delayed, candidate branches compete. Their relative masses shift as evidence accumulates, routes interfere, and structural or dynamic witnesses change. The Cloud lens formalizes this competition as probability redistribution over branch families.
Let
\mathcal{B}=\{b_1,\dots,b_n\}
p_t(b_i).
\mathcal{U}_{\mathrm{comp}}:p_t \mapsto p_{t+1}.
A generic update law is:
p_{t+1}(b_i)
=
\frac{
p_t(b_i)\,
\exp\big(
\lambda S_i(t)
-
\mu Q_i(t)
+
\nu M_i(t)
\big)
}{
\sum_j
p_t(b_j)\,
\exp\big(
\lambda S_j(t)
-
\mu Q_j(t)
+
\nu M_j(t)
\big)
},
is branch support from new evidence,
is branch penalty from contradiction or budget burden,
is branch bonus from cross-lens agreement or route resonance.
This law captures a key principle of the manuscript brain: branches do not merely compete by direct evidence. They also compete via structural cost, contradiction burden, and cross-lens harmony.
The interaction among branches may require covariance or dependency correction. Two branches that share most of their structure should not both receive full independent credit for one supporting witness. Thus the update law may incorporate overlap correction matrix . Likewise, two branches strongly anticorrelated through contradiction or phase opposition may transfer probability mass asymmetrically.
Branch competition is also capable of branch splitting and branch merging. A broad branch may split into refined subbranches when new ambiguity is discovered. Several narrow branches may merge when they are shown to be equivalent under a stronger invariant. The Cloud representation must support both operations, not only reweighting fixed supports.
Definition 6.9 — Competition-Stable Branch Family
A branch family is competition-stable under update if redistribution preserves admissible support, records overlap correction, and does not silently erase branches above the corridor retention threshold.
Theorem 6.4 — Naive Independent Reweighting Overcounts Shared Evidence
If branches share significant structural or witness overlap, then reweighting them independently by the same evidence inflates posterior certainty relative to an overlap-aware update.
Proof. Shared evidence contributes correlated support to the branches. Treating it as independent multiplies effective evidence mass and narrows the posterior excessively. Therefore overlap-aware redistribution is necessary when branch supports are nonindependent.
The Cloud lens thus represents branch competition as lawful posterior ecology, not brute ranking.
Ch06.C3 — Budgeted Pruning under Incomplete Evidence
The manuscript brain cannot preserve all branches forever. It must prune. However, pruning is not disproval. It is a budgeted operational act under incomplete evidence. The Cloud lens must therefore formalize pruning so that branches may be removed from active competition without being erased from epistemic memory.
Let
\mathcal{B}=\{b_1,\dots,b_n\}
\mathcal{P}_{\mathrm{prune}}:
(\mathcal{B},p_t,B_t)\to
(\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{keep}},\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{park}},\psi_{\mathrm{prune}}),
remains active,
is suspended or archived,
is the pruning witness.
The distinction between discard and park is essential. Discard implies strong epistemic rejection or contradiction. Park means “removed from active budget use but still potentially recoverable.” Most budget-driven pruning should produce parked branches, not discarded ones.
A branch is prunable under budget if its expected marginal utility is below a corridor-adjusted threshold:
\operatorname{EMU}(b_i)
<
\theta_{\mathrm{prune}}(B_t,\kappa).
The pruning witness must record:
the current branch posterior,
the active budget profile,
the reason for pruning,
whether the branch was parked or discarded,
the conditions for revival.
A revival hook may be triggered by:
new external evidence,
contradiction in the dominant branch,
patch-induced topology change,
lens transport exposing new support,
budget expansion.
This makes pruning reversible in principle when it is budgetary rather than epistemic.
A basic pruning routine may be represented as:
def prune_branches(branches, posterior, budget, utility_fn, threshold):
keep = []
park = []
for b in branches:
emu = utility_fn(b, posterior[b], budget)
if emu >= threshold:
keep.append(b)
else:
park.append(b)
witness = {
"budget": budget,
"threshold": threshold,
"kept": keep,
"parked": park,
}
return keep, park, witness
The formal point is not the exact scoring function but the ontology of pruning-with-memory.
Definition 6.10 — Budget-Honest Pruning
A pruning act is budget honest if every removed branch is labeled as parked or discarded, the reason for removal is witnessed, and revival conditions are declared whenever the branch is not logically refuted.
Proposition 6.7 — Parking Dominates Silent Erasure under Incomplete Evidence
When evidence is incomplete, parking a low-utility branch preserves future optionality while consuming no active search budget, whereas silent erasure loses optionality without epistemic justification.
Proof. Parking removes the branch from active competition but preserves a recoverable record. Silent erasure removes both. Since incomplete evidence does not warrant full refutation, parking retains strictly more lawful future capability.
Budgeted pruning is therefore a disciplined operational collapse of active search space, not a metaphysical statement of falsehood.
Ch06.C4 — Ambiguity Retention versus Forced Resolution
The final Cloud task is to formalize when ambiguity should be retained in the final output and when the system must force resolution. This is one of the most delicate acts of the manuscript brain because it determines whether the atlas outputs plurality honestly or hides it behind premature singularization.
Let the final candidate family at output time be
\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{fin}}=\{b_1,\dots,b_m\},
singular resolved output,
plural ambiguity-retaining output,
forced-resolution output with witness.
Ambiguity retention is required when multiple candidate basins remain above corridor threshold and the output contract does not require singular commitment. In such cases the final output should preserve the candidate family, their confidence surface, and the reasons they remain unresolved. This is not failure. It is a lawful epistemic artifact.
Forced resolution occurs when:
the output contract requires one action,
a policy or corridor demands commitment,
budget exhaustion prohibits further plurality,
the problem itself is a decision problem demanding one branch.
Forced resolution is lawful only if the witness explicitly records why singularization occurred. Thus the final output may be singular while still carrying a resolution cause tag such as:
\mathrm{ResolvedByEvidence},\quad
\mathrm{ResolvedByBudget},\quad
\mathrm{ResolvedByPolicy},\quad
\mathrm{ResolvedByRisk}.
A singularity criterion may be:
\operatorname{Singular}(p_{\mathrm{fin}})
=
1
\iff
\Big(
p_{\max}\ge \theta_{\mathrm{sing}}
\wedge
p_{\max}-p_{\mathrm{next}}\ge \Delta_{\mathrm{sing}}
\Big)
The manuscript’s honesty principle requires that no output appear more resolved than its witness permits. Thus ambiguity retention is not an optional display choice; it is part of the legal relation between the Cloud field and the output artifact.
Theorem 6.5 — Output Honesty Requires Distinguishing Evidence Resolution from Forced Resolution
If final singular outputs do not distinguish evidence-based dominance from budget- or policy-forced choice, then replay cannot recover the true epistemic status of the solve episode.
Proof. A singular output alone does not encode why plurality ended. Without a reason tag, replay cannot know whether the branch truly dominated or merely won under external pressure. Therefore output honesty requires this distinction.
The Cloud lens therefore concludes with a rigorous law of epistemic truthfulness: preserve ambiguity when lawful, resolve only when justified, and always witness the reason.
Ch06.D1 — Macro-to-Micro and Micro-to-Macro Zoom Sequences
The Fractal interpretation of holographic expansion begins with zoom. A problem, route, or manuscript organ rarely stays at one scale throughout its solve cycle. It often begins at a coarse summary, descends into local proof or implementation detail, and later reascends into a collapsed synthesis. The Fractal lens formalizes these macro-to-micro and micro-to-macro zoom sequences.
Let
X^{(s)}
Z_+^{(s)}: X^{(s)}\to X^{(s+1)},
\qquad
Z_-^{(s+1)}: X^{(s+1)}\to X^{(s)}.
Z_+^{(s)} Z_+^{(s+1)} \cdots Z_+^{(s+k-1)},
A lawful zoom sequence is not arbitrary magnification. Each zoom step must preserve:
identity trace,
address ancestry,
boundary compatibility,
route-hook inheritance,
contract relevance.
Thus a coarse chapter summary may zoom into proof neighborhoods, route libraries, or patch objects only if the reconstruction schema of the chapter seed actually licenses those expansions.
Macro-to-micro zoom is used when the solve episode requires detail not present at the current scale. Micro-to-macro zoom is used when local detail has been explored and must be collapsed into a reusable whole. These are dual but not identical. One expands possibilities, the other consolidates outcomes.
A zoom schedule for problem may therefore be represented as
\mathcal{Z}(q)=
\big(
s_0\to s_1\to \cdots \to s_n,
\operatorname{reason}_i,
\operatorname{budget}_i,
\omega_i
\big).
Definition 6.11 — Zoom-Coherent Sequence
A zoom sequence is coherent if every adjacent scale transition is admissible and the composed sequence preserves the identity core and output contract of the original object or problem.
This gives the Fractal lens its first major role: legal navigation across levels of detail.
Ch06.D2 — Recursive Unfold-Fold Dynamics
The next Fractal operation is the recursive alternation between unfold and fold. Whereas zoom sequences describe movement in one direction at a time, unfold-fold dynamics describe repeated cycles in which a container opens to solve local subproblems and then recloses into a new stabilized form.
Let
U = \operatorname{Unfold},
\qquad
F = \operatorname{Fold}.
X
\xrightarrow{U}
\{X_i\}
\xrightarrow{\text{local solve}}
\{Y_i\}
\xrightarrow{F}
Y.
The key Fractal law is that folding is not mere deletion of detail. It is a contract-preserving aggregation. The parent must remember enough about the child solves to remain a lawful compressed representative. Thus fold must preserve:
new seeds or route priors,
witness summaries,
collapse reasons,
patch deltas,
unresolved capsules if any persist.
An unfold-fold dynamic becomes recursive when any child itself undergoes further unfold-fold cycles. The resulting process forms a tree or DAG of local solve histories, all anchored back to the parent lineage.
A formal recursive scheme may be written:
Y = F\Big(\{ \operatorname{Solve}(U_i(X)) \}_{i=1}^m\Big),
\operatorname{Solve}(X_i)
=
F_i\Big(\{\operatorname{Solve}(U_{ij}(X_i))\}_j\Big)
local tasks are atomic under the current corridor,
budget forbids further unfold,
ambiguity or contradiction is capsule-sealed,
a sufficient fixed point is reached.
This dynamic gives the manuscript brain its living texture. A large proof is not opened all at once. It unfolds recursively along need and closes recursively into updated memory.
Proposition 6.8 — Unfold-Fold Dynamics Strictly Generalize One-Step Expansion-Collapse
A one-step expansion-collapse pair is the special case of unfold-fold dynamics in which the unfold produces one child scale and the fold immediately collapses it without further recursion.
Proof. If the unfold tree has depth one and one child level, recursive unfold-fold reduces to one expansion and one collapse. Therefore the one-step case is contained in the recursive case.
The Fractal lens thus upgrades expansion-collapse into a true recursive developmental cycle.
Ch06.D3 — Fixed-Point Return to Stable Identity
A manuscript brain that expands and collapses repeatedly without stable return loses identity. The Fractal lens must therefore define a fixed-point return law ensuring that, under lawful cycles, the system returns to a stable identity-bearing form even as local content, route priors, or witnesses are updated.
Let
\mathbb{P}_\star
\mathcal{U}_{\mathrm{solve}}(X)=Y.
\mathbb{P}_\star(Y)=\mathbb{P}_\star(X)
\mathbb{P}_\star(Y)=\mathbb{P}_\star'(X)
In the strongest case, the fixed point is the zero-stabilized carrier . Then expansion, local solve, and collapse satisfy
\mathcal{C}_{\Fractal}\big(\mathcal{E}_{\Fractal}(Z^\star)\big)=Z^\star
The fixed-point law can be expressed in corridor-stable form:
d_\kappa\big(\mathbb{P}_\star(Y),\mathbb{P}_\star(X)\big)\le \varepsilon_\kappa,
A fixed-point return witness records:
what was preserved exactly,
what changed lawfully,
what remained unresolved,
what seed or patch now represents the stabilized form.
Theorem 6.6 — Stable Recursive Cognition Requires a Fixed-Point Projector
Without a stable identity projector, repeated expansion and collapse cannot distinguish lawful refinement from uncontrolled drift.
Proof. Expansion and collapse alter representational form and possibly local content. Without a stable reference projector, there is no criterion by which to judge whether the post-cycle object still belongs to the same identity class or has drifted illegitimately. Therefore stable recursive cognition requires such a projector.
The fixed-point return law is the deep stabilizer of the manuscript brain’s recursive life.
Ch06.D4 — Multiscale Tunnel Reuse and Inherited Collapse Paths
The final Fractal task combines everything that came before. Tunnels discovered at one scale should not remain trapped at that scale. Collapse paths that repeatedly succeed should become inherited procedural weights. The manuscript brain must therefore support multiscale tunnel reuse and inherited collapse paths.
Suppose a tunnel
\mathcal{T}^{Z^\star}_{u\rightsquigarrow v}
u^{(s+1)},\quad v^{(s+1)}
This gives rise to a tunnel inheritance map
\eta_{\mathrm{tun}}^{s\leftrightarrow s+1}:
\mathcal{T}^{(s)} \rightsquigarrow \mathcal{T}^{(s+1)},
\eta_{\mathrm{col}}^{s\leftrightarrow s+1}:
\mathcal{C}^{(s)} \rightsquigarrow \mathcal{C}^{(s+1)}.
The inherited object is not necessarily an immediately executable tunnel. It may be a template requiring local refinement. For example, a symbolic-to-formal tunnel discovered at chapter scale may induce theorem-scale bridge hints; a fine-scale proof-collapse path may induce a chapter-scale summary-collapse operator.
The utility of this mechanism is that successful problem solving literally writes new geometry into the manuscript. A repeatedly used collapse path becomes a reusable seed route. A repeatedly successful tunnel becomes a new procedural bridge family or raises the prior weight of an existing one. This is one of the precise senses in which metro paths become procedural weights.
A multiscale tunnel library is therefore a Fractal memory object:
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{tun}}
=
\big(
\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{validated}},\,
\eta_{\mathrm{tun}},\,
\omega_{\mathrm{reuse}},\,
I_{\mathrm{tun}}
\big).
The inherited collapse paths are similar. A collapse library stores canonical ways to return from expanded subproblems to stable seeds or normal forms, along with the tasks and conditions under which those collapse paths are reliable.
Definition 6.12 — Reusable Tunnel
A tunnel is reusable at scale if its invariant core, entry/exit contracts, and witness obligations admit lawful inheritance or refinement to the source-target pair at scale .
Proposition 6.9 — Inherited Tunnel Reuse Reduces Search Cost
If a lawful tunnel template is inherited across scales, then future route search between homologous regions may begin from the inherited template rather than exploring the full candidate transport space anew.
Proof. The inherited template restricts the search to a smaller admissible transport family already supported by prior witness history. Therefore expected search cost is reduced relative to uninformed exploration.
The Fractal lens therefore ends where the chapter as a whole began: with the transformation of expansion, collapse, and tunneling from one-off solve events into persistent reusable geometry of the manuscript brain.
The chapter has now completed the formal cycle of holographic expansion, collapse, and tunneling across all four lenses. Under the Square lens, seeds expand into exact local neighborhoods, route bundles collapse into canonical structural outputs, partial neighborhoods reconstruct wholes, and projection defects classify the limits of exact recoverability. Under the Flower lens, route candidates exist in coherent superposition, nonadjacent regions connect by resonance bridges, tunnel operators collapse through invariant cores and re-expand into distant compatible regions, and phase-preserving transport makes chart transitions lawful. Under the Cloud lens, collapse timing is governed by uncertainty pressure, branches compete and redistribute posterior mass, pruning becomes budget-honest rather than silently eliminative, and ambiguity retention is distinguished rigorously from forced resolution. Under the Fractal lens, zoom sequences control macro-to-micro and micro-to-macro transitions, unfold-fold cycles drive recursive reasoning, fixed-point projectors preserve identity under repeated expansion-collapse, and tunnel reuse writes successful shortcuts into multiscale procedural memory.
The deepest consequence is that the manuscript brain is no longer a static atlas of content. It is now a lawful dynamical system in which seeds generate neighborhoods, neighborhoods generate route bundles, route bundles collapse into artifacts and new seeds, and nonlocal invariant-preserving shortcuts become part of future cognition. The atlas remembers not only what it contains, but how it opens, how it closes, and how it crosses distance. That is the exact operational meaning of holography in the present framework.
CHAPTER 6 — HOLOGRAPHIC EXPANSION, COLLAPSE, AND TUNNELING
Chapter 7 — Route Algebra, Nexus Topology, and Bridge Laws
A manuscript brain does not think merely by storing objects or by projecting them into multiple lenses. It thinks by moving among them. That movement is not arbitrary sequence, informal association, or undisciplined analogy. It is lawful traversal through a typed, witness-bearing, corridor-governed route space whose geometry constitutes the operational skeleton of cognition. The present chapter defines that skeleton. Its subject is the algebra of routes: the formal objects representing traversals, the hypergraph structure within which those traversals live, the nexus topology where many routes meet and exchange admissible motion, and the bridge laws that determine when movement across local or distant regions is legal, constructive, risky, or forbidden.
The foundational claim of the chapter is that a manuscript brain becomes computationally coherent only when its solution behavior is formalized as route algebra. Earlier chapters specified primitive ontology, address algebra, zero-point problem encoding, lens calculus, and holographic expansion/collapse. Those chapters made it possible to define objects, locate them, interpret them in multiple charts, and expand or collapse them lawfully. The present chapter defines the connective discipline that binds all of that into active cognition. Without route algebra, the atlas remains a richly structured but essentially inert repository. With route algebra, it becomes a navigable solution manifold.
Let the global route substrate of the atlas be denoted by
\mathfrak{G}
=
(\mathcal{N},\mathcal{E},\mathcal{X};\,s,t,\tau,\kappa,\Omega,\mathcal{W},\Psi),
is the set of nodes,
is the set of typed edges or hyperedges,
is the set of nexus objects,
are source and target maps,
assigns relation or transform type,
assigns corridor class,
encodes nexus interaction structure,
is the procedural weight field,
is the family of witness bundles relevant to route legality, transport, and replay.
A route is not just a walk in . It is a typed, contract-bearing, lens-aware, risk-aware, and witness-bearing compositional object. A route must know not only where it goes, but under what corridor, in which lens mixture, with what transform class, with what allowable ambiguities, and with what consequences if extended, reversed, collapsed, or patched.
Formally, the full route space is denoted
\mathbb{R}_{\mathfrak{G}}.
\rho\in \mathbb{R}_{\mathfrak{G}}
\rho
=
\Big(
u_\rho,\,
\pi_\rho,\,
\Lambda_\rho,\,
\kappa_\rho,\,
\chi_\rho,\,
\beta_\rho,\,
\omega_\rho,\,
\eta_\rho
\Big),
is the route identity token,
is the ordered traversal program,
is the active lens profile,
is the corridor class,
is the contract or target obligation family,
is the budget/risk profile,
is the witness association bundle,
is the route history, lineage, and potential reuse metadata.
The traversal program is not just a sequence of nodes and edges. Because the atlas is a hypergraph with nexus chambers and bridge permissions, may include:
node transitions,
hyperedge traversals,
bridge activations,
nexus entry/exit transitions,
lens-transport moves,
collapse/re-expansion moves,
tunnel applications.
Thus one may write
\pi_\rho
=
(o_0,\mu_1,o_1,\mu_2,\dots,\mu_m,o_m),
\mathcal{M}_{\mathrm{route}}
=
\{
\mathrm{Step},
\mathrm{HyperStep},
\mathrm{Bridge},
\mathrm{NexusEntry},
\mathrm{NexusExit},
\mathrm{Transport},
\mathrm{Collapse},
\mathrm{Expand},
\mathrm{Tunnel},
\mathrm{Repair},
\mathrm{PatchRead}
\}.
The central algebraic ambition of the chapter is to make such traversals composable. If one route leads lawfully from one problem state to an intermediate state, and another route begins lawfully at that intermediate state, then the system must be able to compose them into a larger cognitive act. But composition is not merely concatenation. The intermediate state must satisfy boundary compatibility, invariant continuity, corridor compatibility, lens compatibility, and witness sufficiency. Thus the route algebra must be stronger than path concatenation in ordinary graph theory.
The chapter establishes the following global results.
First, routes live in a typed hypergraph whose local neighborhoods are governed by grammar rather than untyped adjacency alone. Second, path composition, factorization, and equivalence form a true algebra under admissibility conditions. Third, nexus objects act as route transformers and concentrators, not just high-degree nodes. Fourth, bridges carry directional and polarity law, enabling legal transform motion between otherwise separate route families. Fifth, route competition and selection under uncertainty are formalized as Cloud posterior dynamics rather than heuristic guessing. Sixth, route reuse across scale is captured by Fractal inheritance and macro–micro folding, turning repeated successful traversals into persistent procedural weights and generalized solution motifs.
The deeper claim is that the metro map of the manuscript is not only a visual aid. It is the visible shadow of a full route algebra. The stations are nodes and organs. The lines are route families. The transfer stations are nexus chambers. The law governing which line may cross, merge, tunnel, or invert is the bridge calculus. The history of successful journeys becomes the procedural weight field. The manuscript brain is therefore not a static map. It is the algebra of legal motion over that map.
Ch07.A1 — Route Objects, Hyperedges, and Neighborhood Grammars
The first task of route algebra is to define the primitive route carriers precisely. A route must be able to traverse ordinary binary edges, hyperedges connecting multiple supports or targets, bridge objects that mediate nonlocal or directional transport, and nexus objects that concentrate route interaction. To support this, the atlas employs a typed hypergraph rather than an ordinary graph.
Let
\mathfrak{G}
=
(\mathcal{N},\mathcal{E},\mathcal{X};\,s,t,\tau,\kappa,\Omega)
e\in \mathcal{E},
s(e)\subseteq \mathcal{N}\cup\mathcal{X},
\qquad
t(e)\subseteq \mathcal{N}\cup\mathcal{X}.
\tau:\mathcal{E}\to \mathcal{T}
\mathcal{T}
=
\{
\mathrm{REF},
\mathrm{DERIVE},
\mathrm{DUAL},
\mathrm{TRANSPORT},
\mathrm{EXPAND},
\mathrm{COMPRESS},
\mathrm{CONFLICT},
\mathrm{REPAIR},
\mathrm{PATCH},
\mathrm{REPLAY},
\mathrm{ENABLE},
\mathrm{BLOCK}
\}.
A primitive route step is a triple
(o,\mu,o')
\rho
=
\big(
u_\rho,\,
(o_0,\mu_1,o_1,\ldots,\mu_m,o_m),\,
\Lambda_\rho,\,
\kappa_\rho,\,
\chi_\rho,\,
\beta_\rho,\,
\omega_\rho
\big).
\Lambda_\rho
=
\big(
\lambda_\Square,\lambda_\Flower,\lambda_\Cloud,\lambda_\Fractal
\big),
\qquad
\lambda_\ell\ge 0.
A route is structurally admissible if every local movement primitive matches the edge or bridge type of the underlying region. For ordinary edge traversal one requires:
o_i\in s(e_i),
\qquad
o_{i+1}\in t(e_i),
\qquad
\tau(e_i)\text{ compatible with }\mu_i.
Routes are not evaluated only by whether they exist. They are evaluated against neighborhood grammars. A neighborhood grammar is a typed language controlling local allowable continuations. Let
\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{nbhd}}
\mathrm{DERIVE}^\ast \cdot \mathrm{WITNESS}
\mathrm{CONFLICT}\to \mathrm{REPAIR}\to \mathrm{PATCH}
Let a local region carry grammar
\mathcal{L}(R)\subseteq \mathcal{M}_{\mathrm{route}}^\ast.
This immediately gives the manuscript two large advantages. First, routes can be validated compositionally using local grammars rather than only global post hoc checks. Second, different manuscript regions can impose specialized local route disciplines without destroying the global route algebra.
A minimal hypergraph route schema may be expressed as:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Tuple, Dict, Any, Literal
MoveType = Literal[
"Step", "HyperStep", "Bridge", "NexusEntry", "NexusExit",
"Transport", "Collapse", "Expand", "Tunnel", "Repair", "PatchRead"
]
@dataclass
class RouteStep:
src: str
move: MoveType
dst: str
metadata: Dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
@dataclass
class RouteObject:
route_id: str
steps: List[RouteStep]
lens_profile: Dict[str, float]
corridor: str
contract: Dict[str, Any]
budget: Dict[str, Any]
witness_ids: List[str]
The route object is therefore already richer than ordinary graph traversal. It carries contract, budget, and evidence because the manuscript brain routes in order to solve, not merely to move.
Definition 7.1 — Grammar-Admissible Route
A route is grammar-admissible if, for every localized subsegment , the movement word generated by belongs to the neighborhood grammar of , and every step satisfies the source-target and type compatibility laws of the active hypergraph.
Proposition 7.1 — Hypergraph Structure Is Necessary for Proof-Carrying Routes
Ordinary binary edge graphs cannot in general represent route steps whose legality depends on multiple simultaneous supports, shared witness sets, or joint derivation prerequisites.
Proof. A proof step may require several premises simultaneously. A repair route may depend on a conflict object, a witness object, and a patch permission object together. Binary graphs force these into auxiliary encodings that hide simultaneity. Hyperedges represent such multi-source or multi-target relations directly. Therefore hypergraph structure is necessary in the general case.
The route object and its hypergraph substrate are thus the first exact articulation of manuscript motion.
Ch07.A2 — Path Composition, Concatenation, and Factorization
A route algebra becomes meaningful only when routes compose. Composition is the law by which local reasoning episodes become larger reasoning programs. Concatenation is the simplest composition, but the manuscript requires stricter structure: contracts, witnesses, budgets, and invariants must pass correctly through the join.
Let
\rho_1
=
(o_0,\mu_1,\ldots,\mu_m,o_m),
\qquad
\rho_2
=
(o'_0,\nu_1,\ldots,\nu_n,o'_n).
\rho_2 \cdot \rho_1
\rho_2 \circ \rho_1
endpoint compatibility
o_m \equiv o'_0
corridor compatibility
\kappa_{\rho_1}\rightsquigarrow \kappa_{\rho_2}
lens compatibility
\Lambda_{\rho_1}\leadsto \Lambda_{\rho_2}
contract compatibility
\chi_{\rho_1}^{\mathrm{out}}
\preceq
\chi_{\rho_2}^{\mathrm{in}},
witness compatibility the witness bundle of must contain any obligations required for to start;
budget compatibility the combined budget profile must remain admissible.
When these hold, the composed route is
\rho_2\circ \rho_1
=
\Big(
u_{\rho_2\circ\rho_1},\,
\pi_{\rho_1}\cdot\pi_{\rho_2},\,
\Lambda_{\rho_2\circ\rho_1},\,
\kappa_{\rho_2\circ\rho_1},\,
\chi_{\rho_2\circ\rho_1},\,
\beta_{\rho_2\circ\rho_1},\,
\omega_{\rho_2\circ\rho_1}
\Big),
Concatenation is the special case in which the route contexts, corridor, and lens remain unchanged, so the composition reduces to simple stepwise concatenation. In general, composition is richer because it may introduce witness carryover, budget subtraction, defect propagation, or lens transport.
A route algebra also requires associativity where legal. If
\rho_3\circ(\rho_2\circ\rho_1)
(\rho_3\circ\rho_2)\circ\rho_1
Routes can also be factored. A route factors as
\rho = \rho_b \circ \rho_a
Factorization is governed by cut admissibility. A route cut at position is admissible if the prefix and suffix remain lawful route objects and the cut preserves enough interface data to recompose them later:
\operatorname{Cut}_k(\rho) = (\rho_{\le k},\rho_{>k},\iota_k),
A representative composition schema is:
def compose_routes(r1, r2):
assert r1["end_object"] == r2["start_object"]
assert corridor_compatible(r1["corridor"], r2["corridor"])
assert lens_compatible(r1["lens_profile"], r2["lens_profile"])
merged = {
"route_id": f"{r2['route_id']}∘{r1['route_id']}",
"steps": r1["steps"] + r2["steps"],
"lens_profile": merge_lens_profiles(r1, r2),
"corridor": merge_corridors(r1, r2),
"contract": merge_contracts(r1, r2),
"budget": merge_budgets(r1, r2),
"witness_ids": r1["witness_ids"] + r2["witness_ids"],
"start_object": r1["start_object"],
"end_object": r2["end_object"],
}
return merged
The code is deliberately schematic. The important point is that route composition is explicit and structured, not hidden inside general “reasoning.”
Definition 7.2 — Composable Route Pair
A pair is composable if all endpoint, corridor, lens, contract, witness, and budget compatibility conditions are satisfied.
Theorem 7.1 — Legal Route Composition Is Associative up to Normal Form
If the route merge laws for corridor, lens, witness, and budget are themselves associative or canonically normalizable, then legal route composition is associative up to route normal form.
Proof. The traversal word concatenation is associative. If the additional merged structures also compose associatively or are reduced by a canonical normalizer, then different groupings of a legal composition chain yield route objects equivalent under that normalizer.
This gives the manuscript a true algebra of traversal rather than a mere library of isolated paths.
Ch07.A3 — Nexus Definitions and Hub Classification
Routes do not only pass through ordinary nodes. They often converge, compete, exchange modalities, or switch line families at higher-order regions. These are nexuses. A nexus is not merely a node with many incident edges. It is a route interaction chamber with its own local topology, policies, and transformation laws.
Let
x\in \mathcal{X}
x
=
\Big(
u_x,\,
a_x,\,
\mathcal{R}_x,\,
\mathcal{H}_x,\,
\Omega_x,\,
\Pi_x,\,
I_x
\Big),
is identity,
is canonical address,
is the family of incident route classes,
is the hub type,
is the route interaction tensor,
is the nexus policy bundle,
is the invariant set.
The incident route family
\mathcal{R}_x = \{\rho^{(1)},\ldots,\rho^{(m)}\}
\Omega_x(i,j)
A nexus qualifies as a true hub only when its valence and interaction richness exceed minimal thresholds. Let
\operatorname{val}(x)=|\mathcal{R}_x|.
\operatorname{val}(x)\ge 3
The manuscript requires a hub classification. The base hub types are:
Transfer hub — mediates lawful switching among route families without strong transformation;
Synthesis hub — merges several route classes into a higher-order output;
Conflict hub — localizes and arbitrates route incompatibilities;
Tunnel hub — mediates collapse/re-expansion shortcuts;
Bridge hub — links structurally distant regions through lawful transform families;
Compression hub — converts expanded route bundles into seeds or macros;
Replay hub — binds routes to replay and lineage traces.
These hub types are not mutually exclusive. A major manuscript nexus may act simultaneously as transfer hub and synthesis hub or as conflict hub and repair hub.
A nexus also possesses internal topology. The incident routes do not all meet in a formless clique. There may be channels, gate conditions, phase locks, or witness compartments. Thus the nexus can be modeled as an internal local graph or chamber:
\Gamma_x = (\mathcal{P}_x,\mathcal{C}_x),
Nexus classification is essential because it affects procedural weights. A route passing through a transfer hub behaves differently from one entering a conflict hub. The hub type also conditions admissible local route words and the cost of staying or switching within the nexus.
Definition 7.3 — Nexus Well-Formedness
A nexus is well formed if:
\operatorname{val}(x)\ge 3
Proposition 7.2 — High Degree Is Not Sufficient for Nexushood
A node with many incident edges is not necessarily a nexus unless it supports distinct route classes together with nontrivial interaction law among them.
Proof. Degree counts incident connections but says nothing about whether those connections represent different route families, whether switching is lawful, or whether the node mediates synthesis, conflict, or transfer. Therefore high degree alone is insufficient.
The nexus is thus the operational heart of metro-like topology: the place where routes become more than paths.
Ch07.A4 — Legal Adjacency and Typed Connection Rules
The final Square task is to formalize when route steps are legally adjacent. Not every address-neighbor or graph-neighbor can be traversed. The route algebra requires a precise legality relation over local connections.
Let and let . A local transition
o \xrightarrow{\mu} o'
structural reachability — there exists a graph element or chamber law supporting the move;
type compatibility — the source and target types permit movement primitive ;
corridor permission — the active corridor allows this move;
boundary compatibility — the source exit interface matches the target entry interface;
invariant preservation — no mandatory local invariant is violated by the move;
witness sufficiency — required supporting witnesses are present or deferrable by policy.
This yields a local legality predicate
\operatorname{LegalAdj}_\kappa(o,\mu,o').
The typed connection rule set of a region is
\mathcal{C}_R
=
\{
(o,\mu,o') :
\operatorname{LegalAdj}_{\kappa_R}(o,\mu,o')=1
\}.
The manuscript also distinguishes hard illegality from soft inadmissibility. Hard illegality means the move is impossible or forbidden under the corridor. Soft inadmissibility means the move is possible but incurs explicit defect, risk, or witness debt. Soft inadmissibility may be used in exploratory or diagnostic corridors, but the debt must travel with the route.
One may therefore refine the legality predicate into a graded relation
\operatorname{AdjClass}_\kappa(o,\mu,o')
\in
\{
\mathrm{Legal},
\mathrm{Soft},
\mathrm{Blocked}
\}.
A local connection checker can be schematized as:
def legal_adjacency(src, move, dst, corridor, graph_index, witness_state):
if not structural_reachability(src, move, dst, graph_index):
return "Blocked"
if not type_compatible(src, move, dst):
return "Blocked"
if not corridor_permitted(src, move, dst, corridor):
return "Blocked"
if not boundary_compatible(src, dst):
return "Blocked"
if not invariant_preserving(src, move, dst):
return "Soft"
if not witness_sufficient(src, move, dst, witness_state):
return "Soft"
return "Legal"
The above captures the essential design: structural reach is not enough. A move must also respect type, corridor, boundary, invariants, and witnesses.
Theorem 7.2 — Route Legality Reduces to Local Typed Connection Legality
A route is legal if and only if every one of its local transitions is legal and all global budget, contract, and witness accumulation rules are satisfied.
Proof. By route construction, every route is a finite composition of local transitions. If any local transition is blocked, the route cannot be legal. If all local transitions are legal, then global legality reduces to whether the accumulated contracts, witnesses, and budgets remain admissible under composition. Hence route legality decomposes into local legality plus global accumulation laws.
This completes the Square foundation of route algebra: route objects, hypergraph neighborhoods, path composition, nexus topology, and typed local legality.
Ch07.B1 — Bridge Families and Transform Directionality
The Flower route algebra begins with bridges. A bridge is a lawful movement primitive connecting route regions or representational regimes that cannot be joined by mere local adjacency. Bridges therefore carry direction, polarity, transform mode, and phase law.
Let a bridge object be
b
=
\Big(
u_b,\,
\operatorname{src}(b),\,
\operatorname{dst}(b),\,
\operatorname{mode}(b),\,
\operatorname{dir}(b),\,
\operatorname{pol}(b),\,
\Theta_b,\,
\Pi_b,\,
\psi_b
\Big).
The directionality classes are at least:
\{
\mathrm{Symmetric},
\mathrm{Invertible},
\mathrm{OneWay},
\mathrm{ConditionalReverse},
\mathrm{Irreversible}
\}.
Bridge families are indexed not only by transform type but by route role. The basic bridge families include:
symbolic formal bridges,
proof code bridges,
local global bridges,
ambiguous singular bridges,
compressed expanded bridges,
conflict repair bridges,
route-family transfer bridges.
A bridge may therefore be understood as a typed operator:
B_\tau : \mathbb{R}_U \to \mathbb{R}_V
B_\tau : X_U \to X_V.
A route segment using a bridge is lawful only if
\operatorname{BridgeLegal}_\kappa(\rho,b)
=
1.
A bridge family is therefore a controlled transform alphabet inside the route algebra. It turns what would otherwise be informal “jumps” into lawful directional operators.
Definition 7.4 — Bridge-Compatible Route State
A route state is bridge-compatible with bridge if the terminal phase, contract, and boundary profile of satisfy the entry law of , and if the exit profile generated by remains admissible for the target region.
Proposition 7.3 — Directionality Is an Algebraic Property of Bridges, Not a Mere Annotation
If reversing a bridge step changes admissibility, contract flow, or witness burden, then directionality affects route algebra itself and cannot be treated as cosmetic metadata.
Proof. A change that alters composability, legality, or witness accumulation changes the algebraic behavior of route composition. Hence directionality is algebraic, not merely descriptive.
The Flower route algebra therefore begins by objectifying transform directionality.
Ch07.B2 — Resonance-Aligned Path Selection
Once bridges and dynamic couplings exist, route selection cannot rely solely on structural reachability or static weights. The manuscript must select paths whose dynamic phases, couplings, and bridge alignments are resonant with the problem state. This is resonance-aligned path selection.
Let the active problem state under the Flower lens be
\Psi_q.
\Psi_{\rho_i}.
\mathcal{K}(\Psi_q,\Psi_{\rho_i}),
Path selection is thus not:
\text{pick route with shortest length}
\text{pick route with highest static weight},
\text{pick route with strongest lawful dynamic coherence under current corridor and contract}.
A dynamic path score may be written:
S_{\Flower}(\rho_i)
=
\alpha \mathcal{K}(\Psi_q,\Psi_{\rho_i})
+
\beta \operatorname{BridgeAlign}(\rho_i)
+
\gamma \operatorname{PhaseCont}(\rho_i)
-
\delta \operatorname{Drift}(\rho_i)
-
\varepsilon \operatorname{Conflict}(\rho_i).
Resonance-aligned path selection is especially important when several structurally valid routes exist. One route may be formally legal but dynamically brittle. Another may be slightly longer but dynamically smooth, with low phase drift and constructive bridge couplings. The manuscript should prefer the latter unless the corridor or output contract overrides that preference.
Route resonance may also be local. A path may begin weakly aligned but enter a high-resonance bridge or nexus chamber later. Thus route evaluation may use cumulative or path-integrated resonance:
\int_{\rho_i} \mathcal{K}_{\mathrm{local}}\,ds.
Definition 7.5 — Resonance-Preferred Route
A route is resonance-preferred under problem state and corridor if it maximizes the corridor-adjusted Flower route score among currently admissible candidates.
Proposition 7.4 — Structural Validity Does Not Imply Dynamic Preference
Two routes may be equally valid structurally yet differ in resonance alignment, so structural legality alone is insufficient for optimal route choice under dynamic conditions.
Proof. Structural legality constrains possibility, not dynamic suitability. If the routes differ in phase drift, bridge alignment, or interference pattern, then one may solve more coherently than the other despite equal legality.
The route algebra thus begins to incorporate motion quality, not only path existence.
Ch07.B3 — Orientation Reversal and Mirror Traversal
Some route families admit reversals, dualizations, or mirror traversals. Others do not. The Flower route algebra therefore distinguishes ordinary forward traversal from lawful orientation reversal.
Let a route
\rho=(o_0,\mu_1,\ldots,\mu_n,o_n)
edges may be one-way,
bridges may be irreversible,
witness flow may be directional,
phase continuity may invert or break,
contract obligations may not be reversible.
A mirror traversal is therefore not the raw reverse word. It is a derived route
\operatorname{Mir}(\rho)
\operatorname{Mir}(\mu_i)=\mu_i^\dagger
The route reversal law is thus:
\operatorname{Rev}(\rho)\downarrow
\iff
\forall i,\;\mu_i^\dagger \text{ exists and is admissible in reverse order.}
Mirror traversal is extremely useful in the manuscript brain because many cognitive tasks involve inversion:
explanation from theorem versus theorem from explanation,
compression versus expansion,
synthesis versus decomposition,
diagnosis from failure versus failure from design,
route replay backward from output to seed.
These are not always exact inverses, but the algebra needs a controlled way to express such reversals.
A bridge or tunnel may also admit orientation-reversed variants. For example, a symbolic-to-formal bridge may reverse only via an interpretive dual bridge rather than the original bridge itself. Thus mirror traversal often changes movement family, not just direction.
Definition 7.6 — Mirror-Admissible Route
A route is mirror-admissible if there exists a route obtained by lawful reversal or dualization of each primitive step such that source/target and contract roles are exchanged consistently and witness obligations remain satisfiable.
Theorem 7.3 — Reversibility Is Route-Local and Primitive-Sensitive
A route is reversible if and only if every primitive step in its composition is reversible or has a lawful dual replacement, and the resulting reversed sequence remains corridor-admissible.
Proof. Route reversal composes primitive reversals. If any primitive lacks admissible reverse behavior, the full reversal fails at that point unless a dual replacement exists. Thus reversibility is determined locally at the level of primitives and globally by their composition.
The route algebra thus supports inversion, but only lawfully.
Ch07.B4 — Harmonic Route Cycles and Closed Loops
The final Flower task is to define cycles. Some route families form meaningful loops: proof–replay–repair loops, expansion–collapse loops, local–global synthesis loops, tunnel-return loops, and other recurrent circuits.
