Fifth, regeneration under uncertainty must preserve ambiguity and error bounds, not silently overresolve them. Sixth, recursive memory shells and multiscale seeds permit repeated compaction and expansion without dissolving identity or destroying future routeability.
This is the deeper meaning of Q-Shrink. It is not merely “compression.” It is the exact theory of how to store the manuscript’s intelligence in compact, routeable, lawfully regenerable form.
Ch09.A1 — Seed Packets, Memory Cells, and Chunk Graphs
The Square foundation of regenerative memory begins from the primitive memory carriers: seed packets, memory cells, and chunk graphs. The manuscript brain does not remember an object by storing its entire expanded neighborhood. It stores a seed packet embedded in a cell and connected into a graph of chunk relations sufficient for lawful later regeneration.
A seed packet is the irreducible Square carrier of compressive memory. It is defined as
\sigma
=
\Big(
u_\sigma,\,
a_\sigma,\,
\chi_\sigma,\,
\mathcal{H}_\sigma,\,
\mathcal{S}_\sigma,\,
\mathcal{I}_\sigma,\,
\mathcal{W}_\sigma
\Big),
is the seed identity,
is the canonical anchor address,
is the identity core of the remembered object or region,
is the route-hook family,
is the reconstruction schema,
is the invariant subset preserved by the seed,
is the witness residue sufficient to justify the seed’s compression status.
The seed is not equivalent to a summary sentence, a token vector, or a generic embedding. It contains a canonical identity core, typed route hooks, and a reconstruction law. Without these, the seed would be informative but not regeneratively sufficient.
A memory cell is the smallest persistent addressable container in which one or more seed packets are stored. A memory cell is
c
=
\Big(
u_c,\,
a_c,\,
\Sigma_c,\,
\partial c,\,
\operatorname{meta}_c,\,
I_c
\Big),
is the cell identity,
is the cell address,
is the family of resident seed packets,
is the boundary interface of the cell,
is metadata such as compression mode, timestamp, lens signature, or replay class,
is the cell invariant set.
A cell differs from a seed. A seed is a minimal carrier of one remembered state or motif. A cell is an addressable chamber that may contain one seed or a tightly coupled family of seeds, often because their joint storage improves regeneration economy. A theorem statement, its witness shell, and its canonical route motif might share a cell. A route macro, its replay trace seed, and its patch delta pointer might share another.
A chunk graph is the explicit graph among cells and seeds:
\mathfrak{C}
=
(\mathcal{C},\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{chunk}}),
\{
\mathrm{depends\_on},
\mathrm{expands\_to},
\mathrm{compresses\_from},
\mathrm{witnesses},
\mathrm{replays},
\mathrm{aliases},
\mathrm{mirrors},
\mathrm{patches},
\mathrm{inherits}
\}.
A chunk graph is not a convenience overlay. It is the explicit condition that turns isolated seeds into a memory system. Without it, the atlas would store many compact objects but would not know how to traverse them for reconstruction.
A memory seed packet is well formed only if its anchor address resolves, its route hooks are typed, its invariant subset is declared, and its reconstruction schema is replayable. A memory cell is well formed only if every resident seed is well formed and the cell boundary interface matches the intended chunk graph relations.
A minimal schema is:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, List, Any
@dataclass
class SeedPacket:
seed_id: str
address_anchor: str
identity_core: Dict[str, Any]
route_hooks: List[Dict[str, Any]]
reconstruction_schema: Dict[str, Any]
invariant_subset: Dict[str, Any]
witness_residue: Dict[str, Any]
@dataclass
class MemoryCell:
cell_id: str
address: str
seeds: List[SeedPacket]
boundary: Dict[str, Any]
metadata: Dict[str, Any]
invariants: Dict[str, Any]
@dataclass
class ChunkGraph:
cell_ids: List[str]
chunk_links: List[Dict[str, Any]]
The deeper significance of seed packets and memory cells is that they preserve why a memory is recoverable. They do not merely store that a thing existed. They store the lawful minimum from which it can re-enter cognition.
Definition 9.1 — Regenerative Seed Sufficiency
A seed packet is regeneratively sufficient for task class under corridor if its reconstruction schema and route hooks can regenerate the invariant subset of the remembered object required for without violating the defect bound of .
Proposition 9.1 — Memory Without Chunk Graphs Is Not Routeable
A collection of seed packets lacking explicit chunk relations cannot, in general, support lawful multistep regeneration because it lacks typed memory-to-memory traversal.
Proof. Regeneration of complex regions often requires reopening supporting seeds, witness residues, or inherited macro shells. Without chunk relations, the system cannot know which auxiliary memory objects must be activated or in what order. Therefore routeable regeneration requires a chunk graph.
The Square memory engine therefore begins by objectifying compressive memory at the seed, cell, and chunk-graph levels.
Ch09.A2 — Canonical Compaction and Decomposition Rules
The manuscript brain cannot allow arbitrary compression. If two equivalent atlas regions are compacted in unrelated ways, later replay, patch comparison, and cross-episode reuse become unstable. The memory system therefore requires canonical compaction: a deterministic compression discipline mapping admissible regions into standard memory objects.
Let
X\in \mathbb{O}_{\mathrm{atlas}}
\mathcal{Q}_{\kappa,\chi,B}(X)
=
m_X,
The first phase is support extraction. The operator determines the minimal support substructure of relevant to the current memory contract . For example, a proof-replay contract requires stronger witness preservation than a general thematic recall contract. This support extraction yields:
\operatorname{Supp}_{\kappa,\chi}(X)\subseteq X.
The second phase is equivalence reduction. Redundant substructures inside the support are quotiented under the active equivalence relation:
\widetilde{X}
=
\operatorname{Quot}_{\sim_{\kappa,\chi}}\big(\operatorname{Supp}_{\kappa,\chi}(X)\big).
The third phase is factorization. The reduced structure is decomposed into memory-meaningful factors such as identity core, route hooks, invariant shell, witness residue, ambiguity capsule, and optional replay trace:
\widetilde{X}
\rightsquigarrow
\big(
\chi_X,\,
\mathcal{H}_X,\,
\mathcal{I}_X,\,
\psi_X,\,
\Gamma_X,\,
\eta_X
\big).
The fourth phase is seed assembly. These factors are assembled into one or more canonical seed packets and cells:
\Sigma_X
=
\operatorname{Assemble}_{\kappa,\chi}\big(
\chi_X,\mathcal{H}_X,\mathcal{I}_X,\psi_X,\Gamma_X,\eta_X
\big).
The fifth phase is normalization. The resulting memory object is serialized and ordered canonically:
m_X
=
\operatorname{NF}_{\mathrm{mem},\kappa,\chi}(\Sigma_X).
Canonical compaction is therefore not mere deletion of detail. It is a structured decomposition of a region into standard reconstructive components.
The inverse process is decomposition for regeneration. Given a memory object , the recall engine must know how to split it back into reconstructive tasks:
m_X
\rightsquigarrow
\big(
\chi_X,\,
\mathcal{H}_X,\,
\mathcal{I}_X,\,
\psi_X,\,
\Gamma_X,\,
\eta_X
\big)
\rightsquigarrow
\widehat{X}.
Canonical compaction also requires a compaction contract lattice. Different contracts demand different retained factors. Let:
\chi_{\mathrm{mem}}
\in
\{
\mathrm{IdentityOnly},
\mathrm{StructuralRecall},
\mathrm{RouteRecall},
\mathrm{WitnessRecall},
\mathrm{ReplayRecall},
\mathrm{PatchRecall},
\mathrm{FullLossless}
\}.
\mathrm{IdentityOnly}
\preceq
\mathrm{StructuralRecall}
\preceq
\mathrm{RouteRecall}
\preceq
\mathrm{WitnessRecall}
\preceq
\mathrm{ReplayRecall}
\preceq
\mathrm{PatchRecall}
\preceq
\mathrm{FullLossless}.
A representative compaction skeleton is:
def compact_region(region, memory_contract, corridor):
support = extract_support(region, memory_contract, corridor)
reduced = quotient_region(support, corridor, memory_contract)
factors = factorize_region(reduced, memory_contract)
seeds = assemble_seeds(factors, memory_contract)
return normalize_memory_object(seeds, corridor, memory_contract)
Definition 9.2 — Canonical Compaction Correctness
A compaction is correct if and only if:
\operatorname{Regen}_{\kappa,\chi}(m_X)
\approx_{\kappa,\chi}
X
Theorem 9.1 — Canonical Compaction Is Idempotent up to Memory Normal Form
If compaction uses deterministic support extraction, equivalence reduction, factorization, and memory normalization, then:
\mathcal{Q}(\mathcal{Q}(X))
\equiv
\mathcal{Q}(X)
Proof. Once a region is reduced to its canonical memory factors and normalized, reapplying the same compaction pipeline cannot remove anything further or rearrange it except into the same normal form.
This makes memory storage replayable and comparable across episodes.
Ch09.A3 — Lossless Memory Kernels and Bounded-Loss Regimes
Not all memory use cases require the same fidelity. Some require full replay. Some require only identity and route recovery. Some allow bounded degradation of wording or local detail. The manuscript therefore distinguishes between lossless memory kernels and bounded-loss memory regimes.
A lossless memory kernel for object is a compact representation
K_X^{\mathrm{lossless}}
\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{mem}}(K_X^{\mathrm{lossless}})=X
A bounded-loss regime is defined by an invariant subset
I_{\kappa,\chi}(X)
\varepsilon_{\kappa,\chi}\ge 0.
d_{\kappa,\chi}\big(
\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{mem}}(K_X^{\mathrm{bnd}}),X
\big)
\le
\varepsilon_{\kappa,\chi}
This yields a spectrum of compression classes:
\mathcal{K}_{\mathrm{mem}}
=
\{
\mathrm{ExactKernel},
\mathrm{RouteKernel},
\mathrm{WitnessKernel},
\mathrm{SummaryKernel},
\mathrm{AmbiguityKernel},
\mathrm{SpeculativeKernel}
\}.
The bounded-loss regime is not a loophole for careless compression. It is a lawful declaration of which aspects may be abstracted and by how much. This declaration is a first-class part of the memory object:
\delta_m
=
\big(
\delta_{\mathrm{struct}},
\delta_{\mathrm{route}},
\delta_{\mathrm{wit}},
\delta_{\mathrm{phase}},
\delta_{\mathrm{ambig}}
\big).
Lossless and bounded-loss kernels may coexist for the same underlying region. A theorem family might have an exact replay kernel for proof use and a cheaper summary kernel for broad navigation. The memory system treats these as related but distinct memory contracts, not as contradictions.
Definition 9.3 — Memory Fidelity Class
A memory object has fidelity class if its regeneration guarantees correspond exactly to the invariant set and defect budget declared by .
Proposition 9.2 — Lossless Storage Is Unnecessary for All Tasks but Necessary for Some
If all tasks were forced to use lossless memory kernels, storage and retrieval would become inefficient; if no task could use lossless kernels, strict replay would become impossible. Therefore the manuscript requires both exact and bounded-loss regimes.
Proof. Efficiency demands abstraction for many tasks, while strict replay demands exact kernels for some tasks. Hence a mixed regime is necessary.
The memory system therefore supports graded fidelity without losing formal control.
Ch09.A4 — Storage-Efficient Serialization of Cognitive State
Memory is not only about what is preserved but how it is stored. The atlas requires memory objects to serialize compactly, deterministically, and in a way that supports hashing, patch linking, and fast access. The Square memory layer therefore defines a storage-efficient serialization discipline.
Let a memory object be
m
=
\Big(
u_m,\,
a_m,\,
\sigma_m,\,
\operatorname{sh}_m,\,
\mathcal{R}_m,\,
\psi_m,\,
\delta_m,\,
\Lambda_m,\,
\Theta_m,\,
\Gamma_m,\,
I_m
\Big).
\operatorname{Ser}_{\mathrm{mem}}:
\mathbb{M}_{\mathrm{mem}}\to \Sigma^\ast
determinism,
canonical field ordering,
efficient representation of repeated structure,
stable hashing,
explicit version tagging,
replay-safe decoding.
Storage efficiency is improved by exploiting several structural facts:
many memory objects share the same lens or corridor metadata,
many route hooks can be stored by reference rather than copy,
witness residues often compress strongly via template encoding,
seed schemas can be factored into reusable grammars,
chunk graphs can be delta-coded relative to local cell clusters.
A canonical memory serialization is therefore factored:
\operatorname{Ser}_{\mathrm{mem}}(m)
=
\operatorname{Head}(m)\;\Vert\;
\operatorname{Core}(m)\;\Vert\;
\operatorname{Hooks}(m)\;\Vert\;
\operatorname{Witness}(m)\;\Vert\;
\operatorname{Defect}(m)\;\Vert\;
\operatorname{Footer}(m),
stores ID, address, version, and contract class,
stores seed core and invariant subset,
stores route hooks and chunk references,
stores witness residues or links,
stores error and ambiguity envelopes,
stores hashes and optional replay links.
One may also exploit dictionary-based compression for common schemas. Let be the schema dictionary. Then repeated structure is stored as dictionary references rather than raw expansion.
A basic deterministic serializer may be sketched as:
def serialize_memory_object(mem_obj):
record = {
"id": mem_obj["id"],
"address": mem_obj["address"],
"contract": mem_obj["contract"],
"seed": mem_obj["seed"],
"shell": mem_obj["shell"],
"hooks": mem_obj["hooks"],
"witness": mem_obj["witness"],
"defect": mem_obj["defect"],
"version": mem_obj["version"],
}
return canonical_encode(record)
The importance of storage-efficient serialization is not merely implementation efficiency. It also affects the atlas’s cognitive rhythm. Faster serialization and deserialization mean more agile patching, quicker replay, more responsive route search, and more viable always-on persistence.
Definition 9.4 — Canonical Memory Serialization
A memory serialization is canonical if it is deterministic, round-trippable, and stable under repeated encoding of normal-form memory objects.
Theorem 9.2 — Canonical Serialization Preserves Memory Identity under Storage
If memory objects are first normalized and then serialized canonically, then the resulting byte or string form preserves identity for hashing and patch comparison.
Proof. Normalization removes representational variance. Canonical serialization maps equal normal forms to equal encodings. Therefore the encoded form may serve as an identity-preserving storage surrogate.
This completes the Square foundation of regenerative memory: seeds, cells, chunk graphs, canonical compaction, fidelity classes, and efficient serialization.
Ch09.B1 — Cross-Lens Encoding Signatures
Memory in a holographic manuscript cannot be neutral with respect to lens. The same remembered object may later need to be recalled under a structural, dynamic, probabilistic, or recursive view. The Flower interpretation of memory therefore introduces cross-lens encoding signatures: dynamic metadata describing how the memory was encoded relative to the four base lenses.
For a memory object , define the lens signature
\Lambda_m
=
\big(
\lambda_{\Square}(m),\,
\lambda_{\Flower}(m),\,
\lambda_{\Cloud}(m),\,
\lambda_{\Fractal}(m)
\big),
\qquad
\lambda_\ell(m)\ge 0.
A richer cross-lens signature includes not only weights but encoding maps:
\mathcal{E}_m^\ell:
\chi_m \rightsquigarrow K_\ell(m),
recall the same object structurally,
recall it as a dynamic route pattern,
recall it as an uncertainty field,
recall it as a multiscale macro.
The cross-lens signature therefore acts as a routing hint for regeneration. Given an active problem context with desired lens profile , the recall engine compares it to the memory’s encoding signature:
\operatorname{Align}(\Lambda_q,\Lambda_m).
Encoding signatures also allow memory selection to be smarter. When several candidate memory objects match structurally, the system can prefer the one whose lens signature better matches the current use.
Definition 9.5 — Lens-Complete Memory Object
A memory object is lens-complete for a context class if its encoding signature includes sufficient preserved information to regenerate the required representation under the relevant lens or lawful transport.
Proposition 9.3 — Lens-Neutral Compression Loses Reconstructive Power
If memory compression ignores how the object was represented across lenses, later recall must infer that structure from scratch, increasing error and cost.
Proof. Cross-lens information influences how the same seed should be reopened. If it is omitted, recall must reconstruct it indirectly or guess, which is less reliable.
The memory system therefore remembers not only content, but how content was lens-shaped.
Ch09.B2 — Phase-Preserving Memory Transforms
A memory object is not only lens-shaped; it is phase-shaped. Routes, proofs, contradictions, and syntheses often have a dynamic order or activation rhythm. If memory erases this entirely, later recall may recover content but not the dynamic conditions under which that content was effective. The Flower memory layer therefore defines phase-preserving memory transforms.
Let the phase profile of an encoded object be
\Theta_m
=
\big(
\theta_m,\,
\dot{\theta}_m,\,
m_m,\,
\alpha_m
\big),
A phase-preserving memory transform is a compression map
\mathcal{Q}_{\Theta}:
X \to m
\Theta_m.
\operatorname{Trace}\big(\Theta_X,\Theta_{\widehat{X}}\big)\downarrow
This does not require that the full original continuous trajectory be stored. It requires only that the memory preserve enough dynamic trace to support:
route-order reconstruction,
phase-aligned recall,
tunnel entry conditions,
resonance-based selection,
replay of critical dynamic transitions.
For instance, a repair loop memory should preserve whether repair followed contradiction immediately or only after a decomposition phase. A theorem synthesis memory should preserve whether witness consolidation occurred before or after transport to executable schema. These are phase-bearing distinctions that matter.
A compressed phase record may be symbolic or numeric. Examples include:
\Theta_m
=
(\mathrm{derive}\to\mathrm{witness}\to\mathrm{collapse}),
\Theta_m
=
\big(
\theta_0,\{\Delta\theta_i\}_{i},\operatorname{mode\_sequence},\alpha_{\mathrm{peak}}
\big).
Definition 9.6 — Phase-Faithful Memory
A memory object is phase-faithful for task class if its compressed phase record is sufficient to reconstruct the dynamic ordering constraints relevant to .
Theorem 9.3 — Phase-Faithful Memory Supports Dynamic Replay Better than Phase-Blind Memory
If a task depends on route order, dynamic switching, or resonance alignment, then phase-faithful memory yields strictly better replay or reconstruction than phase-blind memory.
Proof. Phase-blind memory omits ordering and dynamic alignment information that is causally relevant for the task. Phase-faithful memory preserves this information. Therefore replay quality is strictly improved whenever such information matters.
The manuscript therefore remembers process rhythm, not only state residue.
Ch09.B3 — Symbol Packs and Resonance-Based Recall Triggers
The memory system must also know when to recall a memory. Exact address queries are only one trigger. Many high-value recalls begin from symbolic similarity, transform resemblance, or dynamic resonance. The Flower layer formalizes this through symbol packs and resonance-based recall triggers.
A symbol pack is a compact symbolic or motif-bearing header attached to a memory object:
\Sigma_m^{\mathrm{sym}}
=
\big(
\tau_m,\,
\mu_m,\,
\zeta_m,\,
\mathcal{A}_m
\big),
is the thematic or symbolic signature,
is the operator or movement motif signature,
is the resonance vector,
is the alias and legacy name family.
The symbol pack is not a replacement for canonical address. It is a noncanonical trigger surface. When a problem state arrives with dynamic or symbolic structure similar to , the memory may be proposed for recall.
A resonance-based trigger is a function
T_{\mathrm{recall}}(q,m)
=
\mathcal{K}\big(
\Sigma_q^{\mathrm{sym}},
\Sigma_m^{\mathrm{sym}}
\big),
This mechanism is crucial for creative or analogical cognition. A theorem memory might be recalled not because its address was named, but because the current problem carries a homologous structural rhythm. A patch memory might be recalled because the present contradiction pulse resembles a stored repair loop. These are not arbitrary associations; they are lawful because the symbol packs and trigger kernels are explicit, inspectable, and witness-aware.
Definition 9.7 — Trigger-Admissible Recall
A triggered recall is admissible if the trigger score exceeds the corridor threshold and the memory’s defect and witness profile are compatible with the intended use.
Proposition 9.4 — Address-Only Recall Underutilizes Manuscript Memory
A memory system that recalls only by exact address or lexical match misses structurally and dynamically homologous memories that could lawfully guide solution search.
Proof. Many useful memories are related by structure, route motif, or dynamic rhythm rather than explicit naming. Address-only recall cannot access these relations unless the caller already knows them. Therefore it underutilizes the memory geometry.
The manuscript therefore supports lawful associative recall without sacrificing rigor.
Ch09.B4 — Temporal Rhythm in Memory Regeneration
Memory recall is not instantaneous in a deep manuscript. Some recalls are cheap and direct; some require phased reconstruction. Some should be opened gradually so that budget and ambiguity can be managed. The Flower memory layer therefore introduces temporal rhythm in regeneration.
Let a memory object be recalled under context . The regeneration is not merely:
m \mapsto \widehat{X}.
m
\to
\widehat{X}^{(0)}
\to
\widehat{X}^{(1)}
\to
\cdots
\to
\widehat{X}^{(k)},
The rhythm of this process is encoded by a regeneration schedule
\mathcal{T}_m
=
\big(
\tau_0,\tau_1,\dots,\tau_k
\big),
shell opening,
witness loading,
ambiguity expansion,
route hook activation,
cross-lens transport,
collapse to output artifact.
A memory that is strong in structural detail but weak in dynamic compatibility may open differently from one whose phase signature matches the current problem exactly. Thus regeneration rhythm interacts with phase alignment:
\operatorname{Tempo}(m,q)
=
f\big(\operatorname{Align}(\Theta_m,\Theta_q),B,\kappa,\chi\big).
Temporal rhythm is important because it lets memory remain adaptive and budget-aware. It also supports partial recall. A large theorem memory may open just enough to test route relevance before its witness shell is fully reopened.
Definition 9.8 — Rhythm-Admissible Regeneration
A regeneration schedule is rhythm-admissible if each stage preserves corridor legality and no later stage assumes information not opened by earlier stages or supplied externally.
Proposition 9.5 — Staged Regeneration Dominates Monolithic Recall Under Budget Constraints
When budgets are finite and memory objects are large, staged regeneration allows earlier rejection or acceptance decisions and therefore consumes less expected cost than monolithic full recall.
Proof. Staged recall can terminate early if the memory is irrelevant or already sufficient. Monolithic recall always pays full cost. Hence staged regeneration has lower or equal expected cost under uncertainty.
The Flower memory system thus makes recall a dynamic process, not a blunt database fetch.
Ch09.C1 — Regeneration Confidence and Reconstruction Error
The Cloud memory layer begins with the most basic epistemic question: when a memory object regenerates a region, how certain should the atlas be that the reconstruction is faithful? Regenerative memory must therefore attach confidence and error explicitly to recall.
Let
m \xrightarrow{\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{mem}}} \widehat{X}
\operatorname{Conf}_{\mathrm{regen}}(m,\widehat{X}\mid \xi)
\in [0,1],
\epsilon_{\mathrm{regen}}(m,\widehat{X}\mid \xi)
=
\big(
\epsilon_{\mathrm{struct}},
\epsilon_{\mathrm{route}},
\epsilon_{\mathrm{wit}},
\epsilon_{\mathrm{phase}},
\epsilon_{\mathrm{ambig}}
\big).
These are estimated from:
compression class,
defect envelope stored in the memory object,
amount of reconstruction actually performed,
alignment between memory signature and current problem,
amount of external support or route hooks reopened,
replay history of prior regenerations of the same memory class.
A convenient confidence model is:
\operatorname{Conf}_{\mathrm{regen}}
=
\exp\big(
-\alpha\epsilon_{\mathrm{struct}}
-\beta\epsilon_{\mathrm{route}}
-\gamma\epsilon_{\mathrm{wit}}
-\delta\epsilon_{\mathrm{phase}}
-\eta\epsilon_{\mathrm{ambig}}
\big)
\cdot
\operatorname{ReplayCalib}(m).
A memory object with low reconstruction error in structure but high error in witness detail may still be good for heuristic navigation but poor for strict proof use. Therefore confidence is not one-dimensional in practice. It is contract-specific:
\operatorname{Conf}_{\kappa,\chi}(m,\widehat{X}).
Definition 9.9 — Confidence-Honest Regeneration
A regeneration is confidence honest if its declared confidence and error profile are derived from the memory object’s compression class, reconstruction path, and replay history rather than inferred implicitly from superficial completeness.
Theorem 9.4 — Explicit Error Profiles Prevent Overclaiming in Regenerative Memory
If memory regeneration attaches explicit error profiles to reconstructed artifacts, then downstream synthesis can distinguish strong recall from weak approximate recall and avoid overclaiming exactness.
Proof. Without such profiles, downstream consumers receive only the reconstructed artifact and may treat it as exact. With explicit error profiles, they can adjust confidence or request deeper recall. Therefore overclaiming is reduced.
This is the epistemic shield of regenerative memory: it remembers honestly.
Ch09.C2 — Memory Ambiguity under Partial Seeds
A single seed may lawfully reconstruct more than one admissible region, especially when compression preserved ambiguity rather than collapsing it. The memory system must therefore support memory ambiguity: plural possible regenerations from one partial or bounded-loss seed.
Let the seed packet be . Its reconstruction family is:
\mathcal{R}(\sigma)=\{\widehat{X}_1,\dots,\widehat{X}_n\},
\Gamma_\sigma
=
\big(
\mathcal{R}(\sigma),\,
\pi_\sigma,\,
\Delta_\sigma,\,
\tau_\sigma
\big),
is the prior over candidate regenerations,
is the discrimination schema,
is the collapse policy.
Memory ambiguity arises in several ways:
bounded-loss compression discarded disambiguating details,
alias or legacy naming preserved several interpretations,
route hooks were retained only up to equivalence class,
a symbol pack was intentionally broad,
a partial seed is being used before full shell activation.
Memory ambiguity is not a flaw if preserved lawfully. It allows the atlas to reopen a plural memory and then discriminate with current context instead of pretending there was only one thing stored.
Definition 9.10 — Ambiguity-Preserving Seed
A seed is ambiguity-preserving if its schema and witness explicitly permit multiple lawful reconstructions and its memory object records this plurality rather than falsely singularizing it.
Proposition 9.6 — Partial Seeds Necessarily Increase Ambiguity Relative to Exact Kernels
If a seed omits disambiguating structure that an exact kernel would retain, then the set of lawful reconstructions is weakly larger.
Proof. Omitted distinguishing information relaxes the constraints on reconstruction. Therefore the admissible reconstruction set cannot shrink and may expand.
This makes ambiguity a lawful property of compression, not merely a retrieval problem.
Ch09.C3 — Uncertain Recall Capsules and Fallback Modes
When a memory object cannot be regenerated confidently enough for the current contract, the system must not simply fail or bluff. It must encapsulate the uncertainty and offer fallback modes. The Cloud memory layer therefore introduces uncertain recall capsules.
An uncertain recall capsule is an object
\mathcal{U}_m
=
\Big(
u_{\mathcal{U}},\,
m,\,
\mathcal{R}(\sigma_m),\,
\operatorname{Conf}_{\mathrm{regen}},\,
\epsilon_{\mathrm{regen}},\,
\operatorname{fallback},\,
\omega_{\mathcal{U}}
\Big).
what memory was attempted,
what candidate reconstructions remain,
how confident the system is,
what kinds of fallback are available.
Fallback modes include:
shallow summary recall,
exact address or witness fetch,
cross-lens trigger search,
request for external disambiguation,
park-and-continue with weaker surrogate memory,
replay-seed deep regeneration.
The purpose of the capsule is to preserve lawful continuity of thought even when strong recall is not available. A synthesis engine may use a shallow summary recall if that is enough for exploratory routing, while refusing to use it for proof obligations. A repair engine may use a surrogate route motif while deeper recall is opened in the background of the current solve trace.
Definition 9.11 — Fallback-Admissible Memory Failure
A memory recall failure is fallback-admissible if the uncertain recall capsule exposes at least one weaker but lawful continuation mode or explicitly states that none exists.
Theorem 9.5 — Uncertain Recall Capsules Strictly Dominate Silent Recall Failure
If a memory regeneration attempt fails or remains uncertain, returning an explicit uncertainty capsule preserves more future reasoning options than silent failure or overconfident substitution.
Proof. The capsule records candidates, confidence, and fallback paths. Silent failure preserves none of these. Overconfident substitution misrepresents them. Therefore the capsule is strictly better.
The manuscript memory system thus degrades gracefully.
Ch09.C4 — Confidence-Aware Memory Selection in Synthesis
The final Cloud memory task is selection. A solve episode may have many candidate memories that are structurally relevant. The atlas must decide which to open, which to keep shallow, and which to ignore for the moment. This decision must be confidence-aware.
Let the candidate memory family for context be
\mathcal{M}_{\mathrm{cand}}(\xi)=\{m_1,\dots,m_k\}.
S_{\mathrm{mem}}(m_i\mid \xi)
=
\alpha\,\operatorname{Trigger}(m_i,q)
+
\beta\,\operatorname{Align}(\Lambda_{m_i},\Lambda_q)
+
\gamma\,\operatorname{Conf}_{\kappa,\chi}(m_i)
+
\delta\,\operatorname{ReuseValue}(m_i)
-
\eta\,\operatorname{RecallCost}(m_i).
Memory selection is therefore a two-stage process:
rank candidate memories by relevance and confidence,
decide recall depth adaptively.
This leads to staged memory use:
high-confidence, low-cost memories may be opened first,
medium-confidence memories may remain shallow until needed,
low-confidence memories may be parked or encapsulated.
A simple confidence-aware selector is:
def select_memories(memories, context, scorer, top_k=5):
scored = []
for mem in memories:
s = scorer(mem, context)
scored.append((s, mem))
scored.sort(reverse=True, key=lambda x: x[0])
return [m for _, m in scored[:top_k]]
The critical point is that the scorer must include regenerative confidence and defect profile, not only thematic or address similarity.
Definition 9.12 — Confidence-Aware Memory Selection
A memory selection policy is confidence-aware if candidate ranking depends materially on regeneration fidelity and not only on relevance or trigger strength.
Proposition 9.7 — Relevance-Only Memory Selection Overuses Brittle Memories
If memory selection ignores regeneration confidence, then strong but brittle symbolic matches may be opened preferentially over more reliable reconstructive memories, increasing downstream error.
Proof. Relevance without fidelity does not measure whether the memory can support the current contract. Therefore relevance-only selection can choose bad recall sources.
The Cloud layer of memory is thus complete: memory carries error, ambiguity, fallback, and confidence-aware selection.
Ch09.D1 — Seed-to-Tree Expansion and Branch Recovery
The Fractal layer begins with the most important regenerative act: expanding one seed into a multiscale memory tree. A seed does not usually regenerate one flat object. It regenerates a structured family of branches, witnesses, hooks, and possible submemories.
Let
\sigma_m
\mathcal{E}_{\Fractal}(\sigma_m,B)
=
\mathcal{T}_m,
\mathcal{T}_m=(V_m,E_m,r_m,\ell_m).
scale,
address anchor,
reconstruction state,
confidence,
defect,
branch priority.
Branch recovery is the process of reopening some or all of these branches under budget . The full tree may be too expensive to open. A lawful branch-recovery policy chooses a subset:
V_m^{\mathrm{open}}\subseteq V_m
The tree structure is crucial because it allows memory to behave like a living expandable object. A replay kernel might open many branches. A thematic recall may open only the top trunk. A route search may open just the branch containing transport motifs.
Definition 9.13 — Fractal Memory Tree
A Fractal memory tree is a rooted multiscale reconstruction graph generated from a seed packet, in which every node corresponds to a lawful partial reconstruction state and every edge corresponds to an admissible zoom or shell-opening move.
Theorem 9.6 — Seed-to-Tree Expansion Generalizes Flat Recall
Flat recall is the special case of seed-to-tree expansion in which the reconstruction tree has one level and all branches are opened at once.
Proof. If the tree depth is one and the open-set equals the whole tree, then branch selection disappears and recall is flat. Therefore flat recall is a degenerate case of seed-to-tree expansion.
The Fractal memory engine therefore treats recall as growth, not lookup.
Ch09.D2 — Nested Summaries and Expandable Memory Shells
A single seed is often too small, and a full tree too large. The intermediate solution is the memory shell: a layered boundary around a seed specifying how much contextual structure should be opened. Memory shells may themselves contain nested summaries.
For memory object , define a shell family
\operatorname{sh}_m
=
\big(
\operatorname{shell}^{(0)}_m,\,
\operatorname{shell}^{(1)}_m,\,
\dots,\,
\operatorname{shell}^{(k)}_m
\big),
\operatorname{shell}^{(j)}_m
=
\big(
\operatorname{radius}_j,\,
\operatorname{allowed\_branches}_j,\,
\operatorname{witness\_depth}_j,\,
\operatorname{ambiguity\_policy}_j
\big).
Nested summaries are summary artifacts stored at intermediate shell levels. They are not arbitrary prose condensations. They are checkpoint summaries:
S_m^{(j)}
Expandable memory shells solve a key problem: the atlas can keep many memories in compact form while still allowing progressively richer recall. This makes the manuscript scalable and responsive.
Definition 9.14 — Shell-Coherent Memory
A memory is shell-coherent if each shell level expands lawfully into the next, preserving the invariant subset promised at the lower shell and adding only admissible new detail.
Proposition 9.8 — Nested Summaries Preserve More Regenerative Structure than Freeform Summaries
A shell-based summary is tied to a specific reconstructive boundary and therefore can be expanded lawfully; a freeform summary may preserve content but not lawful regeneration paths.
Proof. The shell summary is generated from and linked to a specific reconstruction state. A freeform summary need not preserve route hooks, witness shells, or address anchors. Therefore shell-based summaries preserve more regenerative structure.
The memory shell is thus the Fractal boundary object mediating compactness and recoverability.
Ch09.D3 — Multiscale Caching and Inherited Reconstruction Paths
Repeated regeneration of the same memory tree can be expensive. The atlas therefore requires multiscale caching: storing partially expanded forms and the reconstruction paths that led to them, so that future recall can reuse them.
A cache entry at scale for memory object is
C_m^{(s)}
=
\big(
m,\,
s,\,
\widehat{X}_m^{(s)},\,
\pi_m^{(s)},\,
\operatorname{validity}_m^{(s)}
\big),
is the partially expanded object,
is the reconstruction path used,
states whether the cache remains consistent with current atlas state.
The important thing is that caches are not standalone. They inherit from the seed and its shells. Thus a cache entry remains tied to canonical memory identity and may be invalidated if patch lineage or route hooks change.
Inherited reconstruction paths are the stored expansion traces:
\pi_m^{(s)}=
(\sigma_m \to \operatorname{shell}^{(1)} \to \cdots \to \widehat{X}^{(s)}).
A cache system must be multiscale because different tasks reopen memories to different depths. Proof replay might need shell , while route seeding needs shell . Both should be cacheable.
Definition 9.15 — Cache-Valid Reconstruction Path
A cached reconstruction path is valid if all seeds, hooks, and witness dependencies used in the path remain consistent with the current atlas lineage and no invalidating patch has occurred.
Proposition 9.9 — Multiscale Caching Reduces Average Recall Cost
If the same memory objects are reopened at several recurring shell depths, then caching those shell states reduces expected future regeneration cost.
Proof. A cached shell state bypasses some or all of the reconstruction steps needed from the seed. Therefore future recall from that level costs less than re-expanding from the seed every time.
The memory engine therefore learns not only content but efficient reopening strategies.
Ch09.D4 — Repeated Compression without Identity Collapse
The final problem of regenerative memory is recursive compression itself. A manuscript memory may be compacted, recalled, patched, and compacted again many times. If this process accumulates distortion, identity will eventually collapse. The Fractal layer therefore defines conditions for repeated compression without identity collapse.
Let
X_0
X_0
\xrightarrow{\mathcal{Q}}
m_0
\xrightarrow{\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{mem}}}
X_1
\xrightarrow{\mathcal{Q}}
m_1
\xrightarrow{\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{mem}}}
X_2
\to \cdots
\mathbb{P}_\star
\mathbb{P}_\star(X_t)
=
\mathbb{P}_\star(X_0)
Repeated compression can fail in several ways:
progressive loss of route hooks,
witness erosion,
shell boundary drift,
ambiguity overcollapse,
alias corruption,
accumulated serialization defect.
To prevent this, the memory system enforces:
canonical compaction idempotence,
defect tracking across cycles,
witness refresh or replay checkpoints,
shell-coherence checks,
lineage-aware cache invalidation,
periodic exact-kernel anchoring for high-value memories.
A quantitative stability condition is:
d_{\kappa,\chi}(X_t,X_0)\le \epsilon_t,
\qquad
\sup_t \epsilon_t \le \varepsilon_{\max}
A repeated-compaction stabilizer may periodically reset by exact replay:
X_t
\rightsquigarrow
X_t^{\mathrm{exact}}
Definition 9.16 — Compression-Stable Memory Class
A memory class is compression-stable if repeated application of its compression–regeneration cycle preserves the identity core and keeps task-relevant defect within a bounded envelope.
Theorem 9.7 — Canonical Seeds, Shells, and Witness Refresh Yield Compression Stability
If memory objects use canonical compaction, shell-coherent regeneration, explicit defect tracking, and periodic witness refresh for high-value classes, then repeated compression can remain bounded and identity-preserving.
Proof. Canonical compaction prevents drift from representational variance. Shell coherence preserves regeneration structure. Defect tracking makes loss visible and controllable. Witness refresh restores or validates critical support surfaces. Therefore repeated compression remains bounded.
This concludes the Fractal memory layer. The manuscript brain now possesses recursive, scalable, lawful memory that can compact itself repeatedly without forgetting what it is.
The chapter has now completed the formal memory engine of the manuscript brain. Under the Square lens, memory became an addressable system of seed packets, cells, and chunk graphs, subject to canonical compaction, fidelity classes, and stable serialization. Under the Flower lens, memory acquired cross-lens encoding signatures, phase-preserving transforms, symbol packs for lawful associative recall, and staged temporal regeneration. Under the Cloud lens, memory gained explicit regeneration confidence, error profiles, ambiguity-preserving seeds, uncertain recall capsules, and confidence-aware memory selection. Under the Fractal lens, memory became a recursive growth process: seeds expanding into trees, trees bounded by shells, shells accelerated by multiscale caches, and repeated compaction stabilized against identity collapse.
The deepest consequence is that the manuscript now remembers in the same way it thinks: holographically, lawfully, and adaptively. A remembered theorem is not just text stored somewhere. It is a seed with an address anchor, route hooks, witness residue, lens signature, phase trace, ambiguity policy, and recursive shell schedule. A remembered route is not merely a cached answer. It is a reusable growth pattern in the solution geometry. A remembered contradiction is not an embarrassment to be hidden. It is a capsule that may reopen the right repair routes later. This is the exact formal meaning of regenerative memory. Q-Shrink is therefore not a side subsystem. It is the memory organ by which the manuscript brain becomes persistent without becoming bloated, reusable without becoming opaque, and compressed without ceasing to be alive.
CHAPTER 9 — REGENERATIVE MEMORY, SEED COMPRESSION, AND Q-SHRINK
Chapter 10 — Query Ingestion, RAG Routing, and Retrieval Programs
A manuscript brain does not retrieve by scattering similarity queries over a flat corpus and then hoping that a downstream generator will assemble the right answer from disconnected fragments. Such a method is too weak for a routeable, witness-bearing, self-updating atlas. The present chapter therefore formalizes retrieval as a lawful cognitive act. Its subject is not retrieval in the narrow information-retrieval sense alone, but the full machinery by which a normalized problem state enters the memory substrate, constructs target neighborhoods, opens the right shells, traverses admissible retrieval fronts across multiple lenses, detects contradictions during search, allocates finite search budgets, assembles partial hits into route bundles, and emits reusable retrieval programs for future episodes. In ordinary terms one might call this “RAG.” In the present framework it is more precise to call it retrieval-augmented route generation and retrieval-program execution.
The foundational claim of the chapter is that retrieval in a holographic manuscript is itself a route problem. The system is not selecting inert chunks. It is constructing a lawful path through memory seeds, atlas regions, route motifs, witness surfaces, and cross-lens transports in order to obtain a candidate support field sufficient for later synthesis. The output of retrieval is therefore not a bag of passages. It is a structured retrieval result:
\mathfrak{R}_t(q_0)
=
\Big(
\mathcal{C}_t,\,
P_t,\,
\mathcal{K}_t,\,
\mathcal{F}_t,\,
\mathcal{B}_t,\,
\rho_t^{\mathrm{ret}},\,
\psi_t^{\mathrm{ret}}
\Big),
is the candidate support family,
is the relevance distribution over candidates,
is the contradiction and conflict field discovered during retrieval,
is the set of active search fronts,
is the active retrieval budget and early-stopping state,
is the retrieval route bundle,
is the retrieval witness bundle.
The retrieval program is thus a first-class executable object. Given zero-point problem state , atlas state , memory substrate , chunk graph , route graph , and procedural weight field , the retrieval operator is:
\mathfrak{R}_t:
(q_0,S_t,\mathbb{M}_{\mathrm{mem}},\mathfrak{C},\mathfrak{G},W_t)
\to
(\mathcal{C}_t,P_t,\mathcal{K}_t,\rho_t^{\mathrm{ret}},\psi_t^{\mathrm{ret}}).
This chapter is organized according to the four-lens crystal discipline. Under the Square lens it defines retrieval packets, target neighborhood construction, address-first lookup, crystal indexing, lexical-semantic anchoring, and admissibility filters. Under the Flower lens it defines lens-specific retrieval passes, resonance querying, spectral matching across distant but homologous regions, chart-hopping, and phase-tuned retrieval under changing intent. Under the Cloud lens it defines relevance distributions, contradiction detection during retrieval, search budget allocation across competing fronts, and confidence-guided expansion with lawful early stopping. Under the Fractal lens it defines recursive retrieval programs, coarse-to-fine search cascades, branch pruning, neighborhood growth, route-bundle assembly from partial hits, and reusable retrieval macros that can later function as learned retrieval skills.
The chapter makes a deeper claim as well. Retrieval is where the memory chapter and the route chapter fuse. Memory stores compact reconstructive potentials; route algebra knows how to move. Retrieval is the act by which the atlas opens exactly enough memory to make later thinking lawful and economical. It is therefore the operational gateway from stored intelligence to active intelligence.
Ch10.A1 — Query Packets and Target Neighborhood Construction
The first act of lawful retrieval is to convert a normalized problem state into a retrieval packet: a structured query object whose fields are tuned not for general solve logic alone but specifically for support acquisition. A zero-point problem state already contains task class, ambiguity envelope, lens priors, output contract, and decomposition structure. The retrieval layer refines this into a packet that specifies where and how to look.
Let the retrieval packet space be
\mathbb{Q}_{\mathrm{ret}}.
q_{\mathrm{ret}}
=
\Big(
u_{\mathrm{ret}},\,
q_0,\,
A_q,\,
T_q,\,
\Lambda_q^{\mathrm{ret}},\,
\chi_q^{\mathrm{ret}},\,
\kappa_q,\,
B_q^{\mathrm{ret}},\,
\Gamma_q^{\mathrm{ret}},\,
\omega_q^{\mathrm{ret}}
\Big),
is the retrieval-event identity token,
is the source zero-point problem state,
is the anchor family,
is the target-neighborhood specification,
is the retrieval lens profile,
is the retrieval contract,
is the corridor class,
is the retrieval budget,
is the retrieval ambiguity and uncertainty profile,
is the packet witness.
The anchor family contains all currently usable points of entry into the atlas. These may include:
exact canonical addresses,
lifted local names,
seed addresses,
symbol-pack triggers,
route-motif triggers,
bridge or tunnel class hints,
chapter/lens/function coordinates,
output-contract target types,
explicit prior memory references from the current state.
The retrieval packet must not assume that only one anchor exists. High-quality retrieval usually begins from several anchor classes at once. For example, a theorem-oriented query may supply:
a chapter address,
a theorem kind bucket,
a route motif hint,
a witness requirement,
a patch prohibition. These act jointly to define the search manifold.
The most important construct generated at this stage is the target neighborhood. Retrieval must not search the entire atlas naively. It constructs one or more target neighborhoods:
T_q
=
\{N_1^{\mathrm{tgt}},\dots,N_r^{\mathrm{tgt}}\},
N^{\mathrm{tgt}}
=
\big(
\mathcal{A}^{\mathrm{seed}},
\mathcal{H}^{\mathrm{chunk}},
\mathcal{R}^{\mathrm{route}},
\mathcal{W}^{\mathrm{wit}},
\mathcal{D}^{\mathrm{depth}},
\mathcal{P}^{\mathrm{policy}}
\big),
is the seed and address starting set,
is the chunk-graph search horizon,
is the permitted retrieval-route family,
is the minimum witness class required,
is the search depth or shell-opening bound,
is the neighborhood policy.
The target-neighborhood constructor is therefore:
\mathcal{N}_{\mathrm{tgt}}:
q_{\mathrm{ret}}
\to
\{N_i^{\mathrm{tgt}}\}.
The neighborhood constructor is also ambiguity-aware. If the query packet carries ambiguity over target object family, then the constructor may spawn several target neighborhoods corresponding to different target hypotheses rather than prematurely merging them.
A minimal retrieval packet schema is:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, List, Any
@dataclass
class RetrievalAnchor:
kind: str
value: Any
confidence: float
metadata: Dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
@dataclass
class TargetNeighborhood:
seed_addresses: List[str]
chunk_horizon: Dict[str, Any]
route_families: List[str]
witness_requirements: Dict[str, Any]
shell_depth: int
policy: Dict[str, Any]
@dataclass
class RetrievalPacket:
retrieval_id: str
problem_ref: str
anchors: List[RetrievalAnchor]
target_neighborhoods: List[TargetNeighborhood]
lens_profile: Dict[str, float]
contract: Dict[str, Any]
corridor: str
budget: Dict[str, Any]
ambiguity: Dict[str, Any]
witness: Dict[str, Any]
The deeper purpose of the retrieval packet is to turn the problem into a memory-opening program. It is the direct analogue of the zero-point packet from Chapter 4, but specialized for support acquisition rather than full solve orchestration.
Definition 10.1 — Retrieval-Packet Admissibility
A retrieval packet is admissible if it contains at least one lawful anchor, at least one target neighborhood specification, an explicit retrieval contract, and a retrieval budget compatible with the corridor and current solve phase.
Proposition 10.1 — Retrieval without Target Neighborhoods Degenerates into Flat Search
If a normalized problem is not refined into one or more explicit target neighborhoods, then retrieval cannot exploit atlas topology and reverts toward globally ranked similarity search.
Proof. Without target neighborhoods, search has no explicit bounded regions, front horizons, or route families to privilege. Candidate generation therefore defaults to globally ranked matching, which ignores the atlas’s local geometry.
The retrieval packet is therefore the first true support-acquisition object of the manuscript brain.
Ch10.A2 — Address-First Lookup and Schema-Driven Search
The strongest retrieval signal in a routeable manuscript is not semantic proximity but lawful addressability. Whenever the problem packet contains exact or partially resolvable address anchors, retrieval must privilege them before broader semantic passes. This is the principle of address-first lookup. However, exact address is not always available, so the atlas also requires schema-driven search, where the expected object type and relational form guide retrieval even when explicit names do not.
Let the address anchor family be:
A_q^{\mathrm{addr}} \subseteq A_q.
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{addr}}:
A_q^{\mathrm{addr}} \times S_t
\to
(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{addr}},\Gamma^{\mathrm{addr}},\psi^{\mathrm{addr}}),
is the set of exact or approximate address-resolved candidates,
is the ambiguity or confidence structure of the lookup,
is the lookup witness.
Address-first lookup begins with exact resolution of canonical addresses:
a \mapsto o
chapter/lens/function restriction,
kind-bucket restriction,
local name lifting,
seed-anchor expansion,
address-alias maps,
mirror-lift maps from local scopes.
The result is not always singular. Partial address anchors may yield an ambiguity family:
\Gamma^{\mathrm{addr}}
=
(\mathcal{H}^{\mathrm{addr}},\pi^{\mathrm{addr}},\Delta^{\mathrm{addr}},\tau^{\mathrm{addr}}).
Address-first lookup is necessary because exact or structured address information tends to dominate purely lexical evidence in replay fidelity. When the atlas knows where to look, it should not pretend it only knows what words look similar.
Yet some queries lack direct address anchors. The manuscript then uses schema-driven search. A schema-driven search does not ask “which text sounds like the query?” It asks “what object kind, relation pattern, witness class, and contract shape would a lawful answer require?” This produces a search template:
\mathcal{S}_q
=
\big(
K_q,\,
R_q,\,
W_q,\,
C_q,\,
\Pi_q
\big),
is the expected object-kind family,
is the expected relation or route family,
is the expected witness class,
is the candidate context region,
is the schema policy.
For example, a proof request may define:
K_q=\{\mathrm{TheoremNode},\mathrm{WitnessNode},\mathrm{ReplayKernel}\},
\quad
R_q=\{\mathrm{DERIVE},\mathrm{WITNESS},\mathrm{REPLAY}\}.
K_q=\{\mathrm{FailureNode},\mathrm{PatchNode},\mathrm{ReplayNode}\},
\quad
R_q=\{\mathrm{CONFLICT},\mathrm{REPAIR},\mathrm{PATCH},\mathrm{REPLAY}\}.
Schema-driven search then traverses chunk graphs, route graphs, and kind-indexes to find structures matching the expected object-role signature, even if the lexical surface is sparse.
A basic schema-guided lookup skeleton is:
def schema_driven_search(schema_template, object_index, relation_index):
candidates = []
for obj_id, obj in object_index.items():
if obj["kind"] in schema_template["expected_kinds"]:
rels = relation_index.get(obj_id, [])
if any(r["type"] in schema_template["expected_relations"] for r in rels):
candidates.append(obj_id)
return candidates
The important formal point is that address-first and schema-driven search are complementary, not competing. Address-first search exploits known coordinates. Schema search exploits expected structural role. Combined, they produce a far more lawful candidate set than flat relevance ranking.
Definition 10.2 — Address-Dominant Retrieval
A retrieval pass is address-dominant if exact or partial canonical address evidence takes precedence over lexical or resonance evidence whenever it exists and is lawful.
Theorem 10.1 — Address-First plus Schema Search Strictly Dominates Lexical-Only Search in Replay-Oriented Atlases
In an atlas whose objects, witnesses, and routes are canonically typed and addressed, the combination of address-first lookup and schema-driven search yields a candidate set with higher replay fidelity than lexical-only search for any task requiring structural correctness.
Proof. Lexical-only search uses surface similarity and ignores explicit address and role structure. Address-first search recovers exact locations when available, and schema search recovers objects by structural role when direct naming is absent. These signals are more tightly correlated with replayable support for structurally constrained tasks.
This establishes the retrieval engine’s first law: search the crystal as a crystal before you search it as text.
Ch10.A3 — Lexical-Semantic Anchoring inside Crystal Indices
Even a routeable atlas must support lexical and semantic entry. Many queries will not contain explicit addresses, and even when addresses are present, lexical or symbolic anchors may reveal adjacent support objects. The manuscript therefore defines lexical-semantic anchoring inside explicit crystal indices.
Let the index family of the atlas be
\mathfrak{I}
=
\{
\mathfrak{I}_{\mathrm{addr}},
\mathfrak{I}_{\mathrm{kind}},
\mathfrak{I}_{\mathrm{alias}},
\mathfrak{I}_{\mathrm{sym}},
\mathfrak{I}_{\mathrm{route}},
\mathfrak{I}_{\mathrm{wit}},
\mathfrak{I}_{\mathrm{phase}},
\mathfrak{I}_{\mathrm{mem}}
\}.
indexes canonical and lifted addresses,
indexes type-kinds and bucket classes,
indexes aliases and legacy names,
indexes symbol packs and thematic signatures,
indexes route motifs and macro paths,
indexes witness classes and proof residues,
indexes dynamic phases and modes,
indexes memory contract and shell classes.
A lexical-semantic anchor is a query-side object
\alpha
=
\big(
\operatorname{lex}(\alpha),\,
\operatorname{sym}(\alpha),\,
\operatorname{kind}(\alpha),\,
\operatorname{phase}(\alpha),\,
\operatorname{ctx}(\alpha)
\big).
The lexical-semantic anchoring operator is:
\mathcal{A}_{\mathrm{lexsem}}:
\alpha \times \mathfrak{I}
\to
(\mathcal{C}_\alpha,P_\alpha,\psi_\alpha),
The score of candidate object under anchor may be written:
S_{\mathrm{lexsem}}(o\mid \alpha)
=
\lambda_1\,\operatorname{LexMatch}(o,\alpha)
+
\lambda_2\,\operatorname{AliasMatch}(o,\alpha)
+
\lambda_3\,\operatorname{SymMatch}(o,\alpha)
+
\lambda_4\,\operatorname{KindCompat}(o,\alpha)
+
\lambda_5\,\operatorname{PhaseCompat}(o,\alpha)
+
\lambda_6\,\operatorname{AddrNear}(o,\alpha)
The crystal index ensures that lexical-semantic anchors do not float free from topology. A symbolic term that appears in many places is not treated as equally relevant everywhere. Its route, witness, and neighborhood context shape the final candidate ranking.
A minimal anchor lookup skeleton is:
def lexical_semantic_anchor_search(anchor, crystal_indices):
scores = {}
for idx_name, idx in crystal_indices.items():
for candidate, score in idx.lookup(anchor):
scores[candidate] = scores.get(candidate, 0.0) + score
return sorted(scores.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
This code is schematic. In the manuscript brain the indices are typed and their outputs later fused through Cloud relevance distributions, not merely added.
Definition 10.3 — Crystal-Anchored Lexical Retrieval
A lexical-semantic retrieval pass is crystal-anchored if all lexical or symbolic candidates are resolved through typed atlas indices rather than through unconstrained text-space similarity alone.
Proposition 10.2 — Crystal-Anchored Semantics Preserve Routeability Better than Global Embedding Similarity
Embedding similarity can find semantically related text, but without crystal anchoring it may return objects lacking the route, witness, or contract relevance required for lawful later synthesis.
Proof. Semantic similarity is weaker than typed atlas compatibility. Crystal anchoring constrains semantic matches by address, type, route, witness, and memory-shell context. Therefore it preserves routeability more effectively.
The lexical-semantic layer thus becomes lawful support acquisition rather than generic search.
Ch10.A4 — Retrieval Contracts and Admissibility Filters
The candidate family generated so far may still be too broad, too shallow, too risky, or too witness-poor for the downstream solve contract. Retrieval therefore requires explicit retrieval contracts and admissibility filters that shape what counts as sufficient support.
A retrieval contract is not identical to the final output contract. The output contract says what artifact must eventually be produced. The retrieval contract says what support object family must be gathered to make such production lawful. Formally:
\chi_q^{\mathrm{ret}}
=
\big(
\operatorname{support\_kinds},\,
\operatorname{witness\_min},\,
\operatorname{memory\_fidelity},\,
\operatorname{ambiguity\_policy},\,
\operatorname{shell\_depth},\,
\operatorname{route\_yield}
\big).
theorem nodes,
witness nodes,
replay kernels,
high memory fidelity,
ambiguity retention until proof support is discriminated,
shell depth sufficient for route reconstruction.
An exploratory synthesis may instead require:
chapter summaries,
motif routes,
symbolic memory seeds,
lower witness threshold,
shallow shells,
plurality-permitting ambiguity policy.
The admissibility filter family is:
\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{adm}}
=
\{
\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{legal}},
\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{contract}},
\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{budget}},
\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{confidence}},
\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{witness}},
\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{defect}}
\}.
\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{adm}}
=
\bigcap_{F\in\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{adm}}} F(\mathcal{C}).
The legality filter removes candidates that are unreachable, blocked by corridor, or illegal to use under the current solve phase. The contract filter removes candidates of the wrong type or insufficient support class. The budget filter removes candidates whose shell or replay cost is too high for current search. The confidence filter removes very brittle or under-calibrated memories unless exploratory policy permits them. The witness filter ensures minimal evidence support. The defect filter suppresses candidates whose stored defect exceeds the active tolerance.
This filtering is crucial because the manuscript brain must avoid a common failure of naive retrieval systems: returning many semantically plausible but procedurally useless items. The retrieval contract ensures the candidate set is shaped for action, not just similarity.
Definition 10.4 — Retrieval-Admissible Candidate
A candidate object is retrieval-admissible under packet if it passes legality, contract, budget, confidence, witness, and defect filters relative to and .
Theorem 10.2 — Retrieval Contracts Are Necessary for Downstream Lawfulness
If retrieval returns semantically relevant but contract-incompatible support, then later synthesis may fail despite apparent relevance. Therefore retrieval contracts are necessary for lawful solve preparation.
Proof. Downstream synthesis depends on witness class, route support, shell depth, and confidence, not merely semantic overlap. Therefore candidate relevance without contract compatibility is insufficient.
This completes the Square foundation of retrieval: packets, neighborhoods, address/schema lookup, lexical-semantic anchoring, and admissibility filtering.
Ch10.B1 — Resonance Querying and Transform-Based Search
The Flower retrieval layer begins where exact address and lexical anchoring become insufficient. Many powerful recalls are triggered not by names or addresses but by dynamic resemblance: the same transformation pattern, the same oscillation between symbolic and formal surfaces, the same repair rhythm, the same proof tension. Retrieval must therefore support resonance querying.
Let the active problem carry a Flower signature
\Phi_q
=
\big(
\theta_q,\,
m_q,\,
\alpha_q,\,
\Sigma_q^{\mathrm{sym}},\,
\mathfrak{r}_q
\big),
is the phase state,
is the mode family,
is intensity,
is the symbol pack,
is the route-motif or transform signature.
Each memory, route motif, or atlas region carries its own Flower signature
\Phi_x.
\mathcal{Q}_{\mathrm{res}}:
\Phi_q \times \Phi_x
\to
\mathbb{R},
S_{\mathrm{res}}(x\mid q)
=
\mu_1\,\cos(\theta_q-\theta_x)
+
\mu_2\,\operatorname{ModeCompat}(m_q,m_x)
+
\mu_3\,\operatorname{SymbolRes}(\Sigma_q^{\mathrm{sym}},\Sigma_x^{\mathrm{sym}})
+
\mu_4\,\operatorname{TransformCompat}(\mathfrak{r}_q,\mathfrak{r}_x)
+
\mu_5\,\operatorname{PhaseTrace}(x,q).
The key retrieval value of resonance querying is that it exposes homologous solution dynamics. A problem asking to “turn a poetic or symbolic framework into rigorous formal structure” may resonate strongly with prior symbolic-to-formal bridge sequences, even if those memories share almost no surface vocabulary with the current prompt. A repair query may resonate with memories of contradiction-to-replay loops rather than lexical “repair” labels.
Resonance querying is also transform-based search. Instead of asking what object resembles the words of the query, it asks what object underwent a similar transformation. Formally, one may define a transform signature:
\tau_q^{\mathrm{tr}}
=
\big(
\operatorname{src\_regime}(q),\,
\operatorname{dst\_regime}(q),\,
\operatorname{required\_bridges}(q),\,
\operatorname{phase\_change}(q)
\big),
Definition 10.5 — Resonance-Admissible Candidate
A candidate is resonance-admissible if its dynamic and transform signature exceeds the corridor’s minimum resonance threshold and if its witness and defect profile allow use under the active retrieval contract.
Proposition 10.3 — Transform-Based Search Finds Solutions Hidden from Lexical Search
If two problems require homologous transformations but use very different surface vocabularies, lexical search may miss their relationship while transform-based resonance search may recover it.
Proof. Lexical search depends on surface overlap. Transform-based search depends on source-to-target regime similarity and route motif compatibility. Therefore it can recover homologous transformations even when lexical overlap is low.
The Flower retrieval engine thus opens the manuscript to lawful analogy and dynamic homology.
Ch10.B2 — Spectral Matches across Distant but Homologous Regions
Resonance querying compares dynamic signatures at a local or symbolic level. The next step is deeper: comparing entire route or narrative shapes through spectral signatures. This allows retrieval of distant regions whose global motion patterns are homologous even when their local components differ.
Let a candidate memory, route family, or atlas region have a stored Flower spectral signature
S_{\Flower}(x)=\{c_k(x)\}_{k=0}^{N-1}.
S_{\Flower}(q)=\{c_k(q)\}_{k=0}^{N-1}.
S_{\mathrm{spec}}(x\mid q)
=
\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}
w_k\,
\operatorname{sim}\big(c_k(q),c_k(x)\big),
High spectral match indicates that the problem and the candidate unfold in similar rhythms or route alternations. Examples include:
repeated global-local-global theorem development,
diagnose–repair–replay cycles,
symbolic-to-formal collapse patterns,
proof-by-case oscillation,
expansion–recompression motifs.
Spectral matching is particularly useful when retrieving from distant regions of the atlas. A local theorem region and a high-level design chapter may share little lexical or even object-kind overlap, but still express the same oscillatory structure of construction. Such homologous regions are invaluable for cross-domain route generalization.
Spectral retrieval also supports negative evidence. A candidate may match strongly in lexical content but have a very different spectral profile, indicating that it solves a superficially similar task with a different cognitive rhythm and may not be the right support memory.
Definition 10.6 — Spectral-Homologous Candidate
A candidate is spectral-homologous to the current problem if its spectral signature lies within the corridor’s allowed alignment distance from the problem signature.
Proposition 10.4 — Spectral Retrieval Generalizes Motif Retrieval
Where motif retrieval matches local transform patterns, spectral retrieval matches global route shape; therefore spectral retrieval is strictly broader when large-scale flow matters.
Proof. A local motif may repeat many times inside one global spectral pattern. The spectral signature captures the full arrangement and frequency of such motifs across the route or narrative. Hence it contains strictly more global information.
The retrieval engine can therefore recognize similarity in motion, not just content.
Ch10.B3 — Chart-Hopping during Partial Retrieval
Retrieval often begins in one lens and becomes more efficient in another. An exact address lookup may identify a Square region, but the best next move may be a Flower resonance pass, a Cloud ambiguity pass, or a Fractal shell opening. This is chart-hopping.
A retrieval state at step is
\mathcal{S}_t^{\mathrm{ret}}
=
\big(
\mathcal{C}_t,\,
P_t,\,
\Lambda_t,\,
\mathcal{F}_t,\,
B_t,\,
\omega_t
\big),
\mathfrak{m}_{\ell\to \ell'}:
\mathcal{S}_t^{(\ell)}
\to
\mathcal{S}_{t+1}^{(\ell')}
Chart-hopping is lawful only if:
the current candidate state admits a valid transport trace into the target lens,
the target lens offers new or cheaper retrieval affordances,
the hop respects budget and corridor,
the witness bundle records the change.
Examples:
Square address lookup yields a shallow theorem shell; hop to Flower to find homologous transport routes;
Cloud ambiguity over target regions suggests hop to Fractal to open coarse shells and compare higher-level structures;
Flower resonance identifies a dynamic route motif; hop to Square to fetch exact witness-bearing cells.
A chart-hop is therefore not a loose change of perspective. It is an executable retrieval action. Let the value of hopping from to be:
V_{\mathrm{hop}}(\ell\to\ell')
=
\operatorname{ExpectedGain}_{\ell'}-\operatorname{HopCost}_{\ell\to\ell'}.
Definition 10.7 — Lawful Retrieval Chart-Hop
A chart-hop is lawful if the transported candidate family preserves identity trace and admissible support semantics across the lens change.
Theorem 10.3 — Multi-Lens Retrieval Strictly Dominates Single-Lens Retrieval on Heterogeneous Atlases
If relevant support objects are unevenly distributed across lens-specific encodings, then a retrieval engine that can chart-hop lawfully can find support sets unavailable or too costly under any single fixed lens.
Proof. A fixed-lens engine is constrained to one representation. If some needed support is easier to detect in another lens, the fixed-lens engine either misses it or pays more to infer it indirectly. A lawful chart-hopping engine can move representations and recover it directly.
This is one of the places where the manuscript’s multi-lens nature becomes operational rather than merely descriptive.
Ch10.B4 — Phase-Tuned Retrieval under Shifting Task Intents
A retrieval program is not static. As a solve episode unfolds, the task intent may shift from exploration to formalization, from ambiguity preservation to witness gathering, from diagnosis to repair. The Flower retrieval layer therefore defines phase-tuned retrieval.
Let the active solve phase at time be
\phi_t \in \Phi.
\Pi_{\mathrm{ret}}^{\phi_t}.
in exploratory phase, use broad lexical-semantic and resonance triggers with high novelty and shallow shells;
in formalization phase, increase Square and witness-heavy retrieval;
in repair phase, privilege contradiction, replay, and patch histories;
in collapse phase, prefer high-confidence, low-defect, contract-aligned support.
A phase-tuned retrieval schedule is:
\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{ret}}
=
\big(
\phi_0,\Pi^{\phi_0}_{\mathrm{ret}};
\phi_1,\Pi^{\phi_1}_{\mathrm{ret}};
\dots
\big).
posterior concentration,
budget depletion,
route success or contradiction,
completion of subproblem branches,
external contract changes.
The importance of phase tuning is that one and the same problem rarely benefits from the same retrieval policy throughout the solve. Early retrieval should be broad enough to discover candidate geometry; late retrieval should be narrow enough to support lawful synthesis.
Definition 10.8 — Phase-Coherent Retrieval Policy
A retrieval policy is phase coherent if its parameter changes are synchronized with the solve phase and do not prematurely tighten or loosen support requirements relative to the active output obligations.
Proposition 10.5 — Static Retrieval Policies Misallocate Support over Long Solves
A policy tuned for early exploration is too loose for late proof support; a policy tuned for late proof support is too narrow for early discovery. Therefore static retrieval is suboptimal for multi-phase solves.
Proof. The evidence and support needs of a solve change across phases. A single policy cannot optimize all phases simultaneously.
The Flower retrieval layer is thus complete: dynamic, resonant, spectral, multi-chart, and phase-aware.
Ch10.C1 — Relevance Distributions over Candidate Sets
The Cloud retrieval layer begins by turning candidate sets into explicit relevance distributions. Retrieval in a lawful manuscript must not return only ranked lists. It must represent how strongly each candidate is supported and how that support compares to others.
Given a candidate family
\mathcal{C}=\{c_1,\dots,c_n\},
P_t(c_i\mid q_{\mathrm{ret}})
P_t(c_i\mid q_{\mathrm{ret}})
\propto
\exp\Big(
\lambda_1 S_{\mathrm{addr}}(c_i)
+
\lambda_2 S_{\mathrm{schema}}(c_i)
+
\lambda_3 S_{\mathrm{lexsem}}(c_i)
+
\lambda_4 S_{\mathrm{res}}(c_i)
+
\lambda_5 S_{\mathrm{spec}}(c_i)
+
\lambda_6 S_{\mathrm{mem}}(c_i)
-
\Pi_t(c_i)
\Big),
The relevance distribution is the proper object for:
top-k selection,
ambiguity preservation,
budget allocation among fronts,
early stopping,
downstream route-bundle assembly.
It also allows the manuscript to detect whether retrieval has truly converged. A candidate list with a strong dominant head is different from a flat distribution over many plausible items.
A retrieval relevance object is:
\mathcal{P}_{\mathrm{ret}}
=
\big(
\mathcal{C},\,
P_t,\,
\Sigma_t,\,
\omega_t
\big),
Definition 10.9 — Relevance-Calibrated Candidate Set
A candidate set is relevance-calibrated if its relevance distribution reflects both positive evidence and known penalties or overlaps rather than raw summed scores alone.
Proposition 10.6 — Ranked Lists without Distributions Hide Retrieval Uncertainty
A ranked list says which candidate comes first but not how strongly it dominates or how flat the tail is. Therefore it is insufficient for lawful ambiguity handling and budget allocation.
Proof. Ranking is ordinal; retrieval control requires quantitative or at least structured belief information. Hence distributions are necessary.
This makes candidate retrieval a Cloud belief object rather than a sort order.
Ch10.C2 — Contradiction Detection during Retrieval
Retrieval can surface contradictions before synthesis even begins. Two memories may both be relevant yet mutually incompatible. Two route motifs may imply different target contracts. Two replay kernels may belong to different lineage branches. The retrieval engine must detect this and preserve it explicitly.
Let the candidate family be . A contradiction detector is a function:
\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{contr}}:
\mathcal{C}\times \mathcal{C}
\to
\mathcal{K}_{\mathrm{ret}},
k_{ij}
=
\big(
c_i,\,
c_j,\,
\operatorname{kind}(k_{ij}),\,
\operatorname{scope}(k_{ij}),\,
\operatorname{severity}(k_{ij}),\,
\operatorname{repair}(k_{ij})
\big).
Retrieval contradictions include:
content contradiction,
witness contradiction,
contract mismatch,
lineage divergence,
shell inconsistency,
phase incompatibility,
alias collision or overmerge,
bridge or transport incompatibility.
The contradiction detector uses:
direct claim comparison,
witness class incompatibility,
address and lineage analysis,
phase and lens mismatch,
known contradiction memories,
replay failure history.
The resulting contradiction field is
\mathcal{K}_t
=
\{k_{ij}\}_{i,j}.
Most importantly, contradiction detection during retrieval allows the atlas to avoid a dangerous failure mode: building a later answer from incompatible supports while imagining the contradiction only appears at synthesis time. Early detection permits:
branch separation,
candidate parking,
repair-route retrieval,
stronger witness demands,
plural output if needed.
Definition 10.10 — Retrieval-Stage Contradiction Completeness
A retrieval contradiction field is complete relative to a candidate family if every materially incompatible candidate pair relevant to the active contract is represented by at least one contradiction or tension marker.
Theorem 10.4 — Early Contradiction Detection Reduces Downstream Synthesis Waste
If contradictions are detected at retrieval time, then synthesis need not build and later dismantle invalid support bundles, reducing wasted computation and false confidence.
Proof. Early contradiction markers allow candidate separation or pruning before synthesis. Without them, synthesis may attempt invalid merges and only later discover the problem. Hence early detection saves effort and reduces overconfidence.
The Cloud retrieval engine therefore reasons about relevance and incompatibility simultaneously.
Ch10.C3 — Budget Allocation across Competing Search Fronts
Retrieval usually proceeds along several fronts at once:
address-first lookup,
schema search,
lexical-semantic anchoring,
resonance querying,
spectral matching,
shell opening,
chunk-graph traversal,
route-motif recall.
The budget is finite. The Cloud retrieval layer therefore allocates resources across these fronts explicitly.
Let the front set be
\mathcal{F}
=
\{F_1,\dots,F_m\}.
expected gain ,
expected cost ,
confidence sensitivity ,
contract relevance ,
contradiction exposure .
A budget allocator seeks allocation vector
\mathbf{b}=(b_1,\dots,b_m),
\qquad
\sum_i b_i \le B_{\mathrm{tot}},
\max_{\mathbf{b}}
\sum_i
\Big(
\alpha G_i
+
\beta R_i
+
\gamma K_i
-
\delta C_i
-
\eta D_i
\Big)b_i
Different fronts may also require minimum startup costs or may only become valuable after other fronts have yielded anchors. Thus the allocator may operate in staged form rather than as one flat optimization.
A practical budget object is:
B_t^{\mathrm{ret}}
=
\big(
B_t^{\mathrm{time}},
B_t^{\mathrm{memory}},
B_t^{\mathrm{witness}},
B_t^{\mathrm{risk}},
B_t^{\mathrm{shell}}
\big).
A simple allocator sketch is:
def allocate_search_budget(fronts, total_budget):
scored = []
for f in fronts:
utility = (
f["expected_gain"]
+ f["contract_relevance"]
+ f["confidence_sensitivity"]
- f["cost"]
- f["contradiction_exposure"]
)
scored.append((utility, f))
scored.sort(reverse=True, key=lambda x: x[0])
used = 0.0
allocation = {}
for utility, f in scored:
cost = f["startup_cost"]
if used + cost <= total_budget:
allocation[f["id"]] = cost
used += cost
else:
allocation[f["id"]] = 0.0
return allocation
The key manuscript requirement is not this particular heuristic, but the explicit objectification of search-front budgeting.
Definition 10.11 — Budget-Honest Retrieval
A retrieval process is budget honest if front allocation, parking, and stopping are determined by explicit budget objects and the retrieval witness records any candidate omissions caused by budget rather than irrelevance.
Proposition 10.7 — Competing Fronts Require Explicit Budget Arbitration
If several retrieval fronts compete for finite resources and no explicit budget arbitration exists, then retrieval behavior becomes path-dependent and unreplayable.
Proof. In the absence of explicit allocation, whichever front is processed first or most aggressively consumes resources arbitrarily, making retrieval order and outcome dependent on incidental implementation details rather than declared policy.
This gives retrieval its lawful resource discipline.
Ch10.C4 — Confidence-Guided Expansion and Early Stopping
The final Cloud retrieval task is deciding when enough support has been gathered. If retrieval stops too early, downstream synthesis is under-supported. If it stops too late, the system wastes budget and may overfit to noisy tail candidates. The manuscript therefore uses confidence-guided expansion and early stopping.
Let the active retrieval state at step be:
\mathcal{S}_t^{\mathrm{ret}}
=
(\mathcal{C}_t,P_t,\mathcal{K}_t,B_t,\omega_t).
cumulative support confidence ,
unresolved contradiction burden ,
marginal gain estimate ,
contract adequacy predicate .
The early-stopping policy is:
\operatorname{Stop}_t = 1
\iff
A_t=1
\;\wedge\;
C_t^{\mathrm{sup}}\ge \theta_{\mathrm{sup}}
\;\wedge\;
M_t\le \theta_{\mathrm{gain}}
The adequacy predicate checks whether the currently retrieved support already satisfies:
minimal witness threshold,
required object kinds,
route-support sufficiency,
ambiguity or plurality policy,
shell depth required by the contract.
The marginal gain estimate asks whether one more retrieval expansion is likely to change the candidate distribution materially:
M_t
=
\operatorname{ExpectedGain}(\mathcal{S}_{t+1}^{\mathrm{ret}}\mid \mathcal{S}_t^{\mathrm{ret}}).
The confidence-guided policy must also support stopping with ambiguity. If the contract allows plural support, the retrieval process may stop with several candidate clusters still active, as long as the ambiguity is explicit and sufficiently bounded.
A stopping witness records:
why support was judged sufficient,
what uncertainty remained,
what fronts were left unopened,
what shell depth was reached,
whether stopping was evidence-based or budget-based.
Definition 10.12 — Retrieval Sufficiency
A retrieval state is sufficient under contract if its candidate support, witness class, and confidence profile satisfy the minimum requirements for downstream route-bundle assembly and synthesis.
Theorem 10.5 — Confidence-Guided Early Stopping Dominates Fixed-Depth Retrieval
For heterogeneous problems with variable support requirements, a stopping policy conditioned on contract adequacy and marginal gain yields lower expected retrieval cost than a fixed-depth policy, without reducing adequacy.
Proof. Fixed-depth retrieval oversearches easy cases and undersearches difficult ones. A confidence-guided policy adjusts depth to the actual support need, improving expected efficiency while preserving adequacy.
The Cloud retrieval layer is thus complete: relevance distributions, contradiction fields, budget allocation, and lawful stopping.
Ch10.D1 — Coarse-to-Fine Search Cascades
