The Fractal retrieval layer begins with the observation that search should almost never begin at maximum detail. It should begin with compressed, high-level structures and then descend only where needed. This is the coarse-to-fine search cascade.
Let the atlas provide multiscale memory and region objects:
X^{(0)},X^{(1)},\dots,X^{(k)},
\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{cf}}:
q_{\mathrm{ret}}
\to
\big(
\mathcal{C}^{(0)},\mathcal{C}^{(1)},\dots,\mathcal{C}^{(j)}
\big),
At coarse scale the system searches:
chapter shells,
memory summaries,
route archetypes,
macro bridges,
chunk graph hubs.
Only once a candidate region proves promising does the cascade open:
local cells,
witness shells,
route details,
patch deltas,
fine-scale ambiguity branches.
This yields major efficiency and clarity benefits. It also aligns with the atlas’s holographic design: coarse objects are meant to be informative enough to guide fine search.
The cascade is not merely a heuristic. It is a typed retrieval program:
\Pi_{\mathrm{cf}}
=
\big(
L_0 \to L_1 \to \cdots \to L_j,\,
\mathcal{S}_0,\mathcal{S}_1,\dots,\mathcal{S}_{j-1},\,
\omega_{\mathrm{cf}}
\big),
Definition 10.13 — Coarse-to-Fine Retrieval Program
A retrieval program is coarse-to-fine if every fine-scale search branch is justified by an admissible coarse-scale support state and all scale transitions preserve candidate lineage.
Proposition 10.8 — Coarse-to-Fine Retrieval Reduces Expected Cost on Multiscale Atlases
If many fine regions share coarse summaries or shells, then coarse-to-fine retrieval avoids opening irrelevant fine detail and therefore reduces expected search cost.
Proof. The coarse stage filters broad regions. Fine search is only performed under promising coarse branches. Hence fewer total fine openings occur.
The Fractal retrieval engine therefore begins by respecting scale.
Ch10.D2 — Branch Pruning and Recursive Neighborhood Growth
Once a coarse-to-fine cascade begins, retrieval branches multiply. Some are promising, some weak, some contradictory, some parked. The Fractal layer formalizes recursive neighborhood growth with branch pruning.
Let the retrieval tree be
\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{ret}}
=
(V_{\mathrm{ret}},E_{\mathrm{ret}},r_{\mathrm{ret}},\ell_{\mathrm{ret}}).
current candidate family,
shell depth,
local relevance posterior,
contradiction burden,
expected gain from deeper opening.
Neighborhood growth expands a node by applying one or more operators:
shell opening,
chunk-neighbor expansion,
route-hook following,
chart-hop,
witness uplift,
bridge exploration.
Branch pruning removes or parks nodes whose expected value falls below threshold. As in Chapter 9, pruning is not silent erasure. It produces:
V_{\mathrm{keep}},
\qquad
V_{\mathrm{park}},
\qquad
\psi_{\mathrm{prune}}.
The branch score of node is:
S_{\mathrm{branch}}(v)
=
\operatorname{Relevance}(v)
+
\operatorname{ContractValue}(v)
+
\operatorname{Confidence}(v)
+
\operatorname{PotentialGain}(v)
-
\operatorname{Cost}(v)
-
\operatorname{Contradiction}(v).
Definition 10.14 — Retrieval-Tree Admissibility
A retrieval tree is admissible if every branch expansion is justified by lawful shell, route, or chart operations and every pruning decision is budget- and witness-honest.
Proposition 10.9 — Recursive Neighborhood Growth without Pruning Is Intractable on Dense Atlases
If every admissible branch is expanded indefinitely, then retrieval cost grows combinatorially in the branching factor and shell depth.
Proof. Each branch expansion spawns multiple descendants. Without pruning or stopping, the number of nodes grows exponentially or worse in depth. Therefore dense atlases require pruning.
The Fractal retrieval engine thus combines growth and restraint.
Ch10.D3 — Route-Bundle Assembly from Partial Hits
A critical difference between manuscript retrieval and naive search lies here: partial hits are not the final output of retrieval. They must be assembled into one or more retrieval route bundles that explain how the retrieved objects support one another and how downstream synthesis may move among them lawfully.
Let
\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{hit}}
=
\{h_1,\dots,h_n\}
\mathcal{A}_{\mathrm{ret\_route}}:
\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{hit}} \times \mathfrak{G} \times \mathfrak{C}
\to
\{\rho_1^{\mathrm{ret}},\dots,\rho_m^{\mathrm{ret}}\}.
A retrieval route bundle may need to:
connect a theorem summary to its witness kernel,
connect a patch history to the contradiction that motivated it,
connect a route motif to the memory shells instantiating it,
connect symbolic and formal support regions through bridge records.
This is a constrained route-cover problem. Given terminal hit set , find route family minimizing total action while covering the contract-required support relations:
\mathcal{R}^{\mathrm{ret}}
=
\arg\min_{\mathcal{R}}
\mathcal{A}_t(\mathcal{R}\mid \xi_q)
\operatorname{Cover}_{\chi_q^{\mathrm{ret}}}(\mathcal{R},\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{hit}})=1.
The resulting retrieval route bundle is what later synthesis will consume. It preserves not only what was found, but how it fits together. This is the exact reason RAG in the manuscript brain is route-augmented rather than chunk-aggregated.
A simple assembly skeleton is:
def assemble_retrieval_routes(hits, route_graph, contract):
candidate_routes = generate_connecting_routes(hits, route_graph)
scored = [(score_route_bundle(r, contract), r) for r in candidate_routes]
scored.sort(reverse=True, key=lambda x: x[0])
return [r for _, r in scored[:contract.get("max_route_bundles", 3)]]
Again, the formal point is not the simple heuristic but the existence of a contract-aware route-cover optimization over the hit set.
Definition 10.15 — Retrieval Route-Bundle Sufficiency
A retrieval route bundle is sufficient if it covers the support relations required by the retrieval contract and provides enough lawful traversal information for downstream synthesis or proof search to proceed without reopening the whole retrieval search.
Theorem 10.6 — Partial Hits Alone Are Not Sufficient Retrieval Outputs for Routeable Synthesis
A set of relevant hits without an accompanying support route bundle does not, in general, preserve how those hits jointly support the target artifact.
Proof. The same hit set may support many different interpretations depending on how its members are connected by routes, witnesses, or bridges. Therefore the hit set alone is underdetermined for routeable synthesis.
This is the heart of manuscript retrieval: partial hits become structured support geometry.
Ch10.D4 — Reusable Retrieval Macros for Problem Families
The final Fractal task is to turn repeated retrieval traces into reusable skills. A retrieval program that works repeatedly for a class of problems should become a retrieval macro.
A retrieval macro is a compiled retrieval program:
\mathfrak{M}_{\mathrm{ret}}
=
\Big(
u_{\mathfrak{M}},\,
\operatorname{trigger}(\mathfrak{M}),\,
\operatorname{fronts}(\mathfrak{M}),\,
\operatorname{cascade}(\mathfrak{M}),\,
\operatorname{filters}(\mathfrak{M}),\,
\operatorname{assembly}(\mathfrak{M}),\,
\operatorname{stop}(\mathfrak{M}),\,
\psi_{\mathfrak{M}}
\Big).
specifies the problem family that activates the macro,
lists the retrieval fronts to start,
gives the coarse-to-fine or chart-hop schedule,
gives the contract-sensitive admissibility filters,
defines how partial hits become retrieval route bundles,
defines sufficiency and stopping,
witnesses the macro’s replayed success history.
Typical macro families include:
theorem proof support retrieval,
symbolic-to-formal crosswalk retrieval,
contradiction–repair replay retrieval,
patch lineage lookup,
route motif analog search,
chapter-scale synthesis support retrieval.
Macros are learned from repeated successful retrieval traces by abstraction over shared structure:
\{\Pi_1^{\mathrm{ret}},\dots,\Pi_k^{\mathrm{ret}}\}
\rightsquigarrow
\mathfrak{M}_{\mathrm{ret}}.
its trigger family is stable,
its candidate support quality is consistently high,
its route-bundle assembly is replayable,
its defect and contradiction rate are low enough.
Once canonized, a retrieval macro becomes a first-class atlas object and may itself receive procedural weights, updates, and patch refinements.
A minimal macro schema is:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Dict, List, Any
@dataclass
class RetrievalMacro:
macro_id: str
trigger_signature: Dict[str, Any]
search_fronts: List[Dict[str, Any]]
cascade_policy: Dict[str, Any]
filter_policy: Dict[str, Any]
assembly_policy: Dict[str, Any]
stop_policy: Dict[str, Any]
witness: Dict[str, Any]
Definition 10.16 — Canonizable Retrieval Macro
A retrieval macro is canonizable if it abstracts a family of replay-successful retrieval traces into a reusable program without exceeding the abstraction defect budget of its corridor.
Theorem 10.7 — Retrieval Macros Compress Search Skill
If a family of problems repeatedly uses homologous retrieval programs, then encoding their shared structure as a retrieval macro reduces future search cost while preserving or improving retrieval adequacy.
Proof. The macro reuses the already validated front schedule, filters, and route-bundle assembly pattern, avoiding repeated rediscovery of the same retrieval logic. Therefore expected cost decreases while adequacy remains at least as high as the validated traces that induced the macro.
The retrieval engine therefore ends not merely by finding support, but by teaching itself how to find support better next time.
The chapter has now completed the formal retrieval engine of the manuscript brain. Under the Square lens, a normalized problem became a retrieval packet carrying anchors, target neighborhoods, contract-aware support requirements, address-first lookup, schema-driven search, crystal-indexed lexical-semantic anchoring, and admissibility filters. Under the Flower lens, retrieval became dynamic: resonance queries recovered transform-homologous regions, spectral matching found distant but structurally similar flows, lawful chart-hopping allowed search to move between lenses, and phase-tuned policies adapted retrieval to the changing solve rhythm. Under the Cloud lens, candidate support became a calibrated relevance distribution; contradictions were detected during retrieval rather than after the fact; search budgets were allocated across explicit fronts; and early stopping was governed by support confidence and marginal gain rather than arbitrary depth limits. Under the Fractal lens, retrieval became recursive: it searched coarsely before opening detail, grew and pruned search trees lawfully, assembled partial hits into support route bundles, and compressed repeated successful retrieval traces into reusable macros.
The deepest consequence is that the manuscript no longer “looks things up” in the ordinary sense. It constructs support geometry. A query does not merely pull back similar content. It opens the right memory shells, follows the right route motifs, checks the right witness surfaces, hops to the right chart when needed, and stops when it has enough support to continue thinking lawfully. This is the exact formal meaning of RAG in the present framework. Retrieval is not a preliminary convenience. It is the first active stage of cognition itself.
CHAPTER 10 — QUERY INGESTION, RAG ROUTING, AND RETRIEVAL PROGRAMS
Chapter 11 — Multi-Lens Synthesis and Solution Construction
A manuscript brain does not complete its cognitive act when retrieval ends. Retrieval gathers support. Synthesis transforms support into a lawful answer artifact. This transformation is not identical to freeform generation, stylistic paraphrase, or heuristic aggregation. It is a formally constrained construction in which retrieved objects, route bundles, witness residues, ambiguity capsules, lens projections, and confidence surfaces are assembled into a canonical answer object whose internal structure remains replayable, inspectable, expandable, and patchable. The present chapter defines that construction.
The fundamental problem is the following. By the end of Chapter 10 the atlas can ingest a problem, normalize it, open the right memory shells, traverse lawful retrieval routes, and return structured support families together with contradiction fields and retrieval witnesses. Yet such a support field is not itself an answer. It is an admissible basis for answer construction. The manuscript must therefore specify how support becomes claims, how claims are justified by evidence, how cross-lens projections are aligned, how incompatible interpretations interfere or separate, how uncertainty is propagated into final form without dishonest collapse, and how answers are packaged so that future replay, refinement, compression, and patch suggestion remain possible.
The central synthesis operator of the manuscript is
\mathcal{S}_t:
\big(
q_0,\,
\mathfrak{R}_t(q_0),\,
\chi_q,\,
\kappa_q,\,
\Lambda_q
\big)
\to
\big(
Y_t,\,
\Psi_t^Y,\,
\Delta_t^Y
\big),
is the zero-point problem state,
is the retrieval result bundle,
is the output contract,
is the corridor class,
is the active lens profile,
is the synthesized answer object,
is the answer witness bundle,
is the family of patch suggestions or synthesis residuals.
The answer object is not a string alone. It is a structured object
Y_t
=
\Big(
G_t^{\mathrm{cl}},\,
L_t^{\mathrm{ev}},\,
\Gamma_t^{\mathrm{amb}},\,
C_t^{\mathrm{ans}},\,
\mathcal{L}_t^{\mathrm{rend}},\,
\Pi_t^{\mathrm{prov}},\,
\mathcal{U}_t^{\mathrm{patch}},\,
\mathcal{R}_t^{\mathrm{replay}}
\Big),
is the claim graph,
is the evidence lattice,
is the ambiguity and counterhypothesis field,
is the answer confidence surface,
is the rendering ladder from summary to full detail,
is the provenance bundle,
is the set of patch suggestions or unresolved residuals,
is the replay constructor sufficient to reconstruct the synthesis episode.
The chapter is governed by six synthesis laws.
First, claim legality: every claim in the answer must be traceable to retrieved support and admissible route construction.
Second, evidence monotonicity under no contradiction: added lawful evidence may raise or preserve support for a claim but may not reduce it absent contradiction, risk, or contract conflict.
Third, cross-lens alignment before collapse: no final answer may collapse to canonical form unless the contributing lens projections have been aligned or their residual incompatibility has been explicitly preserved.
Fourth, uncertainty honesty: ambiguity, counterhypotheses, weak witnesses, and forced resolutions must remain explicit in the answer object whenever the corridor requires them.
Fifth, replay stability: the answer object must be structured so that its claim graph, evidence lattice, rendering ladder, and patch suggestions can be replayed or reopened without reconstructing the entire solve from raw text.
Sixth, fractal extensibility: an answer may be stored at many levels of compression, but each compressed form must preserve lawful expansion to stronger forms within its declared defect budget.
The chapter proceeds through the four-lens crystal. Under the Square lens synthesis becomes claim graphs, evidence lattices, answer schemas, dependency orders, and structured replayable answer objects. Under the Flower lens synthesis becomes cross-lens fusion, resonance consensus, interference analysis, and transform alignment before final collapse. Under the Cloud lens synthesis becomes explicit confidence propagation, plural or ambiguity-preserving answer construction, counterhypothesis retention, and calibrated confidence surfaces over claims. Under the Fractal lens synthesis becomes summary-to-full ladders, nested explanation trees, answer-archetype reuse, and patch suggestion generation from synthesis residuals.
This chapter therefore answers the central practical question: how does a lawful manuscript turn retrieved support into a final artifact without losing structure, honesty, or future usefulness?
Ch11.A1 — Claim Graphs and Evidence Lattices
The first act of synthesis is not writing sentences. It is constructing a graph of claims and a lattice of evidence. Every later rendering, proof trace, explanation, or patch suggestion depends on these two objects. Without them, the final answer would be an opaque surface artifact rather than a replayable synthesis.
Let the retrieved support family be
\mathcal{C}_t
=
\{c_1,\dots,c_n\}.
\mathcal{V}_{\mathrm{cl}}
=
\{v_1,\dots,v_m\}.
v_i
=
\Big(
u_i,\,
\operatorname{kind}(v_i),\,
\operatorname{content}(v_i),\,
\operatorname{scope}(v_i),\,
\operatorname{contract}(v_i),\,
\operatorname{status}(v_i)
\Big),
may be definition, theorem, derivation, comparison, diagnostic assertion, repair proposal, route statement, memory fact, ambiguity statement, or patch candidate;
identifies the atlas region or task component to which the claim belongs;
may be asserted, derived, conditional, rival, provisional, or residual.
The claim graph is then
G^{\mathrm{cl}}
=
\big(
\mathcal{V}_{\mathrm{cl}},\,
\mathcal{E}_{\mathrm{cl}},\,
\tau_{\mathrm{cl}},\,
\iota_{\mathrm{cl}}
\big),
are typed claim–claim edges,
assigns edge type,
is the graph invariant bundle.
The edge types include:
\{
\mathrm{supports},
\mathrm{depends\_on},
\mathrm{implies},
\mathrm{specializes},
\mathrm{contradicts},
\mathrm{refines},
\mathrm{generalizes},
\mathrm{conditions},
\mathrm{routes\_to},
\mathrm{patches}
\}.
Claims alone are not enough. They require evidence. The evidence lattice is the partially ordered structure
L^{\mathrm{ev}}
=
\big(
\mathcal{V}_{\mathrm{ev}},\,
\preceq,\,
\wedge,\,
\vee,\,
\mu_{\mathrm{ev}},\,
\iota_{\mathrm{ev}}
\big),
is the set of evidence objects,
is the evidence-order relation,
and are meet and join operations where defined,
stores quantitative support measures,
is the lattice invariant bundle.
The evidence objects include:
direct retrieved cells,
route support bundles,
replay traces,
witness residues,
proof fragments,
contradiction markers,
patch history artifacts,
ambiguity discrimination results.
The partial order captures strength, specificity, or dependency refinement. For example:
a fully replayed proof witness may dominate a shallow summary witness,
a direct theorem cell plus witness shell may dominate an alias-based lexical hit,
a route macro may be weaker than its full replay trace,
a contradiction marker may not dominate a positive witness but may constrain its use.
Each claim is associated with a support ideal or support bundle
\operatorname{Supp}(v)\subseteq \mathcal{V}_{\mathrm{ev}}.
\operatorname{Counter}(v)\subseteq \mathcal{V}_{\mathrm{ev}}.
A key synthesis invariant is support traceability:
\forall v\in \mathcal{V}_{\mathrm{cl}},
\quad
\operatorname{Supp}(v)\neq \varnothing
\;\;\text{or}\;\;
\operatorname{status}(v)\in\{\mathrm{provisional},\mathrm{residual},\mathrm{counterhypothesis}\}.
A second invariant is claim closure under dependency: if a claim depends on , then either appears in the graph or the dependency is explicitly externalized as a gap or assumption object.
One may define the support strength of a claim as a lattice aggregation:
\Sigma(v)
=
\bigvee_{e\in \operatorname{Supp}(v)} e
A minimal answer-support schema is:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, List, Any, Literal
ClaimKind = Literal[
"Definition", "Theorem", "Derivation", "Comparison",
"Diagnosis", "Repair", "RouteClaim", "PatchClaim",
"Conditional", "Counterhypothesis", "Residual"
]
@dataclass
class ClaimNode:
claim_id: str
kind: ClaimKind
content: Dict[str, Any]
scope: Dict[str, Any]
status: str
support_ids: List[str]
counter_ids: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
@dataclass
class EvidenceNode:
evidence_id: str
kind: str
payload: Dict[str, Any]
strength: float
metadata: Dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
@dataclass
class ClaimGraph:
claims: Dict[str, ClaimNode]
edges: List[Dict[str, Any]]
evidence: Dict[str, EvidenceNode]
The significance of the claim graph and evidence lattice is foundational. Every later synthesis operation—dependency ordering, cross-lens fusion, confidence propagation, rendering, patch suggestion—acts on these objects. They are the canonical support-bearing skeleton of the final answer.
Definition 11.1 — Support-Well-Formed Claim Graph
A claim graph is support-well-formed if every non-residual claim is connected to at least one evidence element or inherited support path and all support, contradiction, and dependency relations are typed and replayable.
Proposition 11.1 — Claim Lists Are Strictly Weaker than Claim Graphs
A flat list of claims with local annotations cannot, in general, preserve the dependency, contradiction, and refinement structure necessary for lawful multi-step synthesis and replay.
Proof. Lists encode order at best. Claim graphs encode typed relations among claims. Since lawful synthesis depends on those relations, lists are insufficient in the general case.
The claim graph and evidence lattice therefore provide the exact combinatorial substrate of answer construction.
Ch11.A2 — Canonical Answer Schemas and Output Contracts
A support graph is still not an answer. The answer must be shaped into an artifact that satisfies the active output contract. The manuscript therefore defines canonical answer schemas: formal shapes of admissible answer objects for different contract classes.
Let the output contract inherited from intake and retrieval be
\chi_q.
\mathcal{Y}_{\chi_q}
=
\big(
F_{\chi_q}^{\mathrm{req}},\,
F_{\chi_q}^{\mathrm{opt}},\,
O_{\chi_q},\,
R_{\chi_q},\,
D_{\chi_q},\,
\iota_{\chi_q}
\big),
is the set of required fields,
is the set of optional fields,
is the output order or assembly order,
is the rendering family,
is the defect or ambiguity tolerance budget,
is the schema invariant family.
Different answer classes induce different schemas. A proof-oriented answer schema may require:
theorem statement,
dependency assumptions,
proof body or proof route,
witness bundle,
replay reference,
confidence field,
residual ambiguity field if not fully resolved.
A patch-proposal schema may require:
target region,
contradiction or gap motivating the patch,
delta suggestion family,
expected invariant improvement,
rollback or caution policy,
witness status.
A comparative synthesis schema may require:
compared objects,
claim clusters by criterion,
equivalence and contradiction markers,
confidence surface over criteria,
explicit retained counterhypotheses.
A canonical answer schema thus constrains both content and structure. It specifies not only what must be said, but what formal support must accompany it.
The answer-shaping operator is
\operatorname{Shape}_{\chi_q}:
(G^{\mathrm{cl}},L^{\mathrm{ev}},\Gamma^{\mathrm{amb}},C^{\mathrm{ans}})
\to
Y_{\chi_q}^{\mathrm{raw}},
\operatorname{NF}_{\chi_q}(Y_{\chi_q}^{\mathrm{raw}})=Y_{\chi_q}.
A common base schema exists for all answer objects:
\mathcal{Y}_{\mathrm{base}}
=
\big(
\mathrm{id},\,
\mathrm{contract},\,
\mathrm{claim\_graph},\,
\mathrm{evidence\_lattice},\,
\mathrm{confidence},\,
\mathrm{ambiguity},\,
\mathrm{provenance},\,
\mathrm{replay}
\big).
A typed answer object may be represented as:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, List, Any
@dataclass
class AnswerObject:
answer_id: str
contract: Dict[str, Any]
claim_graph_ref: str
evidence_lattice_ref: str
confidence_surface: Dict[str, Any]
ambiguity_bundle: Dict[str, Any]
provenance: Dict[str, Any]
replay_bundle: Dict[str, Any]
render_layers: Dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
patch_suggestions: List[Dict[str, Any]] = field(default_factory=list)
The answer schema is what allows the manuscript to be code-rigorous. It does not merely “write an answer.” It instantiates a typed answer artifact according to contract.
Definition 11.2 — Schema-Satisfying Answer
An answer object satisfies schema if all required fields are present, all required invariants hold, and any omitted optional fields are explicitly absent rather than silently implied.
Theorem 11.1 — Canonical Answer Schemas Yield Contract-Replayable Artifacts
If answer objects are constructed through contract-specific canonical schemas, then downstream replay can determine exactly what support, rendering depth, and ambiguity policy were promised by the original synthesis.
Proof. The schema encodes all required structural and support obligations of the answer class. Therefore any later replay of the answer object can recover those obligations directly from the artifact itself.
The answer schema is thus the bridge from abstract support structure to an actual answer form.
Ch11.A3 — Dependency Ordering for Synthesis Assembly
Even with a claim graph and answer schema, synthesis cannot assemble claims arbitrarily. Some claims depend on definitions, others on derived lemmas, others on comparison baselines or witness disclosures. The manuscript therefore requires an explicit dependency ordering for answer assembly.
Let the synthesis dependency graph be
\mathfrak{D}^{\mathrm{syn}}
=
\big(
\mathcal{V}_{\mathrm{syn}},\,
\mathcal{E}_{\mathrm{syn}},\,
\tau_{\mathrm{syn}}
\big),
includes claims, evidence summaries, assumption capsules, ambiguity statements, and answer sections;
encodes typed dependencies such as prerequisite, witness-before-use, condition-on, or counterclaim-after-claim.
A claim may depend on in several distinct ways:
definitional dependency,
proof dependency,
witness prerequisite,
contract-interpretation dependency,
comparison baseline dependency,
contradiction disclosure dependency.
These types matter because they influence rendering order. A contradiction disclosure may need to appear immediately after a claim, whereas a deep proof dependency may remain hidden until expansion.
The synthesis assembly operator seeks a topological or partially topological order:
\operatorname{Ord}_{\mathrm{syn}}:
\mathfrak{D}^{\mathrm{syn}} \to \Pi(\mathcal{V}_{\mathrm{syn}}),
When the dependency graph is acyclic, a topological sort is sufficient. However, synthesis often encounters cycles. These may arise from mutually defining structures, recursive explanation patterns, or fixed-point argument families. Therefore the manuscript does not treat every cycle as failure. It computes strongly connected components:
\operatorname{SCC}(\mathfrak{D}^{\mathrm{syn}})
This gives a lawful assembly order:
\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{syn}}
=
(O_1,\dots,O_k),
Dependency ordering is crucial for replay. A replay engine must know not only what claims exist, but in what order they became safe to assert, condition, or expand. Without this ordering, the answer artifact might be complete in content but opaque in constructive logic.
A minimal ordering implementation is:
def synthesis_order(nodes, edges):
dag = collapse_admissible_cycles(nodes, edges)
return topological_sort(dag)
Of course, real synthesis ordering must preserve dependency types and rendering contracts, but the core principle is captured.
The assembly order also interacts with the output contract. A concise answer may present high-level claim nodes before their deep supporting subgraph, while a proof answer may require deeper dependency revelation earlier. Thus the ordering function is not purely graph-theoretic; it is schema-aware.
Definition 11.3 — Dependency-Admissible Synthesis Order
A synthesis order is dependency admissible if every claim, explanation, or answer section appears only after the minimal dependencies required by the active contract and corridor have been satisfied or explicitly deferred.
Proposition 11.2 — Claim Support Alone Does Not Determine Assembly Order
Even if every claim has adequate evidence, the answer may still be malformed if dependencies among claims, assumptions, counterhypotheses, or witnesses are rendered in the wrong order.
Proof. Evidence adequacy is local to a claim. Assembly order depends on interclaim structure. Thus support sufficiency does not determine order.
This makes answer assembly a formal constructive act, not merely a presentation decision.
Ch11.A4 — Structured Answer Objects for Downstream Replay
The final Square task is to package the synthesized answer into a structured answer object suitable for replay, compression, comparison, and patching. This answer object is the canonical output of synthesis before user-facing rendering.
A structured answer object is:
Y
=
\Big(
u_Y,\,
a_Y,\,
\chi_Y,\,
G_Y^{\mathrm{cl}},\,
L_Y^{\mathrm{ev}},\,
\Gamma_Y^{\mathrm{amb}},\,
C_Y,\,
\mathcal{L}_Y,\,
\Pi_Y,\,
\mathcal{R}_Y,\,
\mathcal{U}_Y,\,
I_Y
\Big),
is answer identity,
is answer address,
is the output contract class,
is the claim graph,
is the evidence lattice,
is the ambiguity field,
is the confidence surface,
is the rendering ladder,
is provenance,
is the replay bundle,
is the patch-suggestion bundle,
is the answer invariant family.
The provenance bundle
\Pi_Y
retrieval packet reference,
retrieval route bundle,
synthesis order,
lens-alignment choices,
collapse mode,
any forced resolutions.
The replay bundle
\mathcal{R}_Y
the answer schema,
the claim-support structure,
the ambiguity policy,
the rendering ladder,
the patch residuals.
This does not always require replaying the entire original solve from raw input; the answer object itself may serve as a replayable checkpoint.
A canonical answer serialization then becomes:
\operatorname{Ser}_{\mathrm{ans}}(Y)
=
\operatorname{Norm}_{\mathrm{ans}}(Y)
The replay constructor satisfies:
\operatorname{Replay}(Y)
\to
\widehat{Y}
Definition 11.4 — Replay-Stable Answer Object
A structured answer object is replay-stable if its replay bundle suffices to reconstruct its claim graph, evidence lattice, ambiguity field, and rendering ladder under the declared corridor and contract.
Theorem 11.2 — Structured Answers Are Strictly More Useful than Rendered Text Alone
A rendered text answer may satisfy the user-facing contract but cannot, in general, support replay, comparison, compression, or patch analysis as effectively as a structured answer object.
Proof. Rendered text compresses many internal synthesis relations into a surface string. Structured answer objects preserve claim relations, evidence, ambiguity, and replay structure explicitly. Therefore they support strictly more downstream operations.
The Square synthesis layer is therefore complete: claims, evidence, schemas, dependencies, and canonical answer objects.
Ch11.B1 — Aligning Projections from Multiple Lenses
The Flower synthesis layer begins where the support field is already structured in each lens but not yet fused. The same underlying object or route may appear as a Square claim lattice, a Flower dynamic flow, a Cloud uncertainty surface, and a Fractal multiscale shell. Synthesis must align these projections before it can fuse them lawfully.
Let the retrieved support bundle for some target object be
\mathfrak{B}(x)
=
\big(
K_{\Square}(x),\,
K_{\Flower}(x),\,
K_{\Cloud}(x),\,
K_{\Fractal}(x)
\big).
\mathbb{S}_{\mathrm{syn}},
\mathcal{A}_{\ell\to \ast}:
K_\ell(x)\to \mathbb{S}_{\mathrm{syn}}.
A synthesis atom in is a partially lens-agnostic unit such as:
claim token,
support token,
contradiction token,
dependency token,
route relevance token,
ambiguity token,
patch-residual token.
The alignment maps must preserve an identity trace:
\operatorname{Trace}\Big(
\chi(x),\,
\chi\big(
\mathcal{A}_{\ell\to\ast}(K_\ell(x))
\big)
\Big)\downarrow.
The result of alignment is a multi-lens synthesis bundle:
\mathfrak{B}^\ast(x)
=
\big(
\mathcal{A}_{\Square\to\ast}(K_{\Square}(x)),\,
\mathcal{A}_{\Flower\to\ast}(K_{\Flower}(x)),\,
\mathcal{A}_{\Cloud\to\ast}(K_{\Cloud}(x)),\,
\mathcal{A}_{\Fractal\to\ast}(K_{\Fractal}(x))
\big).
A natural alignment relation is:
a_i \sim_{\mathrm{syn}} a_j
\iff
\operatorname{IDCompat}(a_i,a_j)=1
\;\wedge\;
\operatorname{ContractCompat}(a_i,a_j)=1
\;\wedge\;
\operatorname{TransportCompat}(a_i,a_j)=1.
Alignment errors are possible. One lens may produce no good projection. Another may yield several rival alignments. These are not ignored. They become part of the synthesis residual or ambiguity field.
Definition 11.5 — Multi-Lens Alignment Completeness
A support bundle is multi-lens aligned for a target claim family if every materially relevant lens projection is either aligned into the synthesis manifold or explicitly represented as missing, unstable, or conflicting.
Proposition 11.3 — Multi-Lens Fusion Requires a Common Intermediate Space
Without a common synthesis space, lens projections can only be compared pairwise ad hoc, making lawful global fusion unstable and noncanonical.
Proof. Pairwise ad hoc comparison does not yield a unique or replayable global fusion structure. A common synthesis space provides a shared domain for alignment and clustering. Therefore it is necessary for canonical fusion.
The synthesis engine therefore does not “blend perspectives.” It aligns them.
Ch11.B2 — Resonance Consensus and Phase Conflict Handling
Once lens projections are aligned into common answer atoms, the next task is to determine whether they reinforce one another or conflict dynamically. The Flower synthesis layer models this through resonance consensus and phase conflict handling.
Let be an aligned candidate claim atom in . Suppose each lens contributes a support amplitude:
z_\ell(c)
=
a_\ell(c)\,e^{i\theta_\ell(c)},
\qquad
\ell\in\{\Square,\Flower,\Cloud,\Fractal\},
is the lens-local support magnitude,
is the lens-local phase or orientation.
The consensus phasor of claim atom is
Z(c)
=
\sum_{\ell}
\omega_\ell(c)\,a_\ell(c)e^{i\theta_\ell(c)},
|Z(c)|
\arg Z(c)
This phasor formalism is powerful because it distinguishes:
strong constructive agreement across lenses,
weak but aligned support,
strong support with phase cancellation,
single-lens dominance in the presence of silence elsewhere,
mixed-phase tension.
A claim reaches resonance consensus when:
|Z(c)| \ge \tau_{\mathrm{cons}}
\operatorname{PhaseSpread}(c)\le \delta_{\mathrm{cons}}.
However, not all disagreements are fatal. Phase conflicts occur when several lens projections align to the same claim family but support incompatible orientations. Examples include:
one lens treating a structure as constructive, another as diagnostic;
one lens treating a route as forward, another as reverse-conditional;
one lens emphasizing singular collapse, another emphasizing ambiguity retention.
A phase conflict is represented by
\Phi_{\mathrm{conf}}(c)
=
\big(
\{\theta_\ell(c)\},\,
\operatorname{spread},\,
\operatorname{separability},\,
\operatorname{repair}
\big).
dualization,
conditionalization,
branch splitting,
explanation-level rephrasing,
contract narrowing,
explicit counterhypothesis retention.
If the conflict is separable, the synthesis engine retains several phase-labeled claim variants rather than forcing one merged claim. If it is not separable and the contract demands singularity, then the conflict escalates to the Cloud uncertainty layer for explicit forced resolution or ambiguity output.
Definition 11.6 — Resonance-Consensual Claim
A claim atom is resonance-consensual if its cross-lens phasor magnitude exceeds the corridor’s consensus threshold and its unresolved phase spread does not violate the claim’s contract.
Theorem 11.3 — Phasor Consensus Generalizes Weighted Voting
Ordinary weighted voting is recovered when all lens phases are equal or restricted to ; phasor consensus is strictly richer because it also captures constructive and destructive interaction.
Proof. If for all , then reduces to the weighted scalar sum of amplitudes. When phases vary, cancellation or reinforcement becomes possible, which weighted voting cannot express.
The Flower consensus mechanism therefore turns multi-lens support into a real dynamic agreement object.
Ch11.B3 — Interference Analysis among Competing Interpretations
Synthesis rarely aligns only one candidate interpretation family. Retrieval often returns several structurally plausible support clusters. Lens alignment often produces several possible claim bundles. The synthesis engine must therefore analyze interference among competing interpretations.
Let the competing interpretation bundles be
\mathcal{I}
=
\{I_1,\dots,I_r\}.
I_j
=
\big(
G_j^{\mathrm{cl}},\,
L_j^{\mathrm{ev}},\,
\Gamma_j,\,
Z_j,\,
C_j
\big).
\mathcal{I}_{ij}
=
\operatorname{Overlap}(I_i,I_j)
\cdot
\cos(\theta_{ij})
-
\operatorname{Contradict}(I_i,I_j)
-
\operatorname{ContractClash}(I_i,I_j),
measures shared claim or evidence structure,
measures phase or orientation difference between the bundles,
penalizes incompatible claims,
penalizes incompatible output implications.
Positive suggests constructive merge or hybridization. Negative suggests destructive interference and the need for separation. Near-zero values suggest orthogonality.
This yields the interpretation interference matrix:
\mathbf{M}_{ij}=\mathcal{I}_{ij}.
constructive fusion — merge compatible interpretations into a richer claim bundle;
tensional retention — keep both with explicit tension or conditionalization;
plural answering — present several ranked bundles;
forced selection — choose one under contract or policy and preserve others as counterhypotheses.
Crucially, the engine is not allowed to average destructively interfering bundles into a smooth but false single interpretation. If the interference is strongly negative and separability exists, the bundles must remain separate. This is one of the major truthfulness constraints of the manuscript brain.
Definition 11.7 — Constructively Mergeable Interpretation Pair
Two interpretation bundles are constructively mergeable if their interference score is positive, their contradictions are bounded within corridor tolerance, and the merged bundle remains contract-coherent.
Proposition 11.4 — Naive Averaging of Destructively Interfering Interpretations Is Unsound
If two interpretation bundles destructively interfere strongly, then averaging or flattening them into one claim bundle discards structurally important tension and may produce claims unsupported by either bundle.
Proof. Destructive interference indicates that shared support is outweighed by phase or contradiction mismatch. A naive average mixes incompatible support and may yield claims not actually justified by any constituent.
The synthesis engine therefore treats interpretation plurality as an object of analysis, not an annoyance to be washed away.
Ch11.B4 — Transform Alignment before Final Collapse
Before the answer object may collapse into its final canonical schema, all active claim bundles, counterhypotheses, dynamic routes, and lens projections must be transformed into the representational regime required by the output contract. This is transform alignment before final collapse.
Let the pre-collapse synthesis state be
Y^{-}
=
\big(
\mathfrak{B}^\ast,\,
\mathcal{I},\,
\Gamma,\,
C,\,
\Pi
\big),
is the aligned multi-lens support bundle,
is the active interpretation family,
is the ambiguity field,
is the confidence field,
is provenance.
The output contract determines a target answer schema . Each active synthesis component must therefore admit a transport:
\Tau_{u\to\chi_q}: u \to \mathcal{Y}_{\chi_q}^{\mathrm{loc}},
a Flower phase bundle may need to become an explicit explanation of dynamic reasoning,
a Cloud ambiguity cluster may need to become ranked counterhypotheses,
a Fractal macro motif may need to become a reusable answer ladder reference,
a Square theorem graph may need to become the canonical structural core of the answer.
Transform alignment is therefore an operator family:
\mathcal{T}_{\chi_q}
=
\{
\Tau_{u\to\chi_q}
\}_{u\in Y^{-}}.
The final collapse operator
\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{syn}}_{\chi_q}
Y
=
\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{syn}}_{\chi_q}(Y^{-}_{\mathrm{aligned}}).
This step is vital because many synthesis failures arise not from bad retrieval or bad claims, but from collapsing before all supporting materials have been converted into the same representational language. A route motif, a theorem lattice, and an ambiguity basin cannot be naively poured into the same answer slot. They must first be aligned to the answer schema.
Definition 11.8 — Pre-Collapse Transform Completeness
A synthesis state is pre-collapse transform-complete for schema if every indispensable component of the state either admits a lawful transport into or is explicitly represented as residual or nonrendered support.
Theorem 11.4 — Final Collapse without Transform Alignment Is Noncanonical
If a final answer is produced before the active synthesis materials have been transformed into a common schema-compatible representation, then structurally equivalent solve episodes may yield inconsistent outputs and replay fails to be canonical.
Proof. Different incidental representations of the same support would collapse differently if not first aligned. Thus schema-level equivalence would be lost. Canonical collapse therefore requires transform alignment.
This completes the Flower synthesis layer: alignment, consensus, interference, and pre-collapse transport.
Ch11.C1 — Confidence Propagation across Synthesis Layers
Once the claim graph and aligned interpretation bundles exist, synthesis must compute how confidence flows upward from support to claims, from claims to sections, and from sections to the answer as a whole. The Cloud synthesis layer therefore begins with confidence propagation.
Let the evidence lattice provide primitive support confidences:
q(e)\in [0,1],
\qquad
e\in \mathcal{V}_{\mathrm{ev}}.
\ell(v)
=
\sum_{e\in \operatorname{Supp}(v)}
w_e(v)\,\operatorname{logit}(q(e))
-
\sum_{e\in \operatorname{Counter}(v)}
u_e(v)\,\operatorname{logit}(q(e))
-
\pi(v),
is the support contribution weight,
is the counterevidence weight,
is the structural penalty for contradiction, transport defect, or contract mismatch.
The claim confidence is then
C(v)=\sigma(\ell(v)),
positive support raises confidence,
counterevidence lowers it,
penalties lower it,
the propagation law is replayable.
Section confidence propagates from claim confidence. Let a section node contain claim family . Then:
C(s)
=
F_{\chi}\big(
\{C(v)\}_{v\in\mathcal{V}_s},\,
\operatorname{Deps}(s),\,
\operatorname{Conflict}(s)
\big),
At the top level, answer confidence is not necessarily a single scalar. The answer object may store a vector:
C_Y
=
\big(
C_{\mathrm{struct}},
C_{\mathrm{wit}},
C_{\mathrm{replay}},
C_{\mathrm{ambiguity}},
C_{\mathrm{patch}}
\big).
Confidence propagation must also respect dependencies. If claim depends on , then cannot exceed the effective support ceiling imposed by in strict logical corridors. Thus dependency-aware propagation may include:
C(v_j)
\le
g\big(C(v_i), \text{local support of }v_j\big).
A minimal propagation skeleton is:
import math
def logit(p):
eps = 1e-9
p = min(max(p, eps), 1 - eps)
return math.log(p / (1 - p))
def sigmoid(x):
return 1.0 / (1.0 + math.exp(-x))
def claim_confidence(supports, counters, penalty):
score = sum(w * logit(p) for w, p in supports)
score -= sum(w * logit(p) for w, p in counters)
score -= penalty
return sigmoid(score)
Definition 11.9 — Confidence-Monotone Propagation
A confidence propagation rule is monotone if increasing support without changing counterevidence or penalties cannot decrease the propagated confidence of a claim.
Theorem 11.5 — Dependency-Aware Confidence Prevents Unsupported Overstatement
If claim confidence is propagated with dependency ceilings, then no derived claim can become more certain than the weakest indispensable prerequisite class allows.
Proof. The ceiling constraint explicitly bounds claim confidence by prerequisite confidence. Therefore unsupported overstatement is prevented by construction.
This gives the answer object an explicit epistemic backbone rather than one global confidence guess.
Ch11.C2 — Retaining Counterhypotheses in Final Outputs
A lawful manuscript must not hide every non-dominant interpretation. Some rival bundles remain materially plausible and should travel with the answer. This is the role of counterhypothesis retention.
Let the primary claim bundle selected for final collapse be
I^\star.
\mathcal{H}^{-}
=
\{H_1,\dots,H_r\}.
H_i
=
\Big(
G_i^{\mathrm{cl}},\,
L_i^{\mathrm{ev}},\,
C_i,\,
\Delta_i,\,
\operatorname{rank}_i,\,
\operatorname{trigger}_i
\Big),
is the rival claim graph,
is the rival support lattice,
is the rival confidence profile,
is the differentiation set showing how it differs from the primary bundle,
is its ordering under the current contract,
lists conditions under which it would become primary.
Counterhypotheses are retained when:
their confidence exceeds the corridor’s rival threshold,
they are not fully contradicted,
the output contract allows or requires plurality,
forced singular resolution would hide genuine unresolved structure.
Retaining counterhypotheses serves three purposes. First, it preserves answer honesty. Second, it provides future routes for follow-up questions or patching. Third, it helps the memory system by storing what almost won, not only what did.
The final answer object may then carry:
\Gamma_Y^{\mathrm{counter}}
=
\{H_1,\dots,H_r\}.
Definition 11.10 — Material Counterhypothesis
A counterhypothesis is material if its retained support and confidence are strong enough that its omission would make the answer epistemically misleading or reduce future retrieval/replay utility materially.
Proposition 11.6 — Suppressing Material Counterhypotheses Reduces Answer Truthfulness
If a rival interpretation remains materially plausible and is omitted without explicit forced-resolution reason, then the answer overstates certainty.
Proof. The omission makes the output appear more singular than the underlying support warrants. Hence answer truthfulness is reduced.
The manuscript brain therefore retains rivals not as noise, but as lawful epistemic neighbors of the primary answer.
Ch11.C3 — Ambiguity-Preserving Answer Construction
Beyond counterhypotheses, some answer forms must preserve ambiguity directly. The question is not “which interpretation won?” but “what is the most honest answer constructor for the current state?” The Cloud layer therefore defines ambiguity-preserving answer construction.
The manuscript admits several top-level answer constructors:
Singular answer
A^\bullet
Ranked plural answer
A^\star = (H_1 \succ H_2 \succ \cdots)
Conditional answer
A^\Rightarrow
Disjunctive answer
A^\lor = y_1 \lor y_2 \lor \cdots
Interval or bounded answer
A^{[\,]}
Capsule answer
A^{\mathcal{U}}
The answer constructor is chosen by the ambiguity policy:
\tau_{\mathrm{ans}}:
(\Gamma^{\mathrm{amb}},\chi_q,\kappa_q)\to
\mathcal{A}_{\mathrm{constructor}}.
output contract,
residual ambiguity mass,
confidence gaps between leading bundles,
budget state,
whether action or description is being requested.
For example, a decision-support contract may demand singular or conditional answers. A knowledge-preservation contract may prefer ranked plural or capsule answers. A proof contract may allow singular theorem statements but still preserve rival proof strategies in the counterhypothesis field.
The key law is ambiguity honesty:
\text{Constructor}(Y)
This formalism also means that ambiguity is not a fallback formatting gimmick. It is a first-class answer type.
Definition 11.11 — Ambiguity-Honest Answer
An answer is ambiguity honest if its top-level constructor matches the actual residual support structure up to the demands of the output contract and any forced singularization is explicitly witnessed.
Theorem 11.6 — Answer Constructors Form a Strict Refinement Hierarchy
Singular answers are a special case of ranked plural answers; ranked plural answers are a special case of conditional or disjunctive families under certain ordering conventions; capsule answers are the most conservative preserving form. Therefore the answer constructors form a refinement hierarchy by degree of preserved ambiguity.
Proof. A singular answer is a plural answer of cardinality one. A ranked plural family may be encoded as conditional or disjunctive structure if rank is preserved externally. Capsule answers preserve maximal ambiguity with boundary. Hence the constructors can be ordered by preserved ambiguity.
The manuscript therefore knows not only what to say, but how honest the form of saying must be.
Ch11.C4 — Calibrated Confidence Surfaces over Claims
The final Cloud task is to give the answer object a full confidence surface rather than only one or two scalar values. The confidence surface is the field over which claims, sections, counterhypotheses, and residuals are epistemically situated.
Let the answer manifold be the union of its claim graph and rendering ladder:
\mathbb{Y}_{\mathrm{man}}
=
\mathcal{V}_{\mathrm{cl}}
\cup
\mathcal{V}_{\mathrm{sec}}
\cup
\mathcal{H}^{-}
\cup
\mathcal{U}^{\mathrm{res}}.
C_Y:
\mathbb{Y}_{\mathrm{man}}
\to
[0,1]^d,
C_Y(z)
=
\big(
c_{\mathrm{support}}(z),\,
c_{\mathrm{transport}}(z),\,
c_{\mathrm{replay}}(z),\,
c_{\mathrm{ambiguity}}(z),\,
c_{\mathrm{patch}}(z)
\big).
Thus a claim may have:
strong support confidence,
moderate transport confidence due to lens alignment defect,
strong replay confidence,
low ambiguity confidence because rivals remain,
moderate patch confidence if improvement suggestions are uncertain.
The answer surface generalizes scalar answer confidence by preserving where the strong and weak zones lie. This allows:
conservative rendering of fragile claims,
targeted follow-up retrieval,
precise patch suggestions,
better memory compaction,
better downstream decision-making.
A section or top-level answer confidence can be obtained by integrating the surface over the relevant submanifold:
C_{\mathrm{section}}(S)
=
\int_S
\varphi(z)\,C_Y(z)\,dz
The surface is calibrated if, over replay history, claims assigned confidence succeed with frequency approximately under comparable contexts. Thus calibration is not only global but local to answer substructures.
Definition 11.12 — Locally Calibrated Confidence Surface
A confidence surface is locally calibrated if, for each relevant answer subclass, the empirical replay success of claims in a confidence band approximates the confidence assigned to that band within corridor tolerance.
Proposition 11.7 — Confidence Surfaces Strictly Dominate Section-Level Scalars
A section-level scalar may be derived from a confidence surface, but the reverse is impossible in general because the scalar loses spatial distribution of certainty and weakness.
Proof. Aggregation from a surface to a scalar is many-to-one. Therefore the surface contains strictly more information.
The answer object thus carries not just content and support, but a real epistemic landscape.
Ch11.D1 — Summary-to-Full Reconstruction Ladders
The Fractal synthesis layer begins by recognizing that an answer should not exist only at one scale. A good answer can be rendered as a high-level summary, a medium-depth exposition, a detailed proof or construction, and a full replay-ready artifact. These scales must be lawfully linked. This is the role of the reconstruction ladder.
Let the answer object be . A reconstruction ladder is a scale-indexed family:
\mathcal{L}_Y
=
\big(
Y^{(0)},Y^{(1)},\dots,Y^{(k)}
\big),
is the coarsest summary form,
is the deepest stored answer form,
each expands lawfully into .
Each rung is a valid answer object for some weaker contract:
\chi^{(0)} \preceq \chi^{(1)} \preceq \cdots \preceq \chi^{(k)}.
The expansion law is:
\mathcal{E}_{Y}^{(i\to i+1)}:
Y^{(i)}\to Y^{(i+1)}.
\mathcal{C}_{Y}^{(i+1\to i)}:
Y^{(i+1)}\to Y^{(i)}.
\mathcal{C}_{Y}^{(i+1\to i)}\circ \mathcal{E}_{Y}^{(i\to i+1)}(Y^{(i)})
=
Y^{(i)}
The ladder enables:
adaptive presentation,
budget-aware follow-up,
memory compaction,
selective replay,
partial answer refinement without starting over.
A ladder-aware answer schema is particularly important in a manuscript ecosystem where one agent may request a summary and another later request proof-level expansion. Both should remain lawful views of the same answer object.
A minimal ladder schema is:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Dict, List, Any
@dataclass
class AnswerRung:
rung_id: str
level: int
contract: Dict[str, Any]
content_ref: str
expansion_refs: List[str]
metadata: Dict[str, Any]
@dataclass
class AnswerLadder:
answer_id: str
rungs: List[AnswerRung]
Definition 11.13 — Ladder-Coherent Answer
An answer ladder is coherent if each rung is a valid answer for its own contract and every adjacent rung pair is connected by lawful expansion and collapse operators.
Theorem 11.7 — Reconstruction Ladders Generalize Static Answers
A static one-level answer is the special case of a reconstruction ladder of length one.
Proof. With only one rung, there is no expansion or collapse. Therefore the static answer is a degenerate ladder.
The manuscript answer is thus a scalable object, not a one-shot rendering.
Ch11.D2 — Nested Explanations and Expansion-on-Demand
Beyond summary ladders, an answer must support nested explanation. A user or future agent may want one claim expanded, another kept coarse, and a third replaced by a proof sketch or code schema. The answer therefore requires an explanation tree.
Let the explanation tree of answer be:
\mathcal{T}^{\mathrm{exp}}_Y
=
(V_Y^{\mathrm{exp}},E_Y^{\mathrm{exp}},r_Y,\ell_Y).
\ell_Y(v)
=
\big(
\operatorname{claim\_ref}(v),\,
\operatorname{mode}(v),\,
\operatorname{children}(v),\,
\operatorname{confidence}(v),\,
\operatorname{contract}(v)
\big).
concise statement,
expanded explanation,
formal derivation,
example,
route provenance,
patch implication,
ambiguity note.
Expansion-on-demand is then the operation:
\mathcal{E}^{\mathrm{ond}}_Y(v,\operatorname{mode}')
\to
\text{children or alternate rendering of }v.
Nested explanation is particularly valuable because synthesis often produces deep internal structures not all of which should be exposed immediately. A top-level theorem may open into proof skeleton, then into line-by-line derivation, then into route provenance, each as separate explanation children.
The explanation tree must preserve consistency. Expanding a node must not change its parent claim’s content, only reveal its support or elaboration. Thus explanation expansion is subject to:
\operatorname{Collapse}_{\mathrm{exp}}(\operatorname{Expand}_{\mathrm{exp}}(v))
=
v
Definition 11.14 — Explanation-Stable Node
An explanation node is stable if all of its expansion modes are lawful refinements of the same claim or answer unit and do not introduce unsupported content.
Proposition 11.8 — Expansion-on-Demand Reduces Average Answer Cost
If only a subset of answer nodes are ever expanded deeply, then storing the answer as a nested explanation tree lowers expected rendering and replay cost compared with always expanding the whole answer.
Proof. Only the requested branches incur deeper reconstruction cost. Therefore expected total cost is lower than universal expansion.
The answer object thus becomes an interactive, lawful explanation organ of the manuscript.
Ch11.D3 — Pattern Reuse from Prior Solution Archetypes
Synthesis is expensive. The manuscript therefore reuses prior solution archetypes whenever the current support graph matches a known answer pattern. This is not template copying in the crude sense. It is lawful instantiation of a previously validated synthesis pattern.
A solution archetype is a structured object
\mathfrak{A}_Y
=
\Big(
u_{\mathfrak{A}},\,
\operatorname{shape}(\mathfrak{A}),\,
\operatorname{slot\_schema}(\mathfrak{A}),\,
\operatorname{contract\_class}(\mathfrak{A}),\,
\operatorname{witness\_schema}(\mathfrak{A}),\,
\operatorname{reuse\_history}(\mathfrak{A})
\Big).
define → formalize → prove → code,
diagnose → locate contradiction → repair proposal → replay note,
compare two systems → list invariants → list divergences → preserve counterhypotheses,
summary → route map → detailed appendix pointers.
Given a current support state
\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{syn}}(q)
\operatorname{Match}(\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{syn}}(q),\mathfrak{A}_Y)=1
\beta_{\mathfrak{A}}:
\operatorname{slots}(\mathfrak{A})\to
\text{current claims/evidence nodes}.
Y_{\mathfrak{A}} = \operatorname{Instantiate}(\mathfrak{A}_Y,\beta_{\mathfrak{A}}).
Archetype reuse accelerates synthesis and improves consistency across similar problem families. It also makes the atlas more self-developing, because repeated successful solutions are abstracted into reusable answer forms.
However, archetype reuse must remain subordinate to current support. If the match is weak or if the current support contains novel structures, the archetype must be adapted, partially instantiated, or rejected. Thus reuse is never allowed to hallucinate unsupported answer sections.
Definition 11.15 — Lawful Archetype Instantiation
An answer archetype is lawfully instantiated if every mandatory slot is bound to current support, all witness obligations are satisfied, and any unmatched sections are either omitted or marked residual.
Theorem 11.8 — Archetype Reuse Reduces Synthesis Action on Matched Problem Families
If the current support graph matches a previously validated answer archetype above threshold, then instantiating the archetype requires less synthesis action than constructing an equivalent answer from scratch.
Proof. The archetype already provides answer shape, dependency order, and rendering ladder. Synthesis then solves only the binding problem rather than the full construction problem. Hence action is reduced.
The manuscript therefore learns answer shapes just as it learns route motifs and retrieval macros.
Ch11.D4 — Patch Suggestion Generation from Synthesized Results
The final act of synthesis is not always finality. A deep synthesis often reveals gaps, weak bridges, underdeveloped chapters, brittle memories, unresolved contradictions, or missing witness surfaces. The manuscript must therefore be able to extract patch suggestions from the answer itself.
Let the final answer object be
Y
=
(G^{\mathrm{cl}},L^{\mathrm{ev}},\Gamma^{\mathrm{amb}},C_Y,\mathcal{L}_Y,\Pi_Y,\mathcal{R}_Y).
\mathcal{U}^{\mathrm{res}}(Y)
=
\big(
\mathcal{G}^{\mathrm{gap}},
\mathcal{K}^{\mathrm{conf}},
\mathcal{D}^{\mathrm{def}},
\mathcal{W}^{\mathrm{weak}},
\mathcal{R}^{\mathrm{reuse}}
\big),
is the set of explicit support or structural gaps,
is the contradiction or tension field that remained,
is the defect field,
is the set of weak or low-confidence answer zones,
is the set of reusable motifs that deserve canonization.
The patch suggestion generator is:
\mathcal{P}_{\mathrm{suggest}}:
Y \to \{\Delta_1,\dots,\Delta_n\},
\Delta_i
=
\big(
\operatorname{target}(\Delta_i),\,
\operatorname{kind}(\Delta_i),\,
\operatorname{motivation}(\Delta_i),\,
\operatorname{expected\_gain}(\Delta_i),\,
\operatorname{risk}(\Delta_i),\,
\operatorname{witness\_need}(\Delta_i)
\big).
Patch kinds include:
create bridge,
strengthen witness,
expand memory shell,
compress recurrent route into macro,
add counterhypothesis memory,
repair contradictory lineage,
deepen explanation ladder,
create retrieval macro,
add theorem definition cell,
add explicit address or alias mapping.
Patch suggestions are ranked by expected gain relative to cost and risk:
S_{\mathrm{patch}}(\Delta_i)
=
\operatorname{Gain}(\Delta_i)
-
\operatorname{Cost}(\Delta_i)
-
\operatorname{Risk}(\Delta_i).
The profound significance of this step is that synthesis becomes self-improving. The manuscript does not merely answer the current question. It also learns what the atlas should grow next so that future similar questions become easier, safer, or more precise.
A minimal patch-suggestion skeleton is:
def suggest_patches(answer_obj):
suggestions = []
for gap in answer_obj.get("gaps", []):
suggestions.append({
"kind": "FillGap",
"target": gap["target"],
"motivation": gap,
})
for weak in answer_obj.get("weak_claims", []):
suggestions.append({
"kind": "StrengthenWitness",
"target": weak["claim_id"],
"motivation": weak,
})
return suggestions
The actual manuscript implementation will rank, merge, and corridor-filter these suggestions, but the formal principle is already clear: answer residuals are inputs to atlas evolution.
Definition 11.16 — Synthesis-Derived Patch Suggestion
A patch suggestion is synthesis-derived if it is justified by explicit gaps, contradictions, weak-confidence zones, reusable motifs, or defect fields appearing in the answer object or its provenance.
Theorem 11.9 — Residual-Aware Synthesis Generates Self-Improving Scaffolds
If answer synthesis preserves residual structure explicitly, then each answer can emit a lawful family of candidate atlas improvements, making the manuscript increasingly self-developing across repeated solves.
Proof. Residual structure identifies what the answer could not fully stabilize, what it reused successfully, and what it lacked. These are exactly the loci at which future atlas improvements would raise solve quality. Therefore residual-aware synthesis produces self-improvement guidance.
This completes the Fractal synthesis layer. Answers now grow, compress, reuse patterns, and recommend how the atlas itself should evolve.
The chapter has now completed the formal theory of multi-lens synthesis and solution construction. Under the Square lens, synthesis built claim graphs and evidence lattices, shaped them into canonical answer schemas, ordered them by dependency, and packaged them as structured replayable answer objects. Under the Flower lens, synthesis aligned the projections of multiple lenses into a common manifold, computed resonance consensus, handled phase conflicts among competing interpretations, analyzed constructive and destructive interference, and transformed all surviving support into schema-compatible form before final collapse. Under the Cloud lens, synthesis propagated confidence from evidence to claims to sections, retained counterhypotheses rather than hiding them, chose answer constructors that preserve appropriate levels of ambiguity, and assigned a calibrated confidence surface over the entire answer manifold. Under the Fractal lens, synthesis became scalable: every answer acquired a summary-to-full reconstruction ladder, a nested explanation tree, the ability to instantiate prior solution archetypes lawfully, and the ability to emit patch suggestions from its own residuals.
The deepest consequence is that the manuscript now knows how to produce an answer without ceasing to be a manuscript. A final answer is not a terminal leaf that disconnects from the system that generated it. It is a new structured object inside the atlas: addressable, witness-bearing, ambiguity-aware, replayable, expandable, and self-improving. This is the exact formal meaning of “solution construction” in the present framework. The atlas does not merely find support. It composes support into a lawful artifact that can itself become memory, route substrate, patch input, and future support. Synthesis is therefore the crystallization point at which retrieval, route geometry, lens calculus, uncertainty management, and regenerative memory converge into one canonical cognitive act.
CHAPTER 11 — MULTI-LENS SYNTHESIS AND SOLUTION CONSTRUCTION
Chapter 12 — Witness Logic, Corridor Constraints, and Legality Calculus
A manuscript brain becomes trustworthy only when every object, route, transform, retrieval trace, synthesis artifact, memory regeneration, and patch proposal can be judged against explicit legality conditions and accompanied by sufficient witness. Without such a system, all prior chapters remain structurally rich but normatively weak: routes can be found, memories can be reopened, syntheses can be assembled, and weights can be reinforced, yet no formal mechanism exists to determine when these acts are admissible, when they are merely approximate, when they must remain deferred, and when they are outright blocked. The present chapter provides that mechanism. Its subject is witness logic, corridor constraints, and legality calculus.
The chapter introduces the full judgmental layer of the manuscript. This layer is not an optional proof assistant appended to an otherwise complete architecture. It is the discipline by which the atlas knows the difference between:
an exact route and an approximate route,
a lawful bridge and a phase-incompatible bridge,
a replayable answer and a useful but weakly witnessed answer,
a safe patch and a speculative patch,
a bounded uncertainty capsule and an inadmissible unresolved contradiction,
a compact proof witness and a mere summary.
The central object of the chapter is the legality judgment. Let the current legality environment be
\mathfrak{E}_t
=
\Big(
S_t,\,
\mathfrak{W}_t,\,
\chi_t,\,
\Lambda_t,\,
\phi_t,\,
\beta_t,\,
\Gamma_t
\Big),
is the active atlas state,
is the witness registry,
is the active contract bundle,
is the active lens profile,
is the phase profile,
is the budget and risk state,
is the active ambiguity and contradiction field.
Let the corridor class be
\kappa\in \mathcal{K}.
\mathfrak{E}_t \vdash_\kappa x \Downarrow (\lambda_x,\psi_x,\delta_x),
is the object under judgment,
is the legality class,
is the witness bundle,
is the defect and uncertainty profile.
The legality class is drawn from the four-valued legality lattice
\mathcal{L}
=
\{
\mathbf{Exact},
\mathbf{Approx},
\mathbf{Deferred},
\mathbf{Blocked}
\}.
\mathbf{Blocked}
\prec
\mathbf{Deferred}
\prec
\mathbf{Approx}
\prec
\mathbf{Exact}.
The legality calculus is governed by six chapter-wide laws.
First, witness sufficiency: no object may claim stronger legality than its witness bundle supports.
Second, corridor honesty: no route, answer, or patch may be admitted under a stricter legality class than the active corridor allows.
Third, defect visibility: every approximation, unresolved ambiguity, or fragile transport must carry an explicit defect or uncertainty profile.
Fourth, replay necessity: no object may count as fully legal in replay-sensitive corridors unless its witness includes a replay constructor or replay trace sufficient for the relevant contract.
Fifth, constraint propagation: legality of complex routes or syntheses is computed from the lawful propagation of local constraints, not merely asserted at the end.
Sixth, repair closure: a failed or partial legality judgment is never discarded silently; it must either yield a typed failure object, a deferred capsule, or a lawful repair path.
The chapter proceeds by the four-lens discipline. Under the Square lens it defines witness schemas, corridor classes, admissibility predicates, and acceptance contracts. Under the Flower lens it defines dynamic legality of transports, phase-sensitive bridge permissions, transition guards, and constraint propagation through moving route states. Under the Cloud lens it defines error bounds, contradiction budgets, risk scoring under partial verification, approximate legality thresholds, and deployment acceptance under uncertainty. Under the Fractal lens it defines replay chains, recursive repair, certificate bundles for reusable solution classes, and proof compaction laws that preserve replayability.
The deeper claim is that legality is not a post hoc filter. It is the membrane of the manuscript brain. Every solve episode, from memory opening to final patch suggestion, passes through it.
Ch12.A1 — Witness Schemas and Proof-Carrying Packets
The Square foundation of legality begins with the witness itself. A witness in the manuscript is not merely a citation, a proof sketch, or a boolean flag saying “verified.” It is a typed object carrying enough structured support to justify a claim of legality, truth, admissibility, replayability, or bounded approximation within a declared corridor and contract.
Let the global witness space be
\Psi.
\psi
=
\Big(
u_\psi,\,
a_\psi,\,
\operatorname{scope}(\psi),\,
\operatorname{claim}(\psi),\,
\operatorname{prem}(\psi),\,
\operatorname{supp}(\psi),\,
\operatorname{inv}(\psi),\,
\operatorname{grade}(\psi),\,
\operatorname{replay}(\psi),\,
\operatorname{defect}(\psi),\,
\operatorname{sig}(\psi)
\Big),
is identity,
is canonical witness address,
is the object family to which the witness applies,
is the legal or factual proposition asserted,
is the premise family required,
is the evidence or support payload,
is the invariant family that the witness certifies,
is the witness strength,
is the replay constructor or replay trace,
is the approximation or uncertainty record,
is the normalization or signature field for hashing and identity binding.
The witness grade belongs to an ordered strength hierarchy:
\mathcal{G}_\psi
=
\{
\mathrm{Declared},
\mathrm{Checked},
\mathrm{Replayed},
\mathrm{CrossChecked},
\mathrm{StressTested}
\},
\qquad
\mathrm{Declared}\prec\mathrm{Checked}\prec\mathrm{Replayed}\prec\mathrm{CrossChecked}\prec\mathrm{StressTested}.
The witness must also encode the shape of support. The manuscript distinguishes at least five primitive witness schemas:
\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{wit}}
=
\{
\mathrm{Direct},
\mathrm{Derived},
\mathrm{Transport},
\mathrm{Replay},
\mathrm{Contractual}
\}.
A proof-carrying packet is the transportable memory-safe form of one or more witnesses:
\Pi_\psi
=
\Big(
u_{\Pi},\,
\operatorname{scope}(\Pi),\,
\operatorname{claim}(\Pi),\,
\operatorname{schema}(\Pi),\,
\operatorname{prem}(\Pi),\,
\operatorname{payload}(\Pi),\,
\operatorname{replay}(\Pi),\,
\operatorname{hash}(\Pi)
\Big).
A witness is well formed if and only if:
\mathrm{Addr}(\psi)=1,\quad
\mathrm{ScopeTyped}(\psi)=1,\quad
\mathrm{ClaimTyped}(\psi)=1,\quad
\mathrm{PremClosed}(\psi)=1,\quad
\mathrm{ReplayDeclared}(\psi)\vee \mathrm{ReplayWaived}(\psi)=1.
A canonical code schema is:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, List, Any, Literal
WitnessGrade = Literal[
"Declared", "Checked", "Replayed", "CrossChecked", "StressTested"
]
WitnessSchema = Literal[
"Direct", "Derived", "Transport", "Replay", "Contractual"
]
@dataclass
class WitnessObject:
witness_id: str
address: str
scope_ids: List[str]
claim: Dict[str, Any]
premises: List[str]
support_payload: Dict[str, Any]
invariant_bundle: Dict[str, Any]
grade: WitnessGrade
schema: WitnessSchema
replay_bundle: Dict[str, Any]
defect: Dict[str, Any]
signature: Dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
The deepest significance of witness schemas is that they make legality explainable. The atlas can say not merely that a route is legal, but whether it is legal by direct structural evidence, by derived support from already certified steps, by successful transport with a phase trace, by replay, or only by contract-relative allowance.
Definition 12.1 — Witness Sufficiency
A witness is sufficient for claim under corridor if its schema, grade, replay bundle, and defect profile meet the minimum witness requirements imposed by on claims of type .
Proposition 12.1 — Proof-Carrying Packets Are Strictly Stronger than Bare Evidence Attachments
A bare evidence attachment may indicate supporting material, but a proof-carrying packet additionally preserves schema, premises, replay constructor, and defect disclosure. Therefore it supports stronger legality judgments.
Proof. Bare attachments omit the structure needed to reconstruct how the support justifies the claim. Proof-carrying packets preserve this structure explicitly. Hence they are strictly stronger.
The witness object and its packet form are thus the atomic currency of legality.
Ch12.A2 — Corridor Classes and Legality Zones
Legality is never absolute in the manuscript. It is corridor-relative. A route that is acceptable for exploratory synthesis may be too weak for proof replay. A patch suggestion acceptable for internal diagnosis may be unsafe for deployment. The manuscript therefore organizes admissibility through a system of corridor classes and legality zones.
Let the corridor class lattice be
\mathcal{K}
=
\{
\kappa_{\mathrm{strict}},
\kappa_{\mathrm{replay}},
\kappa_{\mathrm{construct}},
\kappa_{\mathrm{explore}},
\kappa_{\mathrm{repair}},
\kappa_{\mathrm{deploy}}
\}.
\kappa_{\mathrm{strict}}
\preceq
\kappa_{\mathrm{replay}}
\preceq
\kappa_{\mathrm{construct}}
\preceq
\kappa_{\mathrm{explore}},
Each corridor class is a tuple
\kappa
=
\Big(
\mathcal{I}_\kappa,\,
\varepsilon_\kappa,\,
\varrho_\kappa,\,
\tau_\kappa,\,
\Pi_\kappa,\,
\mathcal{R}_\kappa
\Big),
is the invariant family required,
is the defect budget,
is the risk tolerance profile,
is the allowed transform family,
is the permission and policy set,
is the replay requirement profile.
Thus a corridor is not a simple label. It is a full local legal regime.
A legality zone is a region of the atlas governed by a corridor class:
\zeta:
\mathbb{O}_{\mathrm{atlas}}\to \mathcal{K}.
Movement across legality zones is not free. If a route crosses from zone to , a corridor transition judgment is required:
\mathfrak{E}_t \vdash_{\kappa_1\leadsto\kappa_2} \rho : \mathrm{transit\_legal}.
A legality zone system also permits local strictness. One chapter or region may admit bounded approximation while another forbids it. This makes the atlas heterogeneous but governable.
Definition 12.2 — Corridor-Safe Zone Crossing
A route or transform crosses from corridor into corridor safely if it satisfies all invariants newly required by or else is explicitly downgraded, blocked, or wrapped as deferred.
Proposition 12.2 — Corridor-Free Legality Collapses Context
If legality were judged without corridor class, the atlas could not distinguish exploratory admissibility from replay or deployment admissibility, and would therefore overstate or understate legal status.
Proof. The same object can be acceptable in one regime and unacceptable in another. Without corridor indexing, the legality judgment loses this distinction.
The corridor system is therefore the zoning law of the manuscript brain.
Ch12.A3 — Admissibility Predicates for Objects and Routes
Witnesses and corridors are prerequisites, but legality requires explicit predicates. The manuscript therefore defines the admissibility predicates for the major artifact classes: objects, routes, transforms, memory recalls, answers, and patches.
Let be an atlas object. The base object admissibility predicate under corridor is:
\operatorname{Adm}_\kappa(x)
=
\operatorname{Addr}_\kappa(x)
\wedge
\operatorname{Type}_\kappa(x)
\wedge
\operatorname{Inv}_\kappa(x)
\wedge
\operatorname{Wit}_\kappa(x)
\wedge
\operatorname{Def}_\kappa(x)
\wedge
\operatorname{Replay}_\kappa(x).
: canonical addressing is valid for corridor use;
: type signature is compatible with intended use;
: required invariants hold;
: witness sufficiency is met;
: defect remains within corridor budget;
: replay requirements are satisfied or waived explicitly.
For routes, admissibility is compositional. Let
\rho
=
(o_0,\mu_1,o_1,\dots,\mu_n,o_n).
\operatorname{Adm}_\kappa(\rho)
=
\Big(
\bigwedge_{i=1}^{n}
\operatorname{LegalAdj}_\kappa(o_{i-1},\mu_i,o_i)
\Big)
\wedge
\operatorname{FlowInv}_\kappa(\rho)
\wedge
\operatorname{Budget}_\kappa(\rho)
\wedge
\operatorname{WitPath}_\kappa(\rho)
\wedge
\operatorname{Contract}_\kappa(\rho).
For transforms , the predicate is:
\operatorname{Adm}_\kappa(T:x\to y)
=
\operatorname{Dom}_\kappa(T,x)
\wedge
\operatorname{Cod}_\kappa(T,y)
\wedge
\operatorname{InvPres}_\kappa(T)
\wedge
\operatorname{PhaseCompat}_\kappa(T)
\wedge
\operatorname{Wit}_\kappa(T).
For answer objects , admissibility is:
\operatorname{Adm}_\kappa(Y)
=
\operatorname{Schema}_\kappa(Y)
\wedge
\operatorname{SupportTrace}_\kappa(Y)
\wedge
\operatorname{ConfidenceHonesty}_\kappa(Y)
\wedge
\operatorname{ReplayBundle}_\kappa(Y)
\wedge
\operatorname{ContractSat}_\kappa(Y).
For memory regeneration:
\operatorname{Adm}_\kappa(m\rightsquigarrow \widehat{X})
=
\operatorname{SeedSuff}_\kappa(m)
\wedge
\operatorname{DefectBound}_\kappa(\widehat{X})
\wedge
\operatorname{ConfidenceHonesty}_\kappa(\widehat{X})
\wedge
\operatorname{ReplayTrace}_\kappa(m,\widehat{X}).
These predicates yield the four-valued legality class by thresholding defect, witness, and replay conditions. For example:
\lambda_x=
\begin{cases}
\mathbf{Exact}, & \operatorname{Adm}_\kappa(x)\land \delta_x = 0,\\[4pt]
\mathbf{Approx}, & \operatorname{Adm}_\kappa^{\mathrm{approx}}(x)\land \delta_x \le \varepsilon_\kappa,\\[4pt]
\mathbf{Deferred}, & \operatorname{Deferrable}_\kappa(x),\\[4pt]
\mathbf{Blocked}, & \text{otherwise.}
\end{cases}
A convenient verifier skeleton is:
def admissibility_class(obj, corridor, env):
if exact_admissible(obj, corridor, env):
return "Exact"
if approx_admissible(obj, corridor, env):
return "Approx"
if deferrable(obj, corridor, env):
return "Deferred"
return "Blocked"
The importance of explicit predicates is that the atlas can now prove legality compositionally rather than by narrative explanation. One can ask precisely why a route is blocked, what witness is missing, whether approximation is within tolerance, and what would be required to upgrade a deferred object to exact.
Definition 12.3 — Exact, Approximate, Deferred, and Blocked Classes
The legality classes are determined by exact admissibility, bounded approximate admissibility, lawful deferability under uncertainty or missing witness, and failure of all preceding conditions.
Theorem 12.1 — Global Route Legality Reduces to Local Admissibility plus Global Accumulation
If local steps, budgets, witness paths, and contract conditions are all explicit, then route legality is decidable by local admissibility and finite global accumulation rules.
Proof. Each local step contributes a finite judgment. Global legality adds finite accumulation of budgets, witnesses, and contract obligations. Therefore route legality is finitely decidable under the explicit calculus.
This makes legality a real calculus rather than a vague notion of “seems okay.”
Ch12.A4 — Contract Formation for Solution Acceptance
The final Square task is to define when a completed synthesis or patch candidate is officially accepted. The manuscript therefore introduces the acceptance contract: a structured obligation bundle describing what must be true for an artifact to count as accepted under a given corridor.
An acceptance contract for artifact class is
\mathfrak{C}_Y
=
\Big(
\chi_Y,\,
\kappa_Y,\,
\mathcal{I}_Y^{\min},\,
\varepsilon_Y,\,
\gamma_Y,\,
\mathcal{R}_Y^{\min},\,
\Pi_Y^{\mathrm{prov}},\,
\mathcal{D}_Y^{\mathrm{allow}}
\Big),
is the output or deployment contract,
is the corridor,
is the minimal invariant family,
is the defect budget,
is the confidence threshold profile,
is the replay requirement,
is the provenance requirement,
is the allowed residual or ambiguity class.
An artifact is accepted when the judgment
\mathfrak{E}_t \vdash_{\kappa_Y} Y \triangleright \mathfrak{C}_Y
\operatorname{Schema}_\kappa(Y)
\wedge
\operatorname{InvMin}(Y,\mathcal{I}_Y^{\min})
\wedge
\operatorname{DefectBound}(Y,\varepsilon_Y)
\wedge
\operatorname{ConfBound}(Y,\gamma_Y)
\wedge
\operatorname{ReplayMin}(Y,\mathcal{R}_Y^{\min})
\wedge
\operatorname{ProvMin}(Y,\Pi_Y^{\mathrm{prov}})
\wedge
\operatorname{ResidualAllowed}(Y,\mathcal{D}_Y^{\mathrm{allow}}).
Different contract classes generate different acceptance surfaces. A strict theorem proof may require exact legality, full replay, and no unresolved contradiction. An exploratory synthesis may allow approximate legality, bounded ambiguity, and partial replay. A patch suggestion may require strong provenance and reversibility but not full theorem-style proof.
The acceptance contract is also the place where forced resolution becomes formal. If an artifact contains residual ambiguity that would normally prevent singular acceptance, the contract may still accept it under a forced-resolution tag if policy and corridor allow that and the forced nature of the decision is explicit in the witness.
A contract checker may be sketched as:
def accept_artifact(artifact, contract, env):
checks = {
"schema": check_schema(artifact, contract),
"invariants": check_invariants(artifact, contract),
"defect": check_defect(artifact, contract),
"confidence": check_confidence(artifact, contract),
"replay": check_replay(artifact, contract),
"provenance": check_provenance(artifact, contract),
"residuals": check_residuals(artifact, contract),
}
return all(checks.values()), checks
