The acceptance contract is what turns synthesis into an officially admitted answer or patch. Without it, the atlas would have structured outputs but no sharp boundary between candidate output and canonized output.
Definition 12.4 — Accepted Artifact
An artifact is accepted if it satisfies its acceptance contract under the current legality environment and receives an admissible acceptance witness packet.
Proposition 12.3 — Contract-Free Acceptance Is Epistemically Opaque
If artifacts are marked accepted without explicit contract formation, then later replay cannot know what threshold or obligations were actually met.
Proof. Acceptance without contract omits the very criteria of admissibility. Therefore replay lacks the normative context that gave meaning to acceptance.
The acceptance contract thus completes the Square layer of legality: witness, corridor, predicate, and acceptance are now all explicit.
Ch12.B1 — Lens-Aware Transport Legality
The Flower legality layer begins where a route or synthesis crosses representational regimes. A transport between lenses is not lawful merely because it produces something useful. It must preserve or explicitly account for identity, phase, witness, and defect. This is lens-aware transport legality.
Let
\Tau_{\ell\to m}:K_\ell(x)\to K_m(x')
\mathfrak{E}_t \vdash_\kappa
\Tau_{\ell\to m}(x)
:
K_\ell(x)\rightsquigarrow K_m(x')
\Downarrow
(\lambda_T,\psi_T,\delta_T).
identity traceability
\operatorname{Trace}\big(\chi(x),\chi(x')\big)\downarrow;
invariant preservation
\mathcal{I}_{\ell\to m}^{\mathrm{req}}
\subseteq
\operatorname{InvPres}(T);
phase legality the dynamic orientation and mode shift induced by the transport are allowed by the corridor;
boundary compatibility the source boundary channels and target boundary channels are matched or transformed lawfully;
defect boundedness
\delta_T \le \varepsilon_\kappa;
witness sufficiency
\psi_T \in \Psi_\kappa^{\mathrm{transport}}.
A transport may be:
exact,
approximate,
deferred,
blocked. The legality class depends on the defect and witness class. For example, Square-to-Cloud transport of a theorem lattice into a confidence field may be approximate but legal if the induced loss of combinatorial detail is declared and acceptable for the contract. The same transport may be blocked in strict replay corridors.
Lens-aware legality is important because cross-lens synthesis and retrieval rely on transport heavily. The atlas must know when a route motif or memory shell may legally be viewed in another chart and when such a move would introduce misleading distortion.
A general transport defect vector is:
\delta_T
=
\big(
\delta_{\mathrm{id}},
\delta_{\mathrm{inv}},
\delta_{\mathrm{phase}},
\delta_{\mathrm{boundary}},
\delta_{\mathrm{witness}}
\big).
Definition 12.5 — Lens-Legal Transport
A transport is lens-legal under corridor if it preserves the corridor’s required invariant family, keeps defect within budget, and carries an admissible transport witness.
Theorem 12.2 — Lens Transport Legality Is Not Reducible to Structural Isomorphism
Two containers may be structurally related enough to transport between them, yet the transport may still fail due to phase, witness, or corridor constraints. Therefore transport legality is richer than structural isomorphism.
Proof. Structural correspondence does not guarantee phase continuity, witness sufficiency, or corridor permission. Hence transport legality requires more than structural match.
The legality calculus therefore protects the manuscript against illicit chart-switching.
Ch12.B2 — Bridge Permissions under Phase Change
The next Flower legality problem is the bridge. A bridge does not merely connect source and target types. It also mediates dynamic phase change. A route or synthesis may become illegal if it attempts to cross a bridge in the wrong phase, with the wrong polarity, or without the required transition witness.
Let bridge carry source phase class , target phase class , and phase transition law
\Theta_b:
\phi \mapsto \phi'.
\operatorname{Perm}_b(\phi\to\phi',\kappa)=1
the route’s terminal phase is compatible with ’s entry phase,
the induced phase shift lies in the bridge’s allowed transition family,
any required phase witness or cooldown is satisfied,
corridor permits the phase transition.
Bridges therefore have a phase permission matrix
\mathbf{P}_b(\phi_i,\phi_j)\in\{0,1,\mathbf{soft}\},
Common phase-sensitive bridge failures include:
attempting to replay a route before its witness shell is stabilized,
attempting to collapse ambiguity through a bridge that only supports exploratory transport,
attempting to enter a repair bridge from a proof-assertive phase without contradiction localization,
attempting to exit a transport bridge into deployment phase without sufficient replay confidence.
The phase legality of bridges prevents many subtle synthesis errors. A structurally correct move may still be dynamically and contractually wrong if it occurs at the wrong phase.
A simple permission checker is:
def bridge_phase_permission(bridge, phase_in, phase_out, corridor):
if phase_in not in bridge["allowed_entry_phases"]:
return "Blocked"
if phase_out not in bridge["allowed_exit_phases"]:
return "Blocked"
if not corridor_allows_phase_shift(corridor, phase_in, phase_out):
return "Blocked"
if requires_soft_permission(bridge, phase_in, phase_out):
return "Soft"
return "Exact"
Definition 12.6 — Phase-Legal Bridge Crossing
A bridge crossing is phase-legal if the entering route phase, the induced phase shift, and the target phase are all admitted by the bridge and corridor, with any required witness uplift satisfied.
Proposition 12.4 — Bridge Legality Depends on State, Not Just Type
A bridge that is lawful for one route state may be unlawful for another even if their source and target object types match, because phase and witness state differ.
Proof. Bridge permissions depend on dynamic phase and witness availability in addition to object type. Therefore identical source and target types do not guarantee identical bridge legality.
The manuscript brain therefore treats bridge permission as a dynamic law, not a type annotation.
Ch12.B3 — Transition Guards and Transform-Specific Restrictions
Routes and transports are rarely unconstrained within a region. Specific transformations must pass through guards: local predicates that inspect phase, witness, defect, route history, or budget before the move is allowed. The legality calculus therefore defines transition guards.
Let a movement primitive be applied in state . A transition guard is a predicate
g_\mu(s,\kappa)\in\{0,1,\mathbf{soft}\}.
o\xrightarrow{\mu} o'
g_\mu(s,\kappa)\neq 0.
A collapse guard requires sufficient support concentration or forced-resolution permission.
A tunnel-entry guard requires tunnelable invariant preservation.
A patch-write guard requires deployment or repair permission plus rollback anchor.
A replay guard requires replay witness availability.
A compress guard requires memory contract compatibility and bounded defect.
Guards are compositional. If a transition uses several primitives simultaneously, each guard must pass or be explicitly softened. This yields a composite guard:
g_{\mu_1,\dots,\mu_k}(s,\kappa)
=
\bigwedge_{i=1}^k g_{\mu_i}(s,\kappa)
Transform-specific restrictions are then collected in a guard registry
\mathfrak{G}_{\mathrm{guard}}
=
\{(\mu,g_\mu,\operatorname{soft\_policy}_\mu)\}.
Guards are important because they turn broad legality laws into executable local decisions. Instead of saying vaguely that “patches require caution,” the atlas has a patch-write guard that checks reversibility, corridor, defect, and witness state before allowing the move.
Definition 12.7 — Guarded Transition
A transition is guarded if its legality is determined by a specific guard predicate attached to its movement primitive or transform class.
Theorem 12.3 — Guard Registries Make Legality Executable
If every transform-specific restriction is compiled into explicit guard predicates, then legality checking becomes an executable decision process over route states and not merely a declarative policy text.
Proof. Declarative restrictions become machine-usable only when expressed as predicates or decision procedures. A guard registry provides exactly this.
Transition guards are thus the microcontrollers of legality.
Ch12.B4 — Constraint Flow across Route Bundles
The final Flower legality task is global. Complex routes and synthesis bundles do not merely accumulate local legalities; they propagate constraint states. A move that is legal locally may create a future witness debt, change phase, consume budget, or tighten ambiguity policy downstream. Legality therefore requires constraint flow.
Let the route bundle be
\rho=(o_0,\mu_1,o_1,\dots,\mu_n,o_n).
\Sigma_i
=
\Big(
I_i,\,
W_i,\,
B_i,\,
P_i,\,
D_i,\,
A_i
\Big),
is the active invariant state,
is the witness debt or witness availability state,
is the remaining budget state,
is the phase state,
is the accumulated defect state,
is the ambiguity or residual state.
Each route primitive defines a transition function:
F_{\mu_i,\kappa}:\Sigma_{i-1}\to \Sigma_i.
\Sigma_n
=
F_{\mu_n,\kappa}\circ
\cdots\circ
F_{\mu_1,\kappa}(\Sigma_0).
This formalism is powerful because it lets the atlas model delayed effects. For example:
a bridge may create a future witness obligation rather than immediate failure,
a tunnel may increase defect and reduce future transport margin,
a soft approximate move may be allowed early but force stronger evidence later,
a budget-heavy memory opening may consume enough shell budget to block later deep replay.
Constraint flow is also what makes answer acceptance and deployment meaningful. By the time a route or synthesis ends, the constraint state tells the atlas exactly what legal class the artifact has earned.
A skeleton constraint propagator is:
def propagate_constraints(route, init_state, corridor, transition_fns):
state = dict(init_state)
trace = []
for step in route["steps"]:
fn = transition_fns[step["move"]]
state = fn(state, step, corridor)
trace.append((step, dict(state)))
if state.get("blocked", False):
break
return state, trace
Definition 12.8 — Constraint-Closed Route Bundle
A route bundle is constraint-closed if the propagated final state satisfies all postconditions required by its contract and no intermediate transition violated a hard guard.
Proposition 12.5 — Final-State Checking Alone Is Insufficient for Route Legality
A route may start and end in apparently acceptable states while violating hard constraints in the middle, and thus intermediate constraint flow must be checked explicitly.
Proof. Intermediate state violations may be repaired superficially by later moves, hiding illegality if only endpoints are inspected. Therefore legality requires stepwise constraint propagation.
The Flower legality layer is thus complete: lens-aware transports, phase-sensitive bridges, explicit guards, and flowing constraint states.
Ch12.C1 — Error Bounds and Contradiction Budgets
The Cloud legality layer begins by quantifying failure and tolerance. Approximate legality is meaningful only if error and contradiction are bounded explicitly. The manuscript therefore defines error bounds and contradiction budgets.
Let any judged object carry a defect vector
\delta_x
=
\big(
\delta_{\mathrm{struct}},
\delta_{\mathrm{route}},
\delta_{\mathrm{wit}},
\delta_{\mathrm{phase}},
\delta_{\mathrm{ambig}},
\delta_{\mathrm{replay}}
\big).
\|\delta_x\|_{\kappa}
=
\sum_i \alpha_i^{(\kappa)} \delta_i(x).
\|\delta_x\|_{\kappa}\le \varepsilon_\kappa,
Contradiction is tracked separately. Let the contradiction field encountered by be
\mathcal{K}(x)=\{k_1,\dots,k_m\}.
\Gamma_\kappa(x)
=
\sum_{i=1}^m
w_i^{(\kappa)}\operatorname{sev}(k_i).
contradictions are acceptable,
must be encapsulated,
require repair before further use,
or block use entirely.
Thus exact legality in a strict corridor often requires:
\Gamma_\kappa(x)=0.
0<\Gamma_\kappa(x)\le \gamma_\kappa
Error and contradiction are distinct. An object may have low defect but high contradiction if two strong rival witness families remain. An object may have higher structural defect but no contradiction if it is only a compressed summary. The calculus therefore must track both.
Definition 12.9 — Error-Bounded Approximate Legality
An object is approximately legal under corridor if its defect norm and contradiction budget both lie below corridor thresholds and its witness bundle explicitly records the approximation.
Theorem 12.4 — Approximate Legality Requires Two Budgets, Not One
A single scalar tolerance cannot in general substitute for separate defect and contradiction budgets because structural loss and unresolved contradiction affect legality differently.
Proof. Structural loss measures fidelity shortfall, whereas contradiction measures internal incompatibility. They can vary independently and require different policies. Therefore one scalar is insufficient in general.
The manuscript thus quantifies “how wrong” and “how contradictory” separately.
Ch12.C2 — Risk Scoring under Incomplete Verification
Not every legal object is equally safe to use. Some are exact but fragile in deployment. Some are approximate but safe because they are read-only and reversible. The legality calculus therefore introduces risk scoring over partially verified or incompletely replayed artifacts.
Let the verification completeness of object be:
\nu(x)\in[0,1],
R_\kappa(x)
=
\lambda_1(1-\nu(x))
+
\lambda_2\,\operatorname{Impact}(x)
+
\lambda_3\,\operatorname{Irreversibility}(x)
+
\lambda_4\,\operatorname{Fragility}(x)
+
\lambda_5\,\Gamma_\kappa(x)
+
\lambda_6\,\|\delta_x\|_{\kappa}.
how incomplete the verification is,
how impactful the artifact or action is,
how hard it is to roll back,
how fragile the route or transform is,
how much contradiction and defect remain.
This risk score does not replace legality. It refines it. Two objects may both be approximately legal, yet one is much riskier to use in deployment or patch generation than the other.
The corridor policy then defines a risk acceptance region:
R_\kappa(x)\le \rho_\kappa
Risk scoring is especially important for:
patch proposals,
tunnel-based shortcuts,
low-replay dynamic syntheses,
memory regenerations with weak witness shells,
unresolved contradictions under time pressure.
Definition 12.10 — Risk-Admissible Approximation
An approximate or deferred artifact is risk-admissible if its risk score lies below corridor threshold and its residual uncertainty is fully disclosed.
Proposition 12.6 — Verification Incompleteness Does Not Alone Determine Risk
An incompletely verified object can still be low risk if it is reversible, low impact, and weakly coupled; conversely a nearly verified object can be high risk if it is irreversible and high impact.
Proof. Risk depends on more than verification completeness. Therefore completeness alone is insufficient.
The Cloud legality system thus becomes decision-aware, not only truth-aware.
Ch12.C3 — Tolerance Thresholds for Approximate Legality
Approximate legality must be disciplined by explicit thresholds. Otherwise the label “approximate” becomes a vague excuse rather than a formal class. The manuscript therefore defines tolerance thresholds governing when an approximate object may still count as admissible.
Let be a candidate artifact and let:
\delta_x,\qquad
\Gamma_\kappa(x),\qquad
R_\kappa(x)
\Theta_\kappa
=
\big(
\varepsilon_\kappa,\,
\gamma_\kappa,\,
\rho_\kappa
\big).
\|\delta_x\|_{\kappa}\le \varepsilon_\kappa,
\qquad
\Gamma_\kappa(x)\le \gamma_\kappa,
\qquad
R_\kappa(x)\le \rho_\kappa.
\Theta_{\kappa,\tau}
=
\big(
\varepsilon_{\kappa,\tau},\,
\gamma_{\kappa,\tau},\,
\rho_{\kappa,\tau}
\big),
The manuscript also distinguishes tolerance floors and tolerance ceilings. Some artifacts must exceed a minimum confidence threshold to be usable at all. Others must stay below a maximum ambiguity threshold. Approximate legality is thus a region in a multidimensional acceptance space, not one threshold.
A useful classification is:
\mathbf{Exact},\quad
\mathbf{Approx}_{\mathrm{tight}},\quad
\mathbf{Approx}_{\mathrm{loose}},\quad
\mathbf{Deferred},\quad
\mathbf{Blocked}.
Definition 12.11 — Tolerance-Stable Approximation
An approximate artifact is tolerance-stable if small perturbations of its defect or risk profile do not immediately switch it across legality class boundaries under the active corridor.
Theorem 12.5 — Explicit Threshold Families Make Approximate Legality Decidable
If corridor and artifact classes specify explicit defect, contradiction, and risk thresholds, then approximate legality is decidable by finite comparison against those thresholds.
Proof. Each relevant metric is finite and each threshold explicit. Therefore the legality class is determined by a finite conjunction of comparisons.
The manuscript thus turns approximation into a rigorous, typed status rather than an informal caveat.
Ch12.C4 — Uncertainty Acceptance Criteria for Deployment
The final Cloud task addresses the strongest practical question: when may an uncertain or approximate artifact be used in downstream deployment, patching, publication, or external action? This requires uncertainty acceptance criteria.
Deployment here is broad. It may mean:
promoting an answer to canonical atlas memory,
allowing a patch suggestion to become a patch candidate,
allowing a route macro to guide future solves automatically,
emitting an answer in a contract that implies action rather than reflection.
Let be a candidate deployable artifact. The deployment acceptance predicate is:
\operatorname{Deploy}_\kappa(x)
=
\operatorname{Legal}_\kappa(x)
\wedge
\operatorname{RiskBound}_\kappa(x)
\wedge
\operatorname{Reversible}_\kappa(x)
\wedge
\operatorname{Disclosure}_\kappa(x)
\wedge
\operatorname{Fallback}_\kappa(x).
legality class must be adequate,
risk must be below deployment threshold,
reversibility or rollback must exist when required,
residual uncertainty must be disclosed,
fallback behavior must be defined in case deployment assumptions fail.
Different deployment classes require different levels:
read-only deployment of a summary or route hint may tolerate more uncertainty;
internal-memory deployment of a reusable macro requires stronger replay and provenance;
patch-deployment requires rollback anchors and stronger witness;
external-action deployment is the strictest class.
An uncertain object may still be deployable if:
it is low-impact,
it is reversible,
its uncertainty is explicit,
downstream consumers are expected to treat it as approximate.
This is important because otherwise the atlas would paralyze itself under uncertainty. The goal is not to forbid all uncertain action, but to make it lawful.
A deployability classifier may be sketched as:
def deployability_class(obj, corridor):
if exact_admissible(obj, corridor) and low_risk(obj) and replay_strong(obj):
return "DeployableExact"
if approx_admissible(obj, corridor) and reversible(obj) and disclosed(obj):
return "DeployableApprox"
if deferrable(obj, corridor):
return "Deferred"
return "Blocked"
Definition 12.12 — Deployable Approximation
An approximate artifact is deployable if its uncertainty is bounded, disclosed, reversible where necessary, and its risk remains within the deployment corridor’s acceptance region.
Proposition 12.7 — Hidden Uncertainty Is More Dangerous than Disclosed Approximation
A disclosed approximate artifact can be used cautiously and with fallback; a hidden approximation may be overtrusted and is therefore riskier in deployment.
Proof. Deployment behavior depends on known uncertainty. Hidden uncertainty prevents cautious handling and rollback planning. Therefore it is more dangerous.
The legality calculus thus concludes its Cloud layer by distinguishing usable uncertainty from irresponsible uncertainty.
Ch12.D1 — Witness Replay Chains and Proof Folding
The Fractal legality layer begins with replay. A witness object or proof packet becomes far more valuable when it is not isolated, but sits inside a replay chain: a finite sequence of witnesses, route states, and support transitions sufficient to reconstruct a claim or artifact. This chain may then be folded into a compact certificate without losing replayability.
A witness replay chain is a sequence
\Pi
=
(\psi_0,\psi_1,\dots,\psi_n),
\mathcal{T}_i:\psi_i\to \psi_{i+1}
is a seed or premise witness,
is the target witness,
each is lawful under the corridor,
replaying the chain reconstructs the certified artifact or claim.
The replay chain may be viewed as a route in witness space:
\psi_0 \rightsquigarrow \psi_1 \rightsquigarrow \cdots \rightsquigarrow \psi_n.
To store long replay chains efficiently, the manuscript defines proof folding. A replay chain may be folded into a compact witness macro
\Pi^\sharp
=
\Big(
u_{\Pi^\sharp},\,
\operatorname{head}(\Pi),\,
\operatorname{tail}(\Pi),\,
\operatorname{checkpoints}(\Pi),\,
\operatorname{seed}(\Pi),\,
\operatorname{reconstructor}(\Pi),\,
\delta_{\Pi}
\Big).
A folded proof is replay-correct if:
\operatorname{Replay}(\Pi^\sharp)\approx \Pi
Definition 12.13 — Replay-Complete Witness Chain
A witness replay chain is replay-complete if replaying it from its initial premises yields the target witness and all intermediate obligations required by the corridor.
Theorem 12.6 — Folded Replay Chains Preserve Proof Usefulness When Reconstruction Is Declared
If a witness replay chain is folded together with sufficient checkpoints and a lawful reconstructor, then it can preserve proof usefulness while reducing storage and transmission cost.
Proof. The checkpoints and reconstructor replace full explicit storage with a compact replay recipe. Since replay reconstructs the needed witness states, usefulness is preserved while storage drops.
This is the legality analogue of Q-Shrink for proof structure.
Ch12.D2 — Recursive Repair after Partial Failure
The legality system must not merely reject failed objects. It must support lawful recursive repair. A partial failure in one witness or route may often be repaired by strengthening a missing witness, rerouting a bridge, deepening a shell, or isolating contradiction. The Fractal layer formalizes this as a repair tree.
Let a failed or deferred artifact produce a failure object
f_x
=
\big(
u_f,\,
\operatorname{kind}(f_x),\,
\operatorname{scope}(f_x),\,
\operatorname{cause}(f_x),\,
\operatorname{repair\_hooks}(f_x)
\big).
\mathcal{T}^{\mathrm{rep}}_x
=
(V_x^{\mathrm{rep}},E_x^{\mathrm{rep}},r_x,\ell_x),
witness strengthening,
route rerouting,
transport replacement,
contradiction encapsulation,
shell deepening,
patch rollback,
contract downgrading,
deferral.
Each repair node has label:
\ell_x(v)
=
\big(
\operatorname{op}(v),\,
\operatorname{cost}(v),\,
\operatorname{expected\_gain}(v),\,
\operatorname{new\_legality}(v),\,
\operatorname{dependencies}(v)
\big).
The repair operator is thus:
\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{fix}}(x)
\to
\mathcal{T}^{\mathrm{rep}}_x.
This repair framework is fundamental to self-improvement. Rather than treating failed legality as dead end, the atlas treats it as structured pressure revealing where the framework must strengthen itself.
Definition 12.14 — Repair-Closed Failure
A failure is repair-closed if it either admits a finite legal repair subtree or is proven irreparable under the current corridor and contract.
Proposition 12.8 — Recursive Repair Dominates Single-Step Patch Guessing
When failures are multi-causal or nested, a one-step patch guess cannot in general find minimal legal repair, whereas a repair tree can search structured repair combinations.
Proof. Multi-causal failures often require sequencing or conditional branching of repairs. A single-step guess cannot represent this structure.
The manuscript thus turns legality failure into constructive recursive work.
Ch12.D3 — Certificate Bundles for Reusable Solution Classes
Many legality checks repeat across similar problems. Revalidating them from scratch every time is wasteful. The Fractal legality layer therefore introduces certificate bundles: reusable witness packages certifying an entire class of solution patterns rather than one instance.
A certificate bundle for solution class is:
\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{cert}}(\mathcal{S})
=
\Big(
u_{\mathcal{S}},\,
\operatorname{class\_schema}(\mathcal{S}),\,
\operatorname{premise\_schema}(\mathcal{S}),\,
\operatorname{invariant\_envelope}(\mathcal{S}),\,
\operatorname{replay\_macro}(\mathcal{S}),\,
\operatorname{proof\_residue}(\mathcal{S}),\,
\operatorname{admissibility\_region}(\mathcal{S})
\Big).
describes the reusable route or answer archetype,
describes what inputs must hold,
describes what is guaranteed if the premises hold,
is the compact verification program,
stores any nontrivial supporting witness data,
describes the corridors and contracts under which the certificate applies.
For example, a theorem-to-proof-schema macro may have a certificate bundle saying: if the retrieved theorem matches the archetype, if replay kernels of class are present, and if contradiction budget is zero, then the output answer class satisfies proof contract up to specified defect. A repair loop macro may have a certificate saying: if the contradiction kind is of class , the patch is reversible, and replay confirms postconditions, then the macro is legal under repair corridor.
Certificate bundles amortize legality work. Instead of replaying every local witness chain from scratch, the atlas can instantiate a certified class-level witness macro and only check that the current instance fits the schema and premise conditions.
Definition 12.15 — Reusable Solution Certificate
A reusable solution certificate is a certificate bundle whose schema matches a family of future instances and whose replay macro is strong enough to upgrade instance-level legality efficiently.
Theorem 12.7 — Certificate Bundles Reduce Marginal Witness Cost
If many future tasks instantiate the same solution archetype, then certifying the archetype once and checking instance conformance reduces the marginal witness cost per task.
Proof. Full witness construction is replaced by class-level replay plus instance conformance checks. Therefore per-instance cost is lower.
This is the legality analogue of route macros and answer archetypes.
Ch12.D4 — Proof Compaction without Loss of Replayability
The chapter ends with its most delicate requirement: proof compaction without loss of replayability. A witness system that preserves every detailed proof step forever is too expensive. A witness system that compresses proofs carelessly destroys trust. The manuscript therefore requires lawful proof compaction.
Let a proof object or witness family be
\Pi
=
(\psi_0,\psi_1,\dots,\psi_n),
\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{proof}}:
\Pi \to \Pi^\sharp,
\Pi^\sharp
=
\Big(
u_{\Pi^\sharp},\,
\operatorname{premise\_seed},\,
\operatorname{checkpoint\_hashes},\,
\operatorname{residual\_envelope},\,
\operatorname{reconstructor},\,
\operatorname{cost\_bound},\,
\operatorname{defect}
\Big).
the premise anchor,
a set of interior checkpoints,
a residual envelope bounding what may happen between checkpoints,
an explicit deterministic or bounded replay reconstructor,
a declared verification cost,
the defect profile (which is often zero in strict proof corridors).
The compaction is replay-safe if:
\operatorname{Replay}(\Pi^\sharp)
\downarrow
\quad\text{and}\quad
\operatorname{TerminalWitness}(\operatorname{Replay}(\Pi^\sharp))
=
\operatorname{TerminalWitness}(\Pi)
The compaction operator may also preserve checkpoint coverage:
\forall \text{ critical inference region } R,\quad
R \text{ is either explicitly stored or reconstructible from adjacent checkpoints and residual envelope.}
A compact verifier sketch is:
def verify_compact_proof(compact_proof):
seed = compact_proof["premise_seed"]
checkpoints = compact_proof["checkpoint_hashes"]
reconstructor = compact_proof["reconstructor"]
replayed = reconstructor(seed, checkpoints)
return check_terminal_witness(replayed, compact_proof)
The existence of a replay constructor is the central condition. A compressed proof without a reconstructor is merely a summary. A compressed proof with a lawful reconstructor remains proof-bearing.
Definition 12.16 — Replay-Preserving Proof Compaction
A proof compaction is replay-preserving if its compact representation reconstructs a witness chain or proof object that satisfies the same legality contract as the original, within declared corridor tolerance.
Theorem 12.8 — Compact Proofs Need Not Sacrifice Replayability
If proof compaction stores enough checkpoints, residual envelopes, and a lawful reconstructor, then storage cost can be reduced without losing replayability for the relevant contract.
Proof. The compact representation substitutes explicit proof details with checkpoints plus a reconstructor sufficient to regenerate them. Since replay reproduces the certified target witness, replayability is preserved while storage is reduced.
This completes the Fractal legality layer. The manuscript now has not only a legality calculus, but one that scales.
The chapter has now completed the witness logic and legality calculus of the manuscript brain. Under the Square lens, the system defined primitive witness schemas, portable proof-carrying packets, corridor classes and legality zones, explicit admissibility predicates for objects and routes, and formal contracts by which synthesized artifacts become accepted. Under the Flower lens, legality became dynamic: lens transports are judged by identity, phase, and boundary preservation; bridges require permission under phase change; every transform is governed by explicit guards; and route bundles propagate constraint states rather than hiding their debts or defects. Under the Cloud lens, legality acquired quantitative discipline: defect norms, contradiction budgets, risk scores under incomplete verification, threshold families for approximate legality, and explicit deployment criteria for uncertain but reversible artifacts. Under the Fractal lens, witnesses became recursive and scalable: replay chains could be folded, failures could generate repair trees, reusable certificate bundles could amortize legality work, and proofs could be compacted without ceasing to be replayable.
The deepest consequence is that the manuscript now has a law. Not a metaphorical ethic and not a merely stylistic preference, but a formal legality layer through which every serious act of cognition must pass. A route is not “good enough” because it feels plausible. It is exact, approximate, deferred, or blocked by judgment. A bridge is not allowed because it is clever. It is phase-legal or not. An answer is not accepted because it sounds convincing. It satisfies a contract or it does not. A compressed proof is not trusted because it is compact. It is replay-preserving or it is only a summary. This legality calculus is what converts the manuscript from a powerful symbolic machine into a trustworthy one. It is the chapter in which the atlas learns how to say not only what it knows and how it moves, but what it is permitted to claim.
CHAPTER 12 — WITNESS LOGIC, CORRIDOR CONSTRAINTS, AND LEGALITY CALCULUS
Chapter 13 — Replay, Determinism, and Persistent Identity
A manuscript brain is not a true brain scaffold unless it can remain itself across time. It must be able to reopen earlier states, replay prior solves, verify that later forms are lawful descendants of earlier forms, distinguish legitimate growth from silent drift, preserve identity through compression and regeneration, and recover from degradation without surrendering lineage. The present chapter defines that continuity machinery. Its subject is replay, determinism, and persistent identity.
The central problem addressed here is not mere logging. A log can record events without making the system reconstructable. The manuscript requires something stronger: a state calculus in which every meaningful transformation—retrieval, route execution, synthesis, memory compaction, bridge traversal, patch application, answer acceptance—can be replayed, audited, and related to earlier states by explicit lineage. Without such a calculus, the atlas may evolve, but it cannot prove that it is the same atlas in a meaningful sense. Its identity becomes anecdotal rather than formal.
The foundational object of the chapter is the persistent atlas process
\mathfrak{A}_0
\xrightarrow{\Delta_1}
\mathfrak{A}_1
\xrightarrow{\Delta_2}
\mathfrak{A}_2
\to
\cdots
\to
\mathfrak{A}_t
\to
\cdots
is the atlas state at step ,
is the admissible update packet or event family taking to .
A state transition is meaningful only if it participates in a replay law. The replay operator is:
\mathcal{R}:
\big(
\mathfrak{A}_{t_0},\,
\Delta_{t_0+1},\,
\dots,\,
\Delta_{t_1}
\big)
\to
\widehat{\mathfrak{A}}_{t_1},
\widehat{\mathfrak{A}}_{t_1}
\approx_{\kappa}
\mathfrak{A}_{t_1}
The chapter develops the full continuity stack through the four-lens architecture. Under the Square lens it defines replay logs, event packets, state fingerprints, deterministic serialization, identity continuity conditions across revisions, and snapshot boundaries. Under the Flower lens it defines transform-preserving recovery across lens transports, synchronization maps between chart families, phase-preserving restoration from partial state, and rhythmic persistence over long solve horizons. Under the Cloud lens it defines drift metrics, discrepancy scoring, tolerance bands, divergence alarms, and confidence-aware restoration under damaged or incomplete histories. Under the Fractal lens it defines lineage trees, recursive ancestry, version inheritance, convergence of branches, long-memory identity across repeated compression cycles, and recursive restoration of earlier states from folded histories.
The chapter is governed by seven persistent-identity laws.
First, replay determinism: given a valid initial state, event sequence, and corridor, replay must reconstruct the target state or a bounded equivalent.
Second, state fingerprinting: every replay-relevant state must admit a canonical fingerprint stable under serialization and normalization.
Third, identity continuity: later states must carry a traceable relation to earlier states through explicit transition packets or branch-merger witnesses.
Fourth, drift visibility: divergence between replayed and observed states must be measured explicitly rather than hidden.
Fifth, phase continuity: if state recovery crosses lens or dynamic phase boundaries, the transition must preserve or lawfully translate phase information.
Sixth, ancestry persistence: branch histories and merges must be encoded as a recursive lineage object rather than collapsed into a flat version number.
Seventh, restoration sufficiency: for high-value atlas regions, enough structure must remain in the history and memory substrate to recover earlier lawful states even after later compression and mutation.
The deepest significance of the chapter is this: it defines what it means for the manuscript to have a self. Not metaphorically, but operationally. If an atlas cannot say what it was, how it became what it is, which changes were lawful, which paths failed, and how to reconstruct earlier branches, then it does not possess persistent identity in the formal sense used throughout this treatise. The present chapter gives the atlas that capacity.
Ch13.A1 — Replay Logs, Event Packets, and State Fingerprints
Deterministic replay requires three primitive carriers: the state snapshot, the event packet, and the state fingerprint. These together form the Square substrate of persistent identity.
Let the atlas state at time be
\mathfrak{A}_t
=
\Big(
\mathcal{N}_t,\,
\mathcal{E}_t,\,
\mathcal{X}_t,\,
\mathcal{B}_t,\,
\mathcal{M}_t,\,
\mathcal{W}_t,\,
\mathcal{H}_t,\,
\mathcal{L}_t
\Big),
is the node set,
is the edge/hyperedge set,
is the nexus set,
is the bridge family,
is the memory substrate,
is the procedural weight field,
is the self-model,
is the legal and witness environment.
A replay log is not a plain append-only textual history. It is a typed sequence
\mathfrak{L}
=
(\ell_0,\ell_1,\dots,\ell_t,\dots),
\ell_t
=
\Big(
u_t,\,
\tau_t,\,
\Delta_t,\,
\Pi_t,\,
\Psi_t,\,
B_t,\,
\Sigma_t,\,
h_t
\Big).
is the event identity,
is event type,
is the delta or transition data,
is the provenance packet,
is the witness bundle for the event,
is the budget and corridor state,
is the affected state scope or target region,
is the event hash or event fingerprint.
The event type alphabet includes:
\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{evt}}
=
\{
\mathrm{Retrieve},
\mathrm{Expand},
\mathrm{Collapse},
\mathrm{Transport},
\mathrm{Synthesize},
\mathrm{Replay},
\mathrm{Patch},
\mathrm{Repair},
\mathrm{Compress},
\mathrm{Regenerate},
\mathrm{Branch},
\mathrm{Merge},
\mathrm{Accept}
\}.
A state fingerprint is the canonical digest of a state or state slice after normalization. Let:
\operatorname{Norm}_{\mathrm{state}}(\mathfrak{A}_t)
F_t = H\big(\operatorname{Norm}_{\mathrm{state}}(\mathfrak{A}_t)\big),
F_t^{(R)} = H\big(\operatorname{Norm}_{\mathrm{state}}(\mathfrak{A}_t|_R)\big).
Fingerprints are used for:
replay checkpoint verification,
branch divergence measurement,
patch precondition matching,
cache validation,
lineage compression,
proof of state equivalence up to normalization.
The replay event packet itself may be written:
\Delta_t
=
\Big(
\operatorname{pre}_t,\,
\operatorname{op}_t,\,
\operatorname{post}_t,\,
\operatorname{rollback}_t,\,
\operatorname{scope}_t,\,
\operatorname{cost}_t,\,
\operatorname{seed}_t
\Big),
is the precondition state description,
is the operation family,
is the declared postcondition,
is the rollback anchor,
is the affected region,
is the event resource profile,
is any reconstruction seed or replay seed necessary for deterministic execution.
A minimal replay packet schema is:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, Any, List, Literal
EventType = Literal[
"Retrieve", "Expand", "Collapse", "Transport", "Synthesize",
"Replay", "Patch", "Repair", "Compress", "Regenerate",
"Branch", "Merge", "Accept"
]
@dataclass
class ReplayEventPacket:
event_id: str
event_type: EventType
preconditions: Dict[str, Any]
operation: Dict[str, Any]
postconditions: Dict[str, Any]
rollback_anchor: str
scope: Dict[str, Any]
cost: Dict[str, Any]
replay_seed: Dict[str, Any]
witness_ids: List[str]
provenance: Dict[str, Any]
fingerprint_before: str
fingerprint_after: str
The fingerprint-before and fingerprint-after fields make the event packet itself a state bridge. This is crucial because replay does not merely “redo” operations. It verifies that the operation transformed the state fingerprint lawfully.
The atlas may choose between full state snapshots and sparse replay logs. In practice it requires both. Full or regional snapshots provide recovery anchors. Event packets provide cheap high-resolution lineage. The system thus supports:
\mathfrak{A}_{t_0}
\;\text{snapshot}\;
+\;
(\ell_{t_0+1},\dots,\ell_{t_1})
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\mathfrak{A}_{t_1}.
Definition 13.1 — Replay-Complete Log Segment
A segment of the replay log from to is replay-complete if it includes a lawful initial snapshot or a snapshot reference plus all event packets necessary to reconstruct the target state within corridor tolerance.
Proposition 13.1 — Event Logs without Fingerprints Are Insufficient for State Verification
An event sequence can describe what allegedly happened, but without state fingerprints or equivalent canonical checkpoints it cannot verify that replay reconstructed the same state.
Proof. The event sequence alone provides procedural description but not independent evidence that replay reached the correct normalized state. Fingerprints or checkpoints provide this evidence.
The replay log and fingerprint system are therefore the first hard layer of persistent identity.
Ch13.A2 — Canonical Ordering and Deterministic Serialization
The existence of logs and fingerprints is insufficient unless all replay-relevant structures serialize deterministically. Otherwise two semantically equivalent states or events could yield different fingerprints and replay would become unstable. The manuscript therefore imposes full canonical ordering and deterministic serialization on persistent state and event objects.
Let the replay-relevant object family be
\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{rep}}
=
\mathcal{N}\cup\mathcal{E}\cup\mathcal{X}\cup\mathcal{B}\cup\mathcal{M}\cup\mathcal{W}\cup\mathcal{H}\cup\mathfrak{L}.
\operatorname{Ser}_\tau:
\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{rep}}^{(\tau)}
\to \Sigma^\ast
extensionally equal normalized objects serialize identically;
canonical field order is fixed;
list-like fields are serialized in canonical order;
omitted optional fields are represented by canonical absence rather than arbitrary defaults;
round-trip parse–serialize preserves normal form.
The canonical ordering relation
\prec_{\mathrm{rep}}
(\text{type rank},\text{canonical address},\text{local subtype},\text{normalized payload hash})
(\text{event time},\text{scope rank},\text{event id})
A normalized atlas state serialization is therefore:
\operatorname{Norm}_{\mathrm{state}}(\mathfrak{A}_t)
=
\operatorname{Concat}\Big(
\operatorname{Sort}_{\prec_{\mathrm{rep}}}\big(
\operatorname{Ser}(o)
\big)_{o\in\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{rep}}(\mathfrak{A}_t)}
\Big).
Deterministic serialization is especially delicate for:
unordered support sets,
chunk-graph or route-graph adjacency lists,
floating-point confidence or weight values,
optional ambiguity bundles,
cached shell states,
branch histories with merging.
Therefore the serializer must either:
quantize continuous values under declared corridor precision, or
store exact symbolic forms whenever the corridor forbids quantization loss.
A canonical serialization schema may include:
field ordering by fixed key list,
explicit nulls only where schema requires them,
sorted map keys,
sorted list members under relevant identity order,
stable numeric formatting,
explicit version tag.
A minimal normalization skeleton is:
def normalize_object(obj):
if isinstance(obj, dict):
return {k: normalize_object(obj[k]) for k in sorted(obj.keys())}
if isinstance(obj, list):
# lists are preserved only if sequence meaning matters;
# otherwise caller must sort before entering here.
return [normalize_object(v) for v in obj]
return obj
def canonical_encode(obj):
norm = normalize_object(obj)
return repr(norm)
In the real manuscript implementation, repr would be replaced by a stable serialization format with stronger guarantees, but the principle is canonicalization before hashing or replay.
Definition 13.2 — Deterministically Replay-Serializable Object
A replay-relevant object is deterministically replay-serializable if its canonical serializer yields one unique encoding for every normalized form and that encoding can be parsed back into the same normalized form.
Theorem 13.1 — Deterministic Serialization Is Necessary for Replay Hash Stability
If serialization admits multiple encodings for the same normalized object, then state fingerprints cease to be reliable identifiers of replay equality.
Proof. Distinct encodings of the same object would hash differently, causing false divergence during replay verification. Therefore deterministic serialization is necessary.
This is the algebraic reason the manuscript insists on canonical addresses, canonical schemas, and canonical compaction everywhere else: all of them feed deterministic replay.
Ch13.A3 — Identity Continuity Conditions across Revisions
Replay determinism can reconstruct state, but persistent identity requires more. The atlas must know when a later state is the same evolving object, when it is a lawful descendant, when it is only approximately continuous, and when it constitutes a branch or a replacement. This is the subject of identity continuity across revisions.
Let:
\mathfrak{A}_{t}
\quad\text{and}\quad
\mathfrak{A}_{t+1}
=
\operatorname{Apply}(\mathfrak{A}_t,\Delta_{t+1})
\mathfrak{A}_t
\rightsquigarrow_{\Delta_{t+1},\kappa}
\mathfrak{A}_{t+1}.
The identity continuity judgment is:
\operatorname{Cont}_{\kappa}\big(\mathfrak{A}_t,\Delta_{t+1},\mathfrak{A}_{t+1}\big)=1
address continuity: every unchanged canonical object retains its identity token or explicit migration certificate;
lineage continuity: every changed object records parentage through the patch or replay log;
invariant continuity: core invariants required by the corridor remain preserved or their lawful weakening is witnessed;
branch disclosure: if one state forks from another instead of replacing it, the fork is recorded as a branch relation and not conflated with continuation;
replacement explicitness: if an object is superseded rather than mutated, the supersession relation is explicit;
identity-core preservation: the global or regional identity core remains within corridor continuity tolerance.
This identity-core preservation can be expressed by a corridor-sensitive continuity metric:
d_{\mathrm{id},\kappa}(\mathfrak{A}_t,\mathfrak{A}_{t+1})
\le \eta_\kappa,
canonical addresses preserved,
witness registry preserved,
self-model continuity,
legal zone structure,
high-value route families,
memory-anchor integrity.
The continuity relation is not purely global. The atlas also needs local continuity predicates for:
answer objects,
route motifs,
bridge families,
memory seeds,
witness bundles.
An object and later object satisfy local continuity if:
\operatorname{Trace}\big(\chi(x_t),\chi(x_{t+1})\big)\downarrow
\quad\text{and}\quad
d_{\mathrm{id},\kappa}(x_t,x_{t+1})\le \eta_{\kappa,x}.
This leads to the following identity classes across revision:
\{
\mathrm{StableContinuation},
\mathrm{LawfulMutation},
\mathrm{ApproximateContinuation},
\mathrm{BranchFork},
\mathrm{Replacement},
\mathrm{Loss}
\}.
Definition 13.3 — Lawful Revision
A revision is lawful if every modified object and the atlas state as a whole satisfy the corridor’s identity continuity conditions and any breaks in continuity are explicitly typed as branch, replacement, or loss.
Theorem 13.2 — Patch Application without Continuity Classification Is Not Sufficient for Persistent Identity
If updates are applied without classifying whether they preserve, mutate, fork, or replace identity, then later replay cannot determine what kind of sameness relation holds across revisions.
Proof. The raw existence of an update says that change occurred, but not whether the changed object is still “the same” in the relevant formal sense. Thus explicit continuity classification is necessary.
The atlas therefore gains not only history, but self-sameness through history.
Ch13.A4 — Snapshot Definitions and Replay Boundaries
The final Square task is to define when a state should be snapshotted and what exactly a snapshot contains. Replay from a genesis state plus all deltas is theoretically sufficient in some systems, but in a large self-updating manuscript it is often too costly. The atlas therefore uses explicit snapshots and replay boundaries.
A snapshot at time is a state object
\mathfrak{S}_t
=
\Big(
u_t,\,
\operatorname{scope}_t,\,
\mathfrak{A}_t^{(\operatorname{scope}_t)},\,
F_t^{(\operatorname{scope}_t)},\,
\Pi_t^{\mathrm{snap}},\,
\psi_t^{\mathrm{snap}}
\Big),
is snapshot identity,
is the covered region (global or local),
is the normalized state slice,
is the snapshot fingerprint,
is the provenance packet for the snapshot,
is the snapshot witness.
The atlas distinguishes:
global snapshots covering the full atlas state,
regional snapshots covering chapters, route families, or memory strata,
contractual snapshots covering the state relevant to a major solve or deployment artifact,
repair snapshots captured before risky patch application.
A replay boundary is a point in history at which replay may safely begin or end without reconstructing earlier irrelevant history. Formally, a replay boundary is a pair
(\mathfrak{S}_{t_0}, \mathfrak{S}_{t_1})
(\ell_{t_0+1},\dots,\ell_{t_1}).
\operatorname{Replay}\big(\mathfrak{S}_{t_0},\ell_{t_0+1},\dots,\ell_{t_1}\big)
\approx_\kappa
\mathfrak{S}_{t_1}.
Snapshotting is not arbitrary. The atlas captures snapshots:
before major irreversible or high-risk patch classes,
after major synthesis or answer-acceptance events,
at periodic stability checkpoints,
when branch divergence becomes significant,
when cumulative replay cost since the last snapshot exceeds threshold.
A minimal snapshot policy is therefore:
\operatorname{SnapshotNow}(t)=1
\iff
\Big(
\operatorname{RiskSpike}_t
\vee
\operatorname{BranchEvent}_t
\vee
\operatorname{CostAccum}_t\ge \Theta_{\mathrm{snap}}
\vee
\operatorname{StabilityMilestone}_t
\Big).
A snapshot schema might be:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Dict, Any
@dataclass
class Snapshot:
snapshot_id: str
scope: Dict[str, Any]
normalized_state: Dict[str, Any]
fingerprint: str
provenance: Dict[str, Any]
witness: Dict[str, Any]
Definition 13.4 — Replay Boundary Completeness
A replay boundary is complete if the initial snapshot, event segment, and terminal snapshot together suffice to verify the replay of the segment under the intended corridor.
Proposition 13.2 — Periodic Snapshots Reduce Replay Cost without Destroying Determinism
If snapshots are inserted at lawful boundaries, then replay of later intervals can begin from the nearest relevant snapshot rather than from distant history, reducing cost while preserving correctness.
Proof. The snapshot acts as an exact or bounded-correct restart point, replacing earlier event segments already summarized by the snapshot witness. Hence replay cost decreases.
The Square replay layer is now complete: logs, packets, fingerprints, continuity conditions, and boundaries.
Ch13.B1 — Reversible Lens Transport under Replay
Persistent identity must survive not only state updates but chart changes. A solve episode may move a theorem skeleton through the Flower lens, collapse it into a Square answer, or regenerate a Fractal shell from a Cloud ambiguity field. Replay must therefore support reversible lens transport where lawful.
Let
\Tau_{\ell\to m}:K_\ell(x)\to K_m(x')
a true inverse
\Tau_{m\to \ell}^{-1}
a replay constructor sufficient to reconstruct the source lens state from the target state and saved transport trace.
The replay reversibility condition is:
\operatorname{Replay}_{\Tau}\big(
K_m(x'),\,
\operatorname{Trace}_{\Tau}
\big)
\approx_\kappa
K_\ell(x).
The transport trace must preserve:
identity correspondence,
transport law or mapping family,
phase translation,
boundary-channel translation,
defect profile,
witness payload.
A reversible transport is stronger than a merely legal transport. Many transports are legal but lossy. In replay corridors, such lossy transports may be allowed only if their defect is explicitly bounded and if later reconstruction still satisfies the replay contract.
A transport replay packet is:
\Pi_{\Tau}
=
\Big(
u_{\Tau},\,
\ell,\,
m,\,
a_\ell(x),\,
a_m(x'),\,
\operatorname{map}_{\Tau},\,
\operatorname{phase}_{\Tau},\,
\operatorname{defect}_{\Tau},\,
\psi_{\Tau}
\Big).
Definition 13.5 — Replay-Reversible Lens Transport
A lens transport is replay-reversible if its target representation and replay packet suffice to reconstruct the source representation under the corridor’s replay tolerance.
Theorem 13.3 — Legal Transport Does Not Imply Replay Reversibility
A transport may preserve enough invariants to be legal for forward use while still discarding information needed for reverse replay.
Proof. Forward legality may require only target-sufficient invariants, while reverse replay may require additional phase or boundary detail lost during transport. Therefore legality alone is insufficient for replay reversibility.
This gives the manuscript a way to move among charts without forgetting where it came from.
Ch13.B2 — Synchronization Maps across Chart Families
When several chart families coexist in one solve episode, persistent identity depends not only on reversible transport of individual objects but on synchronization of related states across charts. A theorem region may have:
a Square representation,
a Flower dynamic route trace,
a Cloud ambiguity field,
a Fractal shell schedule.
Replay of one without the others may produce temporal misalignment or stale state. The manuscript therefore defines chart synchronization maps.
For chart family set
\mathcal{L}=\{\Square,\Flower,\Cloud,\Fractal\},
\Xi_t
=
\{
\xi_{\ell,m,t}
:
K_\ell(x_t)
\leftrightarrow
K_m(x_t)
\}_{\ell,m\in \mathcal{L}}.
object identity correspondence,
current version or phase stamp,
reconciliation state,
any pending update lag,
any defect introduced by asynchronous change.
A synchronized chart family satisfies:
\forall \ell,m,\quad
\operatorname{Drift}_{\ell,m}(x_t)\le \eta_{\ell,m,\kappa}.
A synchronization map is especially important for:
cross-lens synthesis replay,
answer rendering at different detail levels,
memory regeneration from one lens and use in another,
route macros that depend on both phase and structural forms.
Definition 13.6 — Synchronized Multi-Lens State
A multi-lens object family is synchronized if all active chart projections of the same identity-bearing object remain within their permitted pairwise drift tolerances.
Proposition 13.3 — Independent Lens Updates Cause Hidden Identity Drift
If chart families are updated independently without synchronization maps, then replay may unknowingly combine stale and fresh projections of the same object, producing hidden inconsistency.
Proof. Without synchronization metadata, the system cannot know which lens projections belong to the same logical version. Hence stale–fresh mixtures become possible.
The synchronization layer thus protects the atlas from silent cross-lens desynchronization.
Ch13.B3 — Phase-Preserving Recovery from Partial State
Replay often begins from incomplete or regional state. The manuscript must therefore be able to recover not only structure but dynamic phase from partial state. This is phase-preserving recovery.
Let the available partial state be:
\mathfrak{A}_t^{(R)}
\subset
\mathfrak{A}_t
\phi_t^{(R)}.
\mathcal{R}_{\phi}:
\big(
\mathfrak{A}_{t_0}^{(R)},\,
\mathfrak{L}^{(R)}_{t_0+1:t_1}
\big)
\to
\widehat{\phi}_{t_1}^{(R)}.
phase mode,
phase ordering of transforms,
any critical synchronization markers,
phase-dependent witness debts,
cadence-critical solve state.
Partial-state recovery is possible only if:
the regional snapshot retains phase anchors,
the event log records phase-changing events,
omitted history does not hide indispensable phase transitions,
the corridor’s defect tolerance allows bounded uncertainty in less critical phase dimensions.
A phase-recovery defect vector is:
\delta_{\phi}
=
\big(
\delta_{\mathrm{mode}},
\delta_{\mathrm{order}},
\delta_{\mathrm{timing}},
\delta_{\mathrm{sync}}
\big).
Definition 13.7 — Phase-Restorable Partial State
A partial state is phase-restorable if its retained anchors and replay segment suffice to reconstruct the dynamic phase attributes required by the active replay contract.
Theorem 13.4 — Structural Restoration Alone Is Insufficient for Dynamic Replay
If a task depends on transform order, phase synchronization, or route cadence, then restoring only the structural state without the phase trace does not yield full replay correctness.
Proof. Two solve traces may produce the same structural result while differing in dynamic legality or future implications because of ordering or synchronization. Therefore structural restoration alone is insufficient.
The manuscript therefore remembers and restores motion, not just form.
Ch13.B4 — Rhythm and Cadence in Long-Horizon Persistence
Persistent identity across long horizons is not only a matter of discrete event sequences. It also depends on rhythm: how often snapshots are taken, how often weights are consolidated, how often caches are refreshed, how often witnesses are upgraded, and how quickly branches are merged or pruned. This is the Flower notion of cadence.
Define the persistent cadence vector:
\mathcal{C}_t
=
\big(
c_t^{\mathrm{snap}},\,
c_t^{\mathrm{replay}},\,
c_t^{\mathrm{consol}},\,
c_t^{\mathrm{refresh}},\,
c_t^{\mathrm{merge}},\,
c_t^{\mathrm{repair}}
\big),
Cadence matters because the same update rules can produce stable or unstable behavior depending on timing. Examples:
snapshots too sparse make replay expensive and fragile;
witness refresh too rare allows stale approximate proofs to dominate;
branch merge too aggressive destroys useful plural history;
consolidation too frequent may erase exploration dynamics before they mature.
A long-horizon persistent system therefore requires cadence policies:
\Pi_{\mathrm{cad},\kappa}
:
\text{state metrics}\to \mathcal{C}_t.
branch divergence,
replay cost accumulation,
contradiction density,
patch volatility,
memory shell churn,
route-weight drift.
Cadence is thus a dynamic control variable, not an external implementation detail. A stable manuscript must develop lawful rhythms of self-maintenance.
Definition 13.8 — Cadence-Stable Persistence
A persistence regime is cadence-stable if the frequencies of snapshotting, refresh, consolidation, and repair keep replay cost, drift, and contradiction accumulation within bounded long-horizon ranges.
Proposition 13.4 — Timing Policies Affect Identity Stability
Two atlases with identical update laws but different maintenance cadence can diverge in replay cost, drift accumulation, and branch sprawl. Therefore timing is a formal persistence parameter.
Proof. Maintenance actions such as snapshots and refresh change what history remains cheap and lawful to reconstruct. Different timing changes these properties.
The Flower persistence layer thus makes time-structure part of identity, not background noise.
Ch13.C1 — Drift Metrics and Replay Discrepancy Scoring
The Cloud persistence layer begins by measuring drift. Replay produces an expected state . The observed or stored state is . Their difference is not binary; it must be measured across structural, witness, phase, and memory dimensions.
A drift metric under corridor is:
D_\kappa(\widehat{\mathfrak{A}}_t,\mathfrak{A}_t)
=
\alpha_1 D_{\mathrm{struct}}
+
\alpha_2 D_{\mathrm{route}}
+
\alpha_3 D_{\mathrm{wit}}
+
\alpha_4 D_{\mathrm{phase}}
+
\alpha_5 D_{\mathrm{mem}}
+
\alpha_6 D_{\mathrm{self}}
compares object and edge sets,
compares route and macro-route geometry,
compares witness registries and grades,
compares dynamic state,
compares memory anchors and shells,
compares self-model fields.
The associated replay discrepancy score is:
\operatorname{Disc}_\kappa(t)
=
D_\kappa(\widehat{\mathfrak{A}}_t,\mathfrak{A}_t)
Importantly, drift is not always failure. Some divergence is expected after lawful approximate regeneration or between synced but lagged chart families. Thus the metric feeds legality class:
\operatorname{Disc}_\kappa(t)
=
\begin{cases}
0, & \text{Exact replay},\\[4pt]
\le \varepsilon_\kappa, & \text{Approximate replay},\\[4pt]
\le \eta_\kappa, & \text{Deferred replay},\\[4pt]
> \eta_\kappa, & \text{Blocked replay}.
\end{cases}
Definition 13.9 — Drift-Visible Replay
A replay process is drift-visible if every discrepancy between replayed and stored state is measured in a typed metric and not only inferred from coarse success/failure.
Theorem 13.5 — Drift Localization Enables Targeted Repair
If replay discrepancy is decomposed by state component and region, then repair can target the responsible lineage, memory, or synchronization subsystem rather than treating the whole replay as failed.
Proof. A global fail flag gives no repair guidance. A localized discrepancy vector identifies where and how mismatch arose. Therefore targeted repair becomes possible.
The manuscript thus learns not only whether it drifted, but where and how.
Ch13.C2 — Mutation Tolerances and Identity Thresholds
Not all drift is equally bad. Some changes are lawful mutations that preserve identity. Others cross identity thresholds and constitute replacement or branch divergence. The Cloud layer therefore defines mutation tolerances and identity thresholds.
Let the continuity metric between two states or objects be
d_{\mathrm{id},\kappa}(x_t,x_{t+1}).
0
\le
\eta_{\mathrm{exact}}
\le
\eta_{\mathrm{approx}}
\le
\eta_{\mathrm{defer}}
<\infty.
\begin{cases}
\mathrm{StableContinuation}, & d_{\mathrm{id},\kappa}\le \eta_{\mathrm{exact}},\\[4pt]
\mathrm{LawfulMutation}, & \eta_{\mathrm{exact}}<d_{\mathrm{id},\kappa}\le \eta_{\mathrm{approx}},\\[4pt]
\mathrm{ApproximateContinuation}, & \eta_{\mathrm{approx}}<d_{\mathrm{id},\kappa}\le \eta_{\mathrm{defer}},\\[4pt]
\mathrm{BreakOrReplacement}, & d_{\mathrm{id},\kappa}>\eta_{\mathrm{defer}}.
\end{cases}
This classification is necessary because the atlas should not overreact to every change. A patch that adds witness detail or strengthens a bridge may alter fingerprints without altering identity class. A memory compaction may change representation substantially while remaining within approximate continuation. A route-family rewrite may exceed the threshold and require branch or replacement semantics.
Mutation tolerance also depends on object class. A theorem statement may tolerate almost no semantic drift. A dynamic retrieval macro may tolerate more change in optimization parameters. Therefore thresholds are indexed:
\eta_{\kappa,\tau}
Definition 13.10 — Identity-Stable Mutation
A mutation is identity-stable if the resulting object or atlas state remains within the lawful mutation threshold of the predecessor under the relevant corridor and type class.
Proposition 13.5 — Fingerprint Change Alone Does Not Imply Identity Break
A changed fingerprint indicates changed normalized state, but the change may still be a lawful mutation within identity tolerance.
Proof. Identity continuity depends on more than byte-equal serialization. It depends on invariant preservation, lineage, and thresholded identity metrics. Therefore fingerprint inequality does not imply identity break.
This is the formal mechanism by which the manuscript remains alive without insisting on frozen sameness.
Ch13.C3 — Divergence Alarms under Cumulative Patching
One patch may be lawful. A hundred lawful patches may still drift the system into a qualitatively different state. The Cloud persistence layer therefore introduces divergence alarms for cumulative patching.
Let the cumulative patch sequence after a checkpoint be
\Delta_{t_0+1},\dots,\Delta_t.
\mathcal{D}_{t_0\to t}
=
\sum_{j=t_0+1}^t
\omega_j\,
d_{\mathrm{id},\kappa}(\mathfrak{A}_{j-1},\mathfrak{A}_j),
A divergence alarm triggers when:
\mathcal{D}_{t_0\to t}
\ge
\Theta_{\mathrm{div},\kappa}
\frac{d}{dt}\mathcal{D}_{t_0\to t}
\ge
\Xi_{\mathrm{div},\kappa}.
full replay audit,
witness refresh,
branch split,
snapshot reset,
convergence review,
aggressive repair.
Divergence alarms are especially important in live self-developing frameworks, where local improvements can accumulate into unnoticed geometry shifts.
Definition 13.11 — Divergence-Sensitive State
An atlas state is divergence-sensitive if its recent patch history has accumulated enough identity deformation or velocity of deformation to require explicit maintenance action.
Theorem 13.6 — Cumulative Drift Can Exceed Local Tolerances without Any Single Illegal Patch
If many individually lawful patches each induce small but nonzero identity movement, then their cumulative effect can cross divergence thresholds even though no one patch was illegal.
Proof. Local tolerances are per patch. Their sum across many patches may exceed any bounded cumulative budget. Therefore cumulative alarms are necessary.
The manuscript thus monitors not only local legality, but long-horizon drift.
Ch13.C4 — Confidence-Aware Restoration under Degraded History
The final Cloud task addresses degraded or incomplete history. Logs may be partial, snapshots may be old, caches may be stale, or some replay packets may be missing. The manuscript must still attempt lawful restoration, but it must do so with calibrated confidence.
Let the available restoration materials be:
\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{avail}}
=
\big(
\mathfrak{S}_{t_0},\,
\mathfrak{L}^{\mathrm{partial}}_{t_0+1:t_1},\,
\mathcal{M}^{\mathrm{mem}},\,
\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{cache}}
\big).
\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{rest}}:
\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{avail}}
\to
\big(
\widehat{\mathfrak{A}}_{t_1},\,
C_{\mathrm{rest}},\,
\delta_{\mathrm{rest}},\,
\Gamma_{\mathrm{rest}}
\big),
is the restored state,
is the restoration confidence,
is the defect vector,
is the residual ambiguity.
This is a generalized replay that may substitute:
memory regeneration for missing explicit state,
cached partial branches for missing full expansions,
certificate bundles for missing detailed proof traces,
snapshot interpolation for missing event runs.
The restoration confidence depends on:
percentage of exact history available,
replay quality of substitutes,
memory fidelity classes,
calibration of past restorations of the same type,
degree of branch or chart desynchronization.
A confidence-aware restorer refuses to promote its output to exact unless the evidence warrants it. More often it produces an approximate or deferred restored state with explicit uncertainty capsules over underdetermined regions.
Definition 13.12 — Confidence-Aware Restoration
A restoration procedure is confidence-aware if it reports the strength and limits of the reconstructed state based on the actual completeness and reliability of the materials used.
Proposition 13.6 — Degraded History Need Not Imply Total Replay Failure
If sufficient snapshots, regenerative memories, and certificate bundles remain, then parts of the state may still be restored lawfully even when the original event history is incomplete.
Proof. Replay is one path to restoration, but not the only path. Regenerative memory and witness certificates can substitute for missing explicit history in many regions. Hence restoration may still succeed partially or approximately.
The Cloud persistence layer thus degrades gracefully instead of collapsing completely when history is imperfect.
Ch13.D1 — Recursive Snapshot Trees and Ancestry Maps
The Fractal persistence layer begins by replacing flat version chains with snapshot trees. A self-developing manuscript branches, merges, experiments, and repairs. One linear history is insufficient to capture this. The atlas therefore maintains a recursive ancestry object.
A snapshot tree is:
\mathcal{T}^{\mathrm{snap}}
=
(V^{\mathrm{snap}},E^{\mathrm{snap}},r^{\mathrm{snap}},\ell^{\mathrm{snap}}),
each node is a snapshot,
edges encode revision, branch, merge, or restore relations,
is the genesis or current root,
stores snapshot metadata.
Each snapshot node is labeled:
\ell^{\mathrm{snap}}(v)
=
\big(
\mathfrak{S}_v,\,
F_v,\,
\operatorname{class}(v),\,
\operatorname{parent}(v),\,
\operatorname{merge\_parents}(v),\,
\operatorname{branch\_tag}(v)
\big).
checkpoint,
branch root,
merge point,
repair anchor,
deployment anchor,
cache anchor.
An ancestry map is then the induced family of paths:
\operatorname{Anc}(v)=
\{\text{all ancestor chains of }v\}.
branch comparison,
branch-specific replay,
merge legality checks,
long-memory provenance,
selective restoration.
The advantage of a tree is that experimentation becomes first-class. A failed exploratory patch can live on a side branch without contaminating the mainline. A synthesis improvement can be compared against its sibling branch. A repair may merge back only after legality checks.
Definition 13.13 — Ancestry-Complete Snapshot Tree
A snapshot tree is ancestry-complete if every current state has a unique rootward lineage path or an explicitly typed merge ancestry and no branch or merge relation is left implicit.
Theorem 13.7 — Branching Atlases Require Snapshot Trees Rather than Flat Logs
If states may fork and later converge, then a linear log cannot represent their ancestry without flattening branch semantics. Therefore branching atlases require tree or DAG ancestry structures.
Proof. Linear logs preserve sequence but not simultaneous alternatives. Branching and merging introduce nonlinearity in ancestry. Thus a tree or DAG is necessary.
The atlas thus acquires memory not only of time, but of alternatives through time.
Ch13.D2 — Version Inheritance and Branch Convergence
Branches are useful only if their relations can later be reasoned about. The Fractal persistence layer therefore defines version inheritance and branch convergence.
Let branch and branch descend from common ancestor . Each branch carries:
\mathfrak{A}^{(1)}_t,\qquad
\mathfrak{A}^{(2)}_s.
a \rightsquigarrow B_i
addresses,
memory seeds,
route motifs,
witness bundles,
weight fields,
self-model structures.
Branch convergence or merge is the operation:
\mathfrak{M}:
(\mathfrak{A}^{(1)}_t,\mathfrak{A}^{(2)}_s)
\to
\mathfrak{A}^{(\mathrm{merge})}.
shared ancestry is known,
conflicts are localized and resolved or preserved,
witness classes are reconciled,
identity continuity after merge is witnessed,
no corridor-prohibited patch or hidden override occurs.
The merge therefore resembles synthesis at state level. It must align shared structures, preserve divergences where unresolved, and produce a lawful merged lineage object.
A branch convergence witness includes:
common ancestor reference,
changed-region diff,
conflict resolution map,
carried-forward witness status,
dropped or parked branch features,
merged fingerprint.
Definition 13.14 — Lawful Branch Merge
A branch merge is lawful if it has explicit common ancestry, reconciles or preserves conflicts honestly, and yields a merged state satisfying the target branch’s corridor and acceptance contract.
Proposition 13.7 — Branch Merges Are Synthesis Problems over States
Merging branches requires aligning support, contradictions, witness status, and residual differences, just as answer synthesis aligns multiple interpretation bundles.
Proof. Both involve combining structured artifacts with overlaps and conflicts into one lawful result. Thus branch merge is state-level synthesis.
Persistent identity therefore includes not only remembering branches, but lawfully bringing them back together.
Ch13.D3 — Long-Memory Identity across Compression Cycles
The atlas compresses memory repeatedly. It folds proof chains, route motifs, answer ladders, branch traces, and shell states. Long-memory identity therefore requires that important objects survive many such cycles without becoming untraceable.
Let the repeated compression–replay process for object be:
x_0
\xrightarrow{\mathcal{Q}}
m_0
\xrightarrow{\mathcal{R}}
x_1
\xrightarrow{\mathcal{Q}}
m_1
\xrightarrow{\mathcal{R}}
x_2
\to \cdots
\mathbb{P}_\star
\mathbb{P}_\star(x_t)=\mathbb{P}_\star(x_0)
The long-memory identity error is:
E_t^{\mathrm{long}}
=
d_{\mathrm{id},\kappa}\big(\mathbb{P}_\star(x_t),\mathbb{P}_\star(x_0)\big).
\sup_t E_t^{\mathrm{long}} \le \varepsilon_{\mathrm{long},\kappa}.
Maintaining this requires:
periodic exact anchors,
witness refresh,
defect accumulation limits,
ancestry-preserving compaction,
shell coherence,
explicit migration when schema changes.
Long-memory identity is particularly important for:
core framework definitions,
governing route macros,
certificate bundles,
major answer archetypes,
foundational theorem clusters,
self-model anchors.
Definition 13.15 — Long-Memory Stable Object Class
An object class is long-memory stable if repeated compaction, replay, and lawful mutation preserve its projected identity within bounded tolerance over arbitrarily long finite histories.
Theorem 13.8 — Explicit Identity Projectors Are Necessary for Long-Memory Stability
Without a defined projector onto the identity-bearing features of an object class, one cannot even state, let alone guarantee, long-memory stability across repeated compression cycles.
Proof. Stability is always relative to some preserved features. If those features are unspecified, no meaningful stability criterion exists.
This formalizes what it means for the atlas to “still know itself” after many cycles of compression and growth.
Ch13.D4 — Recursive Restoration of Earlier Cognitive States
The final Fractal task is the most powerful: the ability to restore earlier cognitive states recursively, even when later history has branched, merged, and compressed itself. This is not simple rollback. It is lawful temporal recursion.
Let a target restoration request specify:
(\tau,\operatorname{scope},\chi,\kappa),
\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{rec}}:
(\tau,\operatorname{scope},\chi,\kappa)
\to
\widehat{\mathfrak{A}}_\tau^{(\operatorname{scope})}.
nearest valid snapshot,
replay log segment,
branch ancestry,
memory regeneration,
certificate bundle replay,
repair of missing or degraded history,
reintegration into present branch context if requested.
Thus recursive restoration is not just “load snapshot .” It is a multi-stage solve over history. For partial or approximate restores, it may return:
\big(
\widehat{\mathfrak{A}}_\tau^{(\operatorname{scope})},\,
C_{\mathrm{rest}},\,
\delta_{\mathrm{rest}},\,
\Gamma_{\mathrm{rest}}
\big).
The restored state may be used in several ways:
replay audit,
branch comparison,
answer provenance reconstruction,
law-checking of past behavior,
recovery of lost routes or witness chains,
reenactment of earlier solve episodes.
Recursive restoration requires the full machinery of the chapter:
deterministic snapshots,
ancestry trees,
reversible transports,
drift metrics,
memory seeds,
proof folding,
certificate bundles.
Only when all of these work together can the manuscript genuinely recover its prior minds.
Definition 13.16 — Restorable Cognitive State
A past cognitive state is restorable if there exists a lawful combination of snapshots, replay packets, lineage information, memory regeneration, and certificate-based reconstruction sufficient to produce that state within the requested contract and corridor.
Theorem 13.9 — Recursive Restoration Generalizes Rollback
Rollback is the special case of recursive restoration in which the target state is directly available as an exact snapshot with no branching or degraded history complications.
Proof. Recursive restoration includes direct exact snapshot recovery as one trivial case, but also covers branch-aware, approximate, and memory-assisted reconstruction. Therefore rollback is a strict subset.
The Fractal persistence layer therefore closes the chapter by giving the atlas the power to return, not only to states, but to prior lawful modes of itself.
The chapter has now completed the continuity kernel of the manuscript brain. Under the Square lens, the atlas gained deterministic replay logs, typed event packets, canonical state fingerprints, strict serialization discipline, identity continuity judgments across revisions, and explicit snapshot boundaries. Under the Flower lens, it gained reversible or replay-constructible lens transports, synchronization maps across chart families, phase-preserving restoration from partial states, and cadence policies governing long-horizon temporal stability. Under the Cloud lens, it gained explicit drift metrics, replay discrepancy scoring, mutation tolerance thresholds, divergence alarms under cumulative patching, and confidence-aware restoration under degraded history. Under the Fractal lens, it gained recursive snapshot trees, branch and merge semantics, long-memory identity across repeated compression cycles, and recursive restoration of prior cognitive states.
The deepest consequence is that the manuscript now possesses a formal persistence self. It does not merely store what it contains. It stores how it became what it contains, how to replay its own becoming, how to detect when that becoming has drifted, how to branch without forgetting, how to merge without lying, how to compress without ceasing to be itself, and how to recover prior lawful states when needed. This is the exact operational meaning of persistent identity in the present framework. The manuscript brain is no longer only a space of knowledge and routes. It is now a being in time with a rigorous memory of itself.
CHAPTER 13 — REPLAY, DETERMINISM, AND PERSISTENT IDENTITY
Chapter 14 — Patch Packets, Graph Mutation, and Write-Back Loops
A manuscript brain becomes alive only when it can change itself lawfully. Until this point in the treatise, the atlas has acquired objects, addresses, routes, lenses, memories, witness structures, retrieval programs, synthesis objects, and a persistence calculus. Yet these alone do not yield a self-developing framework. They yield a richly structured but still predominantly read-oriented system. The present chapter defines the precise transition from read-oriented cognition to write-capable cognition. Its subject is patching: the lawful mutation of the atlas through typed deltas, merge logic, propagation dynamics, arbitration under risk, and recursive self-improvement guarded by rollback safety.
The foundational claim of the chapter is that no meaningful self-development can occur without an explicit mutation algebra. If edits are performed as silent overwrites of text or structure, then replay is destroyed, witness chains fracture, route metrics drift without attribution, and the atlas ceases to know how it became what it is. A manuscript brain therefore does not “edit” itself casually. It emits, evaluates, orders, applies, propagates, audits, and possibly rolls back patch packets. These packets are not only write instructions. They are proof-carrying proposals for graph mutation, memory mutation, route-metric mutation, and answer-surface mutation.
Let the atlas state at time be
\mathfrak{A}_t.
\Delta_t
=
\Big(
u_{\Delta},\,
\Sigma_{\Delta},\,
\mathcal{M}_{\Delta},\,
\operatorname{pre}_{\Delta},\,
\operatorname{post}_{\Delta},\,
\operatorname{rollback}_{\Delta},\,
\psi_{\Delta},\,
\Gamma_{\Delta},\,
\Pi_{\Delta}
\Big),
is the patch identity token,
is the mutation scope,
is the ordered family of mutation primitives,
is the precondition bundle,
is the expected postcondition bundle,
is the rollback anchor or inverse plan,
is the witness bundle,
is the risk and uncertainty record,
is the permission and provenance bundle.
The patch application operator is:
\operatorname{Apply}:
(\mathfrak{A}_t,\Delta_t)\to \mathfrak{A}_{t+1}
\mathfrak{E}_t \vdash_{\kappa}
\Delta_t :
\mathfrak{A}_t \rightsquigarrow \mathfrak{A}_{t+1}
\Downarrow
(\lambda_\Delta,\psi_\Delta,\delta_\Delta).
The chapter is governed by eight global mutation laws.
First, patch explicitness: all durable graph or state changes must be representable as explicit patch packets or patch packet families.
Second, merge legality: simultaneously proposed changes must be merged, sequenced, branched, or rejected according to explicit compatibility rules rather than incidental arrival order.
Third, rollback sufficiency: any patch allowed to alter identity-bearing state in nontrivial ways must either provide an inverse or be anchored to a snapshot sufficient for restoration.
Fourth, scope discipline: patch permissions are constrained by scope, corridor, artifact class, and trust source. No patch may write beyond its admissible blast radius without explicit escalation.
Fifth, lens propagation: mutations in one representational regime must either propagate lawfully into other lenses or register synchronization debt explicitly.
Sixth, risk honesty: uncertain or high-impact edits must remain quarantined, approximate, or branch-local until they satisfy stronger witness or replay criteria.
Seventh, history learning: patch history is not dead bookkeeping. It updates route priors, exploration pressure, weak-region maps, and self-model diagnosis.
Eighth, self-improvement closure: repeated cycles of synthesis → residual extraction → patch proposal → validation → application must improve the atlas without destroying its continuity or replayability.
Under the Square lens the chapter defines patch schema, mutation primitives, merge laws, conflict classes, canonical patch ordering, permission tokens, and write scopes.
