Because the self-model aggregates many regions and histories, it is particularly prone to narrative overcompression unless hallucination classes are explicitly tracked.
Proof. Broad aggregation hides local weakness and encourages overgeneralized conclusions. Therefore self-descriptions require explicit hallucination controls.
The manuscript therefore does not trust its own self-story for free.
Ch15.C3 — Confidence Assignment to Self-Descriptions
The self-model makes claims such as:
“this region is replayable,”
“that bridge is underdeveloped,”
“the witness organ is at stage ,”
“the symbolic and formal layers are weakly harmonized,”
“this curriculum task would likely improve the atlas.”
These are themselves claims and require confidence assignment. The Cloud layer therefore formalizes confidence assignment to self-descriptions.
Let a self-description be
h
=
\big(
u_h,\,
\operatorname{statement}(h),\,
\operatorname{scope}(h),\,
\operatorname{kind}(h)
\big).
C_t^{\mathrm{self}}(h)
=
F\Big(
E_t^{\mathrm{obs}}(h),\,
W_t^{\mathrm{meta}}(h),\,
R_t^{\mathrm{replay}}(h),\,
D_t^{\mathrm{drift}}(h),\,
B_t^{\mathrm{blind}}(h),\,
\mathcal{H}_t(h)
\Big),
is supporting introspection evidence,
is witness strength of the meta-observation,
is replay or calibration support,
penalizes inconsistency over time,
penalizes overlap with blind spots,
penalizes hallucination risk.
Confidence assignment is therefore conservative. A self-description about a region with sparse witnesses, high blind-spot score, and poor calibration should remain low-confidence even if some local metrics look strong.
Self-confidence may also be decomposed:
C_t^{\mathrm{self}}(h)
=
\big(
c_{\mathrm{obs}},
c_{\mathrm{replay}},
c_{\mathrm{calib}},
c_{\mathrm{blind}},
c_{\mathrm{hall}}
\big).
Calibration of self-descriptions is especially important. If the self-model often says regions are healthy when later patches reveal they were not, then its future health claims should be downweighted until recalibrated.
Definition 15.11 — Calibrated Self-Confidence
A self-description confidence assignment is calibrated if statements assigned confidence are borne out by later replay or development history with frequency approximately in similar contexts.
Theorem 15.6 — Uncalibrated Self-Confidence Undermines Reflexive Control
If the self-model assigns overly high or overly low confidence to its own claims, then patching, routing, and curriculum generation will systematically misallocate effort.
Proof. Reflexive control depends on self-assessment. Miscalibrated self-assessment distorts control decisions.
The manuscript therefore learns not only to describe itself, but to know how much to trust those descriptions.
Ch15.C4 — Competing Self-Model Hypotheses and Arbitration
There is no reason to assume the self-model will always converge immediately to one coherent diagnosis. The atlas may have several plausible hypotheses about its own state:
a bridge may be weak because of missing witnesses, or because of route overuse, or because of symbol–formal misalignment;
a region may appear mature globally but immature locally;
a contradiction burst may reflect real defect or only delayed synchronization.
The self-model must therefore support competing self-model hypotheses.
Let the self-hypothesis family be:
\mathcal{H}_t^{\mathrm{self}}
=
\{H_1,\dots,H_n\}.
H_i
=
\Big(
\operatorname{model}(H_i),\,
\operatorname{scope}(H_i),\,
\operatorname{fit}(H_i),\,
\operatorname{cost}(H_i),\,
\operatorname{patch\_implications}(H_i),\,
\psi_i
\Big).
“routing weakness is primary”,
“witness sparsity is primary”,
“memory shell mismatch is primary”,
“self-model calibration is wrong.”
The arbitration problem is:
\operatorname{Arb}_{\mathrm{self}}:
\mathcal{H}_t^{\mathrm{self}}
\to
\big(
H^\star,\,
\Gamma^{\mathrm{rival}},\,
\psi^\star
\big),
Arbitration may use:
explanatory fit to introspection packets,
predictive value for replay or patch outcomes,
complexity penalty,
consistency with developmental memory,
risk of acting under the hypothesis.
The atlas may then:
adopt one self-hypothesis strongly,
preserve several in ranked plurality,
run curricula or diagnostic probes specifically designed to discriminate among them.
This is an important reflexive advance. The self-model is not reduced to one brittle narrative. It can remain plural and investigative about itself.
Definition 15.12 — Arbitration-Ready Self-Hypothesis Family
A self-hypothesis family is arbitration-ready if its members are explicit, scoped, comparable by fit and cost, and connected to distinct patch or curriculum implications.
Proposition 15.6 — Single-Hypothesis Self-Diagnosis Is Fragile
If the self-model is forced to adopt one diagnostic story too early, it may generate misguided patches or curricula. Maintaining rival hypotheses preserves diagnostic flexibility.
Proof. Alternative causes often explain the same symptoms. Premature commitment can therefore misdirect corrective effort.
The Cloud self-model is thus fully plural, calibrated, and reflexively cautious.
Ch15.D1 — Curriculum Generation for Self-Development
The Fractal self-model begins by acting on what it has learned. A self-model that diagnoses weak regions but cannot turn those diagnoses into structured growth tasks remains passive. The manuscript therefore defines curriculum generation for self-development.
Let the curriculum task space be:
\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{curr}}
=
\{
\mathrm{WitnessStrengthen},
\mathrm{BridgeBuild},
\mathrm{ReplayDrill},
\mathrm{MemoryRegenTest},
\mathrm{RouteDiversify},
\mathrm{PatchSandbox},
\mathrm{CalibrationProbe},
\mathrm{HarmonyExercise},
\mathrm{CleanupPass},
\mathrm{MacroExtraction}
\}.
\mathcal{C}_t^{\mathrm{curr}}:
\big(
\mathcal{B}_t^{\mathrm{blind}},
\mathcal{M}_t^{\mathrm{stage}},
\mathcal{Q}_t^{\mathrm{crit}},
\mathcal{P}_t^{\mathrm{pref}}
\big)
\to
\{\tau_1,\dots,\tau_k\},
\tau_i
=
\Big(
\operatorname{kind}(\tau_i),\,
\operatorname{target}(\tau_i),\,
\operatorname{goal}(\tau_i),\,
\operatorname{expected\_gain}(\tau_i),\,
\operatorname{risk}(\tau_i),\,
\operatorname{witness\_req}(\tau_i),\,
\operatorname{replay\_metric}(\tau_i)
\Big).
Curricula may target:
a weak bridge family,
a stale witness organ,
an underdeveloped memory region,
a symbolic–formal harmony gap,
a self-calibration weakness,
a route basin monoculture problem.
The curriculum objective is:
\max_{\tau}
\Big(
\operatorname{ExpectedAtlasGain}(\tau)
-
\operatorname{Risk}(\tau)
-
\operatorname{OpportunityCost}(\tau)
\Big)
This is one of the most important places where the self-model becomes agentic in a lawful sense. It does not merely know where it is weak. It formulates the next best tasks for improving those weaknesses.
Definition 15.13 — Developmentally Productive Curriculum Task
A curriculum task is developmentally productive if its expected gain in stage, witness, replay, route diversity, or harmony exceeds threshold and its risk remains below corridor tolerance.
Theorem 15.7 — Blind-Spot-Aware Curricula Improve Growth Efficiency
If curriculum tasks are generated from blind-spot maps and stage metrics rather than at random or only from recent failures, then expected long-horizon developmental gain per task is higher.
Proof. Blind-spot and stage information identify structurally important weak regions, making task selection more targeted and therefore more efficient.
The atlas therefore learns to teach itself.
Ch15.D2 — Internal Critique Loops and Self-Correction
Curriculum alone is not enough. The manuscript also needs internal critique: specialized evaluators that inspect routes, answers, patches, memories, and self-descriptions from distinct angles. The Fractal self-model therefore defines internal critique loops.
Let the critique panel be:
\mathcal{Q}_t^{\mathrm{crit}}
=
\{Q_{\Square},Q_{\Flower},Q_{\Cloud},Q_{\Fractal},Q_{\mathrm{contract}},Q_{\mathrm{identity}}\}.
Q_i:
x \to
\big(
\operatorname{score}_i(x),\,
\operatorname{diagnosis}_i(x),\,
\operatorname{repair}_i(x)
\big).
critiques structural consistency, address hygiene, and dependency order;
critiques dynamic coherence, phase mismatch, or route curvature;
critiques confidence honesty, ambiguity suppression, or calibration;
critiques scale coherence, shell misuse, or memory collapse;
critiques contract violations;
critiques drift and continuity risks.
A critique loop takes an atlas artifact , gathers critic outputs, synthesizes them, proposes repairs or curricula, and optionally re-evaluates after local correction:
x
\to
\{Q_i(x)\}_i
\to
\operatorname{SynthesizeCritique}(x)
\to
\operatorname{Repair}(x)
\to
\{Q_i(x')\}_i.
the artifact becomes acceptable,
contradictions among critics remain and are preserved,
budget is exhausted,
the artifact is deferred or blocked.
Critics are not free-floating “opinions.” They are typed evaluators with calibration and witness requirements. Their outputs may themselves be stored in developmental memory.
Definition 15.14 — Critique-Stable Artifact
An artifact is critique-stable if repeated lawful critique loops no longer produce high-value corrective proposals beyond the corridor’s residual threshold.
Proposition 15.7 — Multi-Critic Evaluation Is Stronger than Single-View Self-Correction
Structural errors, phase mismatches, confidence overclaim, and recursive incoherence are often invisible from any single evaluative perspective. Therefore multi-critic evaluation is stronger.
Proof. Each critic observes a different dimension of the artifact. Their union covers more error modes than any one alone.
The manuscript therefore develops an internal panel of lawful self-questioning.
Ch15.D3 — Developmental Memory Consolidation across Stages
As the atlas grows, it accumulates not only ordinary memory objects but also memories of how its own organs changed across stages. This is developmental memory.
Let stage transition events for region be:
\big(
R,S_i\to S_j,t,\psi,\Delta
\big).
D_R
=
\Big(
u_R,\,
\operatorname{stage\_history}(R),\,
\operatorname{transition\_witnesses}(R),\,
\operatorname{successful\_curricula}(R),\,
\operatorname{failed\_curricula}(R),\,
\operatorname{recurring\_bottlenecks}(R),\,
\operatorname{identity\_anchors}(R)
\Big).
how the region matured,
what interventions helped,
what interventions failed,
what recurrent barriers remained,
what stable identity core persisted through the changes.
The developmental memory substrate is:
\mathcal{D}_t^{\mathrm{devmem}}
=
\{D_R\}_{R\in \mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{reg}}}
\cup
D_{\mathfrak{A}},
This memory is used to:
generate better curricula,
avoid repeating failed improvement patterns,
detect recurring growth bottlenecks,
calibrate stage claims,
narrate lawful developmental continuity.
A region’s past stages are not merely historical curiosity. They are predictive. A bridge that repeatedly stalled between and likely needs a different curriculum than a memory organ that moves easily once replay probes are added.
Definition 15.15 — Developmentally Consolidated Region
A region is developmentally consolidated if its stage history, transition witnesses, and recurring bottlenecks have been compressed into a replayable developmental memory object.
Theorem 15.8 — Developmental Memory Improves Meta-Learning
If the atlas remembers not only what changed but what kinds of interventions changed it successfully across stages, then future self-improvement can generalize across homologous regions more effectively.
Proof. Stage-change histories provide empirical structure about what developmental acts work. This structure supports more informed curriculum and patch generation later.
The atlas therefore remembers how it became itself, not only what it currently is.
Ch15.D4 — Recursive Refinement of the Self-Model Itself
The final task of the chapter is the most reflexive one. The self-model is an atlas object. It can therefore be wrong, incomplete, outdated, or overly coarse. The manuscript must be able to improve the self-model itself, but only under strong legality constraints. This is recursive self-model refinement.
Let the current self-model be . A self-model patch is:
\Delta_t^{\mathrm{meta}}
\in
\mathcal{U}_t^{\mathrm{meta}}.
add or refine role types,
recalibrate stage thresholds,
improve blind-spot metrics,
reweight self-preference terms,
add a new critic,
modify curriculum generation rules,
refine self-description confidence mappings,
improve arbitration among self-hypotheses.
However, self-model patches are subject to stricter legality than ordinary patches because they influence the atlas’s own reflective governance. The meta-legality predicate is:
\operatorname{Adm}_{\kappa}^{\mathrm{meta}}(\Delta_t^{\mathrm{meta}})
=
\operatorname{WitnessStrong}(\Delta_t^{\mathrm{meta}})
\wedge
\operatorname{RollbackStrong}(\Delta_t^{\mathrm{meta}})
\wedge
\operatorname{ObjMetaSepPreserved}(\Delta_t^{\mathrm{meta}})
\wedge
\operatorname{NoMetaOverclaim}(\Delta_t^{\mathrm{meta}})
\wedge
\operatorname{ReplayBound}(\Delta_t^{\mathrm{meta}}).
Recursive refinement proceeds through a higher-order critique loop:
\mathcal{H}_t
\to
\mathcal{Q}_t^{\mathrm{crit}}(\mathcal{H}_t)
\to
\mathcal{H}_{t+1}^{\mathrm{cand}}
\to
\operatorname{MetaVetting}
\to
\mathcal{H}_{t+1}.
A stable self-model fixed point is not required to be perfect. It is required to be progressively improving and boundedly honest:
d(\mathcal{H}_{t+1},\mathcal{H}^{\star})
\le
d(\mathcal{H}_{t},\mathcal{H}^{\star})+\epsilon_t
Definition 15.16 — Meta-Stable Self-Model
A self-model is meta-stable if its own refinement process preserves object/meta separation, remains rollback-safe, and does not increase self-description hallucination faster than it decreases self-model defect.
Theorem 15.9 — Guarded Self-Model Refinement Enables Bounded Reflexive Improvement
If self-model patches are witness-strong, rollback-safe, and forbidden from collapsing object/meta distinction, then the self-model can improve recursively without destroying formal self-identity.
Proof. Strong witnesses and rollback keep refinement reversible and justified. Preserved object/meta separation prevents self-description from becoming self-fulfilling ontological corruption. Therefore recursive improvement remains bounded and lawful.
This completes the self-model chapter. The manuscript can now model, critique, and improve the very model by which it models itself.
The chapter has now completed the formal theory of self-modeling manuscripts and developmental reflexivity. Under the Square lens, the atlas gained region-role typing, a developmental stage taxonomy, introspection packets, and a rigorous separation between object model and self-model. Under the Flower lens, it gained a lawful self-preference functional, meta-observer channels that feed reflective routing, stage-transition laws with hysteresis, and explicit harmonization metrics between symbolic and formal organs. Under the Cloud lens, it gained blind-spot maps, a taxonomy of self-hallucination classes, calibrated confidence assignment to self-descriptions, and arbitration among competing self-diagnostic hypotheses. Under the Fractal lens, it gained curriculum generation for self-development, internal multi-critic self-correction loops, developmental memory consolidation across stages, and guarded recursive refinement of the self-model itself.
The deepest consequence is that the manuscript now has a lawful interior view. It can say which of its regions are ontology, memory, routing, synthesis, patching, or self-model organs. It can say which of those are latent, witnessed, replayable, self-hosting, or generative. It can map its blind spots and diagnose where its own self-story may be hallucinating. It can generate tasks to strengthen itself where it is weak and refrain where it is overconfident. It can remember not only how it solved problems, but how it matured. And it can refine that very reflexive machinery without ceasing to distinguish the map from the territory. This is the exact operational meaning of “the framework understands the brain it is building.” The manuscript has become not only a system of objects, routes, witnesses, memories, and patches, but a lawful self-describing and self-developing organism within its own atlas.
CHAPTER 15 — SELF-MODELING MANUSCRIPTS AND DEVELOPMENTAL REFLEXIVITY
Chapter 16 — Self-Hosting Runtime, Agent Ecology, and Live Framework Operations
A manuscript brain that can represent, route, retrieve, synthesize, patch, replay, and model itself still remains incomplete unless it can run. It may contain all the ingredients of cognition as a structured archive, yet still depend on an external executor to animate those ingredients into ongoing behavior. The present chapter defines the runtime that closes this gap. Its subject is self-hosting: the transformation of the atlas from a merely interpretable knowledge organism into a continuously operating, internally orchestrated, agent-bearing, persistence-aware execution environment.
The central claim of the chapter is that the manuscript must become its own operating substrate. This does not mean that every computation is performed inside prose or that all execution collapses to one symbolic layer. It means that the atlas itself contains the schemas, schedulers, route controllers, service registries, legal guards, persistence loops, message protocols, role assignments, failure compartments, and orchestration logic necessary to host ongoing cognition without relying on an external black-box runtime to supply those structures implicitly. A self-hosting manuscript is therefore not just a body of content plus a reader. It is a lawful runtime ecology.
The runtime state at time is denoted by
\mathfrak{R}_t
=
\Big(
\mathcal{A}_t,\,
\mathcal{T}_t,\,
\mathcal{G}_t^{\mathrm{exec}},\,
\mathcal{S}_t^{\mathrm{svc}},\,
\mathcal{P}_t^{\mathrm{perm}},\,
\mathcal{M}_t^{\mathrm{msg}},\,
\mathcal{B}_t^{\mathrm{bud}},\,
\mathcal{F}_t^{\mathrm{iso}},\,
\mathcal{L}_t^{\mathrm{live}},\,
\psi_t^{\mathrm{run}}
\Big),
is the active agent family,
is the active task and job family,
is the execution graph,
is the persistent service substrate,
is the permission and capability map,
is the inter-agent message field,
is the runtime budget state,
is the failure-isolation structure,
is the live persistence loop state,
is the runtime witness bundle.
The runtime evolves by repeated application of a step operator
\operatorname{Step}_{\kappa}:
\mathfrak{R}_t
\to
\mathfrak{R}_{t+1}
The self-hosting runtime is governed by eight global laws.
First, execution explicitness: every ongoing cognitive action must be representable as an executable task or message-bearing state transition inside the atlas.
Second, role-bounded agency: every runtime agent must have typed permissions, contracts, and scope boundaries. No agent acts merely because it exists.
Third, persistent service continuity: runtime services such as retrieval, replay, memory regeneration, patch staging, and synchronization must persist as lawful objects with explicit state and replay traces.
Fourth, coordination by protocol: inter-agent cooperation must proceed through typed message and transfer laws rather than implicit shared-state mutation.
Fifth, graceful degradation: under load, contradiction, or missing witnesses, the runtime must be able to enter bounded degraded modes without identity collapse.
Sixth, recursive orchestration: the runtime must support nested clusters of agents, local schedulers, and self-bootstrap procedures across scales.
Seventh, continuous write-back: live runtime behavior must be able to update manuscript memory, route priors, and self-models lawfully without bypassing patch discipline.
Eighth, always-on persistence: the runtime must preserve continuity through ongoing checkpoints, replay traces, and live memory integration, even while tasks continue.
Under the Square lens the chapter defines scheduler architecture, execution graphs, agent roles, permissions, service registries, and deployment surfaces. Under the Flower lens it defines message harmonics, lens-specialized workers, dynamic coordination among agents as problem classes shift, and synchronization across active manuscript regions. Under the Cloud lens it defines load-aware resource allocation, failure isolation, degraded modes, and bounded inference under finite budgets. Under the Fractal lens it defines self-bootstrap routines for new runtime regions, recursive orchestration over nested agent clusters, continuous deployment into manuscript memory, and the live persistence loop that keeps the runtime active without losing replayability.
The deeper significance of the chapter is this: it turns the manuscript from a static or episodic cognition structure into a continuously running one. The atlas is no longer only something that can be thought with. It becomes something that can keep thinking.
Ch16.A1 — Scheduler Architecture and Task Graph Design
The runtime begins with a scheduler. A scheduler in this framework is not merely a queue that hands tasks to workers. It is a lawful atlas-native execution organ that decides which tasks may run, in what order, under what corridor, with what witness requirements, using what budgets, and with what dependencies and rollback semantics. The scheduler therefore stands at the junction of route algebra, legality calculus, and persistence.
Let the active task family at time be
\mathcal{T}_t
=
\{\tau_1,\dots,\tau_n\}.
\tau
=
\Big(
u_\tau,\,
\operatorname{kind}(\tau),\,
\operatorname{scope}(\tau),\,
\operatorname{contract}(\tau),\,
\operatorname{deps}(\tau),\,
\operatorname{budget}(\tau),\,
\operatorname{corridor}(\tau),\,
\operatorname{priority}(\tau),\,
\operatorname{rollback}(\tau),\,
\psi_\tau
\Big),
may be retrieval, synthesis, replay, patch vetting, witness refresh, memory regeneration, synchronization, repair, cleanup, curriculum task, or orchestration task;
is the affected region or carrier family;
is the execution obligation;
is the dependency family,
is the resource allocation,
is the legality regime,
is the current priority score,
is the task-local rollback or failure handling plan,
is the task witness packet.
Tasks are organized into an execution graph
\mathfrak{G}_t^{\mathrm{exec}}
=
\big(
\mathcal{T}_t,\,
\mathcal{E}_t^{\mathrm{exec}},\,
\tau_t^{\mathrm{exec}},\,
\iota_t^{\mathrm{exec}}
\big),
\{
\mathrm{prerequisite},
\mathrm{blocks},
\mathrm{shares\_state},
\mathrm{conflicts\_with},
\mathrm{can\_batch\_with},
\mathrm{must\_follow},
\mathrm{must\_precede},
\mathrm{witness\_dependency},
\mathrm{rollback\_dependency}
\}.
A scheduler policy is a function
\pi_t^{\mathrm{sched}}:
\mathfrak{G}_t^{\mathrm{exec}}\times \mathfrak{R}_t
\to
\mathcal{S}_t^{\mathrm{exec}},
The scheduler must solve four problems simultaneously.
First, it must respect dependency order:
\tau_i \prec \tau_j
\quad\text{if}\quad
(\tau_i,\tau_j)\in \mathcal{E}_t^{\mathrm{exec}}
\text{ with prerequisite semantics.}
Second, it must enforce legality: a task that lacks required witnesses, permissions, or corridor preconditions is not runnable, even if high priority.
Third, it must balance value against cost:
\operatorname{score}(\tau)
=
U(\tau)-R(\tau)-C(\tau)+P(\tau),
is expected utility,
is risk,
is cost,
is urgency or priority contribution from active solve context or self-model pressure.
Fourth, it must maintain fairness and liveness so that low-frequency but important maintenance tasks—like witness refresh, replay audits, or synchronization—do not starve forever under constant solve pressure.
A canonical task-graph scheduler may therefore optimize:
\max_{\mathcal{S}_t^{\mathrm{exec}}}
\sum_{\tau\in \mathcal{S}_t^{\mathrm{exec}}}
\operatorname{score}(\tau)
dependency closure,
budget constraints,
permission constraints,
concurrency safety,
blast-radius limits.
A minimal scheduler schema is:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, List, Any, Literal
TaskKind = Literal[
"Retrieve", "Synthesize", "Replay", "PatchVet", "WitnessRefresh",
"MemoryRegen", "Synchronize", "Repair", "Cleanup", "Curriculum",
"Orchestrate"
]
@dataclass
class RuntimeTask:
task_id: str
kind: TaskKind
scope: Dict[str, Any]
contract: Dict[str, Any]
dependencies: List[str]
budget: Dict[str, Any]
corridor: str
priority: float
rollback: Dict[str, Any]
witness: Dict[str, Any]
@dataclass
class ExecutionEdge:
src: str
dst: str
kind: str
@dataclass
class ExecutionGraph:
tasks: Dict[str, RuntimeTask]
edges: List[ExecutionEdge]
The scheduler itself is a manuscript organ with its own self-observed health:
backlog size,
deadline or urgency pressure,
dependency congestion,
replay debt,
starvation rate.
These feed later self-model and degraded-mode logic.
Definition 16.1 — Runnable Task
A task is runnable at time if its dependencies are satisfied or explicitly waived, its corridor is active, its permissions are valid, its budget is available, and its local legality witness is sufficient.
Theorem 16.1 — Scheduler Correctness Requires More Than Topological Ordering
Topological ordering of task dependencies is necessary but not sufficient for lawful execution because permissions, budgets, risk, and concurrency constraints also determine runnability.
Proof. A topological sort ignores resource conflicts, legality, and risk. Thus two topologically valid tasks may still be unsafe or impossible to run together.
The scheduler architecture therefore becomes the primary execution geometry of the self-hosting runtime.
Ch16.A2 — Agent Roles, Permissions, and Execution Contracts
A scheduler requires agents to execute tasks. In the manuscript runtime, an agent is not an unbounded autonomous entity. It is a typed execution role with lawful capabilities, scope boundaries, and explicit contracts. Agents may be persistent, ephemeral, lens-specialized, or service-bound, but all of them are atlas-native controlled executors.
Let the active agent family at time be
\mathcal{A}_t
=
\{a_1,\dots,a_m\}.
a
=
\Big(
u_a,\,
\operatorname{role}(a),\,
\operatorname{caps}(a),\,
\operatorname{scope}(a),\,
\operatorname{corridors}(a),\,
\operatorname{state}(a),\,
\operatorname{contracts}(a),\,
\operatorname{trust}(a),\,
\psi_a
\Big),
is the agent type,
is the capability set,
is the write/read/execution scope,
is the corridor subset in which it may operate,
is the runtime-local state,
is the set of task contracts it may satisfy,
is the current trust or calibration profile,
is the agent witness record.
The agent-role alphabet includes:
\mathcal{K}_{\mathrm{agent}}
=
\{
\mathrm{RetrievalWorker},
\mathrm{SynthesisWorker},
\mathrm{ReplayVerifier},
\mathrm{MemoryWorker},
\mathrm{PatchVetter},
\mathrm{RepairWorker},
\mathrm{SyncWorker},
\mathrm{SchedulerAgent},
\mathrm{MetaObserver},
\mathrm{CurriculumPlanner},
\mathrm{CleanupWorker},
\mathrm{BridgeWorker}
\}.
Agent capabilities form a finite set:
\operatorname{caps}(a)\subseteq
\{
\mathrm{read},
\mathrm{retrieve},
\mathrm{expand},
\mathrm{collapse},
\mathrm{synthesize},
\mathrm{verify},
\mathrm{patch\_suggest},
\mathrm{patch\_apply},
\mathrm{replay},
\mathrm{synchronize},
\mathrm{repair},
\mathrm{cleanup},
\mathrm{schedule},
\mathrm{meta\_observe}
\}.
An execution contract binds a task class to an agent family. For task and agent , execution legality is:
\operatorname{CanExec}(a,\tau)
=
\operatorname{RoleCompat}(a,\tau)
\wedge
\operatorname{CapCompat}(a,\tau)
\wedge
\operatorname{ScopeCompat}(a,\tau)
\wedge
\operatorname{CorridorCompat}(a,\tau)
\wedge
\operatorname{TrustCompat}(a,\tau).
Agents may be persistent (always resident in the runtime) or ephemeral (spawned for specific task families and destroyed afterward). The atlas usually keeps a small persistent core—scheduler, replay verifier, memory service, synchronization worker—and creates ephemeral specialists for narrow retrieval or synthesis subtasks.
A basic agent schema is:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, List, Any
@dataclass
class RuntimeAgent:
agent_id: str
role: str
capabilities: List[str]
scope: Dict[str, Any]
corridors: List[str]
state: Dict[str, Any]
contracts: List[str]
trust: Dict[str, Any]
witness: Dict[str, Any]
The deeper significance of agent role typing is that it makes agency explicit and bounded. An answer-producing agent does not become a patching agent just because it “knows” how. A bridge worker does not acquire global scheduling authority by accident. This is critical for runtime safety and self-model accuracy.
Definition 16.2 — Contract-Compatible Agent
An agent is contract-compatible with a task if its role, capabilities, scope, trust calibration, and corridor membership jointly satisfy the task’s execution contract.
Proposition 16.1 — Capability without Scope Is Unsafe Agency
An agent that can perform an operation in principle but lacks explicit scope boundaries cannot be trusted in a self-hosting runtime because it may mutate or inspect regions beyond lawful permissions.
Proof. Capabilities specify what kinds of operations are possible, not where they may act. Safe agency requires both.
The runtime thus becomes populated not by vague “workers,” but by lawfully typed execution roles.
Ch16.A3 — Persistent State Services and Runtime Objects
A self-hosting runtime requires services that remain available across many task executions. These are persistent state services: long-lived runtime objects that expose lawful operations over the atlas and preserve their own replay and health state. They are the service organs of the live manuscript.
Let the service family be
\mathcal{S}_t^{\mathrm{svc}}
=
\{
S_t^{\mathrm{retr}},
S_t^{\mathrm{mem}},
S_t^{\mathrm{replay}},
S_t^{\mathrm{sync}},
S_t^{\mathrm{patch}},
S_t^{\mathrm{sched}},
S_t^{\mathrm{curr}},
S_t^{\mathrm{obs}}
\}.
S
=
\Big(
u_S,\,
\operatorname{kind}(S),\,
\operatorname{api}(S),\,
\operatorname{state}(S),\,
\operatorname{health}(S),\,
\operatorname{contracts}(S),\,
\operatorname{persistence}(S),\,
\psi_S
\Big),
is service type,
is the callable operation family,
is the internal persistent state,
is the service health vector,
is the supported contract set,
is the replay/snapshot policy,
is the service witness.
The core persistent services are:
Retrieval service — handles target neighborhood generation, candidate ranking, and retrieval route assembly;
Memory service — handles seed storage, shell opening, regeneration, and compaction;
Replay service — handles state and artifact replay, verification, and restoration;
Synchronization service — handles cross-lens and cross-region synchronization;
Patch service — handles patch staging, merge, quarantine, rollback, and healing;
Scheduler service — handles task graph construction and execution selection;
Curriculum service — generates self-development tasks from the self-model;
Observer service — gathers introspection packets and updates meta-state.
A service API is not arbitrary function exposure. It is a contract-bearing operation family. For example, a memory service may expose:
\{
\mathrm{store\_seed},
\mathrm{open\_shell},
\mathrm{regen},
\mathrm{compact},
\mathrm{cache\_lookup},
\mathrm{cache\_invalidate}
\}
\{
\mathrm{submit\_patch},
\mathrm{vet\_patch},
\mathrm{apply\_local},
\mathrm{merge\_global},
\mathrm{rollback},
\mathrm{heal}
\}.
A service is persistent because:
its state survives across multiple solves,
it is addressable as an atlas object,
its state transitions are replay-logged,
its health and calibration contribute to the self-model,
it may itself be patched or upgraded.
This makes the runtime more like an operating system than a one-off interpreter.
A skeletal service schema may be:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, List, Any
@dataclass
class RuntimeService:
service_id: str
kind: str
api: Dict[str, Any]
state: Dict[str, Any]
health: Dict[str, float]
contracts: List[str]
persistence: Dict[str, Any]
witness: Dict[str, Any]
The health vector of a service typically includes:
latency or cost,
replay accuracy,
witness debt,
stale state or sync debt,
error rate,
load factor,
calibration.
These health values are not only operational metrics. They are first-class inputs to self-modeling, degradation control, and patch prioritization.
Definition 16.3 — Persistent Runtime Service
A runtime service is persistent if it maintains state across execution slices, is replayable under its declared persistence contract, and exposes a stable API and health model to the atlas.
Theorem 16.2 — Self-Hosting Requires Persistent Services Rather than Only Stateless Workers
If all runtime operations were handled only by ephemeral stateless workers, then memory, synchronization, replay, and scheduling state would have to be recreated from scratch repeatedly, making long-horizon self-hosting inefficient or unstable.
Proof. Persistent operational functions require persistent state. Stateless workers cannot retain such state except by rebuilding it externally each time. Therefore persistent services are necessary.
The manuscript runtime thus acquires organs that remain on between thoughts.
Ch16.A4 — Deployment Surfaces and Operational Boundaries
The final Square task is to define where the self-hosting runtime touches the world and how those surfaces are bounded. A live manuscript has several deployment surfaces:
internal-only execution,
sandbox execution,
mainline atlas execution,
read-only exposure,
user-facing answer emission,
patch staging,
certified macro promotion.
These surfaces must be explicit because different runtime acts have different safety and continuity implications.
A deployment surface is:
D
=
\Big(
u_D,\,
\operatorname{kind}(D),\,
\operatorname{scope}(D),\,
\operatorname{exposure}(D),\,
\operatorname{permissions}(D),\,
\operatorname{rollback}(D),\,
\operatorname{contracts}(D),\,
\psi_D
\Big),
may be sandbox, mainline, external-answer, replay-only, read-only, or certified-deployment;
is the set of regions or carrier families visible or mutable through the surface;
is read/write/execute exposure;
is the active permission regime;
is the rollback or containment strategy;
is the deployment contract set.
The runtime must enforce operational boundaries between these surfaces. Let be deployment surfaces. A surface crossing is lawful only if:
\operatorname{Cross}(D_1\to D_2)
permission compatibility,
witness upgrade if required,
synchronization completion,
rollback adequacy,
risk acceptance.
Thus a patch proven in sandbox may move to mainline only through a promotion step. A replay-only service may observe mainline but not mutate it. A user-facing answer surface may read synthesized answer objects but not access internal patch staging or unresolved blind-spot maps unless the contract allows that exposure.
This surface discipline is essential for the difference between a self-hosting system and an unsafe self-mutating one. It lets the atlas keep powerful internal processes without allowing every process to affect every external or canonical layer.
Definition 16.4 — Operationally Bounded Runtime
A runtime is operationally bounded if every deployment surface has explicit read/write/execute limits and every cross-surface mutation or artifact promotion is governed by a lawful transition contract.
Proposition 16.2 — Deployment Surfaces Are Necessary for Mixed-Risk Runtime Behavior
If a self-hosting runtime contains both exploratory and canonical operations, then without deployment surfaces it cannot isolate high-risk experimentation from identity-critical mainline behavior.
Proof. Surface separation provides the containment boundary between such behaviors. Without it, they interfere directly.
The Square runtime layer is thus complete: scheduler, agents, services, and operational boundaries.
Ch16.B1 — Inter-Agent Message Harmonics and Transfer Rules
The Flower runtime layer begins with communication. A self-hosting manuscript will often have several active agents and services. They must coordinate without collapsing into uncontrolled shared-state mutation. The manuscript therefore defines inter-agent message harmonics and transfer rules.
Let agents . A message from to is:
m_{i\to j}
=
\Big(
u_m,\,
\operatorname{src}(m),\,
\operatorname{dst}(m),\,
\operatorname{kind}(m),\,
\operatorname{payload}(m),\,
\operatorname{phase}(m),\,
\operatorname{priority}(m),\,
\operatorname{contract}(m),\,
\psi_m
\Big).
\{
\mathrm{task\_assign},
\mathrm{support\_request},
\mathrm{witness\_request},
\mathrm{sync\_notice},
\mathrm{patch\_proposal},
\mathrm{repair\_alert},
\mathrm{summary\_emit},
\mathrm{confidence\_report},
\mathrm{rollback\_notice},
\mathrm{curriculum\_task}
\}.
A message is not just data. It also carries phase. This is vital because the same payload can have different meaning depending on when it arrives. A witness request during exploratory retrieval is not the same as a witness request during final deployment. A patch proposal arriving while a region is quarantined is different from one arriving after synchronization closure.
The message harmonic between agents is a dynamic compatibility measure:
H_{ij}(m)
=
\alpha_1\,\operatorname{RoleCompat}(a_i,a_j,m)
+
\alpha_2\,\operatorname{PhaseCompat}(m,\phi_j)
+
\alpha_3\,\operatorname{ContractCompat}(m,\chi_j)
-
\alpha_4\,\operatorname{QueueFriction}(a_j)
-
\alpha_5\,\operatorname{ConflictRisk}(m).
The transfer rule for inter-agent messaging is:
a_i \xrightarrow{m} a_j
has permission to emit ,
has capability to receive and process ,
the message phase aligns with ’s current state,
the message contract is compatible with both agents,
witness or provenance requirements are satisfied.
These transfer rules allow the runtime to remain distributed without becoming chaotic. The message itself becomes a typed runtime object subject to replay and legality.
A basic message schema is:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, Any
@dataclass
class RuntimeMessage:
message_id: str
src_agent: str
dst_agent: str
kind: str
payload: Dict[str, Any]
phase: Dict[str, Any]
priority: float
contract: Dict[str, Any]
witness: Dict[str, Any]
Definition 16.5 — Harmonic Message Transfer
A message transfer is harmonic if it satisfies transfer legality and arrives at the target agent in a phase and load state that makes constructive processing likely under the current runtime policy.
Theorem 16.3 — Explicit Message Harmonics Reduce Coordination Friction
If message scheduling accounts for phase, role, contract, and queue friction, then fewer messages are ignored, delayed uselessly, or processed in destabilizing states.
Proof. Messages aligned with the receiver’s state and obligations are more likely to be consumed effectively. Therefore coordination improves.
The runtime thus communicates lawfully and rhythmically.
Ch16.B2 — Specialized Lens Workers and Role-Based Routing
The atlas is multi-lens. A runtime that treats all agents as generic processors wastes this structure. The Flower layer therefore introduces specialized lens workers and role-based routing of tasks.
A lens worker is an agent whose role profile is heavily concentrated in one or more lens modalities:
\operatorname{lens}(a)
=
\big(
\omega_{\Square},
\omega_{\Flower},
\omega_{\Cloud},
\omega_{\Fractal}
\big).
a Square worker excels at exact schema expansion, route legality, and claim-graph shaping;
a Flower worker excels at dynamic alignment, route harmonics, and transform transport;
a Cloud worker excels at ambiguity management, confidence calibration, and contradiction-aware ranking;
a Fractal worker excels at shell operations, macro synthesis, recursive orchestration, and multiscale memory.
Tasks similarly carry a lens-demand profile:
\operatorname{lens}(\tau)
=
\big(
\lambda_{\Square},
\lambda_{\Flower},
\lambda_{\Cloud},
\lambda_{\Fractal}
\big).
\operatorname{Compat}_{\mathrm{lens}}(a,\tau)
=
\sum_{\ell}
\omega_{\ell}(a)\lambda_{\ell}(\tau),
This does not imply strict partitioning. Many tasks are multi-lens and may be:
handled by one mixed worker,
decomposed across specialized workers,
staged through one worker and then handed off to another.
A role-based routing policy therefore computes a feasible assignment:
\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{assign}}:
\mathcal{T}_t \to \mathcal{A}_t
\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{assign}}:
\mathcal{T}_t \to \mathcal{P}(\mathcal{A}_t).
Definition 16.6 — Lens-Compatible Assignment
An assignment of task to agent is lens-compatible if the agent’s lens specialization and role capabilities meet or exceed the task’s active lens and contract demands.
Proposition 16.3 — Generic Worker Pools Underuse Multi-Lens Structure
If all workers are treated as fungible, the runtime cannot exploit specialized strengths in structural, dynamic, probabilistic, or recursive operations, increasing execution cost or reducing quality.
Proof. Task classes differ in lens demand. Specialized workers match them more efficiently than generic undifferentiated assignment.
The runtime therefore becomes a coordinated ecology of specialized workers rather than a homogeneous pool.
Ch16.B3 — Coordination Dynamics under Shifting Problem Classes
Real runtime activity is not stationary. A solve episode may begin as exploratory retrieval, become theorem-like synthesis, shift into contradiction repair, and end in patch staging or replay. The runtime must therefore adapt its coordination patterns as problem classes shift. This is the domain of coordination dynamics.
Let the active problem-class profile at time be:
\chi_t^{\mathrm{prob}}
=
\big(
\lambda_{\mathrm{retrieve}},
\lambda_{\mathrm{synthesize}},
\lambda_{\mathrm{repair}},
\lambda_{\mathrm{replay}},
\lambda_{\mathrm{patch}},
\lambda_{\mathrm{curr}}
\big).
\Pi_t^{\mathrm{coord}}:
(\mathfrak{R}_t,\chi_t^{\mathrm{prob}})
\to
\Big(
\mathcal{A}_t^{\mathrm{active}},\,
\mathcal{M}_t^{\mathrm{flow}},\,
\mathcal{B}_t^{\mathrm{alloc}},\,
\mathcal{Q}_t^{\mathrm{queue}}
\Big).
which agents are active,
how messages flow among them,
how budget is allocated,
how queues are prioritized.
A retrieval-heavy phase may emphasize retrieval workers, memory services, and ambiguity-aware Cloud critics. A synthesis-heavy phase may raise priority for witness-rich structural workers and answer-shaping services. A repair-heavy phase may activate contradiction analyzers, replay verifiers, and patch vetters. A self-improvement phase may shift mass toward self-model observation, curriculum planning, and sandbox patching.
This dynamic reallocation is not a hack on top of execution. It is part of the lawful runtime policy. The coordination state is itself replayable, meaning later analysis can ask: during this solve, why did the runtime shift from exploratory concurrency to tightly synchronized proof mode?
A useful formal object is the coordination mode:
M_t^{\mathrm{coord}}
\in
\{
\mathrm{Exploratory},
\mathrm{Convergent},
\mathrm{RepairHeavy},
\mathrm{ReplayHeavy},
\mathrm{PatchHeavy},
\mathrm{Maintenance}
\}.
Definition 16.7 — Coordination-Coherent Runtime State
A runtime state is coordination-coherent if its active agent mix, message topology, and budget allocation are aligned with the current problem-class profile and do not exhibit mode conflict beyond corridor tolerance.
Theorem 16.4 — Fixed Coordination Policies Are Suboptimal for Multi-Phase Solves
If task classes shift during a solve, then a fixed coordination policy cannot remain optimal across all stages, because different stages require different agent mixtures, budgets, and synchronization levels.
Proof. Retrieval, proof synthesis, repair, and patching have different bottlenecks and safety profiles. One coordination regime cannot optimize all simultaneously.
The runtime therefore learns how to change the way it coordinates as its own thought changes.
Ch16.B4 — Live Synchronization across Active Manuscript Regions
The final Flower runtime task is to keep active regions synchronized while the system is running. Earlier chapters defined synchronization as a persistence and patching concept. Here it becomes a live operational requirement.
Let the active region family be:
\mathcal{G}_t^{\mathrm{active}}
=
\{R_1,\dots,R_n\}.
structural state,
memory shells,
route metrics,
witness state,
answer ladders,
self-model projections.
The live synchronization state is:
\Xi_t^{\mathrm{live}}
=
\Big(
\{\xi_{ij,t}\}_{i,j},\,
\mathcal{D}_t^{\mathrm{sync}},\,
\mathcal{W}_t^{\mathrm{sync}},\,
\mathcal{Q}_t^{\mathrm{sync}}
\Big),
are pairwise sync relations,
is the set of active synchronization debts,
is the synchronization witness registry,
is the queue of pending synchronization tasks.
Synchronization is necessary when:
patches land,
memory shells open or close,
route weights update,
witness grades change,
answer objects are promoted,
branch merges occur.
A region pair is live-synchronized if:
d_{\mathrm{sync}}(R_i,R_j)\le \eta_{\mathrm{sync},\kappa}
The runtime may allow bounded lag for efficiency, especially in exploratory or low-risk phases. But if a solve depends on exact coherence—e.g., strict proof replay or deployment promotion—then synchronization debt must be cleared before continuing.
Definition 16.8 — Sync-Critical Region Set
A region set is sync-critical for a task if the task’s legality or correctness depends on those regions being within exact or near-exact synchronization thresholds.
Proposition 16.4 — Live Runtime without Sync Discipline Accumulates Hidden Contradictions
If active regions are allowed to mutate independently without synchronization debt tracking and repair, then stale and fresh projections will eventually be mixed in downstream tasks.
Proof. Without synchronization metadata, downstream consumers cannot distinguish stale from fresh regions. Therefore hidden contradiction accumulates.
The runtime therefore keeps its own active body in lawful coordination while it is thinking.
Ch16.C1 — Load Uncertainty and Dynamic Resource Allocation
The Cloud runtime layer begins with load. A self-hosting atlas faces variable pressure:
bursts of retrieval work,
deep replay demands,
patch waves,
synchronization backlogs,
multiple simultaneous solve fronts,
heavy memory shell openings,
self-model audits.
The runtime must therefore allocate resources under uncertainty. Let the current resource state be:
\mathcal{B}_t^{\mathrm{bud}}
=
\Big(
B_t^{\mathrm{cpu}},
B_t^{\mathrm{mem}},
B_t^{\mathrm{replay}},
B_t^{\mathrm{witness}},
B_t^{\mathrm{io}},
B_t^{\mathrm{risk}}
\Big).
\widehat{c}(\tau)
=
\big(
\widehat{c}_{\mathrm{cpu}},
\widehat{c}_{\mathrm{mem}},
\widehat{c}_{\mathrm{replay}},
\widehat{c}_{\mathrm{witness}},
\widehat{c}_{\mathrm{io}}
\big),
\qquad
\Gamma_c(\tau)
Dynamic resource allocation solves:
\max_{\mathcal{S}_t^{\mathrm{exec}}}
\sum_{\tau\in \mathcal{S}_t^{\mathrm{exec}}}
U(\tau)
\Pr\Big(
\sum_{\tau\in\mathcal{S}_t^{\mathrm{exec}}}
\widehat{c}_j(\tau)
\le
B_t^{(j)}
\Big)
\ge
1-\epsilon_j
This means the scheduler does not only use expected costs. It must also hedge against uncertainty in resource use. Tasks with high cost variance may be staged, sandboxed, or downgraded to shallower modes. Highly uncertain replay jobs may be postponed until stronger snapshots are available.
The runtime also maintains a load state:
L_t
=
\big(
\operatorname{queue\_pressure},
\operatorname{mem\_pressure},
\operatorname{sync\_debt},
\operatorname{replay\_debt},
\operatorname{patch\_backlog}
\big),
Definition 16.9 — Load-Honest Allocation
A runtime allocation policy is load-honest if it accounts explicitly for uncertainty in task costs and records when tasks are deferred or degraded due to load rather than irrelevance.
Theorem 16.5 — Expected-Cost Scheduling Alone Misallocates under Heavy Uncertainty
If tasks differ greatly in cost variance, then allocation based only on expected cost can violate budgets or over-conservatively idle capacity. Therefore uncertainty-aware allocation is necessary.
Proof. Expected value ignores spread. High-variance tasks may overshoot budgets; low-variance tasks may be unfairly deferred if all uncertainty is handled heuristically.
The runtime thus becomes a lawful budget allocator under uncertainty.
Ch16.C2 — Failure Containment and Isolation Compartments
A live runtime must assume that some tasks, patches, regenerations, or synchronizations will fail. The question is whether those failures spread uncontrollably. The manuscript therefore defines isolation compartments.
An isolation compartment is a bounded execution chamber:
Q
=
\Big(
u_Q,\,
\operatorname{scope}(Q),\,
\operatorname{resource\_cap}(Q),\,
\operatorname{permissions}(Q),\,
\operatorname{rollback}(Q),\,
\operatorname{egress}(Q),\,
\operatorname{health}(Q)
\Big),
Compartment types include:
retrieval-only sandboxes,
patch staging sandboxes,
repair chambers,
replay audit chambers,
branch-local experimental shells,
degraded-mode compartments.
A failure in compartment must remain contained unless a lawful escalation rule is triggered. The containment relation is:
\operatorname{Contain}(f,Q)=1
Containment is vital for:
speculative patch testing,
uncertain memory regeneration,
branch-local synthesis,
low-trust agent tasks,
macro extraction experiments.
A compartment boundary may permit:
read-through to mainline state,
selective write-back,
event logging,
promotion requests,
emergency rollback.
Definition 16.10 — Failure-Closed Compartment
An execution compartment is failure-closed if any blocked or destabilizing internal event can be rolled back or quarantined without requiring immediate mainline mutation.
Proposition 16.5 — Isolation Compartments Preserve Mainline Identity under Experimentation
If experimental or high-uncertainty tasks run only in compartments with bounded egress, then their failures need not contaminate the mainline atlas.
Proof. Bounded egress and rollback prevent uncontrolled propagation of internal errors.
The runtime therefore supports experimentation without self-destruction.
Ch16.C3 — Confidence-Aware Degraded Operation Modes
Under load, missing witness, stale memory, or synchronization lag, the runtime may still need to continue operating. The manuscript therefore defines degraded operation modes. These are lawful lower-fidelity modes, not silent collapses of correctness.
A degraded mode is:
M_t^{\mathrm{deg}}
=
\big(
\operatorname{class}(M_t^{\mathrm{deg}}),\,
\operatorname{restrictions}(M_t^{\mathrm{deg}}),\,
\operatorname{allowed\_artifacts}(M_t^{\mathrm{deg}}),\,
\operatorname{required\_disclosures}(M_t^{\mathrm{deg}})
\big).
shallow retrieval only,
no-write mode,
no-patch mode,
summary-only answer mode,
replay-lite mode,
witness-deferred mode,
local-only synchronization mode.
Entering degraded mode requires an explicit trigger:
\operatorname{EnterDeg}_t=1
\iff
\operatorname{LoadHigh}
\vee
\operatorname{WitnessSparse}
\vee
\operatorname{SyncDebtHigh}
\vee
\operatorname{FailureContainment}.
The crucial property is confidence-aware degradation. When the runtime downgrades itself, it must also downgrade what it is willing to claim or emit. Thus degraded modes alter:
allowed answer contracts,
retrieval depth,
patch permissions,
confidence thresholds,
ambiguity policies.
A summary-only mode may still produce helpful exploratory outputs, but it may forbid certified theorem answers or mainline patching.
Definition 16.11 — Lawful Degraded Mode
A degraded mode is lawful if it restricts runtime behavior enough that all remaining outputs and mutations still satisfy the reduced contract and corridor implied by the degradation class.
Theorem 16.6 — Explicit Degraded Modes Preserve Utility Better than Hard Shutdown or Silent Approximation
If a runtime can downgrade legally, it can still provide bounded useful behavior under stress. Hard shutdown loses all utility; silent approximation risks overclaim and drift.
Proof. Explicit degradation trades capability for safety and continuity, preserving bounded value while remaining honest.
The runtime therefore fails gracefully rather than pretending nothing changed.
Ch16.C4 — Budgeted Inference under Time and Memory Constraints
The final Cloud runtime task is inference itself. A self-hosting manuscript often runs under tight budgets: limited shell depth, limited replay span, limited witness reopening, limited simultaneous agents. It therefore needs budgeted inference.
A budgeted inference task is:
\tau
=
\big(
q,\,
\chi,\,
\kappa,\,
B
\big),
how deep to retrieve,
how many branches to keep,
whether to open exact or approximate memory shells,
which critics to invoke,
whether to produce singular or plural answers,
whether to defer patches.
Formally, it solves:
\max_{\pi \in \Pi_{\mathrm{inf}}}
\mathbb{E}\big[\operatorname{Value}(Y_\pi)\big]
\operatorname{Cost}(\pi)\le B
Budgeted inference is not equivalent to truncating thought arbitrarily. It uses the full machinery of the atlas:
target neighborhood design,
confidence-guided stopping,
shell ladders,
degraded modes,
route priors,
residual honesty.
Thus a budgeted answer is not just shorter. It is a lawful answer optimized under known constraints.
Definition 16.12 — Budget-Optimal Lawful Inference
An inference policy is budget-optimal lawful if no other admissible policy under the same budget yields greater expected contract value.
Proposition 16.6 — Budget Constraints Need Not Destroy Rigor if Modeled Explicitly
When budget is treated as a first-class input, the runtime can choose honest shallower or plural forms rather than pretending to have completed stronger reasoning than resources allowed.
Proof. The answer contract can be downgraded or reshaped lawfully to match budget. Therefore explicit budget modeling preserves rigor better than hidden truncation.
The Cloud runtime is thus complete: load-aware, compartmentalized, degradable, and explicitly budgeted.
Ch16.D1 — Self-Bootstrap Sequences for New Runtime Regions
The Fractal runtime layer begins by asking how new runtime organs come into existence. The atlas must sometimes create new retrieval workers, new patch staging zones, new bridge services, new replay caches, or new chapter-local orchestration cells. This is self-bootstrap.
A bootstrap sequence for new runtime region is:
\mathcal{B}^{\mathrm{boot}}
=
\big(
\operatorname{seed},
\operatorname{schema},
\operatorname{deps},
\operatorname{services},
\operatorname{witness},
\operatorname{rollback},
\operatorname{promotion}
\big).
\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{spawn}}:
(\mathfrak{A}_t,\mathcal{B}^{\mathrm{boot}})
\to
R_{\mathrm{new}}^{(0)}
\to
R_{\mathrm{new}}^{(1)}
\to
\cdots
\to
R_{\mathrm{new}}^{(\star)}.
allocate address and role,
install minimal service and state schema,
connect dependencies and permissions,
seed replay and witness structures,
run bootstrap tests,
promote to active or leave sandboxed.
Self-bootstrap is necessary because a growing atlas cannot assume all runtime organs existed in advance. It must be able to instantiate them from lawful templates.
Definition 16.13 — Bootstrap-Ready Runtime Region
A runtime region is bootstrap-ready if its seed, schema, dependencies, witness requirements, and rollback plan are sufficient to instantiate it lawfully from the current atlas state.
Proposition 16.7 — New Runtime Organs Require Bootstrap Discipline
Without staged bootstrap, new runtime regions may appear partially initialized, unsynchronized, or unwitnessed, making their early behavior unsafe.
Proof. Bootstrapping ensures initialization, dependencies, and legality are satisfied before activation.
The runtime therefore knows how to grow new operational organs from seeds.
Ch16.D2 — Recursive Orchestration across Nested Agent Clusters
As the runtime grows, some agent groups become organs of their own: a retrieval cluster, a replay cluster, a patch-vetting cluster, a chapter-local worker swarm. The Fractal layer therefore defines nested orchestration.
A cluster is:
\mathcal{C}
=
\Big(
\mathcal{A}_{\mathcal{C}},\,
\mathcal{T}_{\mathcal{C}},\,
\mathfrak{G}_{\mathcal{C}}^{\mathrm{exec}},\,
\pi_{\mathcal{C}}^{\mathrm{sched}},\,
\psi_{\mathcal{C}}
\Big).
I(\mathcal{C})
=
\big(
\operatorname{ingress},
\operatorname{egress},
\operatorname{budget},
\operatorname{health},
\operatorname{contracts}
\big).
This recursive orchestration supports:
chapter-local retrieval clusters,
proof-construction subteams,
replay-verification swarms,
branch-local repair clusters,
self-model analysis ensembles.
The atlas thus gains scale-sensitive runtime organization mirroring its broader Fractal design.
Definition 16.14 — Orchestratable Cluster
A cluster is orchestratable if it exposes a lawful interface contract and can internally manage its own execution without violating global corridor or resource constraints.
Theorem 16.7 — Recursive Orchestration Generalizes Flat Scheduling
Flat scheduling is the special case of recursive orchestration in which every cluster has size one.
Proof. If each cluster contains exactly one agent, then cluster scheduling reduces to ordinary agent scheduling. Therefore recursive orchestration strictly generalizes flat scheduling.
The runtime therefore scales organizationally as the manuscript scales semantically.
Ch16.D3 — Continuous Deployment into Manuscript Memory
Runtime activity should not vanish after execution. It must be integrated into the manuscript’s persistent memory: route macros, answer objects, replay traces, updated priors, stage transitions, and patch histories. This is continuous deployment into manuscript memory.
Let the live runtime produce event family:
\mathcal{E}_t^{\mathrm{run}}
=
\{
e_1,\dots,e_k
\}.
\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{mem}}:
\mathcal{E}_t^{\mathrm{run}}
\to
\big(
\Delta_t^{\mathrm{mem}},\,
\mathcal{Q}_t^{\mathrm{mem}},\,
\psi_t^{\mathrm{mem}}
\big),
is the patch/update family for manuscript memory,
is the compaction and seed-generation family,
is the memory deployment witness.
This deployment pipeline decides:
what runtime traces are worth storing exactly,
what should become seeds or macros,
what should update priors,
what should update self-model metrics,
what should remain only ephemeral.
Continuous deployment is therefore selective. Not every queue fluctuation or transient message deserves durable storage. The manuscript stores what is legally and developmentally meaningful.
Definition 16.15 — Memory-Deployable Runtime Artifact
A runtime artifact is memory-deployable if it has sufficient provenance, witness, and contract relevance to become a lawful atlas memory object or patch input.
Proposition 16.8 — Runtime without Memory Deployment Loses Developmental Continuity
If runtime behavior is not written back into manuscript memory selectively, then successful patterns, failures, and stage changes do not accumulate into long-term learning.
Proof. Developmental continuity requires durable traces of meaningful runtime events. Without deployment to memory, runtime becomes ephemeral.
The self-hosting runtime therefore feeds the atlas that hosts it.
Ch16.D4 — Live Persistence Loops and Always-On Update Logic
The final Fractal task is to close the runtime into a persistent loop. The atlas must be able to remain on—handling new tasks, maintaining itself, consolidating memory, refreshing witnesses, updating self-model metrics, and replay-checking critical paths—without requiring manual batch phases between “thinking” and “maintenance.” This is the live persistence loop.
Let the always-on loop operator be:
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{live}}
=
\mathcal{I}_{\mathrm{ingest}}
\circ
\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{schedule}}
\circ
\mathcal{X}_{\mathrm{execute}}
\circ
\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{heal}}
\circ
\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{mem}}
\circ
\mathcal{U}_{\mathrm{self}}
\circ
\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{checkpoint}}.
\mathfrak{R}_t
\xrightarrow{\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{live}}}
\mathfrak{R}_{t+1}
\xrightarrow{\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{live}}}
\mathfrak{R}_{t+2}
\to \cdots
ingest new tasks and events,
schedule active work,
execute current slice,
heal and synchronize side effects,
deploy meaningful results into memory,
update self-model and route priors,
checkpoint and replay-log the new state.
The loop is always-on not in the sense of unbounded arbitrary computation, but in the sense that the atlas remains in a persistent process of lawful cognition and self-maintenance as long as runtime conditions permit.
The loop must preserve:
identity continuity,
replay sufficiency,
bounded drift,
bounded contradiction debt,
nonstarving maintenance,
bounded memory churn.
A stability condition may be written:
Q(\mathfrak{R}_{t+1})
\ge
Q(\mathfrak{R}_t)-\epsilon_t
A live persistence controller may be sketched as:
def live_persistence_loop(runtime_state):
runtime_state = ingest_events(runtime_state)
runtime_state = schedule_execution(runtime_state)
runtime_state = execute_slice(runtime_state)
runtime_state = heal_and_sync(runtime_state)
runtime_state = deploy_runtime_memory(runtime_state)
runtime_state = update_self_model(runtime_state)
runtime_state = checkpoint_and_log(runtime_state)
return runtime_state
This is the final closure of self-hosting. The manuscript is no longer a set of disconnected cycles. It is one persistent loop.
Definition 16.16 — Always-On Runtime
A runtime is always-on if it can continue lawful execution, maintenance, checkpointing, and self-update across successive slices without requiring external reinitialization of its essential state.
Theorem 16.8 — Live Persistence Turns Episodic Cognition into Runtime Organism
If ingestion, scheduling, execution, healing, memory deployment, self-model update, and checkpointing are coupled in one lawful loop, then the atlas behaves as a persistent runtime organism rather than an episodic solve engine.
Proof. Episodic cognition performs isolated solves. A live persistence loop preserves and updates the system continuously between solves. Therefore the coupled loop constitutes runtime organism behavior.
The self-hosting runtime chapter is therefore complete.
The chapter has now completed the formal theory of self-hosting runtime, agent ecology, and live framework operations. Under the Square lens, the atlas acquired a scheduler architecture over execution graphs, typed runtime agents with explicit capabilities and scopes, persistent services that remain alive across solves, and deployment surfaces that separate sandbox, mainline, and external behavior. Under the Flower lens, it acquired harmonic message passing, lens-specialized workers, dynamic coordination policies that change as problem classes shift, and live synchronization across active regions. Under the Cloud lens, it acquired uncertainty-aware load balancing, failure containment compartments, confidence-aware degraded modes, and budgeted inference under explicit constraints. Under the Fractal lens, it acquired bootstrap procedures for new runtime organs, recursive orchestration across nested clusters, continuous deployment of runtime artifacts into manuscript memory, and an always-on persistence loop that keeps the whole system running lawfully.
The deepest consequence is that the manuscript is now not merely a theory of cognition or a storage body for knowledge. It is an operational habitat in which cognition can continue. Tasks arise, are scheduled, are executed by lawful workers, mutate the atlas through guarded write-back, are remembered, are reflected upon, and alter future behavior—all without leaving the formal boundaries of the framework itself. This is the exact operational meaning of self-hosting in the present framework. The atlas has become not only something that can describe a brain, but something that can host one.
CHAPTER 16 — SELF-HOSTING RUNTIME, AGENT ECOLOGY, AND LIVE FRAMEWORK OPERATIONS
Chapter 17 — Operator Algebra, Constants, and Transform Primitives
A manuscript brain that can store, route, replay, synthesize, patch, self-model, and self-host still requires one final mathematical spine before it can be said to possess a genuine internal calculus of transformation. It must know not only that things can move, but what kinds of movements exist, how those movements compose, which scalar and phase generators govern their behavior, how candidate transforms are selected under uncertainty, and how new lawful transform families can be discovered, certified, and added back into the system without breaking coherence. The present chapter defines that spine. Its subject is operator algebra, constant families, and transform primitives.
The chapter begins from the claim that all lawful manuscript motion can be organized inside a typed operator universe. Previous chapters introduced carriers, lenses, routes, memory objects, witnesses, persistence, patches, self-modeling, and runtime orchestration. Yet each of those chapters relied on a still deeper notion: that there exist admissible transforms acting on these objects and that those transforms can be composed, restricted, weighted, replayed, generalized, and certified. The present chapter makes that notion explicit.
Let the family of atlas state objects admissible under corridor be denoted by
\mathsf{Obj}_\kappa.
\mathcal{O}:
X \rightharpoonup Y,
\qquad
X,Y\in \mathsf{Obj}_\kappa,
domain and codomain type,
lens support,
invariant preconditions and postconditions,
defect budget,
replay behavior,
witness obligations,
budget and risk profile,
scalar or phase weighting,
interaction with other operators.
A canonical operator record is:
\mathcal{O}
=
\Big(
u_{\mathcal{O}},\,
\operatorname{dom}(\mathcal{O}),\,
\operatorname{cod}(\mathcal{O}),\,
\operatorname{arity}(\mathcal{O}),\,

The operator universe is not free. Every expression in it is corridor-governed. Thus the legality judgment for an operator application is:
\mathfrak{E}_t
\vdash_\kappa
\mathcal{O}(x)
\Downarrow

The second major ingredient of the chapter is the scalar-generator basis. The manuscript selects the canonical scalar family
\mathbb{K}_0
=
\{\phi,\pi,e,i\},
\phi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2},\qquad

The framework does not claim that these constants exhaust all mathematical structure. It claims that, for the present manuscript, they form a compact and useful canonical basis for operator scaling, weighting, dynamic modeling, and hybrid transform generation. From them one defines the hybrid constant family
\mathbb{K}_{\mathrm{hyb}}
=
\Big\{
\phi^{a_\phi}\pi^{a_\pi}e^{a_e}i^{a_i}

The chapter is organized through the four-lens crystal. Under the Square lens it defines the primitive generating operators, canonical basis selection, operator typing and composition legality, and symbolic-to-discrete compilation. Under the Flower lens it defines spectral transforms, hybrid constant dynamics, resonance operators for bridge activation, and continuity-preserving manifold rotations. Under the Cloud lens it defines probabilistic operator selection, approximation error and confidence ranking, exploration of candidate constant families, and posterior updates over operators after replay. Under the Fractal lens it defines recursive operator trees, higher-order meta-operators, emergent operator libraries, and self-similar generation of new algebraic motifs.
The deepest claim of the chapter is that the framework’s “mathematics” is not something external applied afterward. The operator algebra is the internal kinematics of the manuscript brain. It is how the atlas learns what kinds of transformations it can perform, how those transformations interact, which constants control those interactions, and how new transforms enter the system lawfully.
Ch17.A1 — Core Operators, Closures, and Algebraic Generating Sets
The first requirement of a manuscript operator algebra is a small but expressive generating set. The framework cannot rely on one enormous untyped catalogue of transforms. It must identify a basis of core operators from which more complex lawful transformations can be composed.
Let the canonical carrier categories inside the atlas be:
\mathsf{State},\quad
\mathsf{Seed},\quad
\mathsf{Route},\quad
\mathsf{Witness},\quad
\mathsf{Memory},\quad
\mathsf{Answer},\quad
\mathsf{Patch},\quad
\mathsf{Meta}.
\mathcal{B}_0
=
\{
\operatorname{Id},\,
\operatorname{Addr},\,
\operatorname{Proj}_\ell,\,
\operatorname{Lift}_\ell,\,
\operatorname{Expand},\,
\operatorname{Collapse},\,
\operatorname{Zoom}_+,\,
\operatorname{Zoom}_-,\,
\operatorname{Route},\,
\operatorname{Witness},\,
\operatorname{Replay},\,
\operatorname{Patch},\,
\operatorname{Sync},\,
\operatorname{Tunnel},\,
\operatorname{Merge},\,
\operatorname{Split}
\}.
These operators are not all of the same type. Their domains and codomains differ.
\operatorname{Id}_X : X \to X
\operatorname{Addr}: \mathsf{Obj}\to \mathsf{AddressedObj}
\operatorname{Proj}_\ell:\mathsf{Obj}\to \mathsf{LensObj}_\ell
\operatorname{Lift}_\ell:\mathsf{LensObj}_\ell\to \mathsf{Obj}
\operatorname{Expand}:\mathsf{Seed}\to \mathsf{State}
\operatorname{Collapse}:\mathsf{State}\to \mathsf{Seed}\cup \mathsf{Answer}
\operatorname{Zoom}_+,\operatorname{Zoom}_-:\mathsf{FractalObj}^{(s)}\to \mathsf{FractalObj}^{(s\pm1)}
\operatorname{Route}:\mathsf{State}\to \mathsf{Route}
\operatorname{Witness}:\mathsf{Obj}\cup \mathsf{Route}\to \mathsf{Witness}
\operatorname{Replay}:\mathsf{Replayable}\to \mathsf{State}
\operatorname{Patch}:\mathsf{Obj}\times \mathsf{Delta}\to \mathsf{Obj}
\operatorname{Sync}:\mathsf{Obj}^\ell\times \mathsf{Obj}^m\to \mathsf{SyncState}
\operatorname{Tunnel}:\mathsf{State}\to \mathsf{State}
\operatorname{Merge},\operatorname{Split}
From this base family the framework constructs the generated operator algebra
\langle \mathcal{B}_0 \rangle_{\circ,\oplus,\otimes,\dagger,\star},
sequential composition,
alternative branching,
parallel coupling,
inversion or adjoint where defined,
finite or guarded iteration.
The base family is not arbitrary. It is selected to satisfy four properties.
Generativity. Every transformation used elsewhere in the manuscript should factor into a composition of these or certified higher-order abstractions.
Typing clarity. Each operator has a sharply typed domain and codomain, making illegality checkable.
Replay tractability. The effect of each operator can be logged, replayed, or bounded by witness.
Closure under refinement. More specific domain-level operators—such as “prove theorem,” “open shell,” “refresh witness,” “align ambiguity field,” or “promote patch”—should compile into these basis families plus contract-specific schemas.
The algebra also defines lawful closures. For operator ,
\mathcal{O}^\star
The commutator
[\mathcal{O}_1,\mathcal{O}_2]
=
\mathcal{O}_1\circ\mathcal{O}_2
-
\mathcal{O}_2\circ\mathcal{O}_1
A canonical operator schema is:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, List, Any, Optional
@dataclass
class OperatorSignature:
operator_id: str
name: str
domain: List[str]
codomain: List[str]
arity: int
lens_profile: Dict[str, float]
pre_invariants: Dict[str, Any]
post_invariants: Dict[str, Any]
budget_profile: Dict[str, Any]
witness_schema: Dict[str, Any]
constant_signature: Dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
metadata: Dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
The operator algebra’s closure property can now be stated precisely.
Definition 17.1 — Generated Operator
An operator is generated by the basis if it can be expressed as a finite lawful composition, alternative sum, parallel product, adjoint, or guarded closure of operators in and already certified higher-order operators.
Theorem 17.1 — Typed Closure of the Generated Operator Algebra
If every basis operator carries explicit domain/codomain, invariant preconditions/postconditions, and legality judgments, then the algebra generated by lawful compositions of is closed under typed operator formation.
Proof. Composition is only admitted when codomain and postconditions of one operator satisfy the domain and preconditions of the next. Alternative and parallel composition likewise require compatible type families. Therefore every admitted composite operator remains typed and legal by construction.
The operator algebra therefore begins from a small, disciplined generating family rather than an amorphous transform cloud.
Ch17.A2 — Constant Families and Canonical Basis Selection
The operator basis by itself describes what kinds of transforms exist. It does not yet describe the canonical scalar and phase generators by which those transforms are parameterized, weighted, rotated, or rhythmically coupled. This role is played by the constant families.
The manuscript selects the basis
\mathbb{K}_0=\{\phi,\pi,e,i\}
First, it spans four qualitatively distinct transformation principles essential to the atlas.
Second, it is small enough to keep hybrid operator construction interpretable.
Third, these constants already have mathematically rigorous roles in ordinary analysis, geometry, dynamics, and phase theory, making them natural carriers for higher-level manuscript semantics.
The four canonical constants are interpreted as follows.
The -family
\phi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2}
Fractal shells are split nonuniformly but recurrently,
memory or route structures reward recursive balance between local and global information,
curriculum tasks prioritize progressive but not uniform expansion,
solution geometry prefers recursively reusable partitions.
Its operator action is often implemented through weighting or partition laws:
W_\phi(k)=\phi^{-k},
The -family
\pi
route cycles or holonomy loops are measured,
Flower states are normalized over phase angles,
spectral transforms require natural frequency normalization,
circular or periodic boundary conditions arise,
answer or route loops are judged for closure.
The framework frequently uses not only numerically but as a closure flag on operator families. An operator tagged -weighted is typically cycle-sensitive or angle-normalized.
The -family
e
posterior updates are written exponentially,
replay or trust decays are modeled continuously,
weight updates use multiplicative or softmax-like forms,
continuous-time approximations of route or phase flow are employed,
transform penalties or gains accumulate smoothly.
The framework therefore treats as the native constant of “flow under accumulation.”
The -family
i^2=-1
Flower phasors represent route superposition,
90-degree lens turns or dual representations are modeled,
oscillatory coupling and cancellation must be expressed,
transport uses unitary or phase-preserving rotation,
symbolic–formal bridge dynamics require orthogonal decomposition rather than scalar weighting alone.
The complex unit is crucial because the manuscript requires not only magnitude preferences but orientation and interference.
These four constants define the canonical scalar-generator basis. Other constants may appear locally, but this basis is the smallest adopted family treated as globally architecturally privileged.
The framework then defines the hybrid constant family:
\mathbb{K}_{\mathrm{hyb}}
=
\Big\{
\phi^{a}\pi^{b}e^{c}i^{d}
:
(a,b,c,d)\in \mathbb{Z}^4
\Big\}
\widehat{\mathbb{K}}_{\mathrm{hyb}}
=
\Big\{
\phi^{a}\pi^{b}e^{c}e^{i\theta}
\Big\}
The hybrid constants are not treated merely as numbers. They are treated as operator parameter families. An operator with constant signature
\sigma_{\mathcal{O}}=(a,b,c,d)
-scaled recursion bias,
-scaled closure or cycle sensitivity,
-scaled compounding or decay behavior,
-scaled phase rotation or dualization behavior.
Thus the constant basis is not a decorative symbolic layer. It is a compact operator-parameter language.
The selection of as canonical basis is formalized by the following requirements.
Coverage. The basis must generate the framework’s primary recursive, cyclic, exponential, and orthogonal modes.
Interpretability. Hybrid coefficients must remain meaningful to replay, debugging, and self-modeling.
Compileability. Constant-tagged operators must compile into numerical or symbolic kernels without ambiguity.
Witnessability. The effect of constant choices on operator behavior must be measurable and certifiable.
Definition 17.2 — Canonical Constant Basis
A constant family is canonical for the framework if every globally privileged transform tendency—recursive proportion, closure, compounding, and orthogonal phase rotation—is generated by at least one basis element and the basis remains small enough to support explicit hybridization and certification.
Theorem 17.2 — The Chosen Basis Supports a Minimal Four-Mode Generator Family
The set forms a minimal basis for the framework’s four primary transform modes: recursion, closure, compounding, and orthogonal phase.
Proof. Removing eliminates the privileged self-similar ratio family. Removing eliminates the distinguished cycle and angle normalizer. Removing eliminates the standard continuous compounding generator. Removing eliminates orthogonal phase rotation as a first-class scalar-generator. Thus the basis is minimal relative to these modes.
The operator algebra therefore gains its canonical scalar language.
Ch17.A3 — Operator Typing and Composition Legality
Operators are only useful if the framework knows when they may compose. A lawful manuscript cannot allow arbitrary chaining of transforms. The present section gives the full typing and legality system for operator composition.
An operator type is a tuple
\operatorname{Type}(\mathcal{O})
=
\big(
\operatorname{dom}(\mathcal{O}),\,
\operatorname{cod}(\mathcal{O}),\,
\Lambda_{\mathcal{O}},\,
\chi_{\mathcal{O}},\,
\kappa_{\mathcal{O}},\,
\mathcal{I}^{\mathrm{pre}}_{\mathcal{O}},\,
\mathcal{I}^{\mathrm{post}}_{\mathcal{O}}
\big).
lens regime,
contract family,
corridor class,
pre/post invariants.
For two operators , the composition
\mathcal{O}_2\circ \mathcal{O}_1
type compatibility
\operatorname{cod}(\mathcal{O}_1)\subseteq \operatorname{dom}(\mathcal{O}_2),
invariant compatibility
\mathcal{I}^{\mathrm{post}}_{\mathcal{O}_1}
\models
\mathcal{I}^{\mathrm{pre}}_{\mathcal{O}_2},
lens compatibility the outgoing lens state of is transport-compatible with the incoming lens state of ,
contract compatibility the partial artifact or state produced by is acceptable input under the contract assumptions of ,
corridor compatibility is a lawful corridor transition or inclusion,
witness composability the witness generated or required by can be transferred or lifted into the witness schema of .
This yields a composition legality judgment:
\mathfrak{E}_t
\vdash_\kappa
\mathcal{O}_2\circ \mathcal{O}_1
:
\operatorname{dom}(\mathcal{O}_1)\rightharpoonup \operatorname{cod}(\mathcal{O}_2)
\Downarrow
(\lambda_{\circ},\psi_{\circ},\delta_{\circ}).
The composition defect is not simply the sum of local defects. It may include interaction terms:
\delta_{\circ}
=
\delta_{\mathcal{O}_1}
+
\delta_{\mathcal{O}_2}
+
\delta_{\mathrm{bridge}}
+
\delta_{\mathrm{phase}}
+
\delta_{\mathrm{witness\_handoff}}.
The framework also defines higher algebraic operators.
Alternative composition
\mathcal{O}_1 \oplus \mathcal{O}_2
Parallel composition
\mathcal{O}_1 \otimes \mathcal{O}_2
Adjoint or inverse
\mathcal{O}^\dagger
Guarded iteration
\mathcal{O}^\star
A typed composition validator may be sketched as:
def composable(op1, op2):
if not codomain_subset(op1["codomain"], op2["domain"]):
return False
if not invariants_compatible(op1["post_invariants"], op2["pre_invariants"]):
return False
if not lens_compatible(op1["lens_profile"], op2["lens_profile"]):
return False
if not corridor_compatible(op1["corridor"], op2["corridor"]):
return False
if not witness_schemas_compatible(op1["witness_schema"], op2["witness_schema"]):
return False
return True
This section is the exact algebraic center of operator law. It is here that transforms stop being named moves and become a typed category of legal motion.
Definition 17.3 — Legally Composable Operator Pair
A pair is legally composable if all type, invariant, lens, contract, corridor, and witness handoff conditions are satisfied.
Theorem 17.3 — Operator Composition Is a Typed Partial Operation
Given the above legality conditions, composition in the operator algebra is not total but partial: it is defined exactly on the set of legally composable pairs.
Proof. Some operator pairs satisfy the legality conditions and compose; others do not. Therefore composition is partial.
This makes the operator algebra rigorous enough to support code generation, replay, and proof of legality.
