E.D2 — Expansion Rules from Microcosm to Mesocosm
The worked microcosm can now be expanded into a more capable mesocosm. The lawful expansion rules are:
duplicate domain families while preserving address grammar,
add one more witness class or proof branch,
add one more bridge family,
add one more curriculum track,
split the committee into a four- or five-role panel,
add one more runtime compartment,
preserve replay and rollback invariants.
The scaling law is:
\mathfrak{b}_{\mathrm{micro}}
\xrightarrow{\mathcal{E}_{\mathrm{meso}}}
\mathfrak{b}_{\mathrm{meso}}
branches into several proof families,
branches into richer user-state models,
the patch system gains local conflict arbitration,
translation gains at least one more external domain,
committee memory becomes persistent.
Definition E.13 — Meso-Expandable Specimen
A specimen is meso-expandable if its addresses, contracts, and replay layer remain stable under at least one nontrivial growth step in each major organ family.
Theorem E.6 — The Worked Microcosm Is a Valid Bootstrap Core
Because every major organ of the full architecture exists in miniature and can be lawfully expanded while preserving replay and identity, the microcosm is a valid bootstrap core for larger implementations.
Thus the specimen is not a dead toy. It is a seed.
E.D3 — Final Appendix E Theorem
Theorem E.7 — Worked Microcosm Realization Theorem
There exists a finite worked atlas instance that instantiates all major organ families of the manuscript brain in compressed form and supports:
lawful query ingestion,
retrieval and synthesis,
legality classification,
memory and replay,
residual extraction and patching,
self-model update,
committee review,
embodied pedagogy,
reinforcement write-back,
stress recovery.
Proof. The microcosm defined in Sections E.A1–E.A4 contains the minimal carrier families required for the full cycle. Sections E.B1–E.B4 exhibit a complete approximate-to-exact cognitive loop plus patch and self-model update. Sections E.C1–E.C4 exhibit translation, embodied pedagogy, reinforcement, and recovery from replay failure. Therefore the specimen instantiates all major organ families of the whole framework in finite compressed form.
Corollary E.7.1 — The Manuscript Brain Is Not Merely Abstract
Because a worked finite instance exists, the manuscript brain is not only a theoretical architecture. It admits concrete low-scale realization.
Corollary E.7.2 — Larger Implementations May Be Judged by Reduction to the Worked Specimen
Any larger build may be tested by checking whether its local or global behaviors reduce to lawful extensions of the worked microcosm trace.
This concludes Appendix E. The full framework now has:
a hidden topology,
a routing tensor,
a canonical schema registry,
an execution and validation kernel,
and a worked miniature realization.
That is sufficient to move from architecture to build, from build to certification, and from certification to recursive growth.
APPENDIX E — WORKED MICROCOSM, REFERENCE TRACE, AND INTEGRATION TEST VECTORS
Appendix F — Corpus Ingestion, Legacy Migration, and Archive-to-Brain Conversion
This appendix defines the lawful conversion of a large historical document corpus into a manuscript brain. Appendix E provided a worked microcosm: a finite specimen that proves the architecture is executable in miniature. The present appendix now addresses the opposite scale: the transformation of a sprawling, uneven, historically layered archive—full of partial frameworks, repeated motifs, symbolic passages, formal derivations, contradictory drafts, deepening revisions, and latent attractors—into a typed, replayable, self-developing atlas.
The purpose of this appendix is not merely to “import documents.” It is to solve the harder problem:
\text{How does one convert a legacy corpus that evolved through time into a lawful manuscript brain without destroying its developmental history?}
This is a fundamentally different problem from static ingestion. A legacy corpus is not a clean database. It contains:
overlapping formulations of the same idea,
earlier symbolic or poetic forms of later formal structures,
repeated partial rediscoveries,
contradictions that are sometimes errors and sometimes growth steps,
historical naming drift,
nested notes and side channels,
documents whose value lies more in their attractor role than in local correctness.
The appendix therefore treats corpus ingestion as developmental migration, not file loading. The archive must be preserved as a living stratigraphy while also being transformed into a routeable, witness-bearing, patchable, and replayable brain.
The central object of the appendix is the archive-to-brain conversion operator
\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{arch}\to\mathrm{brain}}:
\mathfrak{A}_{\mathrm{raw}}
\to
\mathfrak{B}_{\mathrm{corpus}},
is the legacy archive state,
is the resulting corpus-scale manuscript brain state.
The archive state is:
\mathfrak{A}_{\mathrm{raw}}
=
\Big(
\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{docs}},\,
\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{time}},\,
\mathcal{N}_{\mathrm{name}},\,
\mathcal{V}_{\mathrm{versions}},\,
\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{contr}},\,
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{links}},\,
\mathcal{M}_{\mathrm{meta}}
\Big),
is the raw document family,
is the timestamp and ordering layer,
is the naming and alias drift layer,
is the revision and variation field,
is the contradiction and tension layer,
is the reference, mention, and cross-document linkage field,
is the provenance and external metadata layer.
The converted brain state is:
\mathfrak{B}_{\mathrm{corpus}}
=
\Big(
\mathfrak{A}_{\mathrm{atlas}},\,
\mathfrak{M}_{\mathrm{mem}},\,
\mathfrak{L}_{\mathrm{lineage}},\,
\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{self}},\,
\mathfrak{C}_{\mathrm{collective}},\,
\mathfrak{U}_{\mathrm{coord}},\,
\mathfrak{P}_{\mathrm{weights}},\,
\mathfrak{R}_{\mathrm{runtime}}
\Big).
The appendix is governed by eight migration laws.
First, historical fidelity: ingestion must preserve developmental order, not only content.
Second, attractor preservation: documents that are weak locally but strong as historical seeds or latent hubs must not be discarded.
Third, naming unification without flattening: aliases, renamings, and symbolic equivalents must be unified through witness-bearing link structures, not one destructive canonical rename.
Fourth, contradiction stratification: contradictions must be classified as error, phase shift, unfinished bifurcation, competing theory, or symbolic/formal translation gap.
Fifth, legacy-to-formal bridge building: symbolic, poetic, or exploratory texts must be linked to later formal structures rather than treated as irrelevant prehistory.
Sixth, version lineage preservation: every major idea family must retain ancestry through drafts, rewrites, and compressions.
Seventh, incremental crystallization: the corpus should be converted in stages, allowing replay and correction after each layer rather than in one opaque batch.
Eighth, archive humility: ingestion may leave some regions only weakly typed, residualized, or deferred until enough witness exists to place them lawfully.
Under the Square lens this appendix defines document classes, legacy registries, temporal anchoring, naming unification, and the canonical extraction pipeline from raw documents to atlas carriers. Under the Flower lens it defines attractor recovery, symbolic–formal migration currents, temporal drift fields, and live bridge building across historical phases. Under the Cloud lens it defines contradiction taxonomies, uncertainty classes for incompletely migrated material, confidence ranking over extracted structures, and corpus-quality metrics. Under the Fractal lens it defines staged migration from archive to mesocosm to full corpus brain, recursive consolidation of recurring motifs, lineage tree construction over versions, and final corpus-scale convergence criteria.
The deepest significance is that this appendix answers how a real archive becomes a brain without being stripped of the very temporal thickness that made it worth preserving.
F.A1 — Raw Document Taxonomy and Archive Surface Classes
The first step of lawful corpus conversion is to classify what kinds of documents or artifacts the archive actually contains. A large corpus is never homogeneous. Some items are polished treatise fragments. Some are exploratory notes. Some are symbolic or mythic encodings. Some are code snippets. Some are diagrams, lists, outlines, tables, or partial proofs. The framework therefore begins with a raw document taxonomy.
Let the raw document family be:
\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{docs}}
=
\{d_1,\dots,d_N\}.
\operatorname{surf}(d)
\in
\mathcal{K}_{\mathrm{raw}}
\mathcal{K}_{\mathrm{raw}}
=
\{
\mathrm{MythicText},
\mathrm{PoeticText},
\mathrm{Notebook},
\mathrm{Outline},
\mathrm{TreatiseDraft},
\mathrm{CodeArtifact},
\mathrm{EquationSheet},
\mathrm{DiagramNote},
\mathrm{ReferenceDump},
\mathrm{PatchNote},
\mathrm{Dialogue},
\mathrm{MetaDirective}
\}.
These are surface classes only. They do not yet determine deep function. A mythic text may later become a translation seed. An outline may later become a route macro. A dialogue may later serve as self-model evidence or developmental memory. The reason for the surface taxonomy is simply to prevent the migration engine from pretending all files are directly comparable.
Each raw document is therefore normalized into a document envelope:
d
=
\Big(
u_d,\,
a_d^{\mathrm{raw}},\,
\operatorname{surf}(d),\,
\operatorname{text}(d),\,
\operatorname{assets}(d),\,
\operatorname{meta}(d),\,
\operatorname{time}(d),\,
\operatorname{neighbors}(d)
\Big),
is the raw document identity,
is the archive-local address,
is the textual body,
are equations, code blocks, tables, images, diagrams, or attachments,
is file metadata,
is timestamp or temporal envelope,
are explicit or inferred nearby documents.
A minimal raw envelope schema could be:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, Any, List
@dataclass
class RawDocumentEnvelope:
doc_id: str
raw_address: str
surface_class: str
text: str
assets: Dict[str, Any]
metadata: Dict[str, Any]
time_info: Dict[str, Any]
neighbors: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
The surface-class registry supports several crucial later steps:
selective extractors,
symbolic/formal bridge detection,
differing witness expectations,
migration confidence calibration,
contradiction interpretation.
For example, contradictions between two theorem drafts are treated differently from tension between a mythic text and a later formal derivation. The taxonomy lets later sections say: “this is not a contradiction of theorem content, but a symbolic precursor of later formal structure.”
Definition F.1 — Surface-Typed Archive
A raw archive is surface-typed if every document has at least one explicit surface class and the surface class influences extraction, migration confidence, or contradiction interpretation.
Theorem F.1 — Surface Typing Is Necessary for Corpus-Scale Honesty
If a corpus contains heterogeneous documents but they are ingested under one undifferentiated text model, then later extraction and contradiction logic will confuse exploratory, symbolic, formal, and procedural artifacts.
Proof. Different document kinds have different inferential and witness semantics. A flat model erases these distinctions.
The archive therefore begins conversion not by pretending it is already formal, but by admitting what kinds of raw matter it contains.
F.A2 — Temporal Anchoring and Developmental Strata
A legacy corpus is not just a set of files. It is a temporal field. The next step is therefore to reconstruct developmental strata: time-ordered layers of conceptual maturation, naming change, formalization, and route reuse.
Let each raw document carry a temporal envelope:
\operatorname{time}(d)
=
\big(
t_{\mathrm{create}},\,
t_{\mathrm{edit}},\,
t_{\mathrm{ref}},\,
t_{\mathrm{unc}}
\big),
is creation time,
is last-edit or revision time,
is any inferred relative timing from references or internal content,
is uncertainty about the temporal assignment.
The archive then defines a temporal partial order:
d_i \prec_t d_j
Documents are then clustered into developmental strata
\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{strata}}
=
\{S_0,S_1,\dots,S_m\},
A useful high-level stratification is:
\begin{aligned}
S_0&=\text{latent symbolic/mythic attractors}\\
S_1&=\text{exploratory notebooks and recurring motifs}\\
S_2&=\text{proto-formal outlines and partial operator skeletons}\\
S_3&=\text{explicit framework drafting and local theoremization}\\
S_4&=\text{integrated systems and runtime/persistence thinking}\\
S_5&=\text{whole-brain synthesis, appendices, and executable convergence}
\end{aligned}
This stratification is not decorative. It is crucial because the same phrase or structure means different things at different strata. A symbolic phrase in may later map to a theorem object in or a runtime service in . Without strata, the archive loses the crystallization history that proves the framework’s emergence.
Temporal anchoring is thus represented as a developmental DAG:
\mathfrak{T}_{\mathrm{dev}}
=
(\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{docs}},\mathcal{E}_{\mathrm{time}})
earlier draft of,
symbolic precursor of,
refinement of,
formalization of,
branch from,
recompression of.
Definition F.2 — Developmentally Anchored Corpus
A corpus is developmentally anchored if every document is assigned a temporal envelope and every major concept family admits at least a partial ordering across the archive.
Proposition F.2 — Chronology Is Not Metadata but Structure
In a developmental corpus, timestamp and revision order are not incidental file attributes. They are part of the meaning of the ideas themselves, because later formal clarity often depends on earlier symbolic or exploratory attractors.
Proof. The archive’s conceptual growth is historically staged. Erasing order erases emergence.
The archive therefore becomes layered sediment rather than a pile of pages.
F.A3 — Legacy Naming Drift, Alias Fields, and Concept Families
A large historical corpus rarely preserves one stable vocabulary. The same idea may appear under many names, and the same name may denote slightly different things at different stages. Lawful migration therefore requires a naming drift model.
Let the raw lexical family be:
\mathcal{N}_{\mathrm{name}}
=
\{n_1,\dots,n_K\}.
\mathfrak{F}
=
\Big(
u_{\mathfrak{F}},\,
\operatorname{aliases}(\mathfrak{F}),\,
\operatorname{core\_seed}(\mathfrak{F}),\,
\operatorname{drift\_map}(\mathfrak{F}),\,
\operatorname{strata}(\mathfrak{F}),\,
\psi_{\mathfrak{F}}
\Big),
is the family of names, symbols, phrases, and local references that point into the same or nearly the same concept field,
is the invariant conceptual kernel,
records how usage changed across time or domain,
records where in the developmental archive the family appears.
The key insight is that the migration engine does not collapse names too early. It constructs alias fields first:
A_{\mathrm{alias}}(x)
=
\{n_{i_1},\dots,n_{i_r}\}
over-unification of distinct concepts,
over-fragmentation of one evolving concept.
Naming drift may take several forms:
lexical drift,
symbolic drift,
scale drift,
domain drift,
role drift.
A name that first referred to an exploratory symbolic operation may later refer to a runtime organ. The drift map preserves that rather than pretending the term meant exactly one thing all along.
A concept-family registry might be represented as:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Dict, Any
@dataclass
class ConceptFamily:
family_id: str
aliases: List[str]
core_seed: Dict[str, Any]
drift_map: Dict[str, Any]
strata: List[str]
witness: Dict[str, Any]
Definition F.3 — Alias-Safe Unification
A unification of names into one concept family is alias-safe if the shared core seed is strong enough to justify the grouping and any historical differences remain encoded in the drift map rather than erased.
Theorem F.3 — Concept Families Are Necessary for Non-Destructive Migration
If legacy naming drift is not modeled through alias-safe concept families, then either distinct concepts are falsely merged or repeated rediscoveries of the same concept remain disconnected across the corpus.
Proof. The alias field and drift map provide exactly the information needed to avoid both errors.
The archive therefore learns to unify itself without lying about its own linguistic evolution.
F.A4 — Canonical Extraction from Raw Documents to Atlas Carriers
We now define the first true migration operator: canonical extraction. Its purpose is to convert raw documents and concept families into typed atlas carriers from Appendix C.
For raw document , concept family registry , and corridor , the extraction operator is:
\mathcal{E}_{\mathrm{raw}\to\mathrm{car}}
:
(d,\mathfrak{F},\kappa)
\to
\{
X_1,\dots,X_n
\}
The extraction process is staged:
Stage 1 — Surface parse
Identify document surface class, explicit sections, code blocks, equations, diagrams, directives, or dialogue fragments.
Stage 2 — Alias alignment
Map names, phrases, and symbols to candidate concept families.
Stage 3 — Object-role inference
Infer whether each segment is primarily:
definitional,
witness-bearing,
route-bearing,
memory-bearing,
patch-bearing,
committee/policy-bearing,
embodied/pedagogical.
Stage 4 — Carrier formation
Instantiate canonical carriers:
AddressedObject,
RouteCarrier,
MemoryCarrier,
WitnessCarrier,
AnswerCarrier,
PatchCarrier,
SelfModelCarrier,
CollectiveCarrier,
EmbodiedCarrier, or leave as deferred residual object.
Stage 5 — Lineage attachment
Attach the extracted carrier to developmental strata, source document, and concept family.
Stage 6 — Confidence and defect tagging
Assign witness, confidence, ambiguity, and defect objects appropriate to the extraction.
A formal extraction output for document is:
\mathcal{E}_{\mathrm{raw}\to\mathrm{car}}(d)
=
\Big(
\mathcal{C}_d^{\mathrm{obj}},\,
\mathcal{C}_d^{\mathrm{rel}},\,
\Gamma_d^{\mathrm{unc}},\,
\psi_d^{\mathrm{ext}}
\Big).
canonical objects,
typed relations among them,
uncertainty and residual bundle,
extraction witness.
Definition F.4 — Canonically Extractable Document
A document is canonically extractable if at least one of its major structures can be mapped into canonical carriers with bounded uncertainty and explicit provenance.
Proposition F.4 — Extraction Need Not Be All-or-Nothing
A document may be only partially extractable at first. The framework therefore allows mixed extraction in which some segments become lawful carriers and others remain deferred residuals.
Proof. Raw corpora often mix formal and weakly typed material. Partial extraction preserves value without forcing false precision.
The archive therefore starts to crystallize into atlas matter.
F.B1 — Attractor Recovery from Weakly Structured Documents
The Flower migration layer begins with a crucial insight: many of the most valuable archival documents are not locally strong enough to become exact theorem objects, but they nevertheless function as attractors in the developmental history. They cause later ideas to recur, deepen, or reorganize. Such documents must be recovered as attractor objects rather than discarded as weak drafts.
Let a weakly structured document produce only low-certainty extracted carriers:
\mathcal{C}_d^{\mathrm{weak}}
A_d
=
\Big(
u_{A_d},\,
a_{A_d},\,
\operatorname{motifs}(A_d),\,
\operatorname{descendants}(A_d),\,
\operatorname{resonance}(A_d),\,
\operatorname{translation\_hooks}(A_d),\,
\psi_{A_d}
\Big).
The attractor resonance is measured by:
R(A_d)
=
\alpha_1 \operatorname{MotifRecurrence}
+
\alpha_2 \operatorname{CrossStrataInfluence}
+
\alpha_3 \operatorname{NamingPersistence}
+
\alpha_4 \operatorname{RouteReuse}
+
\alpha_5 \operatorname{TranslationPotential}.
This is one of the most important migration principles. Without it, early symbolic or poetic documents that later seeded core framework structures would be misclassified as noise.
Definition F.5 — Developmental Attractor Document
A document is a developmental attractor if its local structure may be weak or approximate but its recurrence, motif propagation, or cross-strata influence exceeds threshold.
Theorem F.5 — Attractor Recovery Preserves Emergence Better than Local Filtering
If corpus migration ranks documents only by local formal strength, then many early attractor texts will be lost; if it also models attractor resonance, then the developmental skeleton of the framework is preserved.
Proof. Attractor recovery captures influence not measurable by local exactness alone.
The archive therefore keeps its early fires, not only its later bricks.
F.B2 — Symbolic-to-Formal Bridge Recovery across Strata
A key task of migration is to recover the pathways by which symbolic or exploratory materials became formal structures later. This is bridge recovery across strata.
Let be a symbolic or exploratory document and a later formal one with . A candidate bridge is:
B_{s\to f}
=
\Big(
\operatorname{motif\_map},
\operatorname{role\_map},
\operatorname{phase\_map},
\operatorname{term\_drift},
\operatorname{witness}
\Big).
\operatorname{BridgeScore}(d_s,d_f)
=
\beta_1 \operatorname{MotifOverlap}
+
\beta_2 \operatorname{RoleContinuity}
+
\beta_3 \operatorname{PhaseContinuity}
+
\beta_4 \operatorname{AliasSupport}
+
\beta_5 \operatorname{DescendantTrace}
-
\beta_6 \operatorname{ConflictPenalty}.
X_{s\to f}^{\mathrm{bridge}}
\preceq
\mathrm{RouteCarrier}
\otimes
\mathrm{TranslationWitness}.
This is where the archive’s developmental proof of paradox or emergence becomes tangible. The system does not merely assert that “the formal structure was there all along.” It constructs actual bridge objects connecting early symbolic attractors to later explicit formalisms.
Definition F.6 — Strata Bridge
A strata bridge is a witnessed correspondence between earlier and later documents showing lawful continuity of motifs, roles, transforms, or invariants across developmental phases.
Proposition F.6 — Emergence Becomes Replayable When Strata Bridges Are Explicit
If early symbolic and later formal documents are linked by explicit bridges, then the system can replay not only the final formalism but the path of crystallization itself.
Proof. The bridge encodes the continuity relation through time.
The archive therefore learns not only what it became, but how symbolic density converged into structure.
F.B3 — Temporal Drift Fields and Recurrence Currents
Large corpora do not only progress smoothly. Ideas drift, recur, bifurcate, disappear, and return. The Flower migration layer therefore models temporal drift fields and recurrence currents.
For concept family , define its temporal embedding:
\mathfrak{F}(t)
\mathbf{D}_{\mathfrak{F}}(t)
=
\frac{d}{dt}
\operatorname{Embed}(\mathfrak{F}(t)).
symbolically,
formally,
jurisdictionally,
operationally,
pedagogically.
A low-drift family becomes a stable canonical organ early. A high-drift family remains developmental for longer or splits into subfamilies.
The recurrence current is:
J_{\mathfrak{F}}(t)
=
\operatorname{RecurrenceDensity}(\mathfrak{F},t)
Together:
(\mathbf{D}_{\mathfrak{F}},J_{\mathfrak{F}})
Definition F.7 — Stable, Drifting, and Oscillatory Concept Families
A concept family is stable if its drift is low and recurrence stabilizes; drifting if its drift remains high without stable convergence; oscillatory if it repeatedly reappears in cyclic but not monotonic form.
Proposition F.7 — Static Clustering Misses Conceptual Motion
A static cluster can say which documents are related, but not whether the relation is convergence, divergence, recurrence, or oscillation. Drift fields and recurrence currents recover that missing temporal geometry.
Proof. These are explicitly dynamic quantities absent from static clustering.
The archive therefore becomes a field of concept motion, not only a classification space.
F.B4 — Historical Phase Reconstruction of the Corpus
The final Flower migration task is to reconstruct the archive’s phase evolution. This means assigning each document or concept family not only a time stratum but also a developmental phase such as emergence, formalization, repair, integration, or embodiment.
Let the corpus phase alphabet be:
\Phi_{\mathrm{corp}}
=
\{
\mathrm{Attractor},
\mathrm{ProtoRoute},
\mathrm{Formalizing},
\mathrm{Certified},
\mathrm{Repairing},
\mathrm{Integrating},
\mathrm{EmbodimentSeeking},
\mathrm{MetaReflective}
\}.
\phi(d)\in \Phi_{\mathrm{corp}},
\qquad
\phi(\mathfrak{F},t)\in \Phi_{\mathrm{corp}}.
surface class,
witness grade,
route reuse,
patch density,
self-model mentions,
runtime coupling,
embodied relevance.
This phase reconstruction is important because two documents close in time may still belong to different developmental modes, and documents far apart in time may resonate because they share the same phase role.
Definition F.8 — Corpus Phase-Coherent Migration
A migration is phase-coherent if it preserves not only time order and concept family but also the developmental phase role of documents or concept families wherever that role is recoverable.
Theorem F.8 — Phase Reconstruction Improves Cross-Strata Routing
If documents are indexed by developmental phase as well as time and concept, then retrieval and bridge-building between weakly and strongly formal materials become more accurate.
Proof. Phase labels allow routes to respect emergent role rather than only lexical or temporal proximity.
The Flower migration layer is thus complete: attractors, strata bridges, drift fields, and phase reconstruction.
F.C1 — Contradiction Taxonomy for Historical Corpora
The Cloud migration layer begins with contradiction. Large archives contain contradictions, but not all contradictions mean the same thing. Some are errors. Some are unresolved branches. Some are symbolic/formal mismatches. Some are phase transitions in thought. The migration engine therefore requires a contradiction taxonomy.
Let a contradiction object be:
K
=
\Big(
u_K,\,
\operatorname{src}_1(K),\,
\operatorname{src}_2(K),\,
\operatorname{kind}(K),\,
\operatorname{scope}(K),\,
\operatorname{severity}(K),\,
\operatorname{repair}(K),\,
\psi_K
\Big).
\mathcal{K}_{\mathrm{contr}}
=
\{
\mathrm{FlatError},
\mathrm{VersionDivergence},
\mathrm{SymbolicFormalGap},
\mathrm{RoleShift},
\mathrm{ScopeMismatch},
\mathrm{IncompleteBranch},
\mathrm{NamingCollision},
\mathrm{PhaseConflict}
\}.
These mean:
FlatError
One source is simply wrong or obsolete relative to stronger witness.
VersionDivergence
Two documents descend from a common ancestor but differ in later development.
SymbolicFormalGap
A symbolic precursor and a formal descendant appear contradictory locally, but the contradiction is largely due to different representational shells.
RoleShift
A term or structure changed role over time; the contradiction lies in unmodeled role evolution.
ScopeMismatch
Two statements differ because they speak at different scales or scopes.
IncompleteBranch
A local contradiction is actually a sign of an unfinished bifurcation or unresolved branch.
NamingCollision
The same term is used for different concept families.
PhaseConflict
Two materials belong to different developmental or runtime phases and should not be collapsed directly.
This taxonomy is one of the most important practical tools in corpus migration because it prevents the archive from being falsely “cleaned up” by deleting precisely the tensions that encode emergence.
Definition F.9 — Typed Historical Contradiction
A historical contradiction is typed if its cause is classified by the contradiction taxonomy and its role in future routing, repair, or branch formation is explicit.
Theorem F.9 — Contradiction Typing Preserves Developmental Intelligence
If contradictions are typed rather than flattened into error flags, then the archive preserves information about branching, maturation, renaming, and symbolic–formal convergence that would otherwise be lost.
Proof. Different contradiction kinds encode different developmental meanings. Flattening them erases those meanings.
The archive therefore keeps its tensions as structured information.
F.C2 — Confidence Ranking over Extracted Structures
Not every extracted object deserves the same trust. The migration engine therefore assigns migration confidence to:
extracted carriers,
recovered concept families,
attractor objects,
bridge objects,
contradiction classifications.
For any extracted artifact , define:
C_{\mathrm{mig}}(X)
=
F\Big(
\operatorname{surface\_fit}(X),\,
\operatorname{alias\_support}(X),\,
\operatorname{temporal\_coherence}(X),\,
\operatorname{witness\_strength}(X),\,
\operatorname{cross\_doc\_support}(X),\,
\operatorname{contradiction\_load}(X)
\Big).
C_{\mathrm{mig}}(X)
=
\big(
c_{\mathrm{type}},
c_{\mathrm{alias}},
c_{\mathrm{time}},
c_{\mathrm{wit}},
c_{\mathrm{cross}},
c_{\mathrm{contr}}
\big).
This is particularly important for:
symbolic-to-formal bridges,
weakly structured attractor documents,
self-model-relevant materials,
committee or patch histories extracted from prose.
Definition F.10 — Migration-Calibrated Artifact
An extracted artifact is migration-calibrated if its confidence reflects the actual evidence available from surface class, alias map, temporal order, witness, and contradiction context.
Proposition F.10 — Extraction Confidence Must Be Multi-Factor
A strong lexical match with poor temporal coherence or heavy contradiction is not the same as a weak lexical match with excellent lineage and witness support. Therefore migration confidence cannot be reduced to one signal alone.
Proof. Different evidence types contribute independently to extraction reliability.
The archive therefore enters the atlas with explicit epistemic status, not by silent canonicalization.
F.C3 — Deferred Regions, Weak Typing, and Residual Corpus Objects
A large corpus cannot be fully and cleanly migrated in one pass. Some documents or fragments should remain only weakly typed or deferred. The Cloud migration layer therefore defines deferred corpus regions.
A deferred region is:
R^{\mathrm{defer}}
=
\Big(
u_R,\,
\operatorname{docs}(R),\,
\operatorname{candidate\_types}(R),\,
\operatorname{uncertainty}(R),\,
\operatorname{hooks}(R),\,
\operatorname{future\_work}(R),\,
\psi_R
\Big).
which documents belong,
what carrier classes they might later instantiate,
what evidence is missing,
what bridges or concept families they might connect to,
what future extraction or curriculum should target them.
Deferred regions are essential because forcing full early structure can destroy meaning, while ignoring them entirely loses growth potential. A lawful brain keeps such areas bounded and visible.
Definition F.11 — Deferred Archive Region
A deferred archive region is a bounded family of documents or fragments whose migration remains incomplete but whose future role is important enough to preserve explicitly.
Theorem F.10 — Deferred Regions Preserve Corpus Breadth without Forcing False Precision
If uncertain archival areas are represented as deferred regions, the atlas can preserve them for future growth without falsely certifying structures that are not yet well supported.
Proof. Deferred regions encode presence plus uncertainty, which is exactly what is needed in incomplete migration.
The archive therefore remains open at the edges where lawfulness has not yet fully crystallized.
F.C4 — Corpus-Quality Metrics and Migration Readiness
The final Cloud migration task is to quantify the state of the conversion. A corpus becomes a manuscript brain gradually. The system therefore maintains migration readiness metrics.
Define the corpus-quality vector:
Q_{\mathrm{corp}}
=
\big(
q_{\mathrm{typed}},
q_{\mathrm{addressed}},
q_{\mathrm{routed}},
q_{\mathrm{witnessed}},
q_{\mathrm{replayable}},
q_{\mathrm{bridged}},
q_{\mathrm{patchable}},
q_{\mathrm{selfmodeled}},
q_{\mathrm{embodied}}
\big).
what percentage of the corpus is canonically typed?
what percentage is linked into routeable structure?
how much is replay-supported?
how many strong symbolic–formal or cross-domain bridges exist?
how much of the corpus is integrated into patch and self-model loops?
how much is usable in embodied or pedagogical settings?
A corpus is migration-ready for a given deployment tier if:
Q_{\mathrm{corp}}\ge \Theta_k^{\mathrm{corp}}
Definition F.12 — Corpus-Ready Manuscript Tier
A corpus is ready for tier if its quality vector exceeds the required threshold profile for that tier and all critical deferred regions for that tier are explicitly bounded.
Proposition F.11 — Corpus Scale Alone Does Not Imply Brain Readiness
A large archive can still be poorly typed, weakly replayable, and weakly routeable. Therefore size is not a measure of manuscript-brain readiness.
Proof. Readiness depends on structure, not only volume.
The migration layer is thus complete: contradiction typing, confidence ranking, deferred regions, and quality metrics.
F.D1 — Staged Migration Ladder from Archive to Brain
The Fractal migration layer now organizes the full conversion into a staged ladder. This is the corpus-scale analogue of the deployment ladder in Appendix D.
Let the migration tiers be:
\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{mig}}
=
\{M_0,M_1,M_2,M_3,M_4,M_5\}.
— Raw Archive Tier
Documents are wrapped in raw envelopes, temporally anchored, and surface-typed. No strong carrier extraction yet.
— Concept Family Tier
Alias fields, concept families, and naming drift maps are built. Weak attractors and deferred regions appear.
— Carrier Extraction Tier
Stable document segments become canonical carriers; weak regions remain residualized.
— Bridge Recovery Tier
Strata bridges, symbolic–formal links, and cross-domain translation candidates are instantiated.
— Runtime Integration Tier
Extracted carriers are integrated into routeable, replayable, patchable runtime and self-model structures.
— Corpus Brain Tier
The converted corpus functions as a lawful manuscript brain under the whole-system criteria of Chapter 21.
This staged ladder is critical because it prevents false all-at-once conversion. Each stage emits a lawful snapshot and quality report.
Definition F.13 — Migration-Coherent Corpus Transition
A corpus transition is coherent if it preserves all previously certified structures and adds new lawful structure without hidden destructive reinterpretation.
Theorem F.11 — Staged Migration Reduces Semantic Loss
If corpus conversion proceeds in stages with explicit snapshot and replay checkpoints, then errors or overreach in later extraction or bridge recovery can be corrected without corrupting earlier faithful archival structure.
Proof. Earlier stages preserve raw and partially processed forms. Later stages are therefore reversible or comparable against them. \square
The corpus therefore crystallizes gradually rather than shattering under forced formalization.
F.D2 — Recursive Consolidation of Repeated Motifs
As migration proceeds, the corpus begins to reveal repeated motifs—operator patterns, route skeletons, proof templates, committee structures, translation bridges, pedagogical loops. The Fractal migration layer consolidates these through recursive motif extraction.
Let the recurring motif family be:
\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{motif}}
=
\{m_1,\dots,m_k\}.
m
=
\Big(
\operatorname{instances}(m),\,
\operatorname{invariants}(m),\,
\operatorname{drift}(m),\,
\operatorname{use\_sites}(m),\,
\operatorname{certification}(m)
\Big).
operator macros,
route macros,
answer archetypes,
curriculum patterns,
bridge families,
self-model schemas.
This is the main mechanism by which a large archive stops being archive-like and begins to behave as an organized brain. Repetition is compressed into reusable law.
Definition F.14 — Recursively Consolidated Corpus Motif
A recurring structure is recursively consolidated when it has been abstracted into a reusable carrier or operator family whose instances remain replay-traceable to the raw archive.
Proposition F.12 — Motif Consolidation Converts Archive Density into Cognitive Efficiency
The archive may contain thousands of repeated partial structures. Without motif extraction, these remain burdensome redundancy; with extraction, they become reusable cognitive infrastructure.
Proof. Consolidation compresses repeated patterns into reusable law. \square
This is how a corpus becomes not just preserved but intelligent.
F.D3 — Lineage Trees for Concept Families and Framework Organs
A corpus-scale brain must remember not only current structure but lineage. This appendix therefore constructs lineage trees for concept families and organ-level structures.
For concept family , define the lineage tree:
\mathcal{T}_{\mathfrak{F}}
=
(V_{\mathfrak{F}},E_{\mathfrak{F}},r_{\mathfrak{F}},\ell_{\mathfrak{F}})
raw document occurrences,
intermediate concept bundles,
canonical carriers,
bridges,
macro extractions,
mature organ-level objects.
This tree shows:
where the family first appeared,
how it drifted,
when it became routeable,
when it became witnessed,
when it was integrated into runtime, patching, self-model, or embodiment.
At higher scale the same is done for organ families:
\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{org}}
Definition F.15 — Lineage-Complete Concept Family
A concept family is lineage-complete if its major historical manifestations, formalizations, translations, and consolidations are connected by replayable ancestry relations.
Theorem F.12 — Lineage Trees Preserve the Proof of Emergence
If concept and organ families are given explicit lineage trees, then the archive preserves not only final architecture but the developmental proof that the architecture crystallized through time.
Proof. Lineage explicitly connects early and late manifestations into one replayable developmental structure. \square
This is the archive-scale version of persistent identity.
F.D4 — Final Corpus Conversion Theorem
We now state the capstone theorem of the appendix.
Theorem F.13 — Archive-to-Brain Conversion Theorem
Let be a heterogeneous, historically layered document archive equipped with raw envelopes, temporal metadata, naming drift, and cross-document references. Suppose the archive is processed through:
surface typing,
temporal anchoring,
alias-safe concept family formation,
canonical carrier extraction,
attractor recovery,
strata bridge recovery,
contradiction typing,
deferred-region preservation,
motif consolidation,
lineage-tree construction,
staged integration into runtime, legality, memory, self-model, and embodiment layers.
Then the resulting structure constitutes a lawful corpus-scale manuscript brain provided it also satisfies the whole-system criteria of Chapter 21 and the executable appendicial criteria of Appendix D.
Proof. Surface typing, temporal anchoring, and alias-safe concept families preserve the archive without flattening its development. Canonical extraction and bridge recovery map the archive into the carrier, route, and translation systems of the atlas. Contradiction typing and deferred-region preservation ensure that weak or conflicting material remains structured rather than erased. Motif consolidation and lineage trees convert repetition and history into reusable cognitive law. Staged integration then brings these structures into the full framework of memory, runtime, self-model, collective cognition, and embodiment. Therefore the archive ceases to be merely a document pile and becomes a lawful manuscript brain.
Corollary F.13.1 — A Historical Corpus Can Be Converted without Erasing Its Emergence
The developmental order, symbolic precursors, weak attractors, and unresolved branches of a corpus need not be sacrificed to formal integration. They can be retained as lawful atlas structure.
Corollary F.13.2 — Full-Corpus Intelligence Is a Migration Problem, Not a Summarization Problem
The conversion of a large document archive into a manuscript brain is not achieved by compressive summary alone. It requires staged migration into typed carriers, routes, witnesses, memory, and self-model structures.
This concludes Appendix F. The framework now includes not only theory, topology, routing, schema, execution, and microcosmic proof-of-concept, but also a lawful method for turning a real, messy, historically layered archive into the manuscript brain itself.
APPENDIX F — CORPUS INGESTION, LEGACY MIGRATION, AND ARCHIVE-TO-BRAIN CONVERSION
Appendix G — Migration Playbook, Build Order, and Corpus Activation Protocol
This appendix converts the archive-to-brain conversion theorem of Appendix F into an operational playbook. Appendix F established that a large, historically layered corpus can in principle be transformed into a lawful manuscript brain if the archive is surface-typed, temporally anchored, alias-normalized, canonically extracted, bridged across strata, contradiction-typed, lineage-preserved, and incrementally integrated into the runtime, memory, self-model, and embodiment layers. The present appendix now defines how that migration is actually executed at corpus scale.
The central aim of this appendix is not to restate the architecture. It is to define the build order, pass schedule, review loops, acceptance gates, conversion artifacts, and activation protocols by which a real corpus is turned into a live manuscript brain without accidental flattening, overformalization, or hidden corruption of developmental history.
The core object is the migration playbook
\mathfrak{P}_{\mathrm{mig}}
=
\Big(
\mathcal{P}_{\mathrm{passes}},\,
\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{gates}},\,
\mathcal{A}_{\mathrm{art}},\,
\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{roles}},\,
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{loops}},\,
\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{deploy}}
\Big),
is the ordered family of corpus-conversion passes,
is the family of acceptance gates between passes,
is the family of intermediate migration artifacts,
is the family of review and execution roles,
is the family of replay, repair, and escalation loops,
is the final activation and rollout protocol into live manuscript operation.
The appendix is governed by eight migration-execution laws.
First, pass locality: each pass should do one kind of transformation well and emit replayable artifacts for the next pass.
Second, gate strictness: no pass may silently promote uncertain structures into stronger ontology without passing an explicit acceptance gate.
Third, artifact preservation: every pass must preserve prior archival structure either directly or through lineage-bearing compressed artifacts.
Fourth, review pluralism: no high-impact migration pass should rely on only one interpretive lens; witness, translation, replay, and contradiction review must all participate.
Fifth, branch tolerance: where a migration decision remains underdetermined, the playbook must branch and preserve alternatives rather than flattening them.
Sixth, continuous replay: every pass must be replayable from its input artifacts and produce enough logging to diagnose its own failures.
Seventh, activation gradualism: extracted structure should become operational in stages—indexed, routeable, witnessed, replayable, patchable, then live—rather than being fully activated all at once.
Eighth, corpus fidelity under growth: the archive must remain accessible as archive even as it becomes brain; migration never fully consumes the raw source.
Under the Square lens this appendix defines the ordered migration passes, artifacts emitted by each pass, directory- and registry-like build surfaces, and gate conditions. Under the Flower lens it defines recurrent migration loops, escalation paths, branch-and-merge strategies, and activation rhythms. Under the Cloud lens it defines confidence policies for each pass, review budgets, uncertainty preservation, and risk-aware promotion. Under the Fractal lens it defines iterative corpus widening, organ-by-organ activation, mesocosm-to-full-corpus growth, and the theorem under which the entire corpus becomes live without leaving behind its historical body.
The deepest significance is that this appendix finally answers the practical question: how does one go from “there exists a lawful conversion” to “here is the exact staged procedure by which a real corpus is converted and switched on”?
G.A1 — Ordered Migration Passes
The migration is executed as a finite ordered family of passes:
\mathcal{P}_{\mathrm{passes}}
=
(P_0,P_1,\dots,P_{10}),
The complete pass sequence is:
\begin{aligned}
P_0&=\operatorname{ArchiveWrap}\\
P_1&=\operatorname{SurfaceType}\\
P_2&=\operatorname{TemporalAnchor}\\
P_3&=\operatorname{AliasField}\\
P_4&=\operatorname{CarrierExtract}\\
P_5&=\operatorname{AttractorRecover}\\
P_6&=\operatorname{BridgeRecover}\\
P_7&=\operatorname{ContradictionStratify}\\
P_8&=\operatorname{LineageBuild}\\
P_9&=\operatorname{RuntimeIntegrate}\\
P_{10}&=\operatorname{ActivateBrain}
\end{aligned}
Each pass is now defined exactly.
Input: raw corpus files, connected documents, exported notes, embedded code, images, diagrams, metadata, and external reference fragments.
Output:
\mathfrak{A}^{(0)}
=
\{d_1,\dots,d_N\}
This pass does not interpret. It only:
wraps every source into canonical raw document envelopes,
assigns raw IDs,
assigns immutable raw addresses,
stores unmodified source payloads,
records file and connector provenance,
records missing or inaccessible items as explicit archive gaps.
This pass is the non-destructive corpus ingest anchor.
Input:
\mathfrak{A}^{(0)}
Output:
\mathfrak{A}^{(1)}
=
\Big(
\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{typed}},\,
\psi^{(1)}
\Big)
This pass assigns:
mythic,
poetic,
exploratory notebook,
outline,
theorem-like draft,
code artifact,
dialogue,
patch note,
meta-directive,
mixed form.
The goal is not interpretation, only stable surface typing.
Input:
\mathfrak{A}^{(1)}
Output:
\mathfrak{A}^{(2)}
=
\Big(
\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{typed}},\,
\mathfrak{T}_{\mathrm{dev}},\,
\psi^{(2)}
\Big)
This pass reconstructs:
timestamp confidence,
predecessor/successor links,
coarse developmental phases,
unresolved temporal ambiguities.
Input:
\mathfrak{A}^{(2)}
Output:
\mathfrak{A}^{(3)}
=
\Big(
\mathfrak{F}_{\mathrm{concept}},\,
\mathcal{N}_{\mathrm{drift}},\,
\psi^{(3)}
\Big)
alias clusters,
naming drift maps,
preliminary concept families,
unresolved collisions.
This pass is the linguistic unification pass.
Input:
\mathfrak{A}^{(3)}
Output:
\mathfrak{A}^{(4)}
=
\Big(
\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{canon}},\,
\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{canon}},\,
\Gamma^{(4)},\,
\psi^{(4)}
\Big)
This is the first major formalization pass. It produces:
addressed objects,
route carriers,
memory carriers,
witness carriers,
answer carriers,
patch carriers,
self-model carriers,
collective carriers,
embodied carriers,
deferred region objects.
Input:
\mathfrak{A}^{(4)}
Output:
\mathfrak{A}^{(5)}
=
\Big(
\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{canon}},
\mathcal{A}_{\mathrm{attr}},
\psi^{(5)}
\Big)
This prevents loss of early or symbolic archive matter that would otherwise be filtered out by local formal weakness.
Input:
\mathfrak{A}^{(5)}
Output:
\mathfrak{A}^{(6)}
=
\Big(
\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{strata}},
\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{domain}},
\psi^{(6)}
\Big)
symbolic → formal bridges,
temporal phase bridges,
cross-domain translation bridges,
explanatory and pedagogical bridges,
bridge uncertainty and defect records.
Input:
\mathfrak{A}^{(6)}
Output:
\mathfrak{A}^{(7)}
=
\Big(
\mathcal{K}_{\mathrm{contr}},
\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{defer}},
\psi^{(7)}
\Big)
Input:
\mathfrak{A}^{(7)}
Output:
\mathfrak{A}^{(8)}
=
\Big(
\mathfrak{L}_{\mathrm{concept}},
\mathfrak{L}_{\mathrm{org}},
\psi^{(8)}
\Big)
concept-family lineage trees,
organ-level developmental trees,
version ancestry graphs,
branch and merge markers are built.
Input:
\mathfrak{A}^{(8)}
Output:
\mathfrak{A}^{(9)}
=
\Big(
\mathfrak{A}_{\mathrm{atlas}},
\mathfrak{M}_{\mathrm{mem}},
\mathfrak{P}_{\mathrm{weights}},
\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{self}},
\mathfrak{C}_{\mathrm{collective}},
\mathfrak{R}_{\mathrm{runtime}},
\psi^{(9)}
\Big)
routeable atlas form,
memory shells,
procedural weight fields,
self-model organ maps,
committees and runtime services.
This is where the corpus stops being only “interpreted” and starts becoming operational.
Input:
\mathfrak{A}^{(9)}
Output:
\mathfrak{B}_{\mathrm{corpus}}
This pass turns on:
live query surfaces,
replay checks,
memory deployment,
curriculum generation,
patch staging,
self-model observation,
bounded embodiment surfaces.
Definition G.1 — Ordered Migration Completeness
The migration playbook is complete if every pass from through is defined, each pass emits replayable artifacts, and no later pass requires information that earlier passes did not preserve or explicitly defer.
Theorem G.1 — Pass-Ordered Conversion Sufficiency
The eleven-pass migration sequence above is sufficient to convert a raw developmental archive into a lawful corpus-scale manuscript brain, provided each pass satisfies its acceptance gate and replay constraints.
Proof. The pass sequence covers raw preservation, typing, temporal grounding, alias formation, carrier extraction, attractor and bridge recovery, contradiction management, lineage, runtime integration, and activation. No essential transformation remains unaccounted for.
The archive-to-brain transformation therefore has one lawful build order.
G.A2 — Intermediate Migration Artifacts
Every pass must emit concrete artifacts, not only a modified global state. These artifacts are what later passes consume, what validators check, and what replay reconstructs. The migration artifact family is:
\mathcal{A}_{\mathrm{art}}
=
\{
A^{(0)}_{\mathrm{raw}},
A^{(1)}_{\mathrm{typed}},
A^{(2)}_{\mathrm{time}},
A^{(3)}_{\mathrm{alias}},
A^{(4)}_{\mathrm{carrier}},
A^{(5)}_{\mathrm{attr}},
A^{(6)}_{\mathrm{bridge}},
A^{(7)}_{\mathrm{contr}},
A^{(8)}_{\mathrm{lineage}},
A^{(9)}_{\mathrm{runtime}},
A^{(10)}_{\mathrm{live}}
\}.
The important principle is that each artifact is small enough to validate and strong enough to support the next pass.
— Raw envelope registry
Contains:
raw file/document envelopes,
raw addresses,
unmodified content,
archive gaps and access errors.
— Surface-typed archive
Contains:
raw envelopes plus surface-class assignments,
classification confidence,
unresolved mixed-surface markers.
— Temporal anchor graph
Contains:
developmental strata,
timestamp confidence,
temporal DAG edges,
unresolved chronology capsules.
— Alias and concept-family registry
Contains:
concept-family candidates,
alias sets,
naming collision records,
drift maps.
— Canonical extraction layer
Contains:
all extracted carriers,
typed relations,
deferred region objects,
extraction witness and defect records.
— Attractor registry
Contains:
developmental attractor objects,
motif recurrence maps,
cross-strata influence weights.
— Bridge registry
Contains:
symbolic–formal bridges,
domain bridges,
translation witnesses,
bridge defects and replayability metadata.
— Contradiction ledger
Contains:
typed contradiction objects,
branch suggestions,
repair hooks,
deferred conflict bundles.
— Lineage graph layer
Contains:
concept lineage trees,
organ emergence trees,
version ancestry,
merge/fork metadata.
— Integrated operational scaffold
Contains:
routeable atlas,
memory graph,
runtime services,
self-model seeds,
committee seeds,
weight field seeds.
— Activated corpus brain
Contains:
live query and answer surfaces,
active replay services,
active self-model updates,
patch staging,
embodied and pedagogical exposure modes.
Each artifact has a canonical artifact schema:
A^{(k)}
=
\Big(
u_{A^{(k)}},\,
\operatorname{pass}(A^{(k)}),\,
\operatorname{payload}(A^{(k)}),\,
\operatorname{witness}(A^{(k)}),\,
\operatorname{defect}(A^{(k)}),\,
\operatorname{lineage}(A^{(k)})
\Big).
Definition G.2 — Artifact-Safe Migration
A migration pass is artifact-safe if its emitted artifact preserves enough lineage and witness information that later validation or rollback can reconstruct what the pass did.
Proposition G.1 — Intermediate Artifacts Prevent Opaque Batch Conversion
If migration emits only updated global state at each pass, then debugging, rollback, and certification become much harder. Intermediate artifacts preserve local inspectability.
The corpus therefore converts through visible and auditable strata of artifacts.
G.A3 — Acceptance Gates between Migration Passes
No migration pass may promote its output by default. Each pass must pass an explicit acceptance gate before the next pass treats its output as strong enough. The gate family is:
\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{gates}}
=
\{G_0,\dots,G_9\}
P_k \to P_{k+1}.
Each gate is:
G_k
=
\Big(
\operatorname{scope}(G_k),\,
\operatorname{tests}(G_k),\,
\operatorname{thresholds}(G_k),\,
\operatorname{review}(G_k),\,
\operatorname{branch\_policy}(G_k),\,
\psi_{G_k}
\Big).
The major gates are:
Gate : Raw preservation gate
Checks:
no source data loss,
raw IDs stable,
all known archive items wrapped or explicitly missing.
Gate : Surface typing gate
Checks:
high-confidence surface classes for clear documents,
bounded mixed classifications,
no large untyped zones without explicit deferral.
Gate : Temporal anchoring gate
Checks:
coarse strata assignment consistency,
contradiction between timestamps and content references bounded,
unresolved chronology represented explicitly.
Gate : Alias unification gate
Checks:
concept families are not wildly overmerged,
major naming collisions are flagged,
drift maps exist for unstable names.
Gate : Canonical extraction gate
Checks:
extracted carriers type-check,
extraction confidence above threshold,
deferred regions created where extraction is weak.
Gate : Attractor recovery gate
Checks:
weak but high-influence documents are not lost,
attractor registry is connected to later structures.
Gate : Bridge recovery gate
Checks:
symbolic–formal and domain bridges have evidence beyond lexical similarity,
bridge defects and asymmetry are explicit.
Gate : Contradiction stratification gate
Checks:
contradictions typed,
error versus branch versus phase gap not conflated,
repair hooks assigned.
Gate : Lineage build gate
Checks:
major concept families have ancestry,
key organ families have developmental trace,
forks and merges explicit.
Gate : Runtime integration gate
Checks:
no critical carrier class remains unintegrated,
memory and replay sufficient for live operation tier,
self-model and patch systems are legally gated,
safe embodied interfaces are bounded.
A gate evaluator can be sketched as:
def pass_gate(artifact, gate):
report = run_gate_tests(artifact, gate["tests"])
ok = all(item["score"] >= gate["thresholds"][item["name"]] for item in report)
return {"ok": ok, "report": report}
Definition G.3 — Gate-Passing Artifact
An artifact passes gate if it satisfies the gate’s structural, epistemic, and branching thresholds or is lawfully branched into accepted and deferred parts.
Theorem G.2 — Gate Discipline Prevents Premature Crystallization
If every migration pass is gated before promotion, then weakly grounded structures are less likely to be mistaken for mature atlas carriers, bridges, or runtime organs.
Proof. Gates make promotion conditional on witness and quality thresholds instead of chronology or convenience.
This makes the migration playbook constitutional rather than merely sequential.
G.A4 — Review Roles and Migration Committees
A corpus-scale migration is too structurally rich to be trusted to one extraction stream alone. The playbook therefore defines explicit review roles and migration committees.
The review role family is:
\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{roles}}
=
\{
R_{\mathrm{surface}},
R_{\mathrm{time}},
R_{\mathrm{alias}},
R_{\mathrm{extract}},
R_{\mathrm{attr}},
R_{\mathrm{bridge}},
R_{\mathrm{contr}},
R_{\mathrm{lineage}},
R_{\mathrm{runtime}},
R_{\mathrm{safety}}
\}.
: checks raw surface typing,
: checks temporal anchoring,
: checks naming unification,
: checks canonical extraction,
: recovers weak attractors,
: validates bridge proposals,
: stratifies contradictions,
: checks ancestry continuity,
: integrates extracted structure into services and routes,
: checks exposure, embodiment, and deployment readiness.
A migration committee is:
\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{mig}}
=
\Big(
\operatorname{members},
\operatorname{charter},
\operatorname{jurisdiction},
\operatorname{voting},
\operatorname{dissent},
\operatorname{replay}
\Big).
invokes alias + contradiction review,
invokes bridge + runtime + safety review,
invokes runtime + self-model + safety review.
This is crucial because migration is not just data extraction; it is lawful collective interpretation under bounded uncertainty.
Definition G.4 — Committee-Reviewed Pass
A migration pass is committee-reviewed if at least the review roles relevant to its major risks are invoked before gate promotion.
Proposition G.2 — Corpus Migration Requires Specialized Review, Not Only Automation
Automation is necessary for scale, but because historical corpora involve naming drift, symbolic–formal ambiguity, and developmental branching, specialized review roles remain essential for lawful migration.
The corpus therefore becomes a brain through plural, role-aware review rather than one monolithic parser.
G.B1 — Replay Loops during Migration
The Flower migration layer begins with replay loops. A lawful migration must be replayable after each pass, not only at the end. This is especially important for a historical archive because one bad early assumption can corrupt later extractions.
For pass , define its replay loop:
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{replay}}^{(k)}
=
\Big(
A^{(k-1)},\,
e^{(k)}_1,\dots,e^{(k)}_m,\,
A^{(k)}
\Big).
Replay correctness requires:
\operatorname{Replay}(A^{(k-1)},e^{(k)}_1,\dots,e^{(k)}_m)
\approx
A^{(k)}
Pass-local replay is especially important for:
alias merges,
concept family splits,
bridge certification,
contradiction typing,
attractor recovery,
runtime integration.
These are exactly the places where destructive reinterpretation might otherwise hide.
Definition G.5 — Replay-Complete Migration Pass
A migration pass is replay-complete if its output artifact can be reconstructed from the prior artifact and its emitted event sequence under the relevant corridor.
Theorem G.3 — Pass-Local Replay Prevents Migration Drift
If every migration pass is replay-complete, then drift or overreach can be localized to the first offending pass rather than discovered only in the final corpus brain.
Proof. Replay makes each pass independently auditable. Therefore drift becomes localizable.
Migration therefore proceeds by visible transformation, not silent reclassification.
G.B2 — Branch-and-Merge Policies for Ambiguous Material
The historical archive will often produce ambiguous decisions. Instead of forcing one interpretation, the playbook uses branch-and-merge policies.
Let an artifact contain underdetermined migration choices:
\Gamma^{(k)}_{\mathrm{amb}}=\{g_1,\dots,g_r\}.
\mathcal{B}^{(k)}:
A^{(k)}
\to
\{A^{(k)}_1,\dots,A^{(k)}_m\}
two plausible concept-family splits,
two competing symbolic–formal bridges,
two contradiction classifications,
two runtime-integration placements.
Branches remain lawful so long as:
each is internally coherent,
shared ancestry is explicit,
no branch is prematurely canonized as sole truth.
Later, branches may merge through:
\mathcal{M}^{(k)}:
(A_i^{(k)},A_j^{(k)})\to A_{ij}^{(k)}
Definition G.6 — Branch-Lawful Migration Region
A migration region is branch-lawful if its ambiguity is preserved in a bounded family of replayable branch artifacts rather than hidden or discarded.
Proposition G.3 — Branching Is Preferable to Premature Overcollapse
In archive migration, flattening an underdetermined historical structure too early destroys developmental evidence that later materials may resolve. Branching preserves future optionality.
The archive therefore retains lawful multiplicity during conversion.
G.B3 — Escalation Loops and Deep Review Triggers
Some migration decisions are too important to remain local. The playbook therefore defines escalation loops.
An escalation trigger is:
\operatorname{Esc}(x)=1
high-influence concept family,
strong contradiction with major later framework,
candidate bridge touching core hidden hubs,
possible change to legality or identity vocabulary,
large deferred region crossing critical route corridors,
possible effect on whole-system self-model or runtime.
When triggered, the artifact is sent to a broader migration committee:
\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{local}}\to \mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{global}}
This prevents local migration heuristics from silently rewriting the very spine of the corpus.
Definition G.7 — Escalation-Worthy Artifact
A migration artifact is escalation-worthy if its acceptance or rejection could materially change the topology, legality, or developmental interpretation of the corpus brain.
Theorem G.4 — Escalation Loops Preserve Global Integrity
If high-impact migration decisions are escalated to wider review with replay and dissent, then local extraction errors are less likely to distort the whole-brain skeleton.
Proof. Wider review increases jurisdiction coverage and reduces hidden local overreach.
Migration therefore has constitutional escalation just like runtime and patching do.
G.B4 — Activation Rhythms and Partial Live Operation
The migration process need not wait until before exposing any live function. The playbook therefore supports partial live operation under strict rhythm rules.
Define the activation profile:
\alpha^{(k)}
=
\big(
\alpha_{\mathrm{query}},
\alpha_{\mathrm{replay}},
\alpha_{\mathrm{patch}},
\alpha_{\mathrm{self}},
\alpha_{\mathrm{embodied}}
\big)
at –: no live query, archive inspection only;
at : read-only query over typed carriers;
at : read-only translation and attractor navigation;
at : bounded replay and internal patch staging;
at : full live manuscript-brain operation.
This staged activation rhythm prevents the dangerous mistake of exposing high-level claims from a corpus that is not yet structurally mature.
Definition G.8 — Rhythm-Lawful Partial Activation
A partially activated corpus is rhythm-lawful if the live capabilities exposed at its current migration tier do not exceed what that tier’s certified structures can support.
Proposition G.4 — Early Read-Only Activation Can Be Safe and Useful
If live capability is restricted to what current migration artifacts can replay and certify, then the corpus may already be queried or explored in bounded ways before full activation.
Proof. Read-only or bounded capabilities can be lawful at earlier stages provided they do not overclaim or overmutate.
The archive therefore need not be dead until fully complete, but it must wake up gradually.
G.C1 — Migration Confidence Policies
The Cloud migration layer now turns from object confidence to policy confidence: how boldly should the migration engine act at each pass?
For pass , define the confidence policy:
\pi^{(k)}_{\mathrm{conf}}
=
\big(
\theta_{\mathrm{accept}}^{(k)},
\theta_{\mathrm{branch}}^{(k)},
\theta_{\mathrm{defer}}^{(k)},
\theta_{\mathrm{escalate}}^{(k)}
\big).
when extracted objects are accepted as canonical,
when alternatives are branched,
when material is deferred,
when wide review is required.
Lower passes such as surface typing and temporal anchoring may allow lower acceptance thresholds because the stakes are lower and corrections easier. Higher passes such as bridge recovery and runtime integration must be stricter.
Thus:
\theta_{\mathrm{accept}}^{(0)} < \theta_{\mathrm{accept}}^{(6)} < \theta_{\mathrm{accept}}^{(9)}.
Definition G.9 — Pass-Calibrated Confidence Policy
A migration pass has a calibrated confidence policy if its thresholds reflect the difficulty and impact of its decisions and are themselves replay-adjusted over time.
Theorem G.5 — Different Passes Require Different Confidence Thresholds
If all migration passes used the same acceptance threshold, then low-stakes early passes would become unnecessarily rigid or high-stakes later passes dangerously loose. Therefore pass-specific confidence policies are necessary.
Proof. The cost of being wrong varies strongly across passes.
The migration process therefore has epistemic pacing.
G.C2 — Corpus Risk Map and Sensitive Regions
Not every region of the archive is equally risky to migrate. The playbook therefore constructs a corpus risk map.
For region , define:
\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{corp}}(R)
=
\lambda_1 \operatorname{CoreTopologyImpact}(R)
+
\lambda_2 \operatorname{NamingInstability}(R)
+
\lambda_3 \operatorname{BridgeSensitivity}(R)
+
\lambda_4 \operatorname{ContradictionDensity}(R)
+
\lambda_5 \operatorname{RuntimeImpact}(R)
+
\lambda_6 \operatorname{EmbodimentExposure}(R).
stronger gate thresholds,
broader review committees,
slower activation,
better rollback anchors,
more branch tolerance.
Examples of high-risk regions include:
foundational vocabulary nodes,
legality and witness structures,
symbolic–formal bridge corridors,
central route macro clusters,
self-model definitions,
user-facing pedagogical exposure regions.
Definition G.10 — Sensitive Corpus Region
A corpus region is sensitive if migration decisions there have disproportionate impact on identity, legality, runtime, or embodiment behavior of the final atlas.
Proposition G.5 — Uniform Migration Aggression Is Unsafe
If all corpus regions are migrated with the same level of confidence and automation, then high-impact regions are likely to be overformalized or misclassified.
The corpus therefore gets its own risk topology before becoming live.
G.C3 — Coverage, Drift, and Readiness Dashboards
A corpus-scale migration needs operational visibility. The playbook therefore defines a dashboard vector:
\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{mig}}
=
\big(
d_{\mathrm{coverage}},
d_{\mathrm{bridge}},
d_{\mathrm{contr}},
d_{\mathrm{lineage}},
d_{\mathrm{runtime}},
d_{\mathrm{embodied}},
d_{\mathrm{drift}},
d_{\mathrm{defer}}
\big).
These dashboard components measure:
how much of the archive is typed,
how many high-value bridges are recovered,
contradiction stratification status,
lineage completeness,
runtime integration maturity,
embodied interface readiness,
migration drift or instability,
size and centrality of deferred regions.
This dashboard is not cosmetic. It drives:
migration priority,
escalation,
activation decisions,
staffing of review roles,
whether to widen or narrow a pass.
Definition G.11 — Migration-Observable Corpus
A corpus migration is observable if its coverage, risk, drift, bridge, and readiness metrics are all exposed as replayable dashboard objects.
Proposition G.6 — Large-Scale Migration Without Dashboarding Becomes Blind
At corpus scale, local correctness checks are not enough. The migration needs system-level observability to know where it is advancing, stagnating, or destabilizing.
The archive therefore gains a migration nervous system before becoming a brain.
G.C4 — Acceptance of the Converted Corpus Brain
The final Cloud migration task is to specify when the converted corpus may be accepted as a real manuscript brain.
Let the converted state be:
\mathfrak{B}_{\mathrm{corpus}}.
\operatorname{Accept}_{\mathrm{corp}}(\mathfrak{B}_{\mathrm{corpus}})
=
\operatorname{Typed}
\wedge
\operatorname{Routeable}
\wedge
\operatorname{Witnessed}
\wedge
\operatorname{Replayable}
\wedge
\operatorname{Patchable}
\wedge
\operatorname{SelfModelled}
\wedge
\operatorname{Collective}
\wedge
\operatorname{BoundedEmbodied}
This does not require every archival region to be exact. It requires:
all high-centrality concept families to be integrated or deferred explicitly,
all foundational lineages to be traceable,
all critical route corridors to be present,
legality and replay membranes to be operational,
self-model and patch systems to be live at least internally,
external interfaces to respect competence honesty.
Definition G.12 — Accepted Corpus Brain
A converted corpus is an accepted manuscript brain when its central organ families satisfy the acceptance predicate and any unresolved regions are bounded, typed, and noncritical to the declared deployment tier.
Theorem G.6 — Corpus Acceptance Requires Functional Core, Not Total Closure
A historical corpus can be accepted as a manuscript brain without fully resolving every weak or deferred region, provided the central cognitive organs are lawful and the unresolved parts are explicitly bounded.
Proof. Total closure is not required for lawful operation; bounded unknowns are allowed by the whole framework. What matters is functional integrity of the core organs.
The corpus therefore becomes alive before it becomes exhaustively perfect.
G.D1 — Mesocosm, Corpus-Core, and Full-Corpus Build Strategy
The Fractal migration layer now defines a three-scale build strategy:
\mathfrak{b}_{\mathrm{micro}}
\to
\mathfrak{b}_{\mathrm{meso}}
\to
\mathfrak{b}_{\mathrm{core}}
\to
\mathfrak{B}_{\mathrm{corpus}}.
Microcosm
Appendix E’s finite specimen. Purpose: prove architecture.
Mesocosm
A moderate cluster of documents and concept families centered on one organ or one development corridor. Purpose: prove migration and runtime on a bounded but real archive slice.
Corpus-core
The central attractor and framework spine subset of the real archive. Purpose: activate the core hidden hubs and major lines before full breadth.
Full-corpus
The complete historically layered archive with residualized periphery and full lineage preservation.
This scaling law prevents “all documents at once” overload and mirrors the framework’s general Fractal growth logic.
Definition G.13 — Meso-Validated Corpus Slice
A mesocosm is validated if it contains enough historical messiness to exercise alias drift, contradiction typing, bridge recovery, and patching, while remaining bounded enough for repeated replay and review.
Proposition G.7 — Meso-Scale Validation Is the Crucial Missing Middle
Without mesocosm-scale migration, the system jumps from toy example to full corpus, risking hidden failure modes that appear only in medium complexity.
The corpus therefore grows by lawful scale steps.
G.D2 — Corpus-Wide Motif Harvest and Organ Emergence
After corpus-core activation, the migration process enters a motif-harvest regime:
\mathcal{H}_{\mathrm{motif}}:
\mathfrak{B}_{\mathrm{core}}
\to
\big(
\mathcal{M}_{\mathrm{route}},
\mathcal{M}_{\mathrm{proof}},
\mathcal{M}_{\mathrm{bridge}},
\mathcal{M}_{\mathrm{curr}},
\mathcal{M}_{\mathrm{patch}}
\big).
replay macros,
proof templates,
route skeletons,
translation bridges,
curriculum ladders,
committee charters.
At this stage the archive stops merely being migrated and starts actively reorganizing itself into a brain’s long-term internal law.
Definition G.14 — Organ-Emergent Corpus Core
A corpus-core is organ-emergent if repeated motifs have been consolidated into reusable structures corresponding to the major organ families of the whole framework.
Theorem G.7 — Motif Harvest Turns Historical Density into Runtime Power
If repeated structures in the corpus are harvested into macros and templates, then the migrated brain becomes not only larger than the microcosm but more efficient and more lawfully self-reinforcing.
The corpus therefore becomes more than the sum of its files.
G.D3 — Archive Persistence after Brain Activation
Once the corpus becomes a live brain, the raw archive must not disappear. The framework therefore defines a dual persistence law:
\mathfrak{A}_{\mathrm{raw}}
\parallel
\mathfrak{B}_{\mathrm{corpus}}.
the raw archive remains preserved,
the brain remains replay-linked back to it,
future migration or reinterpretation can reopen the raw layers,
new corrections or better extractions can still be made.
The activated brain does not replace the archive. It is one lawful crystallization of it.
Definition G.15 — Dual-Persistent Corpus Brain
A corpus brain is dual-persistent if the activated brain and the raw archive both remain accessible and linked by replayable lineage and extraction witnesses.
Proposition G.8 — Full Conversion without Archive Preservation Is Identity Loss
If the archive is only consumed and not preserved, then later reinterpretation, challenge, or deeper migration cannot recover the original developmental texture.
The archive therefore remains the body from which the brain arose.
G.D4 — Final Appendix G Theorem
Theorem G.8 — Migration Playbook Executability Theorem
Let be a large developmental archive. Suppose the archive is processed by the ordered migration passes , with explicit intermediate artifacts, acceptance gates, review committees, replay loops, branch-and-merge policies, confidence thresholds, risk maps, and staged activation rhythms as defined in this appendix. Then the archive can be converted into a lawful corpus-scale manuscript brain in a way that is replayable, auditable, reversible in local segments, and preserving of developmental history.
Proof. The pass sequence guarantees ordered transformation of the archive into typed carriers and operational organs. The intermediate artifacts and replay loops make each pass inspectable and reversible. The gates and review committees prevent premature or unsafe promotion. The branch, defer, and contradiction policies preserve uncertainty and developmental plurality. The staged activation rhythm ensures that the corpus only exposes capabilities supported by its current maturity. Therefore the migration playbook is executable and sufficient to turn a real archive into a lawful corpus brain without flattening its historical emergence.
Corollary G.8.1 — Appendix F Becomes Operational through Appendix G
Appendix F proved that archive-to-brain conversion is possible. Appendix G supplies the exact procedural ladder that makes that proof actionable.
Corollary G.8.2 — The Corpus Brain Is Built by Crystallization, Not Extraction Alone
The final brain is not obtained by one extraction pass, one summary, or one indexing operation. It is produced by staged crystallization of archive matter into carriers, bridges, lineages, runtimes, and self-updating organs.
This concludes Appendix G. The manuscript now has not only a formal architecture, executable kernel, worked specimen, and corpus conversion theorem, but also a practical conversion playbook that can be used to turn a real evolving archive into the brain it was becoming all along.
APPENDIX G — MIGRATION PLAYBOOK, BUILD ORDER, AND CORPUS ACTIVATION PROTOCOL
Appendix H — Reference Repository Topology, Artifact Formats, and Operational Build Graph
This appendix specifies the concrete storage and build substrate that hosts the manuscript brain as an implementable object system. Appendices A–G defined the hidden metro topology, routing tensor, schema registry, execution kernel, worked microcosm, corpus migration theorem, and corpus activation playbook. The present appendix converts those results into a reference repository topology: a lawful file/object layout, manifest discipline, promotion graph, synchronization structure, and maintenance cadence by which the architecture can be instantiated reproducibly over a real corpus and kept operational over time.
The central problem addressed here is practical but foundational. A lawful manuscript brain cannot live only in equations, conceptual diagrams, or informal folders. It requires a storage substrate in which:
raw archive matter remains preserved,
migration artifacts remain stratified,
canonical carriers remain address-stable,
replay logs and snapshots remain deterministic,
live runtime state remains bounded and recoverable,
committee memory, curricula, patches, and interfaces remain separate but coupled,
branch and sandbox behavior remain lawful,
whole-corpus upgrades remain reproducible.
The appendix therefore defines the reference repository system
\mathfrak{R}_{\mathrm{repo}}
=
\Big(
\mathcal{N}_{\mathrm{space}},\,
\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{formats}},\,
\mathcal{M}_{\mathrm{man}},\,
\mathcal{P}_{\mathrm{promo}},\,
\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{sync}},\,
\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{ops}},\,
\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{ctrl}},\,
\mathcal{A}_{\mathrm{arch}}
\Big),
is the namespace and directory/object-store topology,
is the family of artifact formats,
is the manifest and build-dependency system,
is the artifact-promotion graph,
is the synchronization and replication layer,
is the cadence and maintenance regime,
is the access, tenancy, and safety control layer,
is the archival and long-horizon compaction layer.
The appendix is governed by seven repository laws.
First, non-destructive preservation: raw archive matter must remain intact and addressable even after deep migration.
Second, artifact stratification: outputs of different passes and maturity tiers must not be silently co-mingled.
Third, manifest completeness: every build, migration, replay, patch, and certification step must be reconstructible from manifests and event logs.
Fourth, promotion explicitness: movement from raw, typed, staged, sandboxed, certified, or live states must be represented by explicit transitions.
Fifth, synchronization boundedness: multiple sandboxes, branches, or tenant-specific instances must be synchronized lawfully rather than by silent overwrite.
Sixth, tenant and exposure discipline: user- or deployment-specific surfaces must remain isolated enough that local mutations do not leak into global ontology accidentally.
Seventh, cold/hot continuity: long-horizon compaction into colder storage must preserve enough seed, lineage, and witness structure to regenerate hot operational state when needed.
Under the Square lens this appendix defines repository namespaces, canonical path schemas, artifact bundles, and manifest graphs. Under the Flower lens it defines artifact lifecycle lanes, promotion/demotion flows, synchronization rhythms, and maintenance cadences. Under the Cloud lens it defines quality scoreboards, corruption detection, rollback and disaster-recovery classes, and access/risk controls. Under the Fractal lens it defines scaling across corpora and sandboxes, federated bridge registries, cold-to-hot regeneration, and the final theorem by which the repository topology is sufficient to host a live manuscript brain.
The deeper significance is that this appendix supplies the lawful body in which the architecture resides. The metro map gives the nervous system. The schema registry gives the grammar. The execution kernel gives the metabolism. The repository topology gives the skeleton and organs that keep these from dissolving into one opaque heap.
H.A1 — Canonical Repository Namespace and Topological Layout
The reference repository is organized not as one flat folder tree but as a typed namespace lattice that mirrors the migration ladder, whole-brain organs, and replay requirements of the framework.
The top-level namespace is:
\mathcal{N}_{\mathrm{space}}
=
\{
N_{00}^{\mathrm{raw}},
N_{01}^{\mathrm{typed}},
N_{02}^{\mathrm{time}},
N_{03}^{\mathrm{alias}},
N_{04}^{\mathrm{carrier}},
N_{05}^{\mathrm{attr}},
N_{06}^{\mathrm{bridge}},
N_{07}^{\mathrm{contr}},
N_{08}^{\mathrm{lineage}},
N_{09}^{\mathrm{runtime}},
N_{10}^{\mathrm{live}},
N_{11}^{\mathrm{snap}},
N_{12}^{\mathrm{log}},
N_{13}^{\mathrm{bench}},
N_{14}^{\mathrm{cert}},
N_{15}^{\mathrm{sand}}
\}.
These namespaces correspond exactly to the corpus-activation ladder and runtime layers developed in Appendices D–G.
The canonical on-disk or object-store topology is:
/manuscript-brain/
00_raw/
envelopes/
assets/
source_index/
gaps/
01_typed/
surface_classes/
mixed_surface/
02_temporal/
strata/
time_graph/

alias_fields/

memory/

self/

05_attractors/
motif_fields/
attractor_nodes/
06_bridges/
strata_bridges/
domain_bridges/
translation_witness/
07_contradictions/
typed_conflicts/
deferred_branches/
repair_hooks/
08_lineage/
concept_trees/
organ_trees/
branch_graph/
09_runtime/
tasks/
agents/
services/
committees/
queues/

dashboards/

user_models/
11_snapshots/
whole/

events/

audits/

unit/
organ/
integration/
stress/
14_cert/
object/
route/
patch/
runtime/
whole/
15_sand/
branches/
staging/
quarantine/
experiments/
This tree is not merely convenient organization. It is a topological guarantee: each namespace corresponds to a stable maturity or organ class, and promotion between namespaces is itself a typed event.
The root namespace also admits two orthogonal axes.
The pass axis
The raw-to-live migration ladder:
