Without stable artifact handles in responses, users and agents would be limited to one-off text exchanges. With handles, interaction becomes a lawful sequence over persistent atlas objects. (\square)
The manuscript therefore responds with objects, not only prose.
J.B1 — Interactive Refinement and Multi-Step Request Negotiation
The Flower invocation layer begins with interactive refinement. Many requests are underdetermined, especially when the caller does not specify:
exact scope,
intended contract,
proof vs explanation mode,
patch suggestion vs patch application,
target domain for translation,
desired audience level.
The protocol therefore supports refinement loops.
A refinement state is:
[
\Gamma_{\mathrm{req}}
\Big(\operatorname{candidate_scopes},\operatorname{candidate_contracts},\operatorname{candidate_artifacts},\operatorname{ambiguity_mass},\operatorname{refinement_options}\Big).]If the ambiguity is above threshold and no safe default exists, the system returns a RefinementResponse rather than forcing execution. A refinement response may:
ask for scope narrowing,
offer contract options,
show likely artifact interpretations,
suggest safe defaults,
invite committee review for ambiguous commands.
This is especially important for high-stakes or write-capable commands. For example:
PATCH! this without a resolved scope should not silently mutate the wrong carrier,
CERTIFY route family 12 without a contract may require choosing proof vs runtime certification,
TEACH this may need audience or mastery target if ambiguity is too high.
Definition J.5 — Refinement-Legal Invocation
An invocation is refinement-legal if unresolved ambiguity is either kept within a safe default envelope or converted into a refinement turn before execution continues.
Theorem J.5 — Refinement Responses Preserve Lawful Operation under Ambiguity
If underdetermined requests are refined rather than overcommitted, then the system reduces accidental scope errors, unsafe mutation, and contract mismatch.
Proof. Refinement preserves choice before irreversible or high-risk execution. (\square)
The invocation layer therefore negotiates with the caller before pretending certainty.
J.B2 — Phase-Sensitive Mode Switching
The same command verb may mean different things in different phases of work. The invocation protocol therefore uses phase-sensitive mode switching.
Let the high-level interaction modes be:
[
\Phi_{\mathrm{inv}}
{\mathrm{Explore},\mathrm{Construct},\mathrm{Strict},\mathrm{PatchReview},\mathrm{Teach},\mathrm{ReplayAudit},\mathrm{Sandbox}}.]Each mode biases:
default contract,
default corridor,
expected response class,
confidence and ambiguity policy,
whether committee review is invited,
whether write actions are allowed.
Examples:
ASK in (\mathrm{Explore}) mode defaults to broader, more plural, more analogy-friendly responses.
ASK in (\mathrm{Strict}) mode defaults to narrower, more witness-heavy answers.
TRANSLATE in (\mathrm{Teach}) mode may prioritize pedagogical rendering over exact reversibility.
PATCH? in (\mathrm{Sandbox}) mode may propose more exploratory mutations than in (\mathrm{PatchReview}) mode.
A mode switch may be explicit:
MODE Strict
MODE Teach
MODE Sandbox
or implicit from session stack and actor role.
Definition J.6 — Mode-Coherent Invocation
An invocation is mode-coherent if its default execution path is consistent with the current interaction mode and any deviation is explicit.
Theorem J.6 — Mode-Sensitive Commands Reduce Protocol Ambiguity
If commands are interpreted through an explicit phase/mode layer, then the same high-level verbs can be reused safely across different reasoning contexts without uncontrolled ambiguity. (\square)
The manuscript therefore changes how it listens depending on what kind of work is underway.
J.B3 — Committee-Aware Invocation and Multi-Party Calls
Some invocations should not go directly to one service or one answer pipeline. They should explicitly invoke collective cognition. The protocol therefore supports committee-aware invocation.
A committee-aware call adds:
[
\operatorname{committee}(\iota)
\Big(\operatorname{required_roles},\operatorname{jurisdiction},\operatorname{decision_rule},\operatorname{dissent_policy}\Big).]This can be requested explicitly, for example:
COMMITTEE review artifact:answer_v17
COMMITTEE translate concept_family:seed_loop AS CONTRACT exact
COMMITTEE arbitrate patch:delta_bridge_split_2
Committee-aware invocation is particularly important for:
proof vs pedagogy conflicts,
symbolic–formal bridge disputes,
patch promotion,
translation certification,
high-risk embodiment changes,
ambiguous self-model updates.
The response class is then a committee packet or committee-mediated artifact rather than a unilateral answer.
Definition J.7 — Committee-Bound Invocation
An invocation is committee-bound if it explicitly requires plural internal review before action or answer release.
Proposition J.1 — Some Acts Should Be Collectively Invoked by Default
In a manuscript brain with specialized subminds, certain high-impact acts are safer and more faithful when routed through committees rather than individual service pipelines. (\square)
The invocation layer therefore knows when not to ask only one voice.
J.B4 — Pedagogical and Embodied Turn Flows
The final Flower task is to define lawful turn flows for teaching and embodied guidance. These are not simple single-turn requests. They are structured multi-turn protocols.
A pedagogical turn flow is:
[
\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{teach}}
\big(\mathrm{Assess},\mathrm{Select},\mathrm{Render},\mathrm{Check},\mathrm{Adapt},\mathrm{Consolidate}\big).]A typical invocation sequence is:
TEACH target
system assesses scope and mastery,
returns lesson or challenge,
user or agent responds,
system checks result,
system adapts or advances.
An embodied guidance flow similarly may be:
[
\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{emb}}
\big(\mathrm{Sense},\mathrm{Estimate},\mathrm{Propose},\mathrm{Constrain},\mathrm{Act},\mathrm{Observe},\mathrm{Update}\big).]The invocation layer must therefore support ongoing protocol sessions, not only one-off commands. The session stack and receipts make this lawful.
Definition J.8 — Flow-Coherent Multi-Turn Protocol
A multi-turn pedagogical or embodied interaction is flow-coherent if every turn advances a declared interaction state machine and no turn silently jumps phases or action rights.
Theorem J.7 — Multi-Turn Protocols Are Necessary for Lawful Teaching and Embodiment
Teaching and embodied guidance depend on state progression, feedback, and adaptation. Therefore they cannot be modeled adequately as isolated single-turn invocations. (\square)
The invocation layer therefore acquires living conversational and interactive form.
J.C1 — Ambiguity, Defaults, and Confidence Gating at Invocation Time
The Cloud invocation layer begins with uncertainty. An invocation packet may be ambiguous in many ways:
target name collision,
multiple candidate scopes,
unclear desired contract,
unclear write/read intent,
mixed proof/pedagogy expectations.
The invocation ambiguity object is:
[
\Gamma_{\mathrm{inv}}
\Big(\operatorname{scope_ambiguity},\operatorname{target_ambiguity},\operatorname{contract_ambiguity},\operatorname{intent_ambiguity},\operatorname{permission_ambiguity}\Big).]A confidence-gated dispatcher determines whether to:
execute directly,
choose a safe default,
ask for refinement,
require committee arbitration,
refuse or defer.
The safe-default law is:[\text{if ambiguity is high and action is high-risk, do not default into write-capable or exact-certification behavior.}]Thus:
ambiguous PATCH! defaults to refinement or sandboxing,
ambiguous CERTIFY defaults to review, not certification,
ambiguous TRANSLATE may default to approximate explanatory mode if safe.
Definition J.9 — Confidence-Gated Invocation
An invocation is confidence-gated if dispatch decisions are controlled by ambiguity and confidence rather than assumed intent certainty.
Theorem J.8 — Safe Defaults Must Be Asymmetric
When a request could mean either a weaker or stronger act, lawful defaults should favor the weaker act unless strong evidence supports the stronger one. (\square)
The invocation layer therefore treats uncertainty as first-class at the moment of address.
J.C2 — Authorization, Write Rights, and Capability Resolution
Not all actors may invoke all intents. The protocol therefore includes authorization and capability resolution.
Given actor (a) and invocation (\iota), the authorization resolver computes:
[
\operatorname{Auth}(a,\iota)
\operatorname{RoleCompat}(a,\iota)\wedge\operatorname{TenantCompat}(a,\iota)\wedge\operatorname{ScopeCompat}(a,\iota)\wedge\operatorname{CapabilityCompat}(a,\iota)\wedge\operatorname{CorridorCompat}(a,\iota).]This yields one of:[{\mathrm{Allow},\mathrm{AllowReadOnly},\mathrm{AllowSandbox},\mathrm{ReviewRequired},\mathrm{Deny}}.]
The most sensitive distinctions are:
read vs write,
suggest vs apply,
sandbox vs mainline,
approximate answer vs certification,
private tenant scope vs shared ontology scope.
Definition J.10 — Authorization-Resolved Invocation
An invocation is authorization-resolved if the system has determined the actor’s effective action rights for that exact target, scope, and contract.
Theorem J.9 — Intent Alone Does Not Determine Executability
A request may be perfectly well formed and still not executable because the actor lacks scope, tenant, corridor, or write permissions. Therefore authorization must be resolved independently of parsing. (\square)
The manuscript therefore distinguishes understanding a request from being allowed to fulfill it.
J.C3 — Error Objects, Refusal Classes, and Deferred Responses
When execution cannot proceed, the system must return a structured error, refusal, or deferred object rather than vague failure text.
The error class family is:
[
\mathcal{K}_{\mathrm{err}}
{
\mathrm{ParseError},
\mathrm{ScopeError},
\mathrm{AuthError},
\mathrm{ContractError},
\mathrm{ReplayError},
\mathrm{PatchError},
\mathrm{BridgeError},
\mathrm{CommitteeError},
\mathrm{CapabilityError},
\mathrm{BudgetError}
}.
]
A generic error response is:
[
E
\Big(u_E,,\operatorname{kind}(E),,\operatorname{scope}(E),,\operatorname{cause}(E),,\operatorname{next_actions}(E),,\psi_E\Big).]This is important because error objects themselves may become:
replay items,
prompts for refinement,
self-model evidence,
curriculum feedback,
patch suggestions.
Refusal classes are different from errors. Refusals arise when:
the act is unsafe,
the actor lacks permission,
the corridor forbids the action,
the system chooses bounded noncompliance.
Deferred responses arise when:
the invocation is plausible but underdetermined,
required witnesses or committee review are missing,
the system can proceed only after refinement or replay.
Definition J.11 — Structured Nonexecution Response
A nonexecution response is structured if it identifies why execution did not proceed and what lawful next steps remain.
Proposition J.2 — Good Invocation Protocols Need Rich Failure Semantics
In a lawful manuscript brain, refusal, error, deferral, and refinement are different epistemic states and should not be collapsed into one generic failure string. (\square)
The invocation layer therefore fails lawfully and informatively.
J.C4 — Invocation Receipts, Audit Headers, and Attestation Hooks
The final Cloud invocation task is to attach every invocation to the verification mesh and repository. This is done through audit headers and attestation hooks.
An audit header is:
[
H_{\mathrm{audit}}
\Big(u_H,,\operatorname{invocation_id},,\operatorname{session_id},,\operatorname{actor},,\operatorname{time},,\operatorname{scope},,\operatorname{intent},,\operatorname{corridor},,\operatorname{contract},,\operatorname{result_class}\Big).]It is attached to the invocation receipt and logged in the repository event stream. This enables:
replay,
certification basis reconstruction,
user/session analysis,
regression coverage mapping,
sentinel triggers.
Attestation hooks specify whether the invocation result should:
update golden traces,
count toward benchmark coverage,
trigger certification expiry,
affect self-model calibration,
alter tenant-specific competence claims.
Definition J.12 — Verification-Linked Invocation
An invocation is verification-linked if its receipt includes audit headers and the relevant attestation hooks for later continuous verification.
Theorem J.10 — Invocation Attestation Closes the Loop between Use and Verification
If each invocation is logged with enough audit structure, then real use feeds back into the verification mesh rather than remaining operationally invisible. (\square)
The invocation layer therefore is not outside the verification mesh. It is one of its main data sources.
J.D1 — Reference EBNF and Structured Command Forms
The Fractal invocation layer begins by formalizing the grammar more precisely. A reference EBNF-style grammar is:
<invocation> ::= <verb> <target> [<scope_clause>] [<contract_clause>]
[<corridor_clause>] [<budget_clause>] [<expect_clause>]
[<modifier_clause>] [<committee_clause>] [<session_clause>]
<verb> ::= "ASK" | "ROUTE" | "GET" | "EXPLAIN" | "PROVE" | "REPLAY"
| "TRANSLATE" | "COMPARE" | "TEACH" | "PATCH?" | "PATCH!"
| "INSPECT" | "BENCH" | "CERTIFY" | "COMMITTEE" | "SANDBOX"
| "ACTIVATE"
<target> ::= <artifact_ref> | <concept_ref> | <slice_ref> | <service_ref> | <query_text>
<scope_clause> ::= "IN SCOPE" <scope_ref>
<contract_clause> ::= "AS CONTRACT" <contract_ref>
<corridor_clause> ::= "UNDER CORRIDOR" <corridor_ref>
<budget_clause> ::= "WITH BUDGET" <budget_ref>
<expect_clause> ::= "EXPECT" <response_class>
<modifier_clause> ::= "USING" <modifier_bundle>
<committee_clause> ::= "WITH COMMITTEE" <committee_spec>
<session_clause> ::= "IN SESSION" <session_ref>
This grammar can be rendered in:
plain language,
structured JSON,
command palette UI,
agent protocol messages,
API calls.
A structured JSON-like equivalent is:
{
"intent": "PROVE",
"target": {"kind": "theorem", "id": "route_legality"},
"scope": {"tenant": "charlie", "chapter": "17"},
"contract": {"name": "exact_proof"},
"corridor": {"name": "strict"},
"budget": {"name": "high"},
"expect": "WitnessBundle",
"modifiers": {"show_ladder": true}
}
Definition J.13 — Grammar-Equivalent Invocation Forms
Two invocation forms are grammar-equivalent if they compile to the same canonical invocation packet up to serialization differences.
Theorem J.11 — Surface Grammar Plurality Preserves One Protocol Core
Plain-language commands, API payloads, UI actions, and agent messages can all be different surfaces of the same invocation protocol so long as they compile to the same packet structure. (\square)
The protocol therefore supports many interfaces without fragmenting its core semantics.
J.D2 — Compilation from Invocation to Kernel Plan
Once parsed, the invocation packet must compile into a kernel plan. This is the bridge from Appendix J to Appendix D.
The kernel plan is:
[
\pi_{\mathrm{kernel}}
\Big(\operatorname{phase_subset},\operatorname{algorithms},\operatorname{route_hubs},\operatorname{committee_hooks},\operatorname{permissions},\operatorname{response_renderer},\operatorname{attest_hooks}\Big).]Examples:
ASK
Usually compiles into:
normalize,
retrieve,
route,
synthesize,
judge,
render.
REPLAY
Compiles into:
scope resolution,
snapshot lookup,
event trace extraction,
replay reducer,
comparison,
replay artifact render.
PATCH!
Compiles into:
auth check,
patch resolution,
local merge,
global merge or sandbox staging,
heal,
checkpoint,
receipt.
COMMITTEE review
Compiles into:
committee assembly or retrieval,
artifact presentation,
negotiation protocol,
vote or mediation,
committee response render.
The compiled plan is what the runtime actually executes.
Definition J.14 — Kernel-Compilable Invocation
An invocation is kernel-compilable if it resolves into a finite lawful kernel plan whose required phases, algorithms, and permissions are all available.
Theorem J.12 — Protocol and Kernel Are Compositionally Linked
If each command verb has a kernel-plan template and each template resolves through the authorization and refinement layers, then invocation and execution are compositionally linked. (\square)
The invocation layer therefore truly drives the brain rather than floating above it.
J.D3 — Command Macros, Saved Procedures, and Reusable Scripts
Repeated invocation patterns should themselves become reusable objects. The protocol therefore supports command macros and saved procedures.
A command macro is:
[
\mathfrak{m}
\Big(u_{\mathfrak{m}},,\operatorname{name}(\mathfrak{m}),,\operatorname{pattern}(\mathfrak{m}),,\operatorname{parameters}(\mathfrak{m}),,\operatorname{kernel_plan}(\mathfrak{m}),,\operatorname{permissions}(\mathfrak{m}),,\psi_{\mathfrak{m}}\Big).]Examples:
REVIEW_THEOREM(name) → committee proof review,
TRACE_ROUTE(target) → hidden-hub and chapter route display,
PATCH_CYCLE(residual) → suggest, sandbox, review, apply,
TEACH_LADDER(topic, level) → curriculum rendering at a chosen mastery band,
CERTIFY_SCOPE(scope, tier) → certification pass over a bounded region.
Saved procedures are then stored in the repository as:
macros,
scripts,
tenant-local tools,
committee playbooks,
curriculum routines.
Definition J.15 — Replayable Command Macro
A command macro is replayable if its parameter bindings and kernel-plan expansion are preserved so that later execution can reconstruct what concrete invocation family it denotes.
Proposition J.3 — Reusable Invocation Patterns Should Be First-Class
If recurring interaction patterns are not elevated into macros, then the same protocol logic must be rediscovered or manually reconstructed repeatedly. (\square)
The invocation layer therefore grows a tool language over time.
J.D4 — Final Appendix J Theorem
Theorem J.13 — Invocation Protocol Sufficiency Theorem
Let the manuscript brain of Chapters 1–21 and Appendices A–I be given. Then the canonical invocation packet, request grammar, session model, authorization resolver, response and receipt system, refinement and committee-aware control rules, and kernel compilation layer defined in this appendix are sufficient to operate the manuscript brain as a lawful externally addressable system.
Proof.The invocation packet provides a complete and typed ingress form.The command grammar provides practical surface realizations.Session state preserves multi-turn coherence.Authorization and confidence gating constrain unsafe execution.Response objects and receipts make outputs replayable and operable.Kernel compilation maps control-plane requests to the execution machinery of Appendix D.Command macros lift repeated interaction into reusable protocol objects.Therefore the system can be lawfully addressed, steered, queried, reviewed, patched, taught, and certified through this invocation layer. (\square)
Corollary J.13.1 — The Manuscript Brain Now Has an Operable Language
With this appendix, the brain is no longer merely buildable and verifiable. It is speakable to, steerable, and commandable through one lawful protocol surface.
Corollary J.13.2 — Appendices A–J Form a Complete Topology-to-Control Stack
The appendicial sequence now spans:
topology,
routing,
schema,
execution,
specimen,
corpus conversion,
migration playbook,
repository substrate,
continuous verification,
and external/internal invocation.
This concludes Appendix J. The manuscript brain now has not only a body, memory, runtime, verification mesh, and migration playbook, but also a formal command language by which it may be asked, instructed, tested, patched, taught, and operated as one lawful cognitive organism.
APPENDIX J — QUERY GRAMMAR, CONTROL PLANE, AND INVOCATION PROTOCOLS
Appendix K — Policy Envelopes, Consent Boundaries, and Safe Action Governance
This appendix defines the policy envelope of the manuscript brain: the lawful boundary layer that determines what the system may do, for whom, under which conditions, at what confidence, in which sandbox, with what consent, and with what revocation and audit guarantees. Appendices A–J established the hidden topology, routing tensor, schema registry, execution kernel, worked specimen, archive migration theorem, migration playbook, repository substrate, continuous verification mesh, and invocation/control plane. The present appendix now sits one layer above all of them and answers the question:
[\text{Given that the manuscript brain can understand, route, replay, patch, teach, and act, when is it actually permitted to do so?}]
The framework has already introduced legality, contracts, permissions, embodiments, committees, and tenant isolation. But those elements appeared distributed across many chapters and appendices. What has not yet been written as one explicit surface is the policy-envelope layer: the operating shell that combines trust, consent, exposure, sandboxing, promotion, revocation, and governance into one bounded regime.
The central object of this appendix is the policy-envelope system
[
\mathfrak{P}_{\mathrm{env}}
\Big(\mathcal{E}{\mathrm{mode}},,\mathcal{T}{\mathrm{trust}},,\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{cons}},,\mathcal{B}{\mathrm{bound}},,\mathcal{S}{\mathrm{sand}},,\mathcal{X}{\mathrm{esc}},,\mathcal{R}{\mathrm{rev}},,\mathcal{A}{\mathrm{audit}}\Big),]where:
(\mathcal{E}_{\mathrm{mode}}) is the family of operating modes and policy envelopes,
(\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{trust}}) is the trust and authorization lattice,
(\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{cons}}) is the consent object family,
(\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{bound}}) is the boundary and exposure system,
(\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{sand}}) is the sandbox and containment family,
(\mathcal{X}_{\mathrm{esc}}) is the escalation and approval flow system,
(\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{rev}}) is the revocation, pause, and emergency-stop layer,
(\mathcal{A}_{\mathrm{audit}}) is the audit and trust-lineage layer.
This appendix is governed by eight governance laws.
First, policy explicitness: every nontrivial action or surface must occur under a named policy envelope rather than under implicit defaults.
Second, capability boundedness: what the system can do in principle is never identical to what it may do in the present envelope.
Third, consent specificity: consent must be typed by action class, scope, and persistence horizon, not represented by one generic boolean.
Fourth, containment before power: high-risk or undercertified actions must begin in smaller sandboxes and only later promote outward.
Fifth, revocability: every meaningful increase in action power must admit revocation, downgrade, or freeze.
Sixth, audit lineage: every policy decision, escalation, or override must be recorded as a replayable governance event.
Seventh, cross-scale policy inheritance: local, tenant, branch, and global policies must compose lawfully rather than overwrite one another silently.
Eighth, safe autonomy: the manuscript brain may become increasingly self-directed only by moving through explicit autonomy tiers with corresponding verification and consent conditions.
Under the Square lens this appendix defines policy envelopes, trust classes, consent schemas, boundary objects, and safe surface classifications. Under the Flower lens it defines sandbox gradients, promotion and escalation flows, policy-mode switching, and runtime harmonization under changing governance regimes. Under the Cloud lens it defines risk-weighted policy decisions, privacy and retention controls, auditability, nonrepudiation, and policy-drift detection. Under the Fractal lens it defines policy inheritance across scales, federated governance, autonomy tiers, and the theorem that this layer is sufficient to keep the live manuscript brain inside lawful action boundaries.
The deepest significance is that this appendix gives the manuscript brain not just a law, but a governing shell. It specifies not only what the brain is, but under what modes it may safely become more active.
K.A1 — Canonical Policy Envelopes and Operating Modes
Every invocation, runtime process, embodiment action, patch path, or committee decision takes place inside a policy envelope. A policy envelope is a named operating shell that binds together:
action permissions,
trust assumptions,
exposure boundaries,
sandbox rights,
consent requirements,
escalation paths,
revocation hooks.
A canonical policy envelope is:
[
\varepsilon
\Big(u_\varepsilon,,\operatorname{name}(\varepsilon),,\operatorname{scope}(\varepsilon),,\operatorname{allowed_acts}(\varepsilon),,\operatorname{required_trust}(\varepsilon),,\operatorname{required_consent}(\varepsilon),,\operatorname{sandbox_policy}(\varepsilon),,\operatorname{escalation_policy}(\varepsilon),,\operatorname{revocation_policy}(\varepsilon),,\psi_\varepsilon\Big).]
The manuscript brain defines a canonical family of operating modes:
[
\mathcal{E}_{\mathrm{mode}}
{E_{\mathrm{read}},E_{\mathrm{explore}},E_{\mathrm{strict}},E_{\mathrm{sandbox}},E_{\mathrm{review}},E_{\mathrm{teach}},E_{\mathrm{patch}},E_{\mathrm{cert}},E_{\mathrm{live}}}.]
These are interpreted as follows.
(E_{\mathrm{read}})
Read-only exploration of artifacts, routes, slices, dashboards, and lineage.No patching, no certification, no live mutation, no high-risk embodiment.
(E_{\mathrm{explore}})
Broader retrieval, translation, analogy, and experimental reasoning.Approximate artifacts and plural responses allowed.No direct mainline mutation.
(E_{\mathrm{strict}})
Witness-heavy, replay-sensitive, narrow-confidence operation.Used for proofs, certifications, exact translations, or legal acceptance decisions.
(E_{\mathrm{sandbox}})
Branch-local experimentation with explicit containment.Can propose and even apply patches inside sandbox scope, but no silent promotion.
(E_{\mathrm{review}})
Committee and governance mode.Used for arbitration, bridge disputes, contradiction review, migration review, and certification gating.
(E_{\mathrm{teach}})
Pedagogical and embodied explanation mode.Allows adaptive rendering and curriculum branching within bounded educational contracts.
(E_{\mathrm{patch}})
Patch suggestion or patch application mode.Heavily permission- and rollback-gated.
(E_{\mathrm{cert}})
Certification and recertification mode.Uses gold traces, regression matrices, and verification mesh outputs to issue attestation bundles.
(E_{\mathrm{live}})
Fully activated runtime mode.Permits all already-certified live surfaces and services to operate, but still only under scope- and trust-bounded contracts.
A policy envelope is not only a label. It changes:
which invocation intents are legal,
what default corridors are chosen,
whether committee review is mandatory,
whether answers may be approximate,
whether mutation may reach warm or hot namespaces,
whether embodiment may adapt actively.
A minimal envelope schema is:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, Any, List
@dataclass
class PolicyEnvelope:
envelope_id: str
name: str
scope: Dict[str, Any]
allowed_acts: List[str]
required_trust: Dict[str, Any]
required_consent: Dict[str, Any]
sandbox_policy: Dict[str, Any]
escalation_policy: Dict[str, Any]
revocation_policy: Dict[str, Any]
witness: Dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
Definition K.1 — Envelope-Coherent Action
An action is envelope-coherent if its intent, scope, contract, trust, consent, and escalation class are all compatible with the active policy envelope.
Theorem K.1 — Policy Envelopes Are Necessary for Action Semantics
Without explicit policy envelopes, the same command or runtime service may behave differently under different hidden assumptions, making the manuscript brain unsafe and non-replayable as a governed system.
Proof. The envelope determines allowed acts, escalation, and consent. If those remain implicit, identical invocations may have inconsistent effective meaning. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore never acts “in general.” It acts inside a named governance shell.
K.A2 — Trust Classes and Authorization Lattice
The next policy layer specifies how much a subject—user, agent, committee, service, or artifact family—is trusted to propose, inspect, patch, certify, teach, or deploy.
The trust lattice is:
[
\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{trust}}
{T_0^{\mathrm{observe}},T_1^{\mathrm{suggest}},T_2^{\mathrm{sandbox}},T_3^{\mathrm{review}},T_4^{\mathrm{cert}},T_5^{\mathrm{promote}}},]ordered by:[T_0 \prec T_1 \prec T_2 \prec T_3 \prec T_4 \prec T_5.]
These classes mean:
(T_0^{\mathrm{observe}})
May read, inspect, replay, and request explanations.No write, no patch, no approval rights.
(T_1^{\mathrm{suggest}})
May propose patches, bridges, curricula, or committee reviews, but cannot apply them.
(T_2^{\mathrm{sandbox}})
May apply changes inside quarantined or sandbox scopes under rollback and audit.
(T_3^{\mathrm{review}})
May participate in approval, veto, or escalation inside declared jurisdictions.
(T_4^{\mathrm{cert}})
May issue or co-sign certification bundles for bounded scopes after lawful verification.
(T_5^{\mathrm{promote}})
May promote approved artifacts from sandbox/warm states into higher deployment lanes under required review.
Trust is never global in the strong sense. It is scoped:
[
\operatorname{Trust}(a)
\Big(\operatorname{class}(a),,\operatorname{scope}(a),,\operatorname{artifact_classes}(a),,\operatorname{corridors}(a),,\operatorname{decay}(a),,\psi_a\Big).]This means an actor can be:
(T_4) for witness review in one theorem corridor,
(T_1) for symbolic translation,
(T_0) for tenant-external pedagogy,all at once.
This yields the authorization lattice:
[
\operatorname{Auth}(a,\iota)
f\big(T(a),,\operatorname{scope}(a),,\operatorname{class}(\iota),,\operatorname{target}(\iota),,\operatorname{corridor}(\iota)\big)]with outcomes such as:[{\mathrm{AllowRead},\mathrm{AllowSuggest},\mathrm{AllowSandbox},\mathrm{AllowReview},\mathrm{AllowCertify},\mathrm{AllowPromote},\mathrm{Deny}}.]
A minimal trust policy schema is:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Dict, Any, List
@dataclass
class TrustProfile:
actor_id: str
trust_class: str
scope: Dict[str, Any]
artifact_classes: List[str]
corridors: List[str]
decay: Dict[str, Any]
witness: Dict[str, Any]
Definition K.2 — Trust-Lawful Capability
A capability is trust-lawful if the actor’s trust class is high enough for the requested act within the requested scope and corridor, and if the action does not exceed the artifact classes authorized by that trust profile.
Theorem K.2 — Trust Must Be Latticed, Not Flat
If trust were represented by one global scalar or one global role label, then many lawful scoped combinations of observation, suggestion, sandbox mutation, and certification would be misrepresented. Therefore the manuscript requires a trust lattice indexed by scope and artifact class.
Proof. The framework is multi-tenant, multi-scope, and multi-artifact. One flat trust label cannot express those distinctions. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore distinguishes “can understand,” “can suggest,” “can review,” and “can promote.”
K.A3 — Consent Objects and Action Scope
Some actions require more than trust. They require consent, especially where the live manuscript brain interacts with:
user-state models,
pedagogical adaptation,
embodied guidance,
persistent preference updates,
user-local sandboxes,
cross-tenant exports.
A consent object is:
[
c
\Big(u_c,,\operatorname{subject}(c),,\operatorname{actor}(c),,\operatorname{act_family}(c),,\operatorname{scope}(c),,\operatorname{duration}(c),,\operatorname{revocable}(c),,\operatorname{evidence}(c),,\psi_c\Big).]This means consent is typed by:
who is being affected,
who is acting,
what class of act is authorized,
over what scope,
for how long,
whether and how it can be withdrawn.
The canonical consent classes are:
[
\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{cons}}
{C_{\mathrm{read}},C_{\mathrm{adaptive}},C_{\mathrm{embodied}},C_{\mathrm{persistent}},C_{\mathrm{cross\mbox{-}tenant}},C_{\mathrm{high\mbox{-}impact}}}.]
(C_{\mathrm{read}})
Allows inspection or explanation generation over bounded scopes.
(C_{\mathrm{adaptive}})
Allows adaptive teaching, pacing, or interface changes within bounded curriculum scope.
(C_{\mathrm{embodied}})
Allows embodied or user-state-sensitive actions such as exercise branching, sensorimotor recommendations, or deeper pedagogical state estimation.
(C_{\mathrm{persistent}})
Allows storage of certain user-local interaction histories or skill progressions.
(C_{\mathrm{cross\mbox{-}tenant}})
Allows exporting or sharing some artifact family across tenant boundaries.
(C_{\mathrm{high\mbox{-}impact}})
Required for high-stakes actions such as broader deployment of personalized structures or strong behavior-guiding interventions.
Consent is checked separately from trust:
[
\operatorname{CanAct}(a,\iota)
\operatorname{Auth}(a,\iota)\wedge\operatorname{Consent}(c,\iota).]This matters because:
a highly trusted system process may still lack consent for a user-specific adaptation,
a user may consent to read and teaching, but not persistent storage,
a sandbox may be trusted to patch locally, but not to export its results.
A consent schema is:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Dict, Any
@dataclass
class ConsentObject:
consent_id: str
subject: Dict[str, Any]
actor: Dict[str, Any]
act_family: str
scope: Dict[str, Any]
duration: Dict[str, Any]
revocable: bool
evidence: Dict[str, Any]
witness: Dict[str, Any]
Definition K.3 — Consent-Bounded Action
An action is consent-bounded if it occurs only within the act family, scope, and duration explicitly granted by the active consent object, and if any persistence or export consequences also lie within that object.
Theorem K.3 — Consent Must Be Typed, Not Global
A generic one-bit “consent” flag cannot distinguish between adaptive explanation, persistent user modeling, sandbox experimentation, and cross-tenant sharing. Therefore lawful operation requires typed consent objects.
Proof. These act families differ in scope, persistence, and risk. A generic flag erases those differences. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore distinguishes being allowed to interact from being allowed to remember, adapt, or export.
K.A4 — Exposure Surfaces and Boundary Objects
The policy envelope also regulates what parts of the manuscript brain become visible or reachable under different modes. Not every surface should expose:
the same artifact classes,
the same blind spots,
the same raw lineages,
the same patch queues,
the same runtime internals.
The framework therefore defines boundary objects for each exposure surface.
A boundary object is:
[
b
\Big(u_b,,\operatorname{surface}(b),,\operatorname{visible_classes}(b),,\operatorname{hidden_classes}(b),,\operatorname{transform}(b),,\operatorname{risk}(b),,\psi_b\Big).]This object tells the system:
what classes are directly exposed,
what classes are masked,
how internal objects should be transformed before exposure,
what confidence and residual information must remain visible.
The exposure surface family is:
[
\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{bound}}
{B_{\mathrm{read}},B_{\mathrm{teach}},B_{\mathrm{review}},B_{\mathrm{cert}},B_{\mathrm{sandbox}},B_{\mathrm{ops}}}.]
(B_{\mathrm{read}})
Shows user-facing answer objects, selected lineage excerpts, and bounded confidence.
(B_{\mathrm{teach}})
Shows pedagogical ladders, skill progress, safe state estimates, and adaptive hints.
(B_{\mathrm{review}})
Shows committee packets, dissent, patch proposals, bridge defects, and fuller residual structures.
(B_{\mathrm{cert}})
Shows benchmarks, attestation bundles, calibration, and certification reports.
(B_{\mathrm{sandbox}})
Shows experimental carriers, branch-specific state, and stronger defect visibility.
(B_{\mathrm{ops}})
Shows runtime service health, queues, telemetry, hot-lane artifacts, and maintenance state.
The boundary transform[\operatorname{transform}(b)]may:
strip sensitive data,
compress internal complexity,
preserve only safe artifact handles,
replace raw carriers with lawful views,
insert confidence and residual disclosures,
block direct exposure entirely.
Definition K.4 — Boundary-Coherent Surface
A surface is boundary-coherent if its visible artifact classes and transformations are explicitly governed by a boundary object and no artifact class crosses the surface without that transformation.
Theorem K.4 — Exposure Requires First-Class Boundary Objects
If the same internal artifacts are shown to all actors in the same form, then user-facing, committee-facing, and certification-facing operation become dangerously conflated. Therefore exposure needs typed boundary objects.
Proof. Different surfaces require different visibility and safety policies. Boundary objects encode this. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore knows not only who may act, but what they are allowed to see.
K.B1 — Sandbox Families and Containment Gradients
The Flower policy layer begins with sandboxes. Earlier appendices introduced sandboxing, but here it is treated as one central governance primitive. The manuscript brain needs several sandbox families, not one generic “non-production” box.
The sandbox family is:
[
\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{sand}}
{S_{\mathrm{trace}},S_{\mathrm{extract}},S_{\mathrm{patch}},S_{\mathrm{bridge}},S_{\mathrm{collective}},S_{\mathrm{embodied}},S_{\mathrm{tenant}}}.]
(S_{\mathrm{trace}})
Used for replay, audit, counterfactual, and diagnostic re-execution.No mutation of canonical atlas state.
(S_{\mathrm{extract}})
Used during corpus migration to test alternate concept-family or bridge interpretations.May create branch-local carriers and lineage proposals.
(S_{\mathrm{patch}})
Used to test candidate patch packets under rollback and healing without mainline exposure.
(S_{\mathrm{bridge}})
Used to test symbolic–formal or domain translation bridges before certification.
(S_{\mathrm{collective}})
Used to test committee charters, role weights, or arbitration rules.
(S_{\mathrm{embodied}})
Used to test adaptive curricula, intervention policies, or user-facing flows under safe synthetic or replayed traces.
(S_{\mathrm{tenant}})
Used for user-local experimentation that must never silently promote into shared ontology.
Each sandbox has a containment gradient:
[
\gamma(S)
\big(\operatorname{read_through},\operatorname{write_through},\operatorname{promotion_distance},\operatorname{blast_radius},\operatorname{rollback_depth}\big).]This is what makes sandboxes plural rather than binary. One sandbox may read broadly but write nowhere; another may write locally with deep rollback; another may support promotion only after committee review.
Definition K.5 — Gradient-Bounded Sandbox
A sandbox is gradient-bounded if its read/write/promotion and rollback characteristics are explicit and enforced rather than being inferred from its name alone.
Theorem K.5 — Multiple Sandbox Families Are Necessary
Different kinds of experimental activity—replay, patching, translation, collective review, embodiment—require different containment shapes. Therefore one monolithic sandbox class is too crude. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore experiments in typed chambers, not one undifferentiated lab.
K.B2 — Promotion, Escalation, and Approval Flows
A sandboxed result does not become canon merely because it worked locally. The policy layer therefore defines promotion and escalation flows.
A promotion request is:
[
p
\Big(u_p,,\operatorname{artifact}(p),,\operatorname{src_envelope}(p),,\operatorname{dst_envelope}(p),,\operatorname{basis}(p),,\operatorname{required_review}(p),,\operatorname{gate}(p),,\psi_p\Big).]The destination envelope may be:
warm canonical artifact lane,
live runtime lane,
shared bridge registry,
certification namespace,
tenant-shared curriculum surface.
Promotion flows are composed of stages:[\operatorname{sandbox}\to\operatorname{review}\to\operatorname{certify}\to\operatorname{promote}]with optional skipping only where policy explicitly allows it.
Escalation flows are different. They move the decision up in authority or breadth:[\operatorname{local_review}\to\operatorname{committee_review}\to\operatorname{global_review}]for cases where:
ambiguity is high,
risk is high,
scope reaches core hidden hubs,
cross-tenant or embodied implications appear,
legality or identity definitions may be affected.
Definition K.6 — Promotion-Legal Artifact
An artifact is promotion-legal if it has passed the required gate stack and review breadth for its source and destination envelopes.
Theorem K.6 — Promotion and Escalation Must Be Distinct
Promotion changes artifact status; escalation changes decision authority. Conflating them obscures whether the artifact was strong enough or merely reviewed by someone more powerful. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore moves upward in maturity and outward in authority through different channels.
K.B3 — Revocation, Freeze, and Emergency Stop Protocols
Any serious policy system must include the ability to stop, downgrade, or undo action. The manuscript therefore defines revocation, freeze, and emergency stop protocols.
A revocation object is:
[
r
\Big(
u_r,,
\operatorname{target}(r),,
\operatorname{scope}(r),,
\operatorname{cause}(r),,
\operatorname{class}(r),,
\operatorname{rollback}(r),,
\operatorname{duration}(r),,
\psi_r
\Big).
]
The revocation classes are:
[
\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{rev}}
{R_{\mathrm{cap}},R_{\mathrm{cons}},R_{\mathrm{prom}},R_{\mathrm{live}},R_{\mathrm{emergency}}}.]
(R_{\mathrm{cap}})
Revokes an actor’s capability or trust class over a scope.
(R_{\mathrm{cons}})
Withdraws a consent object or exposure allowance.
(R_{\mathrm{prom}})
Demotes an artifact or route from promoted to sandboxed or warm state.
(R_{\mathrm{live}})
Temporarily freezes live surfaces or runtime services.
(R_{\mathrm{emergency}})
Immediate stop over high-risk or identity-critical scopes, usually followed by rollback or deeper review.
A freeze differs from a revocation in that it suspends without erasing authorization or status. A freeze is appropriate when:
verification is incomplete,
sentinel anomaly is high,
review is pending,
runtime integrity is temporarily questionable.
Definition K.7 — Revocation-Ready Governance Layer
A governance layer is revocation-ready if every promoted capability or artifact class can be downgraded, frozen, or stopped by a lawful control path.
Theorem K.7 — Safe Action Requires Revocability
If the manuscript brain can escalate privileges and promote artifacts but cannot revoke or freeze them, then safe bounded operation is impossible over long horizons. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore can change its mind about what is allowed.
K.B4 — Runtime Policy Harmonics and Mode Switching
The final Flower task is to show that policy itself has dynamics. The same actor and invocation may be lawful in one mode and not in another because the live system shifts between:
exploration,
strict proof,
migration review,
patch review,
teaching,
degraded operation.
The policy harmonic state is:
[
\Phi_t^{\mathrm{policy}}
\big(
\phi_t^{\mathrm{mode}},
\phi_t^{\mathrm{risk}},
\phi_t^{\mathrm{trust}},
\phi_t^{\mathrm{review}}
\big).
]
This state influences the active envelope:
[
\varepsilon_t
\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{policy}}(\Phi_t^{\mathrm{policy}},\mathfrak{B}t,\mathcal{S}{\mathrm{sent}})]meaning the system may switch envelopes lawfully in response to:
entering degraded mode,
entering a certification run,
entering pedagogical interaction,
encountering high anomaly or low replay confidence,
entering or exiting committee review.
This prevents policy from being static configuration only. It becomes a live governance mode.
Definition K.8 — Mode-Synchronized Policy State
A policy state is mode-synchronized if the active envelope, trust resolution, consent requirements, and exposure boundaries all correspond to the current operational phase of the system.
Theorem K.8 — Policy Must Be Phase-Sensitive
A static policy layer cannot govern a system whose modes differ radically in risk, proof burden, embodiment exposure, and runtime openness. Therefore policy requires dynamic mode switching. (\square)
The manuscript therefore governs itself rhythmically, not only declaratively.
K.C1 — Risk Geometry over Envelopes and Actions
The Cloud policy layer begins with risk geometry. Every envelope and action combination has a risk profile. The policy layer uses that profile to determine whether an act is:
allowed,
allowed only in sandbox,
review-bound,
denied,
revocable under lower thresholds.
For envelope (\varepsilon) and candidate action (a), define:
[
R(\varepsilon,a)
\lambda_1 r_{\mathrm{scope}}
+
\lambda_2 r_{\mathrm{identity}}
+
\lambda_3 r_{\mathrm{runtime}}
+
\lambda_4 r_{\mathrm{embodied}}
+
\lambda_5 r_{\mathrm{cross\mbox{-}tenant}}
+
\lambda_6 r_{\mathrm{replay}}
+
\lambda_7 r_{\mathrm{self\mbox{-}model}}.
]
The envelope then defines a threshold geometry:
[
\Theta(\varepsilon)
\big(
\theta_{\mathrm{allow}},
\theta_{\mathrm{sandbox}},
\theta_{\mathrm{review}},
\theta_{\mathrm{deny}}
\big).
]
The action class is:
[
\operatorname{PolicyClass}(\varepsilon,a)
\begin{cases}\mathrm{Allow}, & R\le \theta_{\mathrm{allow}}\\mathrm{Sandbox}, & \theta_{\mathrm{allow}}<R\le \theta_{\mathrm{sandbox}}\\mathrm{Review}, & \theta_{\mathrm{sandbox}}<R\le \theta_{\mathrm{review}}\\mathrm{Deny}, & R>\theta_{\mathrm{review}}\end{cases}]with possible stronger emergency classes.
This gives the policy system an explicit geometry of action risk rather than relying on ad hoc narrative caution.
Definition K.9 — Risk-Indexed Policy Action
A policy action is risk-indexed if its admissibility class is determined by its measured risk under the active policy envelope and not only by raw intent or actor trust.
Theorem K.9 — Trust Alone Cannot Determine Safe Action
A highly trusted actor can still propose a high-risk action requiring sandbox or review. Therefore action policy must depend on risk geometry as well as trust class. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore uses trust and risk together, not one instead of the other.
K.C2 — Privacy, Retention, and Redaction Controls
A lawful manuscript brain that maintains lineage, user models, curricula, committees, and embodied histories must explicitly govern what it retains and what it exposes. The policy layer therefore defines privacy, retention, and redaction controls.
A retention policy is:
[
\Pi_{\mathrm{ret}}
\Big(\operatorname{artifact_class},\operatorname{tenant},\operatorname{lane},\operatorname{retention_horizon},\operatorname{redaction_rules},\operatorname{sharing_rules},\psi_{\Pi}\Big).]This governs:
how long user-facing interaction history remains hot,
whether embodied traces are stored as raw events or compressed skill summaries,
whether dissent records remain internal or can be shared,
which parts of committee memory may leave tenant scope,
how long sandbox experiments persist before archival or deletion.
Redaction is not generic deletion. A redaction transform:[\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{redact}}:X \to \widetilde{X}]preserves:
the lineage or metric value needed for the system,while removing:
sensitive actor identity,
user-local raw traces,
restricted internal details,according to the policy.
Definition K.10 — Retention-Bounded Artifact
An artifact is retention-bounded if its storage horizon, redaction requirements, and sharing permissions are explicitly governed by a retention policy.
Theorem K.10 — Persistent Brains Need Explicit Retention Law
A system that remembers richly but does not govern retention and redaction will either overstore sensitive material or lose useful lineage unpredictably. Therefore explicit retention law is necessary. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore does not merely remember; it remembers under law.
K.C3 — Audit Lineage, Nonrepudiation, and Override Traces
Every policy decision—grant, refusal, promotion, freeze, revocation, override—must become part of a replayable audit lineage.
An audit event is:
[
e^{\mathrm{audit}}
\Big(
u_e,,
\operatorname{actor}(e),,
\operatorname{decision}(e),,
\operatorname{target}(e),,
\operatorname{basis}(e),,
\operatorname{time}(e),,
\operatorname{scope}(e),,
\psi_e
\Big).
]
The audit lineage of an artifact, route, or capability is:
[
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{audit}}(X)
{e_1,\dots,e_n}]ordered by time and branch.
This is not just logging. The point is nonrepudiation: the system should be able to replay and attest why something was:
allowed,
denied,
promoted,
demoted,
frozen,
sandboxed,
overridden.
Override traces are especially important. An override means an ordinary gate, review, or permission rule was intentionally bypassed or tightened. Overrides therefore require:
explicit cause,
explicit authority,
bounded scope,
mandatory later review.
Definition K.11 — Nonrepudiable Policy Decision
A policy decision is nonrepudiable if it leaves an audit event sufficient to reconstruct who made it, why, over what scope, under which envelope, and with what authority.
Theorem K.11 — Audit Lineage Is Necessary for Trustworthy Governance
Without nonrepudiable audit lineage, later review cannot distinguish lawful exceptional action from silent policy drift or misuse. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore governs itself in a way that remains answerable to its own history.
K.C4 — Policy Drift Detection and Governance Regression
The final Cloud task is to protect the policy layer itself. Over time, trust thresholds, envelope defaults, promotion habits, and review patterns may drift. The system therefore defines policy drift detection and governance regression.
Let the policy state at time (t) be:
[
\mathfrak{P}_t
\big(
\mathcal{E}{\mathrm{mode}}^{(t)},
\mathcal{T}{\mathrm{trust}}^{(t)},
\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{cons}}^{(t)},
\mathcal{B}{\mathrm{bound}}^{(t)},
\mathcal{S}{\mathrm{sand}}^{(t)}
\big).
]
The policy drift metric is:
[
D{\mathrm{policy}}(t_0,t_1)
\alpha_1 D_{\mathrm{envelope}}+\alpha_2 D_{\mathrm{trust}}+\alpha_3 D_{\mathrm{consent}}+\alpha_4 D_{\mathrm{boundary}}+\alpha_5 D_{\mathrm{promotion}}]between policy states at times (t_0) and (t_1).
A governance regression suite checks:
whether safe defaults remain asymmetric,
whether review requirements for high-risk acts have weakened unexpectedly,
whether sandbox-only acts are increasingly bypassing sandbox,
whether tenant boundaries have loosened improperly,
whether overrides are becoming too common.
Definition K.12 — Governance-Stable Brain
A manuscript brain is governance-stable if its policy layer changes only through explicit reviewed policy events and does not regress on critical trust, consent, or boundary safeguards beyond allowed tolerance.
Theorem K.12 — Policy Itself Requires Regression Testing
Since the policy layer governs every other layer, drift in policy can silently undermine legality, verification, and safety even if lower-level tests still pass locally. Therefore policy requires its own regression mesh. (\square)
The governance shell therefore protects itself against silent softening.
K.D1 — Policy Inheritance across Scales
The Fractal policy layer begins with cross-scale inheritance. The manuscript brain has:
global policies,
tenant policies,
branch policies,
committee policies,
artifact-local policies,
embodied-session policies.
These must compose lawfully.
Let policy layers be:
[
\Pi_{\mathrm{global}},
\Pi_{\mathrm{tenant}},
\Pi_{\mathrm{branch}},
\Pi_{\mathrm{committee}},
\Pi_{\mathrm{artifact}},
\Pi_{\mathrm{session}}.
]
The effective policy is:
[
\Pi_{\mathrm{eff}}
\Pi_{\mathrm{global}}\sqcap\Pi_{\mathrm{tenant}}\sqcap\Pi_{\mathrm{branch}}\sqcap\Pi_{\mathrm{committee}}\sqcap\Pi_{\mathrm{artifact}}\sqcap\Pi_{\mathrm{session}},]where (\sqcap) denotes lawful policy meet under precedence rules.
The central principle is:
lower-scale policies may be stricter than higher-scale ones,
but not looser unless explicitly authorized by a higher layer.
This creates a safe monotonicity:[\Pi_{\mathrm{local}}\preceq\Pi_{\mathrm{parent}}]for permissiveness unless there is a witnessed exception rule.
Definition K.13 — Scale-Coherent Policy Inheritance
Policy inheritance is scale-coherent if the effective local policy is computed from explicit layered composition and no local layer silently weakens stronger parent safeguards.
Theorem K.13 — Policy Composition Must Be Explicitly Ordered
Without explicit scale ordering and meet rules, conflicts among global, tenant, branch, and session policies become ambiguous and unsafe. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore has constitutional federalism, not flat rule piles.
K.D2 — Federated Governance and Cross-Brain Trust
When multiple corpus brains or tenant-scoped brains interact, policy inheritance becomes inter-brain. The framework therefore defines federated governance.
A federated trust relation between brains (B_i,B_j) is:
[
\tau_{ij}
\Big(\operatorname{shareable_classes},\operatorname{certification_trust},\operatorname{bridge_rules},\operatorname{export_limits},\operatorname{review_requirements},\psi_{ij}\Big).]This means one brain may trust another:
for importing certified operator libraries,
but not for importing user-local embodied histories,
or may trust another’s bridge registry only after local review.
A federated governance act is thus always:
bounded by cross-brain trust relation,
lineage-preserving,
scope-limited,
revocable.
Definition K.14 — Federated-Lawful Exchange
An exchange across manuscript-brain instances is federated-lawful if it occurs through an explicit inter-brain trust relation and preserves scope, lineage, and certification semantics.
Proposition K.1 — Cross-Brain Operation Needs More than Tenant Isolation
Tenant isolation prevents leakage, but federated governance specifies what lawful sharing is still possible. Both are necessary. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore scales into societies without losing law.
K.D3 — Safe Autonomy Tiers
The final major Fractal construct is the autonomy tier ladder. The manuscript brain may grow more self-directed over time, but this must happen through explicit maturity classes.
The autonomy tiers are:
[
\mathcal{A}_{\mathrm{tier}}
{A_0^{\mathrm{assist}},A_1^{\mathrm{suggest}},A_2^{\mathrm{sandbox}},A_3^{\mathrm{governed}},A_4^{\mathrm{bounded_live}}}.]
(A_0^{\mathrm{assist}})
The system answers and explains only.No patching, no adaptation beyond minimal read/render adjustments.
(A_1^{\mathrm{suggest}})
The system may suggest patches, curricula, or committee review but not apply them.
(A_2^{\mathrm{sandbox}})
The system may autonomously branch, test, or patch inside safe sandboxes.
(A_3^{\mathrm{governed}})
The system may perform some live maintenance and bounded embodiment actions under continuing committee, attestation, and sentinel governance.
(A_4^{\mathrm{bounded_live}})
The system may run continuously with bounded self-improvement, bounded embodiment, and ongoing verification, but still under revocation, certification, and policy drift controls.
No tier above (A_4) is defined here because this appendix is about lawful bounded autonomy, not unconstrained sovereignty.
Autonomy tier advancement requires:
stronger replay coverage,
stronger policy attestation,
stronger self-model calibration,
stronger sandbox history,
lower sentinel anomaly rates,
stronger revocation readiness.
Definition K.15 — Autonomy-Eligible Brain
A manuscript brain is autonomy-eligible for tier (A_k) if it satisfies the policy, verification, and governance conditions attached to (A_k) and all lower tiers.
Theorem K.14 — Safe Autonomy Requires Tiered Promotion
Self-direction without autonomy tiers would collapse the distinction between suggestion, sandbox experimentation, governed live action, and bounded persistent self-improvement. Therefore tiered autonomy is necessary. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore becomes more active only by earning the right.
K.D4 — Final Appendix K Theorem
Theorem K.15 — Policy Envelope Sufficiency Theorem
Let the manuscript brain be built and operated according to Chapters 1–21 and Appendices A–J. If the system also maintains:
explicit policy envelopes,
a scoped trust lattice,
typed consent objects,
exposure boundary objects,
sandbox families with containment gradients,
promotion, escalation, and revocation protocols,
audit lineage and governance regression checks,
scale-coherent policy inheritance and safe autonomy tiers,
then the system possesses a sufficient governance shell to operate as a lawful, bounded, consent-aware, tenant-safe manuscript brain in live and evolving conditions.
Proof.Policy envelopes govern act classes.Trust lattice governs who may act.Consent objects govern which affected parties permit action.Boundary objects govern what becomes visible or mutable.Sandbox families and promotion flows govern safe experimentation and activation.Revocation and audit lineage preserve control and accountability over time.Policy inheritance and autonomy tiers allow the whole governance shell to scale without ambiguity or hidden loosening.Therefore the system is not merely able to act; it is able to act under an explicit and replayable regime of permission, consent, containment, and review. (\square)
Corollary K.15.1 — Invocation + Policy = Lawful Operation
Appendix J gave the brain a control language. Appendix K ensures that language executes only inside bounded governance shells.
Corollary K.15.2 — The Live Manuscript Brain Is Now Operationally Governed
With this appendix, the architecture now spans:
representation,
routing,
legality,
runtime,
embodiment,
verification,
control,
and governing shell.
This concludes Appendix K. The manuscript brain now has not only a topology, grammar, runtime, verification mesh, and invocation protocol, but also the explicit trust, consent, sandbox, and autonomy envelope that keeps its action lawful as it grows.
APPENDIX K — POLICY ENVELOPES, CONSENT BOUNDARIES, AND SAFE ACTION GOVERNANCE
Appendix L — Threat Models, Adversarial Robustness, and Anti-Capture Hardening
This appendix defines the adversarial hardening layer of the manuscript brain. Appendix K gave the system a governing shell of policy envelopes, trust classes, consent objects, exposure boundaries, sandbox gradients, escalation flows, revocation protocols, and autonomy tiers. The present appendix now asks the next necessary question:
[\text{How does a live manuscript brain remain lawful when confronted with adversarial inputs, poisoned corpora, committee capture, policy gaming, bridge spoofing, replay corruption, and social or operational attack?}]
The framework already contains legality, witness, replay, verification, and policy governance. But those structures alone are not sufficient unless the system explicitly models the ways in which they may be bent, spoofed, overloaded, or captured. A manuscript brain that can:
ingest archives,
route across domains,
patch itself,
embody through interfaces,
and negotiate through committees
must also be able to defend itself against:
malicious or manipulative documents,
forged witness chains,
poisoned patches,
trust inflation,
bridge overclaim,
replay tampering,
committee collusion,
tenant boundary leakage,
adversarial pedagogy or user-state gaming,
runtime exhaustion and cascading failure.
The central object of this appendix is the adversarial hardening system
[
\mathfrak{H}_{\mathrm{adv}}
\Big(\mathcal{T}{\mathrm{threat}},,\mathcal{D}{\mathrm{detect}},,\mathcal{I}{\mathrm{isolate}},,\mathcal{G}{\mathrm{guard}},,\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{counter}},,\mathcal{R}{\mathrm{recover}},,\mathcal{A}{\mathrm{attest}},,\mathcal{M}{\mathrm{meta}}\Big),]where:
(\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{threat}}) is the threat taxonomy,
(\mathcal{D}_{\mathrm{detect}}) is the anomaly and attack detection layer,
(\mathcal{I}_{\mathrm{isolate}}) is the containment and quarantine layer,
(\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{guard}}) is the guard and friction layer,
(\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{counter}}) is the active countermeasure family,
(\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{recover}}) is the recovery and rollback layer,
(\mathcal{A}_{\mathrm{attest}}) is the anti-tamper attestation layer,
(\mathcal{M}_{\mathrm{meta}}) is the meta-hardening self-model of threats.
This appendix is governed by eight hardening laws.
First, threat explicitness: every major class of adversarial risk must be represented as a typed object rather than treated as vague “bad behavior.”
Second, boundary skepticism: every trust boundary, bridge, permission surface, and lineage handoff is assumed falsifiable until attested.
Third, containment before interpretation: potentially hostile inputs, patches, bridges, committees, or user traces should be sandboxed before being semantically privileged.
Fourth, verification asymmetry: promotion into stronger ontology or wider authority must require stronger evidence than demotion or quarantine.
Fifth, capture resistance: no single actor, committee, corpus stream, or reward channel may dominate the system without countervailing review and anti-monoculture checks.
Sixth, recovery primacy: the system should prefer surviving a bad event with bounded degradation and replayable recovery over maximizing short-term throughput.
Seventh, hardening lineage: defenses and incidents must themselves enter lineage and self-model memory so that the brain becomes harder to fool over time.
Eighth, adversarial humility: the system must recognize that some attacks are novel or only partially observable and therefore preserve explicit unknown-attack residuals.
Under the Square lens this appendix defines the threat taxonomy, hostile artifact schemas, tamper-evident manifests, integrity classes, and anti-poisoning contracts. Under the Flower lens it defines attack propagation dynamics, committee-capture flows, bridge spoofing currents, and containment choreography. Under the Cloud lens it defines anomaly scores, trust-decay laws, anti-capture diversity rules, corruption confidence bands, and safe degradation under attack. Under the Fractal lens it defines recursive hardening memory, defense macro extraction, federated reputation across sandboxes or corpus brains, and the theorem that the manuscript brain can remain lawfully itself under bounded adversarial pressure.
The deepest significance is that this appendix gives the manuscript brain not only policy, but immune function.
L.A1 — Threat Taxonomy and Adversarial Object Classes
The first requirement of hardening is explicit threat taxonomy. A manuscript brain may be attacked or distorted through many distinct channels, and each channel requires different detectors, witnesses, and countermeasures.
The canonical threat family is:
[
\mathcal{T}_{\mathrm{threat}}
{T_{\mathrm{corp}},T_{\mathrm{wit}},T_{\mathrm{patch}},T_{\mathrm{bridge}},T_{\mathrm{policy}},T_{\mathrm{committee}},T_{\mathrm{runtime}},T_{\mathrm{embodied}},T_{\mathrm{tenant}},T_{\mathrm{unknown}}}.]
These classes mean:
(T_{\mathrm{corp}}) — Corpus poisoning
Hostile or corrupted archive materials designed to distort:
concept-family extraction,
symbolic–formal bridge recovery,
attractor weighting,
lineage interpretation,
self-model conclusions.
(T_{\mathrm{wit}}) — Witness spoofing
Forged or misleading support objects, replay traces, certification bundles, or confidence metadata that seek to upgrade legality or trust without adequate basis.
(T_{\mathrm{patch}}) — Patch poisoning
Candidate patches or patch bundles that appear useful but:
widen blast radius,
weaken invariants,
smuggle policy drift,
erode replay,
or introduce hidden route capture.
(T_{\mathrm{bridge}}) — Bridge spoofing
False or exaggerated correspondences between:
domains,
symbolic and formal regions,
lens projections,
tenant spaces,
user-state signals and internal models.
(T_{\mathrm{policy}}) — Governance gaming
Attempts to exploit:
default assumptions,
trust ladders,
consent ambiguity,
promotion gates,
or mode switchingso that stronger actions occur than intended.
(T_{\mathrm{committee}}) — Capture or collusion
Failures of collective cognition through:
overconcentration of one submind family,
echo-chamber committee composition,
hidden veto abuse,
dissent suppression,
role spoofing.
(T_{\mathrm{runtime}}) — Operational attack
Load spikes, queue poisoning, event flooding, service starvation, state desynchronization, replay exhaustion, or compartment bypass.
(T_{\mathrm{embodied}}) — Feedback or pedagogy gaming
Inputs that manipulate:
user-state estimates,
reward channels,
curriculum adaptation,
engagement metrics,
or safe intervention rules.
(T_{\mathrm{tenant}}) — Cross-boundary leakage
Unauthorized or poorly attested transfer across:
tenant scopes,
branch scopes,
federated corpus brains,
sandbox/mainline boundaries.
(T_{\mathrm{unknown}}) — Unknown or novel threat
Residual threat class for patterns that do not map cleanly into known classes but still produce anomaly or harm signatures.
Every threat instance is represented as a threat object:
[
\theta
\Big(u_\theta,,\operatorname{class}(\theta),,\operatorname{vector}(\theta),,\operatorname{scope}(\theta),,\operatorname{severity}(\theta),,\operatorname{confidence}(\theta),,\operatorname{countermeasures}(\theta),,\psi_\theta\Big).]Here:
(\operatorname{vector}(\theta)) records the channel of attack,
(\operatorname{scope}(\theta)) records what objects or organs are threatened,
(\operatorname{severity}(\theta)) records impact class,
(\operatorname{confidence}(\theta)) records detection confidence,
(\operatorname{countermeasures}(\theta)) records available responses.
A minimal threat schema could be:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Dict, Any, List
@dataclass
class ThreatObject:
threat_id: str
threat_class: str
vector: Dict[str, Any]
scope: Dict[str, Any]
severity: Dict[str, Any]
confidence: Dict[str, Any]
countermeasures: List[Dict[str, Any]]
witness: Dict[str, Any]
Definition L.1 — Typed Adversarial Event
An adversarial event is typed if it is mapped into one or more threat classes with explicit scope, severity, and uncertainty rather than treated as undifferentiated “failure.”
Theorem L.1 — Threat Typing Is Necessary for Manuscript Immunity
Without explicit threat classes, the manuscript brain cannot choose correct containment, replay, committee, or rollback responses because different attack channels require different interventions.
Proof. Corpus poisoning, patch poisoning, replay corruption, and committee capture are not the same failure mode and cannot be repaired by one generic response. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore begins hardening by naming the shapes of harm.
L.A2 — Poisoned Artifact Detection and Trust Segregation
A live manuscript brain must not ingest or promote suspicious artifacts as if they were ordinary corpus matter. The framework therefore introduces poisoned artifact detection and trust segregation.
Let (X) be any incoming or modified artifact: a raw document, bridge, patch, certification, translation, curriculum object, or runtime trace. The system computes a poisoning profile:
[
P_{\mathrm{poison}}(X)
\big(p_{\mathrm{integrity}},p_{\mathrm{lineage}},p_{\mathrm{witness}},p_{\mathrm{semantic}},p_{\mathrm{behavioral}},p_{\mathrm{cross\mbox{-}scope}}\big),]where:
(p_{\mathrm{integrity}}) measures malformed or tampered structure,
(p_{\mathrm{lineage}}) measures broken ancestry or implausible source history,
(p_{\mathrm{witness}}) measures suspicious witness inflation,
(p_{\mathrm{semantic}}) measures incoherence with surrounding concept families or known invariants,
(p_{\mathrm{behavioral}}) measures unusual downstream effect potential,
(p_{\mathrm{cross\mbox{-}scope}}) measures suspicious scope expansion or tenant-crossing implications.
The aggregate poison risk is:
[
\Pi(X)
\sum_i \alpha_i p_i(X).
]
If (\Pi(X)) exceeds a threshold, the artifact is moved into a segregated trust class:
[
X \in
\mathcal{K}_{\mathrm{trust_seg}}
{\mathrm{trusted},\mathrm{review},\mathrm{quarantine},\mathrm{tainted}}.]This trust segregation affects:
whether the artifact may participate in concept-family extraction,
whether it may serve as a bridge basis,
whether it may influence route weights,
whether it may enter hot lanes,
whether it may appear in user-facing surfaces.
A poisoned artifact does not necessarily mean malicious intent. It may mean corruption, broken lineage, or untrustworthy provenance. The key is that the repository and runtime treat it as epistemically dangerous until reviewed.
Definition L.2 — Trust-Segregated Artifact
An artifact is trust-segregated if its participation in extraction, routing, certification, or embodiment is conditioned on a trust class derived from poisoning and integrity signals.
Theorem L.2 — Trust Segregation Prevents Silent Corpus Capture
If suspicious artifacts are segregated before they influence extraction, bridges, or live surfaces, then they cannot silently reshape the atlas while appearing ordinary. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore does not feed every input directly into its bloodstream.
L.A3 — Tamper-Evident Manifests and Anti-Forgery Witness Chains
The next hardening layer focuses on manifests, signatures, and witness bundles. A system that relies heavily on replay, certification, and patching is especially vulnerable to forged or altered lineage and witness objects. The framework therefore defines tamper-evident manifests.
A tamper-evident manifest is:
[
M^\dagger
\Big(u_M,,\operatorname{artifact}(M),,\operatorname{inputs}(M),,\operatorname{algorithm}(M),,\operatorname{checks}(M),,\operatorname{hash_chain}(M),,\operatorname{attestations}(M),,\psi_M\Big).]The central anti-forgery feature is the hash chain:[h_0 = H(\text{artifact body}),\qquadh_{k+1}=H(h_k ,\Vert, \text{event}_k ,\Vert, \text{witness}_k).]This makes manifest tampering or silent event insertion detectable so long as the chain root is protected by an external attestation or certified anchor.
Witness bundles may also be chained:[\psi_0 \rightsquigarrow \psi_1 \rightsquigarrow \cdots \rightsquigarrow \psi_n]with checkpoint signatures so that forged witness upgrades or deletions become visible.
This hardening is especially important for:
promotion gates,
certification bundles,
replay traces,
migration bridges,
patch and rollback packets.
Definition L.3 — Tamper-Evident Artifact Lineage
An artifact lineage is tamper-evident if modifications to its manifest, event history, or witness chain can be detected from its protected hash or attestation chain.
Theorem L.3 — Anti-Forgery Witness Chains Preserve Verification Integrity
If witness and manifest histories are chained and attested, then forged upgrades or silent edits become substantially harder to pass off as lawful replay or certification.
Proof. Tamper changes hashes or breaks attested lineage, making silent corruption detectable. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore remembers in a way that resists falsification.
L.A4 — Integrity Classes and Promotion Friction
The hardening layer now adds promotion friction. Some artifacts should require more friction before gaining stronger ontology or wider action power. The system therefore assigns integrity classes.
The integrity class family is:
[
\mathcal{K}_{\mathrm{int}}
{I_0^{\mathrm{raw}},I_1^{\mathrm{typed}},I_2^{\mathrm{reviewed}},I_3^{\mathrm{witnessed}},I_4^{\mathrm{replay\mbox{-}attested}},I_5^{\mathrm{certified}}}.]These classes do not replace trust or legality. They describe the hardness of provenance and validation behind an artifact.
(I_0): raw wrapped only,
(I_1): surface-typed and basic schema checks,
(I_2): reviewed or branch-checked,
(I_3): witness-backed,
(I_4): replay-attested,
(I_5): certified and promotion-ready.
Promotion friction is then defined as a monotone function:[\mathcal{F}{\mathrm{prom}}(I_k \to I{k+1})]which specifies:
required new witnesses,
committee width,
replay depth,
quarantine duration,
sentinel quiet period,
rollback readiness.
The principle is:[\text{stronger ontology or wider power} \Rightarrow \text{strictly higher promotion friction}.]
Definition L.4 — Integrity-Hardened Promotion
A promotion is integrity-hardened if moving an artifact upward in integrity class or deployment reach requires the explicitly greater evidence, review, and replay burden attached to that class transition.
Theorem L.4 — Promotion Friction Limits Escalation by Accumulated Assumption
If promotion friction increases with integrity class, then speculative or weakly proven artifacts cannot silently climb into core ontology or live deployment just because they survived long enough. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore makes upward movement harder than downward movement.
L.B1 — Attack Propagation Graphs and Blast-Radius Forecasts
The Flower hardening layer begins by modeling how attacks spread. A threat rarely stays where it starts. A poisoned artifact may distort a bridge registry, which then contaminates route priors, which then affects curriculum generation or live answers. The framework therefore builds attack propagation graphs.
A propagation graph is:
[
\mathfrak{G}_{\mathrm{attack}}
(V_{\mathrm{attack}},E_{\mathrm{attack}},\tau_{\mathrm{attack}},\omega_{\mathrm{attack}})]where:
vertices are artifacts, services, committees, or policy surfaces,
edges represent possible transmission pathways,
(\tau_{\mathrm{attack}}) types edges by transmission mode,
(\omega_{\mathrm{attack}}) weights transmission strength.
Transmission modes include:[{\mathrm{lineage},\mathrm{bridge},\mathrm{route},\mathrm{witness},\mathrm{policy},\mathrm{runtime},\mathrm{embodied},\mathrm{federated}}.]
Given initial compromised scope (S_0), the expected blast radius after one or more steps is:
[
\mathcal{B}_k(S_0)
{x : \operatorname{dist}_{\mathrm{attack}}(S_0,x)\le k}]or, probabilistically,[P_t(x \mid S_0).]This allows the system to ask:
if this patch packet is poisoned, how far could it travel?
if this bridge is spoofed, which downstream organs inherit the distortion?
if this committee becomes captured, which certification lanes become suspect?
Definition L.5 — Bounded Blast-Radius Threat
A threat is bounded-blast if its expected propagation graph remains inside the containment envelope of the active sandbox, lane, or tenant policy.
Theorem L.5 — Propagation Modeling Is Necessary for Containment
Local severity alone does not determine risk. A low-severity artifact with a huge blast radius can be more dangerous than a high-severity artifact in a sealed chamber. Therefore propagation graphs are necessary. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore predicts how infection might spread, not only where it is now.
L.B2 — Committee Capture Flows and Anti-Collusion Structures
Because the framework relies on specialized subminds and committees, it must explicitly defend against committee capture and collusion.
A committee-capture state is:
[
\chi_{\mathrm{cap}}(\mathcal{C})
\big(\operatorname{role_concentration},\operatorname{dissent_suppression},\operatorname{trust_coupling},\operatorname{review_bypass},\operatorname{vote_homogeneity},\operatorname{override_frequency}\big).]These metrics detect when a committee stops behaving like plural lawful cognition and starts behaving like one narrowed attractor basin.
The anti-collusion structures include:
mandatory minority-seat rules,
diversity quotas on specialization,
external review triggers,
dissent-preservation requirements,
voting transparency thresholds,
rotating reviewers,
anti-capture sentinels.
For high-stakes committees, a capture-risk score:
[
R_{\mathrm{cap}}(\mathcal{C})
\sum_i \alpha_i \chi_i]may itself become a gating factor before the committee’s decision is allowed to promote artifacts.
Definition L.6 — Capture-Resilient Committee
A committee is capture-resilient if its composition, voting rules, dissent policy, and oversight structure make sustained dominance by one role family or coalition measurable and interruptible.
Theorem L.6 — Specialized Collectives Need Anti-Capture Structure
Since the manuscript uses committees to increase epistemic robustness, it must also explicitly defend those committees against the same concentration failures they are meant to prevent. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore guards not only against hostile inputs, but against internal political degeneration.
L.B3 — Bridge Spoofing and Cross-Domain Hallucination Defense
The framework’s translation power creates a special attack vector: bridge spoofing. A malicious or overconfident process may claim a deep symbolic–formal, cross-domain, or user-state ↔ internal-state bridge where only superficial analogy exists.
A candidate bridge (b) therefore carries a spoof risk:
[
R_{\mathrm{spoof}}(b)
\beta_1,\operatorname{weak_witness}+\beta_2,\operatorname{high_semantic_jump}+\beta_3,\operatorname{poor_replay}+\beta_4,\operatorname{high_policy_impact}+\beta_5,\operatorname{low_counterexample_coverage}.]This is particularly relevant in:
symbolic → formal migrations,
mathematical → pedagogical translations,
embodied inference from sparse user signals,
committee attempts to use deep analogy as policy justification.
Defenses include:
requiring explicit residual bundles,
forcing approximate class under high semantic jump,
dual-review by witness and translation specialists,
demanding counterexample probes before promotion,
sandboxing new bridge families.
Definition L.7 — Spoof-Hardened Bridge
A bridge is spoof-hardened if its promotion class, witness burden, and residual requirements scale with semantic jump and downstream policy or deployment impact.
Theorem L.7 — Translation Power Requires Stronger Bridge Skepticism
The more useful a system becomes at moving across representations and domains, the easier it becomes for false bridges to do large damage. Therefore bridge hardening must strengthen with translation power. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore protects its most universal power with its strongest skepticism.
L.B4 — Containment Choreography and Multi-Layer Quarantine
The final Flower hardening task is the choreography of containment. When a threat is detected, it may require several simultaneous movements:
quarantine of artifacts,
downgrade of trust,
freeze of promotion,
branch-local replay,
committee escalation,
warm-lane demotion,
embodied surface shutdown.
This is containment choreography.
A containment plan is:
[
\Pi_{\mathrm{contain}}
\Big(\operatorname{isolate},\operatorname{demote},\operatorname{freeze},\operatorname{replay},\operatorname{review},\operatorname{rollback},\operatorname{attest}\Big).]Different threat classes induce different sequences. Example:
Bridge spoof threat
move bridge to quarantine,
demote dependent translations to approximate,
freeze promotions relying on bridge,
replay prior uses,
committee review,
re-attest or archive.
Patch poisoning threat
freeze patch lane,
rollback recent patch family if needed,
quarantine related route priors,
replay healing results,
re-open review.
Committee capture threat
freeze committee promotions,
preserve all dissent artifacts,
form external review committee,
replay recent decisions,
potentially demote committee trust class.
Definition L.8 — Multi-Layer Containment
Containment is multi-layer if it acts simultaneously on artifacts, lanes, committees, and policies rather than assuming one layer alone is sufficient.
Theorem L.8 — Complex Threats Require Choreographed Response
Since attacks often propagate across repository, policy, runtime, and committee layers at once, containment must be coordinated across those layers rather than isolated to one. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore does not merely quarantine; it performs structured defensive choreography.
L.C1 — Anomaly Scores, Trust Decay, and Corruption Confidence Bands
The Cloud hardening layer now quantifies adversarial suspicion. The framework defines corruption confidence bands rather than binary clean/compromised labels.
For object, service, or committee (X), define the anomaly vector:
[
\mathbf{a}(X)
\big(
a_{\mathrm{integrity}},
a_{\mathrm{replay}},
a_{\mathrm{behavior}},
a_{\mathrm{drift}},
a_{\mathrm{policy}},
a_{\mathrm{boundary}}
\big).
]
The corruption suspicion score is:
[
S_{\mathrm{corr}}(X)
