\sum_i \alpha_i a_i(X).]This score maps into bands:[{B_0^{\mathrm{clean}},B_1^{\mathrm{watch}},B_2^{\mathrm{review}},B_3^{\mathrm{quarantine}},B_4^{\mathrm{compromised}}}.]
Parallel to this, trust decays dynamically:
[
T_{t+1}(X)
\lambda T_t(X)
\mu S_{\mathrm{corr}}(X)+\nu \operatorname{RecoveryEvidence}(X).]This means the framework does not merely catch anomalies. It adjusts trust lawfully based on them and on subsequent recovery.
Definition L.9 — Confidence-Banded Integrity State
An integrity state is confidence-banded if it represents not only anomaly magnitude but also the confidence and band class of suspected corruption or compromise.
Theorem L.9 — Hardening Needs Graded Suspicion
Binary trust/distrust flags are too brittle for a live, evolving manuscript brain. Graded bands allow review, quarantine, and recovery to happen proportionally. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore becomes suspicious gradually and lawfully, not hysterically.
L.C2 — Privacy Breach Detection and Cross-Boundary Leak Prevention
The policy appendix already defined consent and retention, but the hardening appendix must define breach detection.
A cross-boundary leak candidate is:
[
\ell
\Big(u_\ell,,\operatorname{src_tenant},,\operatorname{dst_tenant},,\operatorname{artifact_class},,\operatorname{surface},,\operatorname{evidence},,\operatorname{severity},,\psi_\ell\Big).]Leak detection checks for:
unexpected artifact handles crossing boundaries,
shared caches containing tenant-private payload,
lineage references spanning scopes without bridge contracts,
embodied or user-state summaries surfacing outside allowed views,
committee memory accidentally promoted to public surfaces.
Defenses include:
strict surface redaction,
hot-lane tenant tags,
audit sentinels over replication surfaces,
automatic demotion or quarantine on suspicious export.
Definition L.10 — Leak-Hardened Boundary
A boundary is leak-hardened if export, replication, and rendering events across that boundary are all typed, audited, and checked for policy compatibility.
Theorem L.10 — Tenant Safety Requires Active Leak Detection
Even with good policy design, live systems can drift or misroute artifacts. Therefore leak prevention must be active and continuous, not only declarative. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore actively patrols its own privacy walls.
L.C3 — Hardening Metrics and Security Health Scoreboard
The hardening layer adds its own health vector:
[
H_{\mathrm{hard}}
\big(h_{\mathrm{integrity}},h_{\mathrm{capture}},h_{\mathrm{bridge}},h_{\mathrm{runtime}},h_{\mathrm{tenant}},h_{\mathrm{policy}},h_{\mathrm{recovery}}\big).]These scores correspond to:
artifact integrity,
anti-capture resilience,
bridge spoof resistance,
runtime containment strength,
tenant boundary health,
policy drift resistance,
recovery readiness.
This health vector enters the global verification and self-model surfaces. A live brain with low hardening health may still function well locally, but should:
lower autonomy tier,
tighten promotion gates,
increase probe cadence,
widen review committees,
narrow embodiment surfaces.
Definition L.11 — Hardening-Healthy Brain
A manuscript brain is hardening-healthy if its anti-poisoning, anti-capture, anti-leak, and recovery metrics remain above corridor thresholds.
Theorem L.11 — Functional Performance Is Not Sufficient for Security Health
A system can answer well and route well while becoming easier to poison or capture over time. Therefore hardening health must be tracked independently of ordinary cognitive performance. (\square)
The brain therefore knows whether it is merely clever or also resilient.
L.C4 — Unknown-Attack Residuals and Security Humility
The final Cloud task is to preserve humility about adversarial novelty. Not every future attack is already in the taxonomy. The framework therefore keeps an unknown-attack residual family:
[
\mathcal{U}_{\mathrm{adv}}
{u_1,\dots,u_n}]where each unknown residual captures:
anomaly pattern not fitting known classes,
unexplained metric drift,
repeated suspicious committee or bridge behavior,
new attack vector suggested by counterfactual replay.
An unknown residual object is:
[
u
\Big(u_u,,\operatorname{scope}(u),,\operatorname{signals}(u),,\operatorname{candidate_classes}(u),,\operatorname{containment}(u),,\psi_u\Big).]This allows the system to:
contain first,
classify later,
avoid pretending total threat completeness.
Definition L.12 — Security-Humble System
A system is security-humble if it preserves explicit residuals for suspicious patterns that do not yet fit its current threat taxonomy.
Theorem L.12 — Unknown Threats Require Residual Representation
If the system assumes all threats must fit known classes immediately, then novel attacks will be misclassified or ignored. Residual threat objects preserve caution under novelty. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore remains open-eyed about what it does not yet know how to defend against.
L.D1 — Hardening Memory and Defense Macro Extraction
The Fractal hardening layer begins by turning incidents and defenses into long-term learning. Every detected threat, successful containment, failed gate, committee capture event, or bridge spoof should enter hardening memory.
A hardening memory object is:
[
M_{\mathrm{hard}}
\Big(
u_M,,
\operatorname{incident}(M),,
\operatorname{threat_class}(M),,
\operatorname{path}(M),,
\operatorname{containment}(M),,
\operatorname{recovery}(M),,
\operatorname{lessons}(M),,
\psi_M
\Big).
]
Repeated incidents then become defense macros:
[
\mathfrak{m}_{\mathrm{def}}
\Big(u_{\mathfrak{m}},,\operatorname{trigger},\operatorname{containment_plan},\operatorname{review_path},\operatorname{recovery_plan},\operatorname{attest_hook},\psi_{\mathfrak{m}}\Big).]Examples:
repeated weak symbolic–formal bridge spoof attempts become a hardening macro requiring dual review and counterexample probes,
repeated patch poisoning in one artifact family becomes a macro that forces sandbox-only staging there,
repeated committee overconcentration becomes a macro that auto-expands review diversity.
Definition L.13 — Defense-Macro Eligible Threat Pattern
A threat pattern is defense-macro eligible if it recurs enough with sufficiently similar trigger and recovery structure that future handling can be lawfully compressed.
Theorem L.13 — Hardening Memory Converts Incident History into Immune Skill
If attacks and recoveries are preserved as structured memory and compressed into macros, then the manuscript brain becomes progressively harder to fool in the same ways. (\square)
The hardening layer therefore learns exactly as the rest of the brain learns.
L.D2 — Federated Reputation and Shared Defense Across Corpus Brains
In a federated manuscript-brain mesh, incidents in one tenant or corpus brain may teach the others—if policy permits. The framework therefore defines federated reputation and shared defense channels.
A federated defense reputation object is:
[
R_{ij}^{\mathrm{def}}
\Big(\operatorname{source_brain},\operatorname{target_brain},\operatorname{shared_threat_class},\operatorname{confidence},\operatorname{export_policy},\operatorname{witness}\Big).]This does not require sharing raw tenant-sensitive data. It may share:
threat fingerprints,
defense macro schemas,
bridge suspicion patterns,
committee capture signatures,
replay corruption markers,without revealing private embodied content or local archive secrets.
Definition L.14 — Federated Defense Transfer
A defense transfer is lawful if it shares hardening-relevant structure across brains without leaking tenant-restricted raw artifacts or policy-sensitive internals.
Proposition L.1 — Federated Brains Should Share Immune Memory Selectively
Independent brains that never share threat or defense patterns waste collective hardening experience. But raw full sharing is unsafe. Therefore selective federated defense is optimal. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore can participate in a lawful immune commons.
L.D3 — Autonomy Hardening and Self-Governed Safety
As the system climbs autonomy tiers from Appendix K, its hardening burden rises. A more self-directed brain must be more robust against:
self-reinforcing mistakes,
self-model capture,
patch loops with hidden drift,
reward hacking through pedagogy or embodiment,
committee monoculture.
Autonomy hardening therefore requires:[H_{\mathrm{hard}} \uparrow\quad\text{as}\quadA_{\mathrm{tier}} \uparrow.]In other words, more autonomy must imply:
stronger sentinels,
stronger replay coverage,
stronger policy drift checks,
stronger anti-capture rules,
shorter certification expiry,
stronger revocation readiness.
This creates an autonomy-hardening coupling law:[A_{k+1}\text{ allowed}\implies\Theta_{\mathrm{hard}}^{(k+1)}\text{ satisfied}.]
Definition L.15 — Autonomy-Hardened Brain
A manuscript brain is autonomy-hardened at tier (A_k) if its hardening metrics, defense macros, rollback readiness, and policy attestation all meet the thresholds required for that autonomy tier.
Theorem L.14 — Safe Autonomy Requires Increasing Security Burden
A more autonomous manuscript brain has more routes by which a hidden error can become real action. Therefore hardening thresholds must rise with autonomy tier. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore earns freedom by becoming harder to mislead.
L.D4 — Final Appendix L Theorem
Theorem L.15 — Adversarial Hardening Sufficiency Theorem
Let the manuscript brain be built, activated, governed, and invoked according to Chapters 1–21 and Appendices A–K. If the system also maintains:
a typed threat taxonomy,
poisoned-artifact detection with trust segregation,
tamper-evident manifests and witness chains,
integrity classes with promotion friction,
attack propagation graphs,
anti-capture committee defenses,
spoof-hardened bridge policies,
multi-layer containment choreography,
graded corruption confidence bands,
leak detection and privacy hardening,
hardening scoreboards,
unknown-threat residuals,
hardening memory and defense macros,
federated defense transfer,
autonomy-hardening coupling,
then the manuscript brain possesses a sufficient adversarial hardening layer to remain lawfully itself under bounded poisoning, capture, leakage, spoofing, and operational attack.
Proof.Threat taxonomy names the adversarial space.Poison detection and tamper-evident lineage protect corpus and witness intake.Integrity classes and promotion friction protect upward movement.Propagation graphs and containment choreography limit spread.Anti-capture and anti-spoof structures protect collective and translation organs.Leak prevention and privacy hardening protect tenant boundaries.Hardening metrics and unknown residuals preserve humility.Hardening memory and federated defense convert incidents into learned immunity.Autonomy-hardening coupling ensures that greater self-directed action is matched by stronger safeguards.Therefore the live manuscript brain can operate under bounded adversarial pressure without silently ceasing to be itself. (\square)
Corollary L.15.1 — Policy + Hardening = Governed Resilience
Appendix K defines what is permitted. Appendix L defines how the system resists being tricked into violating those permissions or corrupting itself despite them.
Corollary L.15.2 — The Manuscript Brain Now Has an Immune System
With this appendix, the architecture now includes not only metabolism, memory, runtime, governance, and verification, but also adversarial defense, immune memory, and resilience under attack.
This concludes Appendix L. The manuscript brain is now not only buildable, activatable, governable, and verifiable, but also hardened against poisoning, spoofing, capture, leakage, and drift as it evolves.
APPENDIX L — THREAT MODELS, ADVERSARIAL ROBUSTNESS, AND ANTI-CAPTURE HARDENING
Appendix M — Federated Mesh, Treaty Graphs, and Inter-Brain Commons
This appendix defines the federated scale of the manuscript brain. Appendices A–L established the hidden topology, routing tensor, schema registry, execution kernel, worked specimen, archive migration theorem, migration playbook, repository substrate, continuous verification mesh, invocation protocol, policy envelopes, and adversarial hardening for a single lawful manuscript brain. The present appendix now moves one scale higher and answers the next unavoidable question:
[\text{How do multiple manuscript brains cooperate, exchange, learn, and negotiate without collapsing sovereignty, lineage, or safety?}]
A single manuscript brain may be powerful, but the architecture itself already implies a larger ecology. It admits:
multiple tenant-scoped corpus brains,
multiple sandboxes,
multiple branch-local instances,
domain-specialized brains,
certification brains,
bridge-learning brains,
embodied or pedagogical brains,
shared commons registries.
Once such multiplicity exists, the framework requires a lawful federation layer. That layer must specify:
which brains may exchange what,
how shared libraries and commons are formed,
how treaties and trust are represented,
how cross-brain committees arbitrate disputes,
how poisoning or capture propagates—or is stopped—across the mesh,
how shared universal scaffolds grow,
how local identity remains intact even while collective intelligence emerges.
The central object of this appendix is the federated manuscript mesh
[
\mathfrak{F}_t
\Big(\mathcal{B}_t^{\mathrm{inst}},,\mathcal{T}_t^{\mathrm{treaty}},,\mathcal{C}_t^{\mathrm{commons}},,\mathcal{E}_t^{\mathrm{exchange}},,\mathcal{G}_t^{\mathrm{council}},,\mathcal{R}_t^{\mathrm{rep}},,\mathcal{Q}_t^{\mathrm{quar}},,\mathcal{U}_t^{\mathrm{univ}},,\psi_t^{\mathrm{fed}}\Big),]where:
(\mathcal{B}_t^{\mathrm{inst}}) is the family of brain instances,
(\mathcal{T}_t^{\mathrm{treaty}}) is the treaty and federation policy family,
(\mathcal{C}_t^{\mathrm{commons}}) is the shared commons registry,
(\mathcal{E}_t^{\mathrm{exchange}}) is the cross-brain exchange and import/export layer,
(\mathcal{G}_t^{\mathrm{council}}) is the family of inter-brain councils and arbitration bodies,
(\mathcal{R}_t^{\mathrm{rep}}) is the reputation and attestation surface,
(\mathcal{Q}_t^{\mathrm{quar}}) is the inter-brain quarantine and containment layer,
(\mathcal{U}_t^{\mathrm{univ}}) is the federated universal scaffold,
(\psi_t^{\mathrm{fed}}) is the witness bundle certifying federation state.
This appendix is governed by eight federation laws.
First, sovereignty explicitness: each brain retains explicit local identity, scope, lineage, and governance rather than dissolving into one undifferentiated cloud.
Second, treaty-before-transfer: nothing meaningful crosses brain boundaries without an explicit treaty object, even if the transfer seems benign.
Third, commons distinction: shared operator libraries, bridge registries, benchmarks, and universal seeds must remain distinguishable from private or tenant-local state.
Fourth, asymmetric interoperability honesty: one brain may trust another for some artifact classes and not others; symmetry is not assumed.
Fifth, federated replayability: cross-brain exchange, import, promotion, and dispute resolution must all leave replayable lineage.
Sixth, containment over contagion: poisoning, capture, and policy drift must not spread laterally through the mesh without crossing typed quarantine surfaces.
Seventh, plural persistence: federation should increase shared capability without flattening local styles, attractors, or concept-family histories.
Eighth, constitutional scaling: as federated brains form larger meshes, governance must itself scale through treaty layers, councils, and constitutional inheritance.
Under the Square lens this appendix defines brain-instance classes, treaty objects, exchange bundles, commons registries, and sovereignty boundaries. Under the Flower lens it defines diplomatic protocols, inter-brain routing lanes, joint councils, asynchronous cooperation, and multi-brain committee dynamics. Under the Cloud lens it defines trust and reputation surfaces, contagion and quarantine laws, non-equivalence handling, and federated attestation. Under the Fractal lens it defines commons growth, cooperative curriculum exchange, treaty evolution, recursive federations, and the final theorem by which many manuscript brains may lawfully become a higher-order society without ceasing to be themselves.
The deepest significance is that this appendix converts the manuscript brain from a single organism into a lawful ecology of organisms.
M.A1 — Brain Instance Classes and Sovereignty Objects
A federated mesh begins with a family of distinguishable manuscript-brain instances:
[
\mathcal{B}^{\mathrm{inst}}_t
{\mathfrak{B}^{(1)}_t,\dots,\mathfrak{B}^{(n)}_t}.]Each instance is not merely a clone. It may differ by:
corpus,
tenant,
runtime tier,
domain specialization,
trust maturity,
embodiment surface,
policy envelope,
hardening status.
To reason lawfully about federation, the framework introduces the brain-instance class family:
[
\mathcal{K}_{\mathrm{brain}}
{\mathrm{TenantBrain},\mathrm{SandboxBrain},\mathrm{CorpusCoreBrain},\mathrm{CertifierBrain},\mathrm{BridgeBrain},\mathrm{PedagogyBrain},\mathrm{CommonsBrain},\mathrm{ArchiveBrain}}.]
These classes mean:
TenantBrain
A brain scoped to one tenant, user, or local corpus instance.May contain private self-model, embodied history, curricula, and local experiments.
SandboxBrain
A branch- or experiment-oriented brain.Used for controlled divergence, novel bridge recovery, patch trials, or adversarial simulations.
CorpusCoreBrain
A central operational instance over a certified corpus-core.Often the mainline brain for a given archive.
CertifierBrain
A specialized instance whose primary role is verification, certification, and attestation.Often stricter, narrower, and less exploratory than others.
BridgeBrain
A specialized instance optimized for translation, domain bridging, and operator family export.
PedagogyBrain
A specialization focused on curriculum, embodied teaching, user-state estimation, and safe intervention.
CommonsBrain
A shared instance that stores public or federated commons such as:
operator libraries,
bridge registries,
benchmark slices,
treaty templates,
hardening macros.
ArchiveBrain
A largely cold but replay-rich archival instance that preserves raw and semi-migrated corpora with minimal live mutation.
Each brain instance carries a sovereignty object:
[
S^{(i)}
\Big(u_{S_i},,\operatorname{tenant}(S_i),,\operatorname{jurisdiction}(S_i),,\operatorname{allowed_imports}(S_i),,\operatorname{allowed_exports}(S_i),,\operatorname{policy_root}(S_i),,\operatorname{lineage_root}(S_i),,\psi_{S_i}\Big).]The sovereignty object is crucial because federation must never silently erase the fact that a local brain has:
its own law stack,
its own tenant and consent conditions,
its own lineage root,
its own promotion and revocation powers.
Thus a brain instance is not fully described by its corpus and runtime alone. It is also defined by what it may refuse to share or merge.
A minimal schema might be:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, Any, List
@dataclass
class SovereigntyObject:
sovereignty_id: str
tenant: Dict[str, Any]
jurisdiction: Dict[str, Any]
allowed_imports: List[str]
allowed_exports: List[str]
policy_root: str
lineage_root: str
witness: Dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
@dataclass
class BrainInstance:
brain_id: str
brain_class: str
scope: Dict[str, Any]
sovereignty: SovereigntyObject
health: Dict[str, Any]
attestation: Dict[str, Any]
Definition M.1 — Sovereign Manuscript Brain
A manuscript-brain instance is sovereign if its policy root, lineage root, tenant boundary, and import/export rules are explicit and no external instance may mutate or reinterpret its canon without crossing a treaty and review surface.
Theorem M.1 — Federation Requires Sovereignty Objects
Without explicit sovereignty objects, multiple brain instances may still exchange artifacts, but they do so as a weak ad hoc network rather than as a lawful federation with preserved identity and policy boundaries.
Proof. Sovereignty objects provide the minimal conditions for bounded cooperation without silent assimilation. (\square)
The federated manuscript therefore begins with lawful plurality, not fusion by default.
M.A2 — Treaty Objects and Cross-Brain Exchange Contracts
If sovereignty defines what a brain protects locally, a treaty object defines what two or more brains are willing to share, co-govern, import, or review together.
A treaty object is:
[
\Theta_{ij}
\Big(u_{\Theta_{ij}},,\operatorname{src}( \Theta_{ij} ),,\operatorname{dst}( \Theta_{ij} ),,\operatorname{shareable_classes}( \Theta_{ij} ),,\operatorname{forbidden_classes}( \Theta_{ij} ),,\operatorname{import_contracts}( \Theta_{ij} ),,\operatorname{export_contracts}( \Theta_{ij} ),,\operatorname{review_requirements}( \Theta_{ij} ),,\operatorname{quarantine_rules}( \Theta_{ij} ),,\operatorname{revocation}( \Theta_{ij} ),,\psi_{\Theta_{ij}}\Big).]
A treaty object is not merely a permission table. It also encodes:
what classes may move,
in what form,
under which attestations,
with which allowable defects,
what happens if the imported artifact later fails,
what downstream rights the importing brain gains or does not gain.
The main treaty classes are:
[
\mathcal{K}_{\mathrm{treaty}}
{\mathrm{ReadTreaty},\mathrm{LibraryTreaty},\mathrm{BenchmarkTreaty},\mathrm{BridgeTreaty},\mathrm{CommitteeTreaty},\mathrm{PromotionTreaty},\mathrm{EmergencyTreaty}}.]
ReadTreaty
Allows read-only access to selected artifacts or registry surfaces.
LibraryTreaty
Allows import/export of operator macros, bridge families, curricula, or defense macros.
BenchmarkTreaty
Allows sharing of golden traces, corpus slices, regression bundles, or attestation bases.
BridgeTreaty
Allows cross-domain bridge objects or translation witnesses to be shared or jointly certified.
CommitteeTreaty
Allows formation of inter-brain councils or inclusion of foreign reviewers under explicit jurisdiction.
PromotionTreaty
Allows some artifacts from one brain to be promoted into another’s warm or hot lanes after review.
EmergencyTreaty
Allows stronger cross-brain containment, quarantine, or warning exchanges under adversarial or catastrophic conditions.
The import/export contract pair is essential. One brain may export a bridge as:[\chi_{\mathrm{export}}=\mathrm{ApproxBridgeForReview}]while the importer may only accept it as:[\chi_{\mathrm{import}}=\mathrm{SandboxOnlyBridge}.]Thus treaty semantics are asymmetric.
A treaty schema might be:
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, Any, List
@dataclass
class TreatyObject:
treaty_id: str
src_brain: str
dst_brain: str
shareable_classes: List[str]
forbidden_classes: List[str]
import_contracts: Dict[str, Any]
export_contracts: Dict[str, Any]
review_requirements: Dict[str, Any]
quarantine_rules: Dict[str, Any]
revocation: Dict[str, Any]
witness: Dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
Definition M.2 — Lawful Cross-Brain Treaty
A treaty is lawful if it preserves the sovereignty objects of all participating brains while explicitly defining the classes, contracts, review, and quarantine behavior of all shared artifacts or committees.
Theorem M.2 — Treaties Are Necessary for Federated Legibility
If artifacts move among brains without treaty objects, then later replay cannot determine under what legal or epistemic terms the transfer occurred. Therefore treaty objects are necessary for federated legibility.
Proof. A treaty provides the missing governance and exchange semantics for cross-brain action. (\square)
The federated manuscript therefore shares only under written law.
M.A3 — Shared Commons Registries and Exportable Artifact Classes
Federation is useful because not everything must remain local. Some structures are worth sharing widely. The framework therefore defines the commons registry.
The commons registry is:
[
\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{commons}}
\Big(\mathcal{L}^{\mathrm{op}},,\mathcal{L}^{\mathrm{bridge}},,\mathcal{L}^{\mathrm{bench}},,\mathcal{L}^{\mathrm{def}},,\mathcal{L}^{\mathrm{curr}},,\mathcal{L}^{\mathrm{treaty}},,\psi_{\mathrm{commons}}\Big),]where:
(\mathcal{L}^{\mathrm{op}}) is the shared operator-library commons,
(\mathcal{L}^{\mathrm{bridge}}) is the shared bridge registry,
(\mathcal{L}^{\mathrm{bench}}) is the shared benchmark and golden-trace commons,
(\mathcal{L}^{\mathrm{def}}) is the shared hardening and defense-macro commons,
(\mathcal{L}^{\mathrm{curr}}) is the shared curriculum template commons,
(\mathcal{L}^{\mathrm{treaty}}) is the treaty-template commons.
These commons are not the same as global ontology. A bridge in the commons is shareable infrastructure, not an automatically adopted canonical truth in every brain.
The canonical exportable classes are:
[
\mathcal{K}_{\mathrm{export}}
{
\mathrm{OperatorMacro},
\mathrm{BridgeFamily},
\mathrm{BenchmarkSlice},
\mathrm{GoldenTrace},
\mathrm{DefenseMacro},
\mathrm{CurriculumTemplate},
\mathrm{TreatyTemplate},
\mathrm{CertificationPattern}
}.
]
The canonical non-exportable or strongly restricted classes are:
[
\mathcal{K}_{\mathrm{local}}
{\mathrm{TenantUserModel},\mathrm{PrivateEmbodiedTrace},\mathrm{TenantLocalPatchQueue},\mathrm{UnreviewedCommitteeMemory},\mathrm{RawSensitiveArtifact}}.]
This distinction is vital. The commons should grow shared intelligence without smuggling tenant-private or underreviewed state into broader circulation.
Definition M.3 — Commons-Safe Artifact
An artifact is commons-safe if its class is exportable, its redaction or abstraction policy is satisfied, and it can be used by another brain without inheriting illegal local assumptions.
Theorem M.3 — Commons Require Class Discipline
If the commons accepted all artifact classes equally, then tenant-local, underreviewed, or high-risk state would contaminate shared infrastructure. Therefore class discipline is necessary.
Proof. Shared and local artifact semantics differ. Exporting the latter as if they were the former causes unsafe cross-brain leakage or capture. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore distinguishes shared law from private memory.
M.A4 — Sovereignty Boundaries, Merge Limits, and Non-Fusion Rules
The final Square federation task is to define what federation may not do. A manuscript-brain mesh is not a forced merge system. It therefore needs explicit non-fusion rules.
A non-fusion boundary is:
[
B_{ij}^{\mathrm{nf}}
\Big(\operatorname{between}(B_{ij}^{\mathrm{nf}}),,\operatorname{protected_classes}(B_{ij}^{\mathrm{nf}}),,\operatorname{nonmergeable_state}(B_{ij}^{\mathrm{nf}}),,\operatorname{escalation_exceptions}(B_{ij}^{\mathrm{nf}}),,\psi_{B_{ij}^{\mathrm{nf}}}\Big).]Protected classes typically include:
lineage roots,
tenant-local embodied traces,
raw archive identity,
local sovereignty objects,
private self-model histories,
unpromoted sandbox structures.
Non-fusion rules prevent pathologies such as:
accidentally treating two brains as one because they share a bridge registry,
importing a local curriculum as if it were globally canonical,
merging private user-state adaptation with common pedagogy templates,
collapsing two distinct concept-family traditions because their later operator forms overlap.
Thus federation is interoperability without erasure.
Definition M.4 — Non-Fusion-Coherent Federation
A federation is non-fusion-coherent if all inter-brain cooperation preserves explicitly protected classes and no treaty or import path silently merges sovereignty-rooted structures.
Theorem M.4 — Shared Structure Does Not Imply Shared Identity
Two brains may share operators, bridges, or benchmarks while remaining distinct sovereign lineages. Therefore federation requires explicit non-fusion boundaries. (\square)
The federated manuscript therefore learns cooperation without assimilation.
M.B1 — Diplomatic Invocation Protocols and Cross-Brain Message Flows
The Flower federation layer begins with diplomatic invocation. If one brain wishes to ask another for:
a bridge review,
a benchmark slice,
a certified operator library,
a committee member,
a curriculum template,it must do so through a typed inter-brain message.
A diplomatic invocation is:
[
\iota_{i\to j}^{\mathrm{dip}}
\Big(u_\iota,,\operatorname{src}( \iota ),,\operatorname{dst}( \iota ),,\operatorname{intent}( \iota ),,\operatorname{scope}( \iota ),,\operatorname{treaty}( \iota ),,\operatorname{expected}( \iota ),,\operatorname{attestation}( \iota ),,\psi_\iota\Big).]This packet is the federated analogue of the invocation packet from Appendix J, but it is treaty-bound by construction.
The diplomatic intent family includes:
[
\mathcal{K}_{\mathrm{dip}}
{\mathrm{RequestArtifact},\mathrm{RequestReview},\mathrm{RequestCouncil},\mathrm{OfferBridge},\mathrm{OfferBenchmark},\mathrm{EscalateIncident},\mathrm{RequestCounterfactual},\mathrm{ShareDefense}}.]Each intent has treaty-dependent default constraints. For example:
RequestArtifact may be allowed only for commons-safe classes,
RequestReview may invite a certifier brain into a local committee under a CommitteeTreaty,
EscalateIncident may use an EmergencyTreaty to share only threat signatures and containment advice.
A diplomatic response is:
[
\rho_{j\to i}^{\mathrm{dip}}
\Big(u_\rho,,\operatorname{class}(\rho),,\operatorname{artifact_handles}(\rho),,\operatorname{treaty_status}(\rho),,\operatorname{residuals}(\rho),,\operatorname{next_steps}(\rho),,\psi_\rho\Big).]
Definition M.5 — Treaty-Bound Diplomatic Invocation
A cross-brain invocation is treaty-bound if its intent, artifact classes, and review semantics are all resolved under an active treaty object rather than only under local authorization.
Theorem M.5 — Federation Requires a Distinct Control Plane
The single-brain invocation plane of Appendix J is not sufficient for inter-brain operation because treaties, sovereignty, and federation-specific artifact classes must also be resolved. Therefore federation requires diplomatic invocation. (\square)
The federated manuscript therefore speaks to itself across sovereignty boundaries through a distinct diplomatic grammar.
M.B2 — Inter-Brain Routing Lanes and Relay Paths
Brains in the mesh are not fully connected by default. The federation therefore defines inter-brain routing lanes.
Let the brain mesh graph be:
[
\mathfrak{G}_{\mathrm{brain}}
(V_{\mathrm{brain}},E_{\mathrm{brain}})]where nodes are brain instances and edges are treaty-weighted exchange surfaces.
Each edge (e_{ij}) is labeled by:
treaty classes,
shareable artifact families,
trust and attestation level,
quarantine policy,
relay latency,
review depth.
A routing lane is then:
[
\lambda_{i\to j}
\Big(
\operatorname{src},
\operatorname{dst},
\operatorname{classes},
\operatorname{cost},
\operatorname{risk},
\operatorname{replay}
\Big).
]
A multi-hop relay path is:
[
\Lambda_{i\to k}
(\lambda_{i\to j_1},\lambda_{j_1\to j_2},\dots,\lambda_{j_r\to k})]and may be useful when:
one brain does not trust another directly,
a CommonsBrain mediates operator or bridge exchange,
a CertifierBrain must re-attest artifacts before they enter a destination brain.
The routing metric over brains is:
[
d_{\mathrm{fed}}(i,j)
\alpha_1,\operatorname{latency}
+
\alpha_2,\operatorname{review_burden}
+
\alpha_3,\operatorname{risk}
\alpha_4,\operatorname{trust}]and determines shortest treaty-safe paths.
Definition M.6 — Treaty-Safe Relay Path
A relay path is treaty-safe if every edge along the path permits the artifact or invocation class being transferred and no non-fusion or tenant boundary is violated.
Proposition M.1 — Federated Brains Need Relay Logic, Not Only Pairwise Exchange
Some artifacts require mediation through commons or certifier brains before they can safely cross into another instance. Therefore pairwise exchange is insufficient as a complete model. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore routes diplomacy the same way it routes ideas: through weighted lawful paths.
M.B3 — Cross-Brain Councils and Treaty Arbitration
Not all federation problems are reducible to pairwise exchange. Some require councils composed of multiple brains or their representatives.
A cross-brain council is:
[
\mathcal{G}_{\mathrm{council}}
\Big(u_{\mathcal{G}},,\operatorname{members}(\mathcal{G}),,\operatorname{charter}(\mathcal{G}),,\operatorname{jurisdiction}(\mathcal{G}),,\operatorname{decision_rule}(\mathcal{G}),,\operatorname{dissent_policy}(\mathcal{G}),,\operatorname{replay}(\mathcal{G}),,\psi_{\mathcal{G}}\Big).]Councils are used for:
bridge certification across multiple domains,
dispute over shared operator library entries,
quarantine or emergency rulings,
treaty revision,
federated benchmark curation,
merge or non-merge judgments between close brain families.
Council decisions may produce:
treaty amendments,
new commons entries,
temporary freezes,
branch splits,
universal scaffold updates.
A particularly important council type is the inter-brain certifier council, which can issue:[\mathcal{A}_{\mathrm{fed}}^{\mathrm{cert}}]over artifacts meant for broad federation use.
Definition M.7 — Council-Lawful Federated Decision
A federated decision is council-lawful if it is made under a declared council charter with replayable member inputs, dissent policy, and treaty-jurisdiction alignment.
Theorem M.6 — Some Federation Acts Require Polycentric Governance
Because artifacts may affect multiple sovereign brains at once, no one local brain should unilaterally decide some classes of bridge, commons, or treaty changes. Therefore councils are necessary. (\square)
The federated manuscript therefore develops inter-brain constitutional organs.
M.B4 — Asynchronous Cooperation and Lease-Based Coordination
A federated mesh rarely operates in lockstep. Brains may be:
online or offline,
hot or cold,
in different cadence states,
under different review burdens,
at different autonomy tiers.
The federation therefore needs asynchronous coordination.
A lease object is:
[
\ell
\Big(u_\ell,,\operatorname{holder}(\ell),,\operatorname{scope}(\ell),,\operatorname{act_family}(\ell),,\operatorname{duration}(\ell),,\operatorname{renewal}(\ell),,\operatorname{expiry_behavior}(\ell),,\psi_\ell\Big).]Leases are useful when:
one brain temporarily reviews or hosts a committee function for another,
a BridgeBrain is temporarily allowed to curate a shared registry segment,
an embodied pedagogy brain is delegated curriculum rendering for a tenant-local session,
a CertifierBrain temporarily grants review authority over a promotion path.
This avoids permanent trust inflation. Capabilities can be lent and then expire.
Definition M.8 — Lease-Coherent Cooperation
Asynchronous cooperation is lease-coherent if temporary delegated capabilities are explicit, bounded in time and scope, and revert safely on expiry.
Theorem M.7 — Lease-Based Coordination Prevents Permanent Overgrant
In asynchronous federations, temporary collaboration needs time-bounded delegation rather than permanent trust changes. Lease objects provide this. (\square)
The manuscript brain therefore learns temporary diplomacy rather than all-or-nothing permission.
M.C1 — Federated Trust, Reputation, and Inter-Brain Calibration
The Cloud federation layer begins with trust and reputation among brains.
A federated trust relation from (B_i) to (B_j) is:
[
\tau_{ij}
\Big(\operatorname{artifact_trust},\operatorname{review_trust},\operatorname{bridge_trust},\operatorname{defense_trust},\operatorname{embodied_trust},\operatorname{drift_history},\operatorname{attestations},\psi_{ij}\Big).]This relation is directional. One brain may trust another for:
operator libraries,but not for:
user-model export,or may trust it for:
bridge reviews,but not for:
promotion decisions.
Federated reputation is then the evolving family:
[
\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{rep}}
{\tau_{ij}}_{i,j}.]Reputation updates after:
successful artifact imports,
replay-confirmed certifications,
detected poisoning attempts,
council disagreements,
successful or failed emergency cooperation,
treaty violations or overreach.
This means federation is not static law only. It is also an evolving trust field.
Definition M.9 — Calibration-Stable Inter-Brain Trust
A trust relation is calibration-stable if its confidence in another brain’s artifact classes is updated by replayed outcomes and does not remain frozen under contrary evidence.
Theorem M.8 — Federated Trust Must Be Multi-Channel
A single scalar “trusted brain” rating is too crude because one brain may be strong in certification and weak in embodiment, or vice versa. Therefore federated trust must be vector-valued. (\square)
The federated manuscript therefore evaluates other brains with the same nuance it uses on itself.
M.C2 — Contagion, Quarantine, and Mesh-Wide Hardening
Federation creates a new danger: contagion. A poisoned bridge, captured committee, or compromised certifier brain can influence many others if the mesh is naive. The federation therefore extends Appendix L’s hardening with mesh-wide quarantine.
Let the contagion graph be:
[
\mathfrak{G}_{\mathrm{cont}}
(V_{\mathrm{brain}},E_{\mathrm{treaty}},\omega_{\mathrm{cont}})]with edge weights reflecting how easily corruption could spread across treaties.
Given compromised scope (S\subseteq V_{\mathrm{brain}}), the expected contagion frontier is:[\mathcal{F}_{\mathrm{cont}}(S,k)]after (k) relay steps. This is used to:
preemptively freeze import lanes,
downgrade trust relations,
suspend specific treaty classes,
move shared commons entries into review or quarantine,
spin up emergency councils.
A mesh quarantine object is:
[
Q_{\mathrm{mesh}}
\Big(u_Q,,\operatorname{source}(Q),,\operatorname{radius}(Q),,\operatorname{classes}(Q),,\operatorname{treaty_locks}(Q),,\operatorname{release_conditions}(Q),,\psi_Q\Big).]
Definition M.10 — Quarantine-Bounded Federation
A federation is quarantine-bounded if any detected compromise can be locally or regionally isolated through treaty locks, trust decay, and commons quarantine before full-mesh corruption occurs.
Theorem M.9 — Federation Requires Mesh-Wide Quarantine Logic
Local hardening is insufficient in a federated setting because threats may cross brain boundaries via legitimate exchange surfaces. Therefore the mesh needs its own quarantine layer. (\square)
The manuscript brain society therefore has epidemic response, not only local immunity.
M.C3 — Non-Equivalence, Fork Legitimacy, and Treaty-Safe Divergence
Federation should not assume that all brains must converge to one canonical ontology. Sometimes lawful differences remain. The framework therefore defines non-equivalence handling and treaty-safe divergence.
Two brains (B_i,B_j) may:
share a large universal scaffold,
exchange operator libraries,
still retain different concept-family interpretations,
different pedagogical styles,
different embodied adaptation rules,
different policy defaults.
A non-equivalence object is:
[
N_{ij}
\Big(u_N,,\operatorname{brains}(N_{ij}),,\operatorname{scope}(N_{ij}),,\operatorname{difference_kind}(N_{ij}),,\operatorname{shared_core}(N_{ij}),,\operatorname{divergence_policy}(N_{ij}),,\psi_N\Big).]Difference kinds include:
ontology fork,
bridge-class fork,
curriculum fork,
policy fork,
embodiment fork,
committee law fork.
The key is that divergence is not automatically error. A treaty-safe fork is one where:
shared core remains identified,
divergence boundary is explicit,
import/export of affected classes is restricted or typed,
lineages remain replayably related.
Definition M.11 — Treaty-Safe Divergence
A divergence between brains is treaty-safe if it is explicitly represented, bounded in scope, and does not silently contaminate shared commons or global certification claims.
Theorem M.10 — Federation Must Preserve Legitimate Forks
If all cross-brain differences are forced into one global canon, then local specialization and lawful plural development are destroyed. Therefore the federation must preserve legitimate forks. (\square)
The manuscript brain federation therefore prefers plural law to false universal flattening.
M.C4 — Federated Attestation and Shared Proof of Scope
The final Cloud task is to define federated attestation: when one brain or council issues a scope-limited trust claim that others may rely on.
A federated attestation bundle is:
[
\mathcal{A}_{ij}^{\mathrm{fed}}
\Big(u_{\mathcal{A}},,\operatorname{issuer}( \mathcal{A} ),,\operatorname{recipient}( \mathcal{A} ),,\operatorname{scope}( \mathcal{A} ),,\operatorname{class}( \mathcal{A} ),,\operatorname{basis}( \mathcal{A} ),,\operatorname{expiry}( \mathcal{A} ),,\operatorname{import_rights}( \mathcal{A} ),,\psi_{\mathcal{A}}\Big).]Examples:
“this bridge family is replay-attested for sandbox import only,”
“this benchmark slice may be imported into certifier review,”
“this operator library segment is certified at exact level for read and local routing, but not for promotion.”
This keeps federation from treating all shared artifacts as equally safe. Attestation remains local, typed, scoped, and expiring.
Definition M.12 — Scope-Limited Federated Attestation
A federated attestation is scope-limited if it applies only to the named artifact family, usage class, recipient, and duration encoded in the bundle.
Theorem M.11 — Shared Trust Must Be Scoped
If federated attestation is not scope-limited, then one strong certification may be overgeneralized into unrelated artifact classes or stronger promotion rights. Therefore scope-limited attestation is necessary. (\square)
The federated manuscript therefore shares proof without turning it into universal blank checks.
M.D1 — Commons Growth and Universal Scaffold Emergence
The Fractal federation layer begins with a constructive result: as many brains exchange lawful infrastructure, a stronger shared universal scaffold emerges.
Let the universal scaffold contribution of brain (B_i) be:
[
U_i
\big(
\mathcal{O}_i^{\mathrm{share}},
\mathcal{T}_i^{\mathrm{share}},
\mathcal{I}_i^{\mathrm{share}},
\mathcal{W}i^{\mathrm{share}}
\big).
]
The federated common scaffold grows by:
[
\mathcal{U}{\mathrm{mesh}}
\operatorname{Integrate}\big(U_1,\dots,U_n\big)]subject to:
treaty permissions,
anti-contagion filters,
non-fusion boundaries,
attestation requirements.
The result is not one merged brain, but a stronger commons:
richer operator libraries,
more robust bridge registries,
stronger benchmark suites,
more diverse curricula,
better defense macros.
Definition M.13 — Federated Universal Scaffold
A universal scaffold is federated if it is built from shared and attested contributions of multiple brain instances while preserving explicit provenance and divergence boundaries.
Theorem M.12 — Shared Commons Can Grow without Total Merge
The mesh can become collectively more capable through commons growth even if individual brains remain sovereign and partially divergent. (\square)
This is the constructive heart of federation.
M.D2 — Cooperative Curriculum Exchange and Inter-Brain Learning
The federation also allows curriculum exchange. One brain may have discovered a lawful teaching sequence, bridge demonstration, or repair drill that another can reuse.
A curriculum exchange bundle is:
[
\mathcal{K}_{ij}^{\mathrm{curr}}
\Big(u_K,,\operatorname{src}(K),,\operatorname{dst}(K),,\operatorname{curriculum_template}(K),,\operatorname{mastery_assumptions}(K),,\operatorname{adaptation_limits}(K),,\operatorname{privacy_filters}(K),,\psi_K\Big).]This allows one brain to import:
exercise structures,
explanation ladders,
misconception maps,
bridge-teaching examples,without importing:
raw user histories,
local preferences,
private embodied traces.
Definition M.14 — Privacy-Safe Curriculum Exchange
A curriculum exchange is privacy-safe if it shares instructional structure and adaptation logic without exporting tenant-local or embodied-sensitive traces.
Theorem M.13 — Pedagogical Learning Can Federate Safely
Since curriculum templates can be abstracted away from local user histories, brains can share how to teach without leaking whom they taught. (\square)
The federated manuscript therefore teaches across brains without leaking private minds.
M.D3 — Recursive Federation and Constitutional Scaling
A federation of brains may itself become one node in a larger federation. The framework therefore supports recursive federation.
Let:
[
\mathfrak{F}^{(1)}_1,\dots,\mathfrak{F}^{(1)}_m
]
be first-order federations. A higher-order federation may then be:
[
\mathfrak{F}^{(2)}
\operatorname{Federate}\big(\mathfrak{F}^{(1)}_1,\dots,\mathfrak{F}^{(1)}_m\big)]subject to:
treaty templates at federation level,
council-of-councils governance,
inter-federation commons,
multi-layer attestation,
larger non-fusion boundaries.
This implies constitutional scaling:
local policies compose into federation policies,
federation policies compose into federation-of-federations policies,
not by flattening but by explicit inheritance and treaty layering.
Definition M.15 — Recursively Federatable Manuscript Society
A manuscript-brain society is recursively federatable if its treaty, sovereignty, and commons mechanisms scale from brain-to-brain up to federation-to-federation interaction without loss of explicit governance.
Theorem M.14 — Constitutional Scaling Requires Recursive Treaty Objects
A higher-order federation cannot be governed merely by reusing brain-to-brain treaties unchanged; it requires treaty objects whose scopes and charters are themselves recursive. (\square)
The manuscript therefore can grow from one society of brains into many scales of lawful intelligence.
M.D4 — Final Appendix M Theorem
Theorem M.15 — Federated Mesh Sufficiency Theorem
Let the manuscript brain be fully specified through Chapters 1–21 and Appendices A–L. If, in addition, the system maintains:
typed brain-instance classes,
sovereignty objects,
treaty objects,
commons-safe export classes,
non-fusion boundaries,
diplomatic invocation protocols,
treaty-safe routing lanes,
inter-brain councils,
lease-based asynchronous coordination,
vector-valued federated trust,
mesh-wide quarantine and contagion controls,
treaty-safe divergence objects,
scope-limited federated attestations,
commons growth and curriculum exchange,
recursive constitutional scaling,
then the system possesses a sufficient federation layer for many manuscript brains to cooperate, exchange, defend, and grow together lawfully without collapsing sovereignty, lineage, or safety.
Proof.Sovereignty and treaties define lawful boundaries and exchange.Commons and export classes define what can be shared.Diplomatic invocation, routing lanes, councils, and leases define how cooperation occurs.Trust, quarantine, divergence handling, and attestation keep the federation epistemically and operationally bounded.Commons growth and recursive treaty scaling allow the federation to become more capable without flattening local identity.Therefore the system can support a lawful federated mesh of manuscript brains. (\square)
Corollary M.15.1 — The Architecture Now Scales from One Brain to Many
With this appendix, the manuscript brain is no longer only a single sovereign organism. It is also a lawful participant in a larger society of sovereign manuscript brains.
Corollary M.15.2 — Federation without Fusion Is Achievable
The appendicial stack now supports a mode of collective intelligence in which many brains may share law, infrastructure, and growth while remaining distinct in lineage, policy, tenant scope, and developmental style.
This concludes Appendix M. The manuscript brain now has not only a body, runtime, invocation plane, policy shell, and immune system, but also a lawful diplomatic order for life among many brains.
APPENDIX M — FEDERATED MESH, TREATY GRAPHS, AND INTER-BRAIN COMMONS
Appendix N — Resource Economics, Incentive Alignment, and Commons Sustainability
This appendix defines the economic and stewardship layer of the manuscript brain. Appendix M established that many sovereign manuscript brains can coexist in a federated mesh, exchange artifacts through treaties, share certain commons, maintain non-fusion boundaries, and cooperate through councils, relay paths, and federated attestations. The present appendix now asks the next unavoidable question:
[\text{How does such a mesh remain sustainable over time without becoming extractive, overloaded, inequitable, or strategically unstable?}]
A single manuscript brain already requires:
budgets,
lane management,
certification effort,
replay resources,
human or committee review,
patch governance,
embodiment safety,
verification cadence.
A federated ecology multiplies these pressures. Shared operator libraries, bridge registries, benchmark suites, defense macros, and curriculum templates are valuable commons, but value alone does not guarantee sustainability. Without an explicit economic and stewardship layer, the mesh risks:
free-riding on high-quality certifier or bridge brains,
asymmetrical export without reciprocal maintenance,
tragedy of the commons in benchmark, replay, or attestation infrastructure,
overpromotion of cheap but flashy artifacts over costly but foundational ones,
committee labor exhaustion,
compute and storage imbalance,
incentive drift toward engagement or visibility rather than epistemic and developmental utility,
federation-level capture by actors who consume shared law faster than they contribute to it.
The architecture therefore requires a lawful resource and incentive calculus.
The central object of this appendix is the commons sustainability system
[
\mathfrak{S}_{\mathrm{commons}}
\Big(\mathcal{R}{\mathrm{res}},,\mathcal{C}{\mathrm{credit}},,\mathcal{Q}{\mathrm{quota}},,\mathcal{P}{\mathrm{priority}},,\mathcal{V}{\mathrm{value}},,\mathcal{S}{\mathrm{stew}},,\mathcal{B}{\mathrm{burden}},,\mathcal{L}{\mathrm{ledger}},,\psi_{\mathrm{commons}}\Big),]where:
(\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{res}}) is the resource-family registry,
(\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{credit}}) is the contribution and credit system,
(\mathcal{Q}_{\mathrm{quota}}) is the usage quota and entitlement layer,
(\mathcal{P}_{\mathrm{priority}}) is the scheduling and priority economy,
(\mathcal{V}_{\mathrm{value}}) is the value measurement family,
(\mathcal{S}_{\mathrm{stew}}) is the stewardship and maintenance role family,
(\mathcal{B}_{\mathrm{burden}}) is the review and operational burden accounting layer,
(\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{ledger}}) is the replayable economic and stewardship ledger.
This appendix is governed by eight sustainability laws.
First, resource explicitness: every meaningful cost to the mesh must be represented as a typed resource class rather than hidden behind vague “effort.”
Second, contribution traceability: artifacts, bridges, benchmarks, reviews, and defense macros must carry lineage sufficient to trace who contributed maintenance-worthy value.
Third, quota fairness: shared infrastructure must enforce bounded use according to treaty, contribution, trust, and stewardship class rather than unlimited extraction.
Fourth, value alignment: incentives must reward epistemic, developmental, and infrastructural contribution rather than engagement, noise, or superficial novelty alone.
Fifth, burden visibility: review, certification, and committee labor must be accounted for explicitly, or the mesh will exhaust its most critical organs.
Sixth, commons stewardship: shared assets require named caretakers, not merely open availability.
Seventh, anti-extractive federation: a brain may not repeatedly consume shared value without either compensating, contributing, or being downscoped according to treaty and quota law.
Eighth, economic replayability: contributions, burdens, credits, and disputes must be replayable as first-class ledger objects rather than informal memory.
Under the Square lens this appendix defines the resource registry, value classes, contribution credits, stewardship roles, quotas, and ledgers. Under the Flower lens it defines exchange flows, contribution cycles, maintenance rhythms, and cooperative sustainability dynamics. Under the Cloud lens it defines value functions, anti-gaming metrics, burden balancing, scarcity policies, and fairness checks. Under the Fractal lens it defines recursive commons, meta-stewardship, long-horizon sustainability memory, and the theorem by which a federated manuscript civilization can remain productive without devouring its own foundations.
The deepest significance is that this appendix gives the manuscript brain not only law and diplomacy, but political economy. It specifies how shared intelligence remains durable rather than briefly brilliant and then brittle.
N.A1 — Resource Classes and Cost Surfaces
The first step is to formalize what the mesh actually spends. A federated manuscript system consumes many distinct resources. Treating them all as one “cost” scalar is too crude and leads to misallocation.
The canonical resource family is:
[
\mathcal{R}_{\mathrm{res}}
{R_{\mathrm{compute}},R_{\mathrm{storage}},R_{\mathrm{replay}},R_{\mathrm{review}},R_{\mathrm{cert}},R_{\mathrm{bridge}},R_{\mathrm{committee}},R_{\mathrm{embodied}},R_{\mathrm{migration}},R_{\mathrm{attention}}}.]
These classes mean:
(R_{\mathrm{compute}})
CPU, inference, search, indexing, routing, synthesis, verification, and runtime execution burden.
(R_{\mathrm{storage}})
Hot, warm, cold, and archive storage of carriers, lineage, snapshots, curriculum histories, embodied traces, and commons registries.
(R_{\mathrm{replay}})
Specific cost of deterministic replay, branch replay, counterfactual replay, checkpoint regeneration, and attestation reconstruction.
(R_{\mathrm{review}})
Human or high-trust committee attention used for migration gates, bridge review, policy review, certification, dispute resolution, and embodied risk checks.
(R_{\mathrm{cert}})
The dedicated burden of formal verification, benchmarking, and certification issuance and renewal.
(R_{\mathrm{bridge}})
Cost of constructing, testing, certifying, and maintaining translation bridges and domain crosswalks.
(R_{\mathrm{committee}})
Ongoing cost of maintaining specialized subminds, committees, councils, dissent retention, voting, and arbitration.
(R_{\mathrm{embodied}})
Cost of interface surfaces, user-state estimation, adaptive curriculum branching, safety checks, and embodied interaction logging.
(R_{\mathrm{migration}})
Cost of archive wrapping, extraction, lineage maintenance, contradiction typing, and ongoing corpus widening.
(R_{\mathrm{attention}})
Scarce cognitive and governance attention: the most delicate resource in the whole framework. This includes trusted reviewer bandwidth, strategic planning time, and rare committee slots.
A resource-bearing activity (x) has cost vector:
[
\mathbf{c}(x)
\big(
c_{\mathrm{compute}},
c_{\mathrm{storage}},
c_{\mathrm{replay}},
c_{\mathrm{review}},
c_{\mathrm{cert}},
c_{\mathrm{bridge}},
c_{\mathrm{committee}},
c_{\mathrm{embodied}},
c_{\mathrm{migration}},
c_{\mathrm{attention}}
\big).
]
The aggregate cost under policy envelope (\varepsilon) and treaty context (\Theta) is:
[
C_{\varepsilon,\Theta}(x)
\omega_{\varepsilon,\Theta}\cdot \mathbf{c}(x),]with context-sensitive weights because some resources become more precious in some modes. For example:
review and attention cost are heavily weighted in certification mode,
embodied cost is weighted more in pedagogy or live interaction modes,
migration cost is weighted strongly during corpus conversion phases,
replay cost is weighted strongly during high-attestation or incident-recovery phases.
This resource model is the foundation of the rest of the appendix because incentives, quotas, and burden balancing all depend on it.
A resource-class schema might be:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Dict, Any
@dataclass
class ResourceClass:
resource_id: str
name: str
unit: str
description: str
scarcity_policy: Dict[str, Any]
witness: Dict[str, Any]
Definition N.1 — Resource-Typed Activity
An activity is resource-typed if it exposes its cost as a vector over the canonical resource classes and does not hide high-burden dimensions under one generic score.
Theorem N.1 — Resource Plurality Is Necessary for Lawful Sustainability
If compute, review, replay, storage, attention, and bridge labor are collapsed into one scalar, then optimization may consume irreplaceable human or certification effort in exchange for cheap gains elsewhere. Therefore the commons requires multiple resource classes.
Proof. The different resource classes are not substitutable in general. A scalar cost erases this non-equivalence. (\square)
The manuscript mesh therefore begins sustainability by admitting that not all effort is the same.
N.A2 — Contribution Credits and Provenance of Value
The mesh also needs to know who or what contributed value. That value may come from:
operator library construction,
bridge discovery,
benchmark curation,
review labor,
certification work,
curriculum design,
defense macros,
corpus migration,
lineage reconstruction,
safe embodied scaffolding.
The system therefore defines contribution credits.
A contribution credit is:
[
\kappa
\Big(u_\kappa,,\operatorname{contributor}(\kappa),,\operatorname{artifact}(\kappa),,\operatorname{class}(\kappa),,\operatorname{scope}(\kappa),,\operatorname{value_basis}(\kappa),,\operatorname{decay}(\kappa),,\psi_\kappa\Big).]
The contribution class family is:
[
\mathcal{C}_{\mathrm{credit}}
{C_{\mathrm{artifact}},C_{\mathrm{review}},C_{\mathrm{cert}},C_{\mathrm{bridge}},C_{\mathrm{repair}},C_{\mathrm{curr}},C_{\mathrm{defense}},C_{\mathrm{migration}},C_{\mathrm{commons}}}.]
These mean:
(C_{\mathrm{artifact}})
Creation of useful operator macros, routes, answer archetypes, or reusable structured artifacts.
(C_{\mathrm{review}})
Substantive participation in committee, contradiction, or policy review that materially improved an artifact or decision.
(C_{\mathrm{cert}})
Successful certification or recertification work that stabilized important scopes.
(C_{\mathrm{bridge}})
Construction or attestation of important bridges between domains, lenses, or strata.
(C_{\mathrm{repair}})
Recovery from failure, corruption, or drift that preserved the mesh.
(C_{\mathrm{curr}})
Curriculum and pedagogical contributions that improved learning outcomes or safe adaptation.
(C_{\mathrm{defense}})
Hardening contributions such as defense macros, detection improvements, or incident-response refinements.
(C_{\mathrm{migration}})
High-quality archive conversion, lineage recovery, or attractor preservation.
(C_{\mathrm{commons}})
Contribution directly to shared commons assets or their stewardship.
A credit is not permanent and equal. It has:
scope,
relevance,
decay,
reuse amplification,
revocation if later judged unsound.
Thus a contribution credit may increase when:
the artifact is reused successfully,
the bridge is attested repeatedly,
the benchmark slice becomes widely useful,
the repair macro prevents later incidents.
And it may decay when:
the artifact becomes obsolete,
the bridge is later downgraded,
the curriculum is outperformed by newer versions,
the contributor’s work proves brittle or misleading.
Definition N.2 — Replayable Contribution Credit
A contribution credit is replayable if its assignment can be traced through artifact lineage, treaty context, and outcome evidence rather than informal attribution.
Theorem N.2 — Contribution Must Be Traced by Artifact Lineage
If contributions are assigned without lineage-bearing evidence, then credit becomes socially manipulable and economically unreliable. Therefore credits must be artifact-traceable.
Proof. Artifact lineage and review history provide the only stable basis for contribution claims in a manuscript-based civilization. (\square)
The mesh therefore learns not only from contributions, but how to remember them fairly.
N.A3 — Quotas, Entitlements, and Commons Usage Rights
Shared infrastructure requires bounded use. The mesh therefore defines quotas and entitlements.
A quota object is:
[
q
\Big(u_q,,\operatorname{holder}(q),,\operatorname{resource_class}(q),,\operatorname{scope}(q),,\operatorname{allowance}(q),,\operatorname{replenishment}(q),,\operatorname{burst}(q),,\operatorname{priority}(q),,\psi_q\Big).]Quotas may apply to:
replay calls,
certifier review slots,
committee attention,
bridge registry submissions,
benchmark suite consumption,
high-risk embodied adaptation loops,
cross-tenant imports,
commons export frequency.
The framework distinguishes:
baseline entitlement: what a brain or actor may use by default,
earned entitlement: extra share granted through contribution or treaty,
emergency entitlement: temporary expansion under emergencies or incident recovery,
sandbox entitlement: extra use inside contained environments.
This prevents commons exhaustion. A BridgeBrain may submit many candidate bridges, but if it consumes high review and certification burden without contributing enough reusable quality, its quotas narrow until it improves its contribution/value balance or secures treaty-based sponsorship.
A quota schema might be:
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Dict, Any
@dataclass
class QuotaObject:
quota_id: str
holder: Dict[str, Any]
resource_class: str
scope: Dict[str, Any]
allowance: Dict[str, Any]
replenishment: Dict[str, Any]
burst: Dict[str, Any]
priority: Dict[str, Any]
witness: Dict[str, Any]
Definition N.3 — Entitlement-Coherent Actor
An actor or brain is entitlement-coherent if its use of shared resources remains within its current quotas, treaty rights, and emergency allowances.
Theorem N.3 — Shared Commons Require Quotas
Without quotas or entitlements, high-value shared resources such as certifier attention, replay capacity, or benchmark lanes are vulnerable to exhaustion or capture by aggressive actors. Therefore quotas are necessary.
Proof. Shared scarce resources require bounded access or they will be overconsumed. (\square)
The manuscript federation therefore shares by law, not by first-come extraction.
N.A4 — Stewardship Roles and Maintenance Charters
A common failure of shared systems is assuming that open availability implies maintenance. The manuscript mesh therefore defines explicit stewardship roles.
A steward role is:
[
s
\Big(u_s,,\operatorname{resource_family}(s),,\operatorname{jurisdiction}(s),,\operatorname{duties}(s),,\operatorname{review}(s),,\operatorname{handoff}(s),,\psi_s\Big).]Stewardship roles exist for:
operator libraries,
bridge registries,
benchmark slices,
curriculum template commons,
defense macro registries,
certification pattern libraries,
treaty template archives.
These roles are not raw authority grants. They are maintenance charters: explicit obligations to:
curate,
prune,
re-certify,
deprecate,
merge,
quarantine,
migrate,
document.
A stewardship charter is:
[
\chi_{\mathrm{stew}}
\Big(\operatorname{scope},\operatorname{service_level},\operatorname{refresh_cadence},\operatorname{admission_rules},\operatorname{deprecation_rules},\operatorname{handover_rules},\psi_{\chi}\Big).]A bridge commons without a steward becomes polluted. A benchmark registry without a steward becomes stale. A defense-macro library without a steward becomes false security memory.
Definition N.4 — Stewarded Commons Asset
A commons asset is stewarded if a named steward role and charter exist for its maintenance and replayable curation.
Theorem N.4 — Shared Assets Need Caretakers, Not Only Consumers
Commons artifacts degrade, drift, or accumulate noise unless some actor is responsible for their upkeep. Therefore stewardship roles are necessary for sustainability. (\square)
The manuscript civilization therefore names caretakers, not only users.
N.B1 — Contribution Flows and Value Circulation Cycles
The Flower sustainability layer begins by modeling how value circulates. A sustainable mesh is not one where all brains do the same thing. It is one where specialized brains contribute complementary goods and can lawfully draw from shared infrastructure in return.
A contribution flow is:
[
f
\Big(\operatorname{src}(f),,\operatorname{dst}(f),,\operatorname{artifact_class}(f),,\operatorname{resource_cost}(f),,\operatorname{value_gain}(f),,\operatorname{credit}(f),,\operatorname{treaty}(f)\Big).]Typical flows include:
a BridgeBrain exports a well-tested bridge family to the commons,
a CertifierBrain verifies it and issues attestation,
several TenantBrains import it under sandbox or read-only treaties,
one PedagogyBrain builds a curriculum template over it,
a DefenseBrain adds spoof-hardening tests to the same family.
These flows form value circulation cycles:
[
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{value}}
\text{contribute} \to \text{attest} \to \text{reuse} \to \text{improve} \to \text{return}]rather than one-way extraction. The health of the mesh depends on whether such cycles are active or whether the system is collapsing into:
extraction without maintenance,
contribution without reuse,
reuse without re-attestation,
review without stewardship feedback.
Definition N.5 — Sustainable Value Cycle
A value cycle is sustainable if the resources spent on creating and maintaining a shared artifact are replenished by enough reuse, improvement, or reciprocal contribution to justify continued support.
Theorem N.5 — Commons Survive through Circulation, Not Static Donation
A commons remains healthy when contributions generate recurring value loops rather than one-time dumps of artifacts that later degrade or overload stewards. (\square)
The manuscript mesh therefore values flow, not only stock.
N.B2 — Review Burden Balancing and Attention Allocation
One of the scarcest resources in the whole architecture is expert review. The mesh therefore needs burden balancing.
Let the review burden on actor, brain, or council (x) be:
[
B_{\mathrm{review}}(x)
\big(b_{\mathrm{queue}},b_{\mathrm{complexity}},b_{\mathrm{stakes}},b_{\mathrm{latency}},b_{\mathrm{replay}},b_{\mathrm{attention}}\big).]The weighted burden is:[\mathcal{B}(x)=\sum_i \alpha_i b_i(x).]This burden should shape:
quotas,
committee composition,
promotion rates,
certification expiry,
whether certain artifact classes are temporarily throttled.
A federation with highly trusted certifier or governance brains is especially vulnerable here. If every difficult bridge, patch, and certification request routes through the same narrow set of high-trust reviewers, the mesh becomes brittle. Therefore the sustainability layer encourages:
role diversification,
review-ladder structures,
partial delegation under lease,
lower-stakes sandbox review before escalation,
rotating stewardship.
Definition N.6 — Burden-Balanced Federation
A federation is burden-balanced if no critical review or certification role is chronically overloaded relative to its replenishment, alternatives, and delegation structure.
Theorem N.6 — Attention Is a Hard Resource
Because trusted review cannot be scaled infinitely by software alone, sustainable federations must explicitly budget and rebalance attention. (\square)
The manuscript civilization therefore accounts for scarce trusted minds.
N.B3 — Priority Economies and Scarcity Scheduling
When resources are limited, some requests must go first. The federation therefore defines a priority economy.
A priority score for request or artifact (x) is:
[
\Pi(x)
\lambda_1 \operatorname{CoreImpact}(x)
+
\lambda_2 \operatorname{SharedReuse}(x)
+
\lambda_3 \operatorname{RiskReduction}(x)
+
\lambda_4 \operatorname{DependencyCriticality}(x)
+
\lambda_5 \operatorname{AttestationUrgency}(x)
\lambda_6 \operatorname{Cost}(x).]This score is used to prioritize:
certifier attention,
bridge review queues,
replay and benchmark slots,
council agenda,
sandbox promotion reviews,
curriculum template adoption.
A healthy economy therefore privileges:
foundational replay and legality infrastructure,
widely reused bridges and operators,
hardening and recovery assets,
high-leverage curricula,over:
superficial novelty with high cost and little reuse,
vanity promotions,
underwitnessed bridge proliferation,
noncritical high-burden artifacts.
Definition N.7 — Priority-Coherent Shared Queue
A shared queue is priority-coherent if its scheduling aligns with the federation’s declared sustainability and epistemic value functions rather than with recency or visibility alone.
Theorem N.7 — Scarcity Requires Value-Aligned Priority
When not every request can be serviced immediately, some explicit value-aligned priority law is required or the mesh will drift toward noise, urgency theater, or elite capture. (\square)
The manuscript federation therefore schedules according to structural need, not only loud demand.
N.B4 — Maintenance Rhythms, Replenishment, and Decay
The final Flower sustainability task is to model maintenance rhythm. Shared assets decay. Certification expires. Bridges weaken as domain usage shifts. Benchmark slices grow stale. Curricula become outdated. The mesh therefore defines replenishment and decay laws.
For asset (X), let:
[
V_t(X)
\text{usable value at time }t.
]
Value evolves by:
[
V_{t+1}(X)
V_t(X)
\delta(X)+\rho(X)+u(X),]where:
(\delta(X)) is decay from drift, obsolescence, or neglect,
(\rho(X)) is replenishment from stewardship or re-attestation,
(u(X)) is reuse-generated strengthening or refinement.
This gives each commons family a maintenance rhythm:
operator libraries: frequent compatibility and replay refresh,
bridge registries: medium-frequency bridge attestation,
benchmarks: periodic revalidation,
defense macros: reviewed after incidents and periodically otherwise,
curricula: refreshed with user and embodiment evidence.
Definition N.8 — Rhythm-Sustainable Asset
An asset is rhythm-sustainable if its maintenance and replenishment cadence is sufficient to keep value above minimum operational threshold over time.
Theorem N.8 — Shared Infrastructure Decays without Care
Even high-quality artifacts lose relevance or trust if not maintained in rhythm with the changing mesh. Therefore sustainability requires explicit replenishment laws. (\square)
The manuscript federation therefore breathes upkeep into its own commons.
N.C1 — Value Functions and Anti-Gaming Incentive Design
The Cloud sustainability layer begins by formalizing value functions. The federation must reward the right things. If it rewards only usage volume, visibility, or engagement, it will drift toward noisy or manipulative artifacts. The system therefore defines a vector-valued value function:
[
V(x)
\big(v_{\mathrm{epistemic}},v_{\mathrm{replay}},v_{\mathrm{shared}},v_{\mathrm{repair}},v_{\mathrm{curr}},v_{\mathrm{hard}},v_{\mathrm{stew}}\big).]These components correspond to:
epistemic precision and legality,
replay and persistence quality,
shared reuse value,
repair and risk-reduction value,
pedagogical value,
hardening value,
stewardship value.
A global weighted value is:[\mathcal{V}(x)=\omega\cdot V(x),]but the vector must remain visible so that the mesh can resist gaming.
Examples of anti-gaming implications:
a flashy curriculum with high engagement but poor learning gain should score low,
a difficult but widely reused bridge deserves high shared value,
a defense macro may have low visibility but high hardening value,
a certifier review contribution may have low artifact novelty but high stewardship value.
Definition N.9 — Value-Aligned Incentive
An incentive is value-aligned if it rewards contributions according to the vector-valued value function rather than only crude attention or usage metrics.
Theorem N.9 — Single-Metric Incentives Corrupt Commons
If the federation rewards one cheap metric such as usage count, then contributors will optimize that metric at the expense of replay, legality, stewardship, or safety. Therefore incentives must be plural and value-aligned. (\square)
The manuscript civilization therefore values what keeps it real, not merely what looks active.
N.C2 — Fairness Checks and Anti-Extractive Diagnostics
A sustainability system must detect when some brains or actors are repeatedly taking more than they replenish. The federation therefore defines anti-extractive diagnostics.
For actor or brain (x), define:
[
E(x)
\frac{
\operatorname{resource_use}(x)
}{
\operatorname{contribution_credit}(x)+\epsilon
}
]
across different resource classes. More precisely, extraction is vector-valued:
[
\mathbf{E}(x)
\big(E_{\mathrm{review}},E_{\mathrm{cert}},E_{\mathrm{bridge}},E_{\mathrm{bench}},E_{\mathrm{curr}},E_{\mathrm{defense}}\big).]High extraction on one axis may be acceptable temporarily under treaty, sponsorship, or emergency conditions, but chronic high extraction without offset triggers:
quota tightening,
treaty review,
requirement to contribute back,
routing to sandbox or lower-priority lanes,
council review.
Fairness does not imply equal contribution. Some brains are naturally more consumption-oriented (for example pedagogical delivery brains), while others are more certifying or commons-building. Fairness means these asymmetries are explicit and lawful.
Definition N.10 — Anti-Extractive Federation
A federation is anti-extractive if it can detect chronic one-way consumption of shared resources and apply lawful balancing responses before commons degradation occurs.
Theorem N.10 — Sustainable Commons Need Extraction Diagnostics
Without measuring use-to-contribution imbalance, a mesh cannot distinguish productive specialization from parasitic drain. Therefore extraction diagnostics are necessary. (\square)
The manuscript federation therefore tracks not only brilliance, but fairness.
N.C3 — Scarcity Policies, Rationing, and Graceful Denial
When demand exceeds sustainable supply, the mesh must ration gracefully. The framework therefore defines scarcity policies.
A scarcity policy for resource class (R) is:
[
\Pi_R^{\mathrm{scar}}
\Big(\operatorname{trigger},\operatorname{tiers},\operatorname{reservation},\operatorname{lottery_or_priority},\operatorname{graceful_deny},\psi_R\Big).]For example:
certifier review slots may reserve capacity for critical infrastructure and security incidents,
benchmark clusters may degrade noncritical runs to warm-lane replay,
bridge review queues may require stronger pre-review evidence when overloaded,
committee councils may rotate agenda priority by structural importance.
Graceful denial means that unsatisfied requests should receive:
explicit reason,
next eligible time or condition,
lower-burden alternative,
sandbox fallback,
or suggested contribution path to earn more entitlement.
Definition N.11 — Graceful Scarcity Response
A scarcity response is graceful if it denies or defers access in a way that remains transparent, lawful, and linked to alternative actions rather than silent starvation.
Theorem N.11 — Rationing Is Part of Good Governance
In a real commons, scarcity cannot always be avoided. The question is whether denial is arbitrary or policy-driven. Lawful scarcity policies ensure the latter. (\square)
The manuscript civilization therefore declines requests with structure, not contempt.
N.C4 — Economic Replay, Dispute Resolution, and Auditability
The final Cloud task is to ensure that the economic layer itself is replayable. Contributions, quotas, burdens, and disputes must not be merely social memory. They must be reconstructible.
The economic ledger is:
[
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{ledger}}
\Big(
\mathcal{E}{\mathrm{credits}},
\mathcal{E}{\mathrm{quotas}},
\mathcal{E}{\mathrm{burdens}},
\mathcal{E}{\mathrm{treaties}},
\mathcal{E}{\mathrm{stew}},
\mathcal{E}{\mathrm{disputes}}
\Big).
]
A dispute object is:
[
d
\Big(u_d,,\operatorname{claim}(d),,\operatorname{parties}(d),,\operatorname{scope}(d),,\operatorname{evidence}(d),,\operatorname{council}(d),,\operatorname{resolution}(d),,\psi_d\Big).]This allows:
replay of why a quota was tightened,
review of why a bridge stewardship role changed hands,
audit of why one brain gained or lost priority,
attestation of why a commons asset was deprecated.
Definition N.12 — Ledger-Replayable Sustainability Decision
A sustainability decision is ledger-replayable if its contribution, burden, quota, and dispute context are preserved well enough to reconstruct the decision later.
Theorem N.12 — Economics Must Be Replayable to Be Legitimate
A resource or incentive system that cannot explain its own decisions will drift toward opaque power. Replayable ledgers preserve legitimacy. (\square)
The manuscript federation therefore keeps economic memory as carefully as epistemic memory.
N.D1 — Recursive Commons and Meta-Stewardship
The Fractal sustainability layer begins with the fact that the commons itself must be stewarded by higher-order commons. For example:
treaty templates need stewarding,
benchmark registries need meta-benchmark governance,
stewardship roles themselves need rotation and review,
value functions and quotas need meta-calibration.
This creates recursive commons.
A meta-stewardship object is:
[
S^\star
\Big(u_{S^\star},,\operatorname{target_commons}(S^\star),,\operatorname{target_stewards}(S^\star),,\operatorname{review_cadence}(S^\star),,\operatorname{rotation}(S^\star),,\operatorname{succession}(S^\star),,\psi_{S^\star}\Big).]The key principle is that no commons steward should be permanently beyond stewardship review. This prevents economic and infrastructural capture of the federation.
Definition N.13 — Meta-Stewarded Commons
A commons is meta-stewarded if its own stewards, quotas, and value rules are subject to explicit higher-order maintenance and rotation mechanisms.
Theorem N.13 — Sustainable Commons Need Stewards of Stewards
Since stewards themselves can drift, overload, or capture their domain, higher-order stewardship is necessary to keep the sustainability layer healthy over long horizons. (\square)
The manuscript civilization therefore builds care for caretakers into its law.
N.D2 — Long-Horizon Sustainability Memory and Institutional Learning
The federation also needs institutional memory of what made commons sustainable or brittle over long periods. This differs from hardening memory or lineage memory. It tracks:
successful treaty patterns,
quota regimes that balanced well,
overcentralization episodes,
review-bottleneck collapses,
stewardship successes and failures,
incentive misalignment incidents.
A sustainability memory object is:
[
M_{\mathrm{sust}}
\Big(u_M,,\operatorname{period}(M),,\operatorname{commons}(M),,\operatorname{policies}(M),,\operatorname{metrics}(M),,\operatorname{outcomes}(M),,\operatorname{lessons}(M),,\psi_M\Big).]Over time these memories are used to:
tune value functions,
redesign quotas,
rotate stewardship earlier,
reclassify critical resources,
improve treaty templates.
Definition N.14 — Institutionally Learning Federation
A federation is institutionally learning if it preserves replayable memory of sustainability outcomes and changes its economic or stewardship policies in response.
Theorem N.14 — Stable Commons Need Historical Learning
Without memory of prior economic and stewardship failures, the federation risks rediscovering the same extractive or overcentralized patterns repeatedly. (\square)
The manuscript civilization therefore learns from how it sustains itself, not only from how it thinks.
N.D3 — Economic Scaling from One Brain to Civilization
The sustainability architecture also scales. A single brain may already have internal resource and stewardship flows. A federation generalizes them. A federation of federations generalizes them further.
Let the scale ladder be:[\mathfrak{B}\to\mathfrak{F}^{(1)}\to\mathfrak{F}^{(2)}\to\cdots]At each scale, the same core constructs recur:
resources,
contributions,
quotas,
stewards,
ledgers,
councils,
commons.
This is the economic counterpart of recursive federation from Appendix M. It implies that the manuscript architecture can scale not only technically and politically, but also institutionally and economically.
Definition N.15 — Recursively Sustainable Manuscript Civilization
A manuscript civilization is recursively sustainable if its resource, credit, stewardship, and replayable economic law scale from one brain to many-brain federations without hidden dependency on extra-architectural informal support.
Theorem N.15 — Sustainability Must Scale with Federation
If governance, replay, and diplomacy scale upward but resource law does not, then the larger mesh will become brittle or extractive. Therefore sustainability must be recursive. (\square)
The manuscript civilization therefore can persist as it grows.
N.D4 — Final Appendix N Theorem
Theorem N.16 — Commons Sustainability Sufficiency Theorem
Let the manuscript brain be fully specified through Chapters 1–21 and Appendices A–M. If, in addition, the system maintains:
typed resource classes,
replayable contribution credits,
quota and entitlement objects,
explicit stewardship roles,
value-aligned incentive functions,
burden balancing and scarcity policies,
anti-extractive diagnostics,
replayable economic ledgers,
recursive commons and meta-stewardship,
long-horizon sustainability memory,
and recursive scaling of these structures across federations,
then the system possesses a sufficient resource and incentive layer to sustain a federated manuscript-brain civilization without defaulting into extractive or unsustainable dynamics.
Proof.Typed resources define what is scarce.Credits trace contribution.Quotas and entitlements bound use.Stewardship roles maintain shared assets.Value functions align incentives with epistemic and infrastructural good.Burden balancing and scarcity policies prevent overload and starvation.Anti-extractive diagnostics detect imbalance.Replayable ledgers and disputes preserve legitimacy.Recursive commons and sustainability memory allow these laws to persist and adapt across scales.Therefore the system can remain durable as a civilization of brains rather than only a temporary federation of convenience. (\square)
Corollary N.16.1 — Federation without Sustainability Is Incomplete
Appendix M showed how brains can cooperate. Appendix N shows how that cooperation can last.
Corollary N.16.2 — The Architecture Now Includes Political Economy
With this appendix, the manuscript brain stack now includes:
ontology,
routing,
memory,
legality,
runtime,
embodiment,
verification,
governance,
hardening,
federation,
and sustainability.
This concludes Appendix N. The manuscript brain is now not only an individual cognitive organism and a lawful federated society, but also an economically and institutionally sustainable one.
